# Negative Evidence Ledger

This is the canonical no-gaps ledger entry point requested by the bold-verify
campaign. Historical rows live in `docs/progress/perf-negative-results.md`; new
campaign closeouts should either update that file directly or add a summary row
here that points to the detailed progress ledger.

> **CAMPAIGN COMPLETE (2026-07-10).** The consolidated capstone — shipped wins by
> axis + every reject with ID · null-control · retry-condition — is
> `docs/PERF_CAMPAIGN_FINAL.md`. All 5 axes closed; HOLD, no more solo
> lever-hunting. The detailed prose rows below remain the source of truth.

| 2026-07-13 | `bd-k2wc7` — ext4 extent index-node range-prefix bound / Codex | Every depth>0 `walk_range` descent parsed a strictly sorted index node, then linearly rejected each child ending at or before the requested start. External 4 KiB index nodes can carry 340 child separators, and the path serves range mapping, punch-hole, collapse-range, insert-range, and mark-written collection. | **KEEP.** Changes the per-index-node skipped-prefix cost from O(entries) to O(log entries). Child selection, recursion/visitor order, returned count, error propagation, half-open boundaries, and corrupt-empty-node behavior are unchanged. Ordering preserved: yes. | 2026-07-11 | `bd-bhh0i`/`bd-opb6l`/cross-path — write→commit→journal profile (`write-bench`, production-ISA/perf) = the CROSS-CUTTING structural cost is the MVCC block-copy / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **SURFACE — the unifying frontier finding: every hot path bottoms out at the MVCC store copying blocks.** Fresh single-thread write→commit→journal profile (write-bench 40k×4KiB --create, v3 symbols, perf -g): top self-time = `__memmove/memcpy_avx` **15.56%** (MVCC versioning + `FsMvccStore::flush_to_device` block copies), then `ShardedMvccStore::commit` 4.16%, `__memcmp_avx2` 3.55% (version-dedup identical-check), `BTreeMap::IntoIter::dying_next` 2.13% (per-commit write_set teardown), jemalloc ~5% (per-write staged-block allocs), `flush_to_device`+`write_block`+`serialize_inode_into`+`alloc_blocks_persist` ~2%. This MATCHES the create profile (write_inode memmove 9.71%) and the read profile (per-block anon alloc 45%): **the recurring dominant cost across create/read/write/getattr is the MVCC store's per-block COPY (staged Vec<u8> per write, block memmove on version install + flush, per-block Arc alloc on read) — NOT a per-path issue.** No byte/ULP-exact micro-lever survives on any path (create/read/write mined to this bedrock; narrowing/parent-defer/arena/thread_local/copy-elim all rejected). | N/A — cross-path profile confirmation, no A/B | **NO byte-exact lever on any hot path**; the gap is the MVCC block-copy architecture | THE structural lever = reduce the MVCC per-block copy: move-not-copy staged blocks into the version chain + copy-on-write / borrowed flush instead of `to_vec` per block (ffs-mvcc versioning/flush redesign) — a big multi-turn correctness-critical effort that would lift ALL write paths at once | write-bench + perf -g v3 symbolized; retry-condition = an ffs-mvcc copy-reduction redesign (owned-buffer handoff on stage_write→commit→install), loom+e2fsck-gated |
| 2026-07-11 | `bd-b9dug` + `bd-bhh0i` — production-ISA rebench + parallel-create contention profile + jemalloc arena-tuning reject / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **STRUCTURAL-target validation: the negative create scaling is REAL (not a build artifact), but the vs-kernel gap is ~1.2-1.4x SMALLER than the opt-z/SSE2 benchmark stated.** (1) **bd-b9dug**: built create-bench on production ISA (`RUSTFLAGS=-C target-cpu=x86-64-v3` + opt-3 + lto=fat, minus PGO) vs the default opt-z/SSE2 build. INTERLEAVED (drift-canceled) A/B: v3 is **1.18-1.40x faster** in absolute throughput at every thread count (t1 74k->93k, t8 46k->54k), so the benchmark binary UNDERSTATES production and the "8.3x@8t" gap is inflated. BUT scaling SHAPE is unchanged: v3 t8/t1 = **0.58x** vs opt-z 0.62x — both negatively scale, so the contention is real + ISA-independent (a first non-interleaved read falsely showed v3 scaling positively = load drift). (2) **CONTENTION PROFILE** (perf -g, v3 symbols, --threads 8, count 16000): the negative scaling = the alloc WRITE lock fully serializing each create (create_inode + add_dir_entry all under it) + overhead (kernel futex ~6% + `parking_lot::RawRwLock::{lock,unlock}_exclusive_slow` ~1.94%). Biggest CPU cost = `ffs_inode::write_inode`'s inherent 4KB block RMW memmove (**9.71%**, `buf.as_slice().to_vec()` @ ffs-inode lib.rs:136, from a SHARED cache Arc so `make_mut` COWs identically = the already-rejected write-path-copy-elim class). MVCC commit 3.25%, dir insert 2.45%, arena (`edata_heap_remove`+`sdallocx`) ~2%, crc 1.03%. (3) **jemalloc ARENA-TUNING** (bead's malloc-arena angle, byte-identical env `_RJEM_MALLOC_CONF`, interleaved t8): `narenas:32` = 1.058x (inside the 23.7% baseline noise), `background_thread:true` 1.015x, `dirty_decay_ms:0` = 0.426x (big LOSS) -> **REJECT** (marginal, not robust; arena is only ~2% of cycles). | **v3 1.18-1.40x faster absolute; scaling 0.58x (v3) vs 0.62x (opt-z) both negative; arena narenas:32 1.058x within noise** | **REJECT arena-tuning; bd-b9dug VALIDATED (bench understates 1.2-1.4x)** | negative scaling CONFIRMED real under production ISA = alloc-write-lock full serialization; write_inode memmove inherent (shared-cache COW); micro-levers exhausted -> only structural fix = PER-GROUP ALLOCATOR SHARDING (split the single alloc RwLock into per-block-group locks so disjoint-group creates don't serialize) | v3 build (target-cpu=x86-64-v3+opt3+lto), interleaved drift-canceled A/B (cv 4-37% under swarm load), perf -g symbolized profile, env-A/B arena tuning; retry-condition = per-group allocator shard (bd-bhh0i structural, multi-turn) |

| 2026-07-10 | `bd-cc-inode-csum-stamp` CLOSE — write-side inode checksum stamp already no-copy / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **Resolved the one pending checksum candidate from the session frontier row: the WRITE-side inode-checksum STAMP.** Read `compute_and_set_checksum` (`crates/ffs-inode/src/lib.rs:270`, the sole write-side stamp, called by `write_inode` @132). It is **no-copy**: it holds `&mut [u8]`, zeroes `i_checksum_lo` (0x7C) and `i_checksum_hi` (0x82) IN PLACE, then does ONE `ext4_chksum(ino_seed, raw)` pass over the whole inode. That is strictly better than the READ-side `verify_inode_checksum` (ext4.rs:2113), which needs THREE segmented `ext4_chksum` calls only because it holds `&[u8]` and cannot zero in place. So there is no copy-vs-segment lever on either side — both are already optimal for their borrow. Cross-checked two neighboring candidates while here: (a) `locate_inode` div→shift strength-reduction is ALREADY-REFUTED (row 2026-07-05, measured 1.00x, DIV throughput hidden by loop ILP; the untracked `benches/locate_inode.rs` is that rejection's documented throwaway, retained not deleted); (b) the skeletal `AttrOnly` metadata parse (skip the 60 B extent copy for non-device getattr) is ALREADY-LANDED (`8314ca8c`, 1.11x, wired at ffs-core `read_inode_attr` lib.rs:10882) — a stale "future candidate" note. **VERDICT: NO LEVER — checksum axis closed (verify + stamp both no-copy); no code changed.** | N/A — code-read close, no A/B needed (no copy exists to eliminate) | **NO LEVER** — stamp already single-pass zero-in-place | N/A | code-read verification of `compute_and_set_checksum`/`verify_inode_checksum`; ledger cross-check confirms locate_inode refuted + skeletal-parse landed; allocation confirmed most-recently-active PEER lane (last 5 `ffs-alloc` commits all `perf(alloc)`); no production/bench/output bytes changed; no local Cargo |

LZO's fresh `Vec` is only the ~4 KiB output, and the LZO1X decode itself is ~2.4 µs/segment (CPU-bound), so the ~7 ns alloc is **<0.3%** — the decode dominates, reuse is noise. **LESSON:** the per-op-allocation-reuse lever wins ONLY when the allocated object is a large FIXED cost relative to the per-op work (deflate window), NOT when it scales with a decode-dominated payload (LZO output). Measure-don't-reason: the '32 allocs/extent' looked like a lever, wasn't. The log shows rch retrieving from its **own** remote dir `/data/projects/frankenfs/.rch-target-<worker>-pool-<hash>` and classifying it as *"Custom CARGO_TARGET_DIR"*. **rch always creates a custom remote target dir, so its custom-target-dir retrieval path — which ships metadata only — is always taken. `env -u CARGO_TARGET_DIR` unsets the *caller's* var (verified) but not rch's own.** `check`/`test` are unaffected (they stream diagnostics, not artifacts). **UNBLOCK = fix rch artifact retrieval for its own remote target dir, or grant one local build.** Until then `bd-ddryj` (18% win) and `bd-zvn7r`(a) both sit blocked. **NO OTHER LEVER REMAINS** — after the cap the residual is 4.67 ms (1.13x): `_copy_to_iter` **12.28%** is paid by the `warm_dst` floor too (which lands at 0.99x of kernel), removing it needs O_DIRECT **measured at 1.00x**; residual `spin_lock_slowpath` **9.32%** is what the cap already cut from 42.27%; per-thread fd cuts insertions 4.2x for **no wall** (p=0.18); `Stealer::steal` 1.13% and `ext4_mpage_readpages` 0.69% are below threshold. **The cold-read lane is closed apart from landing `bd-ddryj`.** | **WIN 1.2095x** (null 1.0232x); vs kernel **1.40x → 1.13x** | effect/null = **8x** the noise floor; 9/9 paired, p=0.0039 | DECIDED: take `bd-ddryj`; **not landed — blocked on artifact retrieval** | **FULL PROVENANCE:** binary `sha256=03b7456d8cd6fa118bd214b2fdf8a03e56cac79e6768b7311613b039c8ae81eb` (`release-perf`, 55,453,584 B, built 04:02:59); allocator `tikv_jemallocator`; **self-time of the function the lever removes** = `native_queued_spin_lock_slowpath` **42.27%** (T=64) → **9.32%** (T=16); **null-control ratio 1.0232x, cv 3.8%/10.4%**; effect cv 4.6%; worker = **local host** (`perf`+`drop_caches` need root — no remote worker possible); `rch` verify worker **`ovh-a`**; `bd-xqzgy` green **6/6** via mandatory recipe. **Zero local cargo builds; disk 76G.** |
| 2026-07-10 | **SCOPE OF THE HARNESS CORRECTION + LEVER VERDICT** / `bd-zvn7r` `bd-ddryj` `bd-kdmu4` / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **DIRECTION OF BIAS: the 64 MiB first-touch is paid ONLY by `ffs-cli read`; kernel/raw-`pread` arms use small buffers. Harness inflation therefore always makes frankenfs look SLOWER, never faster — so EVERY "frankenfs is slower than kernel" verdict in this ledger is CONSERVATIVE AND STANDS. Only the MAGNITUDES are upper bounds; no sign-flip is at risk, and no "frankenfs is faster" claim can be resurrected.** **CONCLUSIONS DRAWN AGAINST THE INFLATED NUMBER** (all used `ffs-cli read` engine time, hence a first-touch of `min(file_size, 64 MiB)`): `bd-q6k00` ext4 extent 128 MiB **1.42x** → sign stands, magnitude is an upper bound; `bd-5koeh` ext4 indirect 50 MiB **1.45x** → same; `bd-5koeh` ext4 fragmented 48 MiB **1.31x** → same; `bd-ddryj` baseline **1.54x / 1.25x** → **SUPERSEDED, measured 1.41x / 1.15x**; `bd-zvn7r` "94% of residual is frankenfs-attributable" → **SUPERSEDED** (floor arm mismatched the destination policy); `bd-zvn7r` "new #1 frame = anon-page churn 28.91%" → **that cluster IS the harness**. I did **not** rescale the indirect/fragmented rows by the 3.10 ms constant: that would be a projection, and projections have been falsified twice on this workload. **⚠️ THE 2.9x MULTI-FILE FIGURE IS *NOT* CORRECTED TO 1.41x — different workload, different harness, and I checked rather than assumed.** `bd-kdmu4`'s headline is **multi-file parallel read** (256×256 KiB, `walk --read-data --parallel`) vs an in-process threaded C reader; my constant is `ffs-cli read`'s **single-file** 64 MiB `STREAM_CHUNK` buffer. They do not transfer: **`walk_one_dir` already reuses one buffer per rayon worker** (`ffs-cli/src/main.rs:3055-3060`, `map_init`; per-file fresh-`Vec` churn fixed in `bd-2x68s`) — the multi-file harness lacks the allocation pattern I measured. **BUT it carries its own author-acknowledged harness component:** the 2026-06-22 CrimsonFox entry records the post-fix profile as `pread` 43.6% plus *"~25% OUTER `walk_one_dir` per-inode `par_iter` coordination … a real FUSE mount dispatches each getattr/read as a separate per-request worker, never via this nested rayon, **so it's a harness artifact not a real-fs cost**"*. So the multi-file number is **partly instrumentation by its own admission (~25%), via a DIFFERENT mechanism**, and its residual real-fs figure was **never isolated**. It needs its own audit against the three gates. **Restating it as 1.41x would repeat, in the opposite direction, the exact error this ledger exists to prevent.** `bd-kdmu4`: **RESOLVED** on O_DIRECT (bypass measured **1.00x**), **UNAUDITED** on its 2.9x premise. **IS THE REMAINING GAP WORTH A LEVER? — YES for exactly one, NO beyond it.** Honest harness-corrected single-file numbers: ffs as-shipped **39.78 ms = 1.41x**; ffs fan-out-capped **32.67 ms = 1.15x**; raw pread into a warm destination **28.00 ms = 0.99x**; kernel dio **28.30 ms**. **YES:** `bd-ddryj` (bound the read fan-out) converts **1.41x → 1.15x**, an **18% wall reduction on the shipped default**, no unsafe, no policy change, 9/9 paired reps (p=0.0039) — blocked only on the build. **NO:** after the cap the residual is **4.67 ms (1.15x)** and the corrected frame table contains no lever — `_copy_to_iter` **12.28%** is paid by the `warm_dst` floor too (which still lands at kernel parity), and removing it needs O_DIRECT/mmap **measured at 1.00x**; `native_queued_spin_lock_slowpath` **9.32%** is the residue the cap already cut from 42.27%, and per-thread fd cuts insertions 4.2x for **no wall** (p=0.18); `Stealer::steal` 1.13% and `ext4_mpage_readpages` 0.69% are below any actionable threshold. **RECOMMENDATION: land `bd-ddryj`, fix the harness (`bd-zvn7r`a), and STOP hunting the single-file cold read** — at 1.15x of a direct-I/O kernel mount, with the raw floor itself at 0.99x, there is no headroom worth a further lever. | **HONEST: 1.41x shipped → 1.15x with the cap**; raw floor 0.99x | **one lever left (`bd-ddryj`, 18% of the shipped wall); none beyond it** | scope: my `ffs-cli read` rows corrected; `bd-kdmu4`'s 2.9x **unaudited, NOT restated** | **METADATA:** binary `sha256=03b7456d8cd6fa118bd214b2fdf8a03e56cac79e6768b7311613b039c8ae81eb` (`release-perf`, 55,453,584 B); allocator `tikv_jemallocator`; self-time of function under test `clear_page_erms` **11.81%** (cluster 28.91%); worker = **local host** (`perf`/`drop_caches` need root); `rch` verify worker **`hz2`**; cv 7.7 / 8.6 / 10.7 / 5.7 / 8.8%. Gates identity+magnitude+impossibility all PASS. `bd-xqzgy` green (**6/6**, `hz2`, mandatory recipe). **Zero local cargo builds.** |
| 2026-07-10 | `bd-zvn7r` **HARNESS QUANTIFIED** / `bd-ddryj` / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **41% OF THE BEST-CONFIG COLD-READ GAP vs KERNEL EXT4 IS HARNESS OVERHEAD — measured at 3.10 ms, not projected. Honest gaps: ffs as-shipped 1.41x (was reported 1.52x), best-config 1.15x (was 1.26x).** **Repro metadata:** binary `sha256=03b7456d8cd6fa118bd214b2fdf8a03e56cac79e6768b7311613b039c8ae81eb` (`release-perf`, 55,453,584 B, built 04:02:59); allocator `tikv_jemallocator` (`ffs-cli/src/main.rs:8`); worker = **local host** (`perf`+`drop_caches` need root — no remote worker possible); `rch` verify worker `vmi1149989`; cv per arm below; **zero local cargo builds**. **HARNESS REBUILT** (the earlier `bigbuf` floor was inadmissible — it timed *slower* than the thing it floored; two bugs, both mine: `bytearray(n)` **memsets** in CPython, and the allocation sat **inside** the timed region): `preader5.py` uses `mmap.mmap(-1,n)` (kernel lazily zero-fills, exactly what jemalloc's `alloc_zeroed` gets — no memset), hoists destination allocation **and** all chunk-list construction **out** of the timed region, uses one persistent pre-warmed `ThreadPoolExecutor` (no thread spawn inside the timer), keeps `drop_caches` outside the timer, and consumes the result via an XOR checksum so nothing can be elided. Two arms differ **only** in destination warmth: `cold_dst` (fresh mmap inside the timer → pays the 64 MiB first-touch, exactly as `ffs-cli read`'s `vec![0u8; 64 MiB]` does) vs `warm_dst` (allocated + pre-faulted before the timer → reads only). **ALL THREE VALIDITY GATES PASS:** *identity* (same XOR checksum; bytes `sha256`-identical to the kernel mount); *magnitude* (`cold_dst` **18,090** faults vs ffs's **17,467** — mechanism reproduced); *impossibility* (`cold_dst` **31.10 ms** < ffs **35.77 ms** — a floor must be faster than what it floors). **MEASUREMENT (9 interleaved reps, medians):** ffs T=64 **42.88 ms** (cv 7.7%); ffs T=16 **35.77 ms** (cv 8.6%); floor `cold_dst` **31.10 ms** (cv 10.7%); floor `warm_dst` **28.00 ms** (cv 5.7%); kernel dio loop T=32 **28.30 ms** (cv 8.8%). **→ destination first-touch = `cold_dst` − `warm_dst` = 3.10 ms.** **HONEST GAP:** T=64 reported **1.52x** → **1.41x** (harness = 3.10/14.58 = **21%** of gap); T=16 reported **1.26x** → **1.15x** (harness = 3.10/7.47 = **41%** of gap). Also `warm_dst` **28.00 ms** ≈ kernel **28.30 ms**: **a raw parallel `pread` into a warm destination is already at kernel parity**, so the residual filesystem overhead is 35.77 − 3.10 − 28.00 = **4.67 ms**. **⚠️ SCOPE — DO NOT OVER-CLAIM:** this invalidates **my own** `ffs-cli read`-based cold numbers by 21–41% of their gap. It does **NOT** establish that `bd-kdmu4`'s headline **"~2.9x slower than kernel"** is an artifact — that came from a **different** harness ("multi-file parallel read, in-process threaded", claimed 41% pread copy tax + 27% nested-rayon coordination) which I have **not** audited. It is **suspect by association**, needs its own audit against the same three gates, and calling it an artifact without measuring it would repeat the exact error this ledger exists to prevent. **`bd-kdmu4` stays RESOLVED on the O_DIRECT question** (bypass measured at **1.00x**); its 2.9x premise is **UNAUDITED, not refuted.** **TOP FRAME, harness attributed:** the 28.91% anon alloc/fault/zero cluster **is the harness**. What remains for real filesystem work at T=16: `_copy_to_iter` **12.28%** — *also paid by the `warm_dst` floor*, so it cannot explain the residual; `native_queued_spin_lock_slowpath` **9.32%** (residual `xa_lock`, already cut from 42.27% by the fan-out cap); `crossbeam Stealer::steal` **1.13%**; `ext4_mpage_readpages` 0.69%. The 4.67 ms residual is frankenfs's own userspace work (user CPU at T=16 is **4.5 ms**, same order) and `perf` cannot resolve it further above the 0.1% cut. **Next step needs a build:** fix `STREAM_CHUNK` / pre-fault outside the timed region, then re-profile. | **HONEST: ffs 1.41x (shipped) / 1.15x (best config)** vs kernel cold; harness = **21% / 41%** of gap | harness constant **3.10 ms** measured; `warm_dst` floor **at kernel parity** | ⚠️ corrects my own reported 1.52x/1.26x; `bd-kdmu4`'s 2.9x **unaudited** | **METADATA:** binary `sha256=03b7456d…81eb`; self-time of function under test = `clear_page_erms` **11.81%** (cluster 28.91%); worker local host, `rch` verify `vmi1149989`; cv 7.7 / 8.6 / 10.7 / 5.7 / 8.8%. Gates: identity + magnitude (18,090 vs 17,467 faults) + impossibility (31.10 < 35.77) all **PASS**. `bd-xqzgy` green (`RCH_REQUIRE_REMOTE=1 env -u CARGO_TARGET_DIR rch exec -- cargo test`, **6/6**, `vmi1149989`). Zero local cargo builds. |

The anon-page cluster is `ffs-cli read`'s 64 MiB staging buffer — a FUSE mount never allocates it.** **Frame named with self-time** (binary `sha256=03b7456d8cd6fa118bd214b2fdf8a03e56cac79e6768b7311613b039c8ae81eb`, `release-perf`, 55,453,584 B; allocator `tikv_jemallocator` @ `ffs-cli/src/main.rs:8`; worker = local host — `perf`+`drop_caches` need root; `rch` verify worker `hz1`): `_copy_to_iter` **12.28%**, `clear_page_erms` **11.81%**, `native_queued_spin_lock_slowpath` **9.32%**, `asm_exc_page_fault` **5.74%**, `rmqueue_bulk` **2.19%**, `do_anonymous_page` **1.57%**, `zap_present_ptes` **1.13%**, `get_page_from_freelist` **0.94%**, `__alloc_frozen_pages` **0.80%**, `__mem_cgroup_charge` **0.77%** → **anon alloc/fault/zero cluster = 28.91% of cycles.** **TRACED TO AN EXACT LINE:** `ffs-cli read` streams via `STREAM_CHUNK = 64 MiB` through one reused `vec![0_u8; 64 MiB]` (`crates/ffs-cli/src/main.rs:2403-2407`). First-touching it faults **16,384 pages**, each `clear_page_erms`-zeroed then immediately overwritten. **Confirmed numerically on the real binary:** `ffs read /small.bin` (4 KiB) = **1,008** faults (process baseline); `ffs read /big.bin` (128 MiB) = **17,474**; difference **16,466 ≈ 16,384 = 64 MiB / 4 KiB**. A per-chunk destination would have faulted **32,768**. And `ffs-block::read_exact_at` already skips the staging buffer for large reads (`lib.rs:573-592`) — **the engine is not the source.** ⚠️ **THEREFORE MY OWN BASELINE (row below) IS CONTAMINATED:** it credited "+6.47 ms frankenfs-attributable, 94% of the residual", but the floor arm preaded into a **128 KiB** buffer (**2,338** faults, glibc-recycled) while ffs first-touches **64 MiB** (**17,474** faults). The kernel comparator never pays that. **The engine-only gap is smaller than 6.47 ms and is currently UNMEASURED**, and the headline gaps (T=64 **1.54x**, T=16 **1.25x**) include this harness constant. **A FAIR FLOOR THAT FAILED THE IMPOSSIBILITY CHECK — INADMISSIBLE, NO REJECT RECORDED:** rebuilding the floor with ffs's real 64 MiB destination policy (`preader4.py bigbuf`, `sha256` identical) **reproduced the mechanism** (17,593 faults vs 17,471) but timed **65.90 ms (cv 16.0%)** against ffs's **35.57 ms (cv 12.2%)** — smallbuf floor 30.60 ms (cv 26.6%), kernel dio 27.50 ms (cv 10.0%), 7 interleaved reps. **A floor cannot be slower than the thing it floors**; the 65.90 ms is python overhead (per-chunk `memoryview` slicing, window rebuild, GIL), so its implied "+35.30 ms destination cost" is **discarded, not recorded**. Same impossibility check that first caught the loop-device artifact in `bd-q6k00`. **Fault count valid; wall time invalid — the 64 MiB first-touch cost stays UNMEASURED and MUST NOT be projected from the 28.91% cycle share** (that method was falsified on this workload: `bd-kdmu4` predicted 1.12–1.85x, measured **1.00x**). **RE-SCOPE:** (a) *harness hygiene* — `STREAM_CHUNK=64 MiB` inflates **every** `ffs-cli read` cold number by an unmeasured constant no kernel arm pays; shrink it, pre-fault outside the timed region, or subtract it; (b) *the real question, still open* — does the **FUSE** read path allocate per-chunk destinations? Must be profiled through the mount, not through `ffs-cli read`. | ⚠️ prior 1.54x / 1.25x are **upper bounds** — they include the harness's 64 MiB first-touch | anon cluster **28.91%** is harness, not engine | ⚠️ corrects my own "94% frankenfs-attributable" | **REJECT METADATA (new rule):** binary `sha256=03b7456d…81eb`; self-time of function under test = `clear_page_erms` **11.81%**, cluster **28.91%**; worker = local host (root-only `perf`/`drop_caches`), `rch` verify on `hz1`; cv per arm 12.2 / 16.0 / 26.6 / 10.0%. The kernel-side story is closed — `xa_lock`, insertions, readahead, granularity and the bypass are all done. **FRESH RANKED FRAME TABLE (ffs, T=16, ≥0.1% self-time):** `_copy_to_iter` **12.28%**, **`clear_page_erms` 11.81%**, `native_queued_spin_lock_slowpath` **9.32%** (was **42.27%** at T=64), `asm_exc_page_fault` 5.74%, `rmqueue_bulk` 2.19%, `__filemap_add_folio` 2.02%, `lru_gen_add_folio` 1.67%, `do_anonymous_page` 1.57%, `mod_memcg_lruvec_state` 1.44%, `crossbeam Stealer::steal` 1.13% (user), `zap_present_ptes` 1.13%, `up_read` 1.09%, `get_page_from_freelist` 0.94%, `__alloc_frozen_pages` 0.80%, `__mem_cgroup_charge` 0.77%, `ext4_mpage_readpages` 0.69%. CPU split: **user 4.5 ms vs sys 188 ms** — frankenfs's userspace code is nearly free; what it costs is the **kernel work its allocation pattern provokes**. **NEW #1 FRAME OWNER: the anonymous-page alloc/fault/zero cluster = 28.91% of cycles**, with **17,459 page faults (17,385 minor) for a read of 32,768 destination pages** — a fresh destination buffer per chunk is faulted, zero-filled, then immediately overwritten by `_copy_to_iter`. Filed as **`bd-zvn7r`**. **RECORDED SO NOBODY CHASES IT AGAIN:** at the production chunk, per-thread fd cuts insertions **16,430 → 3,895 (4.2x; 16.00 → 3.79 per read)** and buys **NO wall time** (1.025x median, 7/9 paired, **p=0.1797, not significant**) — **per-reader `struct file` is a CPU-efficiency change, not a latency fix.** **A PROXY THAT FAILED ITS OWN VALIDITY CHECK — NO REJECT RECORDED:** my raw-pread buffer-reuse proxy (alloc-per-chunk vs reused per-thread buffer, `sha256` identical) showed reuse *slower* (0.957x median, **1/7** paired, p=1.0), but its alloc arm produced only **2,364 page faults vs frankenfs's 17,459** (7.4x fewer) because glibc's **dynamic** mmap threshold makes CPython recycle the freed 128 KiB block. **The proxy never exercises the mechanism, so its null is inadmissible** — the ledger-integrity rule (`5feb977`) applied to a *proxy*: the arm must reproduce the mechanism's **magnitude**, not just its shape. Buffer reuse is **UNTESTED for frankenfs, not refuted**. **DO NOT PROJECT** the 28.91% cycle share into a wall win — that method was already falsified on this exact workload (`bd-kdmu4`: predicted 1.12–1.85x, measured **1.00x**). | **ffs as-shipped 1.54x kernel; best-config 1.25x**; page-cache path now only +0.40 ms | fan-out cap reconfirmed **9/9, p=0.0039**; per-thread fd **null on wall** | new #1 frame = anon-page churn **28.91%**, `bd-zvn7r` | **CODE-EXECUTED PROOF** (`5feb977`): every frame above carries its self-time; anon cluster 28.91%; 17,459 faults vs 32,768 pages; knob engaged (insertions 16.00→3.79/read). **SUBSTRATE-v2:** arms interleaved *inside* each rep, no criterion, no `black_box` (results consumed via `sha256`/XOR/byte counts). `bd-xqzgy` re-verified via `RCH_REQUIRE_REMOTE=1 env -u CARGO_TARGET_DIR rch exec -- cargo test` (remote `vmi1227854`, **6/6**). **Zero local cargo builds.** ⚠️ `bd-zvn7r`/`bd-ddryj` in-tree proof still blocked: rch build returns no artifact. |

| 2026-07-10 | `bd-kdmu4` **OWNER DECISION — CHAIN CLOSED** / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **THE PROJECTION WAS COMPUTED AS REQUESTED, AND IT FALSIFIES ITSELF. Measurement says the bypass buys 0%.** **(1) Per-READ instrumentation** (the requested "where does each insertion originate per read"): a 128 KiB read spans 32 pages, so **32 insertions/read is the one-folio-per-block ceiling**. On the real binary at production chunk (`pread` count constant 1,034): T=1/2/4/8/16/32/**64** → **2.16 / 9.81 / 12.21 / 14.64 / 17.32 / 22.51 / 26.28 insertions per read** = 6.7% / 30.7% / 38.1% / 45.7% / 54.1% / 70.3% / **82.1% of the ceiling**. So "one folio per block" is **82% true — but only at 64 threads**; it is a symptom of concurrency on a shared `f_ra`, not a property of the path. **(2) CAN frankenfs reduce insertions without bypass? YES — 4.2x, at the production chunk, pure buffered** (same T=16, same 1,027 preads, only fd sharing differs): shared fd **16,430 insertions = 16.00/read (50.0% of ceiling)** → per-thread fd **3,895 = 3.79/read (11.9%)**. 100% of insertions originate in `page_cache_ra_order`/`page_cache_ra_unbounded`; `__filemap_get_folio` ≈ 0, so **there is no per-block `filemap_create_folio` fallback that only a bypass could avoid.** The premise "insertions are irreducible without O_DIRECT/mmap" is **REFUTED**; the antecedent for escalating `bd-kdmu4` does not hold. **(3) THE PROJECTION, computed both ways.** *Model A* (Amdahl on spin-wait self-time): T=64 → 37.0 ms × (1−0.4227) = **21.4 ms = 1.73x**; T=16 → 30.1 × (1−0.1096) = **26.8 ms = 1.12x**. *Model B* (aggregate spin-wait ÷ threads, subtracted from wall): T=64 @1M → 1,143 ms/64 = 17.9 ms/thread, wall 38.8 → **20.9 ms = 1.85x**; T=64 @256K → 3,414/64 = 53.3 ms/thread, wall 38.3 → **−15.0 ms, a NEGATIVE WALL TIME.** **Model B is self-refuting and Model A inherits the same defect.** **(4) THE MEASUREMENT** (O_DIRECT eliminates insertions entirely; raw harness, page-aligned `mmap`, `sha256` == kernel mount, no frankenfs code touched): T=16 O_DIRECT **25.7 ms** vs buffered per-thread fd **25.5 ms** = **1.00x (−1%)**; T=64 O_DIRECT 28.4 ms; device floor = raw buffered `pread` T=32 **28.3 ms**, kernel dio loop **26.9 ms**. **Projection 1.12–1.85x. Measurement 1.00x.** Spin-wait is CPU burned by threads *already blocked on the device* — overlapped with I/O, never on the critical path; wall is bounded below by device bandwidth (128 MiB at ~4,700–5,000 MiB/s = 25.5–27 ms). **(5) OWNER DECISION.** After the fan-out cap frankenfs is **30.1 ms vs the kernel's 26.9 ms**: total remaining headroom **3.2 ms (1.12x)**, of which **O_DIRECT captures 0 ms**. Its sole benefit is **1.86x CPU** (323M→174M cycles). **`bd-kdmu4` CANNOT be justified on latency grounds — approve it only if ~150M cycles per 128 MiB read is worth an audited-unsafe/policy change on CPU-efficiency grounds. | 2026-07-10 | `bd-kdmu4` DECISIVE EVIDENCE (owner decision surfaced) / `bd-ddryj` / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | ⚠️ *(the 8.8x insertion figure below is at a **1 MiB** proxy chunk; at the production 128 KiB chunk it is 4.2x and yields no significant wall gain — see the SELF-CORRECTION row above. The insertion-origin and O_DIRECT findings are unaffected.)* **frankenfs CAN reduce folio insertions 8.8x WITHOUT bypassing the page cache. The "only O_DIRECT/mmap can help" conclusion is REFUTED, and O_DIRECT buys 0% of wall time.** Two premises corrected first: **(i) FUSE is not in this path** — `ffs-cli read` `pread`s the image file directly, there is no FUSE round-trip to blame; **(ii) insertions are NOT "one folio per block regardless of request size"** — the `filemap:mm_filemap_add_to_page_cache` tracepoint carries an `order` field, and at T=1 the kernel inserts **order-6 (256 KiB) folios for 128 KiB requests** (506 of them covering **126 of 128 MiB**; mean **62.8 KiB/insert**), while at T=64 it collapses to **28,191 order-0 (4 KiB) folios, 96.3%, mean 4.7 KiB/insert**. The folio is *larger than the request* — order is decoupled from request size, which is precisely why granularity was no lever. **INSERTION ORIGIN, callchain-attributed over 100% of events:** at T=64, `page_cache_ra_unbounded` **57.6%** (16,848, **order-0 only**) + `page_cache_ra_order` **42.4%** (12,413; 11,340 of them order-0) + `__filemap_get_folio` **0.0%** (3). At T=1, `page_cache_ra_order` **90.2%** (emitting ord6 ×506) + `page_cache_ra_unbounded` 9.8% (order-0 only). **Every insertion originates in the readahead path; there is no per-block `filemap_create_folio` fallback.** Under concurrency insertions *migrate* to `page_cache_ra_unbounded`, which allocates only order-0. **THE ANSWER — pure buffered fix, no bypass** (same T=8, same reads, same bytes, `sha256` identical; only fd sharing differs): shared fd = **16,989** insertions / 7.8 KiB mean / 477 large folios covering 44 MiB → **per-thread fd = 1,926** insertions / **69.2 KiB** mean / **1,011** large folios covering **126 MiB** (order-5 folios 232 → **1,000**). **8.8x fewer insertions, entirely page-cache-resident.** **BYPASS QUANTIFIED BY DIRECT MEASUREMENT, NOT PROJECTED** — a projection from the insertion-vs-lock-wait curve was requested and is **declined as invalid**: within a fixed T, `r(ins/MiB, MiB/s)` = +0.15/+0.25, so insertions have no predictive power over throughput (spin-wait is CPU burned while other threads block on the device, i.e. overlapped with I/O); such a projection would forecast a large win where measurement shows none. Measured instead (raw harness, page-aligned `mmap`, per-thread fd, T=16, `sha256` == kernel mount, **no frankenfs code touched**): O_DIRECT 4K/256K/1M = **419 / 4,981 / 4,923 MiB/s** vs buffered per-thread fd **665 MiB/s @4K** and **5,020 MiB/s @1M (25.5 ms)**. **O_DIRECT buys 0% of wall (25.5 vs 25.7 ms) and is 1.6x SLOWER at 4 KiB** (it forfeits readahead); its sole benefit is **1.86x CPU** (323M→174M cycles). **OWNER DECISION SURFACED: `bd-kdmu4` cannot be justified on latency grounds.** The `forbid(unsafe_code)`-compatible fix (per-reader `struct file` + bounded fan-out, `bd-ddryj`) reaches **25.5 ms vs the kernel's 26.9 ms — parity — with zero policy change.** If `bd-kdmu4` is ever approved the justification must be CPU efficiency (~150M cycles per 128 MiB read), not throughput. **Recommendation: do not approve it for latency.** | safe fix reaches **parity** (25.5 vs 26.9 ms kernel); O_DIRECT adds **0%** | **insertions −8.8x, buffered** (16,989→1,926); large folios 477→1,011 | REFUTES "irreducible without bypass"; decisive evidence on `bd-kdmu4` | **CODE-EXECUTED PROOF** (`5feb977` rule): readahead-insertion path hot and provably run — `__filemap_add_folio` **1.04%** self-time, `page_cache_ra_unbounded` **0.40%** self-time; **100%** of 29,264 (T=64) / 2,197 (T=1) insertion events attributed by callchain to `page_cache_ra_*`; knob provably engaged (insertions 16,989→1,926 under the arms). `sha256` identity buffered/O_DIRECT/kernel-mount. **Zero cargo builds, zero new target trees.** O_DIRECT measured as a ceiling only — never implemented, `bd-kdmu4` untouched. |
| 2026-07-10 | `bd-ddryj` (granularity curve — the "reduce insertions" premise is REFUTED as a throughput lever) / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **INSERTIONS DRIVE LOCK-WAIT (CPU), NOT THROUGHPUT (WALL). Reducing folio insertions per MiB does not make the cold read faster.** Swept frankenfs's REAL read granularity via `FFS_READ_CHUNK_BLOCKS` (verified to move `pread` count: 1 blk→32,777, 256 blk→138) at 4K/16K/64K/256K/1M × T=1/16/64, with `filemap:mm_filemap_add_to_page_cache` and **real spin-wait time from `perf lock contention`** (tracepoint mode; all wait attributed to `page_cache_ra_unbounded+0x14b` / `page_cache_ra_order+0x1fe` — the readahead `xa_lock`). Prebuilt binary, **no rebuild**; `drop_caches=3` per run. **KEY NUMBERS** (ins/MiB · wait · MiB/s): **T=1** 4K→1M = 16·0ms·954 / 16·0ms·1272 / 17·0ms·1403 / 18·0ms·1421 / 17·0ms·1391 — insertions **flat in chunk size**, and the 1.49x throughput gain is **pure syscall amortization**. **T=16** = 105·42ms·3123 / 111·84ms·3750 / 134·235ms·3896 / 169·366ms·3611 / 115·233ms·4041. **T=64** = 143·1814ms·2717 / 156·2114ms·3239 / 198·3208ms·3065 / 254·3414ms·3340 / 107·1143ms·3303. **CORRELATIONS:** `r(lockwait, ins/MiB) = +0.80` across all 15 points — insertions genuinely *cause* the contention. But `r(ins/MiB, MiB/s)` **within a fixed T = +0.15 (T=16), +0.25 (T=64)** — insertion count has **no predictive power over throughput**. (The pooled `r = +0.78` is **Simpson's paradox**: thread count raises both; not causal.) **DIRECT COUNTER-EXAMPLE, same T=64:** 256K = 254 ins/MiB, 3,414 ms wait, **3,340 MiB/s** vs 1M = 107 ins/MiB, 1,143 ms wait, **3,303 MiB/s** — **2.4x fewer insertions and 3.0x less lock wait made it 1% SLOWER.** Spin-wait is CPU burned while other threads block on the device: it is *overlapped with I/O*, so it costs cycles, not wall. **All three requested levers REFUTED:** (1) *larger contiguous reads → fewer/bigger folios* — ins/MiB is flat at T=1 and **rises** with chunk at T=16/64 (105→169, 143→254); read size does not select folio order. (2) *readahead batching into one insertion* — **already happens**: at T=1 the kernel yields ~64 KiB folios (67,311 B/insert) even for **4 KiB** reads; concurrency on a shared `f_ra` is what destroys it. (3) *hugepage/large-folio-friendly IO sizes* — folio order is chosen by the readahead state machine, not the read size; **no** IO size recovers order-4 folios at T=64 on a shared fd. **LEVER (c) PAGE-CACHE BYPASS — QUANTIFIED, NOT IMPLEMENTED** (raw harness only, page-aligned `mmap` + `O_DIRECT`, per-thread fd, T=16, `sha256` identical to the kernel mount; **no frankenfs code touched, `bd-kdmu4` stays owner-gated**): O_DIRECT 4K/16K/64K/256K/1M = **419 / 1,139 / 3,216 / 4,981 / 4,923 MiB/s** vs buffered per-thread fd **665 MiB/s @4K** and **5,020 MiB/s @1M (25.5 ms)**. **Bypassing the page cache buys 0% of wall** once fds are per-thread and chunk ≥256 KiB (25.5 ms buffered vs 25.7 ms O_DIRECT), and it is **1.6x SLOWER than buffered at 4 KiB** (it forfeits readahead). Its only benefit is CPU (1.86x fewer cycles). **NET: the shipped 128 KiB default is already near-optimal on granularity; `FFS_READ_CHUNK_BLOCKS` is not worth tuning.** The single wall-time lever remains per-reader `struct file` + bounded fan-out (`bd-ddryj`). **CODE-EXECUTED PROOF for every reject in this row** (per the `5feb977` ledger-integrity rule — a reject measured on an input that never reaches the code is invalid): the granularity knob provably engaged (`pread` count moved **32,777 → 138** across the sweep, a 237x change); the readahead-insertion path under test was hot (`__filemap_add_folio` **1.04%** self-time, `page_cache_ra_unbounded` **0.40%**, and 100% of measured spin-wait attributed to `page_cache_ra_unbounded+0x14b` / `page_cache_ra_order+0x1fe`); folio insertions actually varied **2,230 → 27,174** under the arms. No arm was measured on dead code. | granularity: **no lever** (128 KiB default already near-optimal) | insertions = **CPU/lock-wait lever only**, NOT a throughput lever | measurement; refutes 3 hypotheses | `perf lock contention` real wait time (⚠️ `perf lock record` inflates the run — wait totals compare across arms only, never against the uninstrumented wall); `sha256` identity buffered/O_DIRECT/kernel-mount; **zero cargo builds, zero new target trees**; O_DIRECT measured as a ceiling only, never implemented |
| 2026-07-10 | `bd-ddryj` (REFINED — mechanism corrected one level deeper) / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **CONTENTION SCALES WITH PAGE-CACHE INSERTION COUNT — not with read count, and not intrinsically with thread count.** Instrumented with `filemap:mm_filemap_add_to_page_cache` + `lock:contention_begin` on the prebuilt binary (no rebuild). Sweeping `RAYON_NUM_THREADS` over the 128 MiB cold read: **`pread64` count is CONSTANT at 1,034 for every T** (1→64), so contention cannot scale with reads. What explodes is folio insertions: T=1/2/4/8/16/32/64 → **2,230 / 10,145 / 12,623 / 15,137 / 17,914 / 23,271 / 27,174** (**12.2x**), as bytes-per-insertion collapses **60,187 → 4,940 B** (order-4 large folios → order-0 pages); lock contentions track it 0 → 11,912 and cycles 337M → 1,467M. **ROOT CAUSE, one level below the previous row:** `FileByteDevice` holds `file: Arc<File>` (`ffs-block/src/lib.rs:523`), so all workers `pread` through **one `struct file` and therefore one `file->f_ra`**; N workers' interleaved offsets look non-sequential to that single readahead state machine, so `page_cache_ra_unbounded` stops allocating large folios and falls back to order-0, multiplying `xa_lock`-taking insertions. **CONTROLLED PROOF (same T, same reads, same bytes — only fd sharing differs; `sha256` verified):** at T=8, shared fd = 17,476 inserts / 7,680 B-per / 36.7 ms vs **per-thread fd = 2,978 inserts / 45,070 B-per / 26.0 ms** → **5.9x fewer insertions, 1.41x faster**; self-time `native_queued_spin_lock_slowpath` 2.45%→**0.50%**, `__filemap_add_folio` 2.29%→**0.14%**. **LEVER (a) "larger contiguous reads" — REFUTED AS STATED:** shared fd, T=8, chunk 128 KiB→1 MiB→8 MiB→32 MiB cuts `pread`s 1,027→131→19→**7** (147x fewer syscalls) yet insertions stay flat (15,374 / 18,014 / 17,184 / 17,149) and wall *degrades* 35.2→34.5→60.8→**66.6 ms**. Insertion count is a property of readahead folio order, not read size. The correct form of (a) is **preserve large folios by giving each reader its own `f_ra`**. **LEVER (b)** is subsumed: `xa_lock` is per-`address_space`, but once insertions collapse the lock is barely contended (0.50%); a single-file read cannot be spread across inodes anyway. **LEVER (c) O_DIRECT — QUANTIFIED, DELIBERATELY NOT IMPLEMENTED** (measured only as a ceiling in the raw `pread` harness with page-aligned `mmap` buffers, `sha256` identical; no frankenfs code touched): best-of-T wall = shared fd **30.7 ms** / per-thread fd **26.0 ms** / O_DIRECT **25.0 ms**, against kernel-best **26.9 ms**. **O_DIRECT buys only 1.04x of wall over the safe per-thread-fd fix — but 1.86x of CPU (323M→174M cycles). It is a CPU-efficiency play, not a latency play; not worth an unsafe/policy change to close a 4% wall gap.** Per-thread fd + bounded fan-out reaches **26.0 vs 26.9 ms = kernel parity, with no unsafe.** | per-thread fd + cap: **~1.0x of kernel-best** (parity); O_DIRECT ceiling 0.93x | **WIN 1.41x** (per-thread fd @T=8, raw harness); insertions −5.9x | measurement + mechanism; code lever `bd-ddryj` | `sha256` identity across buffered/O_DIRECT/kernel-mount; `perf stat` tracepoint counts; `perf record` self-time; **zero cargo builds, zero new target trees**; ⚠️ **BLOCKER SURFACED:** the per-thread-fd win is proven in the raw harness, *not yet inside frankenfs* — that needs a modified `FileByteDevice` binary, and `rch exec -- cargo build` cannot return an artifact while `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` is globally exported (remote compile OK, retrieval ~0 bytes); a criterion bench cannot substitute because the effect is cold-path and needs `drop_caches` between reps |
| 2026-07-10 | `bd-ddryj` (NEW lever) / `bd-5koeh` follow-up / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **WHY frankenfs is slower COLD: none of the three suspected causes. It is kernel page-cache `xa_lock` contention caused by frankenfs's own read fan-out.** Ranked self-time frame table (`perf record -F 4999`, prebuilt `release-perf` binary — `strip=false`, so **zero rebuild**; full table in `docs/progress/perf-negative-results.md`): `native_queued_spin_lock_slowpath` **39.07%**, `crossbeam_deque::Stealer::steal` 6.27%, `_copy_to_iter` 5.69%, `clear_page_erms` 5.21%, `crossbeam_epoch::try_advance` 2.59%, `entry_SYSRETQ` 1.84%, `asm_exc_page_fault` 1.28%, `__filemap_add_folio` 1.04%. `perf record -g` resolves the lock exactly: `read_exact_at → pread64 → ext4_file_read_iter → filemap_read → filemap_get_pages (32.71%) → page_cache_sync_ra (31.62%) → page_cache_ra_unbounded → __filemap_add_folio (28.34%)` — **all 64 rayon workers pread the same inode and serialize inserting folios into that one `address_space` xarray.** Cold read converts I/O parallelism into lock contention; this is precisely why the DIO-loop kernel arm is fast (`O_DIRECT` never calls `filemap_add_folio`). **The three candidate causes, each REFUTED or demoted:** (1) *per-block syscall overhead* — REFUTED, only **1,034** `pread64` calls for 128 MiB (~128 KiB each); **code-executed proof: `entry_SYSRETQ_unsafe_stack` 1.84% self-time and `__x64_sys_pread64` on a 32.99% callchain** — the syscall path is hot, it is simply not the cost; (2) *extent-tree walks* — REFUTED **ONLY FOR LOW EXTENT COUNTS (≤14), see the scoping note below**; tax is uncorrelated with extent count (1.35x @2 extents, 1.15x @9, 1.36x @14). ⚠️ **LEDGER-INTEGRITY RE-AUDIT 2026-07-10** (per frankenmermaid `5feb977`): this reject was originally justified by the *absence* of extent frames ≥0.1% self-time — the exact shape of reasoning that invalidated four frankenmermaid rows. Re-checked with `--percent-limit 0`: the code **did execute** — `<ffs_core::OpenFs>::resolve_extent` **0.05% self-time**, extent-map `arc_swap ... Arc<[ffs_extent::ExtentMapping]>::load` **0.10% self-time** (plus kernel-side `ext4_es_lookup_extent` 0.13%). So it is **not dead code** and the row stands *for the inputs tested*. But its self-time is ~0 because a 2-extent file invokes the parse **twice**; `big.bin`=2, `frag.bin`=9, `double_ind.bin`=14 extents. **A high-extent-count file (≥100s of extents) was NEVER measured — that regime is UNTESTED, not refuted** (reopened as `bd-vpypn`); (3) *copy tax* — REAL BUT MINOR, `_copy_to_iter` 5.69% + `clear_page_erms` 5.21% (19,279 page faults zero-filling fresh destination pages) ≈ 11%, not dominant. **CONFIRMED BY PREDICTION, byte-identically, with no rebuild** (`RAYON_NUM_THREADS`, cold, min-of-5, read ms): T=1/2/4/8/**16**/32/64 = 86.2 / 63.3 / 40.4 / 32.0 / **30.1** / 32.9 / **37.0** — an interior optimum at 16, exactly as contention predicts. Contention collapses with it: T=64 spinlock **42.27%**, steal 5.42%, sys CPU **0.446 s** → T=16 spinlock **10.96%**, steal 1.26%, sys CPU **0.158 s**. Paired interleaved A/B, 7 reps: **T=16 faster 7/7, p=0.0156, 1.24x**; `sha256` identical to the kernel mount at both widths. Generalizes: indirect **1.44x**, fragmented **1.19x**. **Kernel gap after the cap: extent 1.37x→1.11x, indirect 1.45x→1.09x, fragmented 1.31x→1.09x slower.** And **WARM wants the same cap** (T=4/8/16/32/64 read = 15.5/9.0/**8.5**/10.0/12.6 ms), so a bounded read fan-out has **no warm-path regression risk** — the rayon default (`nproc`) over-parallelizes reads in both regimes. | after cap: **1.09–1.11x** of kernel-best cold (was 1.31–1.45x) | **WIN 1.24x cold / 1.48x warm** (fan-out 64→16, same binary) | measurement + mechanism; code lever filed as `bd-ddryj` | `perf record`/`perf stat`/`perf report -g` on the prebuilt binary; `sha256` byte-identity at T=16 and T=64 vs kernel mount; paired sign test p=0.0156; **zero cargo builds, zero new target trees** (disk emergency); fan-out sites `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:10108,12677,12819` |

| 2026-07-10 | `bd-bhh0i` formal de-risk / Codex (`cod_ffs`) | **WIN — bounded Loom writer linearizability proof plus separate safety projections; no cutover and no production FS mutation.** The writer projection models the accepted lean eager-commit primitive: outer allocator R -> sorted group guards -> independently mapped/sorted MVCC shard writes -> metrics -> sequence/install -> drop shards -> ready-prefix publication while group guards remain held. A Loom-synchronized ghost history records every invocation/response; sequential replay respects every non-overlap edge and checks each installed shard payload against its explicitly mapped group effect. Separate, explicitly uncomposed projections prove installed-but-unpublished versions hidden behind the Acquire/Release prefix and exercise post-commit `active_snapshots -> shards` pruning with a registered reader. Bounds are **2 groups, 2 shards, 2 writers, 1 allocation each, and at most 1 reader**. Seven finite projections exhaust modeled schedules without permutation/duration/preemption sampling limits: disjoint groups, same group, opposing multi-group requests, disjoint groups with cross-mapped shared shards, exact early abort before metrics/sequence/install, out-of-order ready sequence 2, and prune. The writer proof covers those five enumerated configurations, not every operation allowed by the numeric bounds. Sequential bitmap replay, mapped-payload equality, real-time sequence order, free-count conservation, complete-prefix visibility, and registered-snapshot retention all hold within their stated projection. The older hand model's **168** terminal interleavings are now honestly labeled final-state conservation—not linearizability—and its synthetic publish-mutex p99 **127.449 us** is routing-only, not evidence that the real publication gate is the next bottleneck (prior real MVCC/publish-nowait evidence says otherwise). Explicit exclusions: composition into whole `ext4_create`, crash atomicity, starvation, Single/JBD2, and post-install compensation. | N/A: formal safety evidence only; owner-gated performance cutover was not attempted. | **WIN (de-risk): 7/7 bounded Loom projections pass; for five enumerated writer configurations the allocation/group/payload projection is deadlock-free and linearizable across modeled schedules; zero allocator/MVCC effects at the modeled exact early-abort point.** | N/A | RCH `ovh-a`: **7/7 pass in 3.40 s**. Targeted rustfmt and `git diff --check` pass; UBS and workspace gates recorded with the landing commit. |

| 2026-07-10 | `bd-5koeh` (2 of 6 rows re-derived) / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **TWO MORE 2026-06-21 COLD ROWS FLIP SIGN — ext4 INDIRECT and ext4 FRAGMENTED were each recorded FASTER than kernel; both are SLOWER.** Same corrected method as the `bd-q6k00` row below (private copy + private loop, `losetup --direct-io=on` so the kernel gets its BEST config, `drop_caches=3` before every run, arms interleaved within each rep). **Identity gate passed first:** for each fixture `sha256(kernel mount) == sha256(raw-extent pread) == sha256(ffs-cli read)`, checked BEFORE any timing. **ext4 INDIRECT** (`double_ind.bin`, 50 MiB, 14 extents; image has NO `extent` feature — true legacy indirect): ffs engine 27.1 ms − 8.1 ms per-open startup = **19.0 ms** (2632 MiB/s), raw wall 50.0 ms; kernel best (dio loop, t=32) **13.1 ms** (3817 MiB/s); kernel dio t=1 20.5 ms; raw pread floor (t=32) 14.0 ms; **kernel buffered loop single-stream 40.7 ms ← the old `dd bs=1M` arm, a 3.11x handicap**. → **1.45x SLOWER** (min-of-9); recorded 2026-06-21 as *1.32x FASTER*. **ext4 FRAGMENTED** (`frag.bin`, 48 MiB, 9 extents): ffs 24.8 − 8.2 = **16.6 ms** (2892 MiB/s), raw wall 47.0 ms; kernel best **12.7 ms** (3780 MiB/s); kernel dio t=1 15.3 ms; floor 14.4 ms; **kernel buffered single-stream 36.1 ms ← 2.84x handicap**. → **1.31x SLOWER** (min-of-9); recorded as *1.41x FASTER*. **NOISE DISCLOSED, NOT HIDDEN:** at 48–50 MiB the parallel kernel arm is only ~13 ms, so on a shared box its cv is 8–10% (ffs's own arms: cv 2.3–4.5%). The `cv<5%` keep-gate therefore **FAILS on the sub-15 ms arms**, and an adversarial min-ffs-vs-max-kernel bound does **not** certify the sign. The verdict instead rests on the **paired within-rep sign test** that the interleaved design exists for (each rep compares ffs and kernel under the same instantaneous load): INDIRECT **ffs slower in 9/9 reps, median paired ratio 1.36x, p=0.0039**; FRAGMENTED **8/9 reps, median 1.23x, p=0.039**. Parse+copy tax vs a *same-mode* (buffered) raw pread floor: indirect **1.36x**, fragmented **1.15x**. I/O-mode confound bound (floor/kdioN) is +6.9% and **+13.4%** — for FRAGMENTED that is a third of its 31% gap, so its defensible fs-vs-fs statement is the **1.15x vs same-mode floor**, not the 1.31x. Neither row is a frankenfs win under any framing. | **1.45x / 1.31x SLOWER** than kernel-best cold (min-of-9); median paired **1.36x / 1.23x** | N/A — measurement, no code change | **`0/2/0` LOSS vs kernel** (both recorded as WINS) | sha256 identity across all three arms per fixture before timing; paired sign test p=0.0039 / p=0.039; per-arm cv disclosed (fails <5% on the ~13 ms parallel arms — reported, not suppressed); prebuilt `ffs-cli` reused: **zero cargo builds, zero new target trees** (disk emergency, 90G free); 4 rows still open in `bd-5koeh` |
| 2026-07-10 | `bd-q6k00` (RE-DERIVED; supersedes the row below **and** its prescription) / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **THE 2026-06-21 HEADLINE COLD ROW FLIPS SIGN: "ext4 EXTENT 128 MiB COLD = 1.7x FASTER than kernel" is actually 1.42x SLOWER.** Re-derived on the ORIGINAL fixture (`/var/tmp/ffs_cold_1714591.img`, `big.bin` = 128 MiB, 2 extents), on a private copy + private loop so no peer mount was disturbed. **First, the loop-serialization claim in the row below is itself WRONG — it is a *buffered-mode* artifact, not a property of loop devices.** One ioctl fixes it: same loop mount, same bytes, `drop_caches=3` per run, min-of-5 → `dio=0`: t=1/8/32 = **97.7 / 124.2 / 113.6 ms** (flat, no scaling); `dio=1` (`losetup --direct-io=on`): t=1/8/32 = **52.1 / 25.1 / 28.2 ms** (scales) = **4.9x at t=8 on identical bytes**. So the correct rule is *not* "never loop-mount the kernel side" (my own prior prescription) — it is **give the kernel its best configuration (direct-I/O loop), then compare**. That rehabilitates the mounted-kernel-filesystem comparator the project is supposed to measure against. **Confound bounded, not assumed:** frankenfs preads the image *buffered* while a dio loop reads *direct*, so a third arm (raw parallel `pread` of the same physical extents, buffered, no fs parse) calibrates the mode gap — at t=32 it is within **5.2%** of the dio kernel (28.3 vs 26.9 ms), i.e. device-bound, so the 42% gap is real; at t=1 the mode gap is 2.1x and no single-threaded buffered-vs-direct ratio may be quoted. **HONEST NUMBERS (min-of-5, cv ≤ 5.1% every arm):** frankenfs engine **46.6 ms**, of which **8.4 ms** is per-open startup (superblock + GDT + journal replay, measured by cold-reading a 4 KiB file in the same image) → **38.2 ms of actual read (3351 MiB/s)**; kernel best (dio loop, t=32) **26.9 ms (4758 MiB/s)**; raw pread floor (t=32) **28.3 ms**; kernel buffered loop single-stream **97.0 ms** ← what the original `dd bs=1M` arm measured. Ratios: **1.42x SLOWER than kernel-best** startup-corrected (**2.64x** on raw wall-clock, 71.0 ms), **1.35x slower than the raw pread floor** (= the ext4-parse + safe-copy tax, independently corroborating the 1.68x@1 GiB row below). The original framing (38.2 vs 97.0) yields "2.54x FASTER" — arithmetically true, methodologically worthless. **frankenfs does not beat kernel ext4 on cold extent reads; it never did.** | **1.42x SLOWER** than kernel-best cold (startup-corrected); 2.64x raw wall | N/A — measurement, no code change | **`0/1/0` LOSS vs kernel** (was recorded as a WIN) | sha256 **identical across every arm** (`b6cfaf9d…`, kernel mount == raw-extent pread == `ffs-cli read`); an off-by-one-block extent map was caught by that identity check *before* it could produce a number; min-of-5, cv ≤ 5.1%; arms interleaved within each rep; `drop_caches=3` before EVERY run; harness doc updated (`ffs-block/examples/cold_read.rs`) |
| 2026-07-10 | `bd-q6k00` (NEW) / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **⚠️ INTEGRITY #2 — the kernel side's LOOP DEVICE serializes I/O, inflating every parallel "frankenfs dominates kernel cold" claim.** Proven on **identical physical bytes** (1 GiB, 5 extents, inside a real ext4 image on /data; `drop_caches=3` before every run; min-of-N): reading through the **loop mount** is FLAT across a thread sweep — t=4/8/16/32/64 → **862 / 904 / 852 / 853 / 851 ms (~1200 MiB/s, NO scaling)** — while `pread`ing the **same extents** straight from the image file (no loop, no fs) **scales**: t=8/16/32/64 → **393 / 332 / 277 / 282 ms (up to 3692 MiB/s)**. Loop penalty at matched t=32 on identical bytes = **853.4 / 277.4 = 3.08x**. Corroboration: on a plain 800 MiB file on the same /data ext4 with NO loop, the kernel parallel reader scales 533 ms (t=1) → 185 ms (t=16) = 2.9x. The disk scales; the loop does not. CONSEQUENCE: `ffs-cli read` parses ext4 and preads the IMAGE FILE **directly** (no loop) with rayon (`nproc=64`; "each read chunk uses the chunked-parallel engine internally", main.rs ~2397). Benchmarking that against a loop-mounted kernel is **parallel-vs-serialized**. In my own first run it manufactured an apparent **2.843x (raw) / 3.233x (startup-corrected)** frankenfs win — caught only because frankenfs came out **faster than the raw-device floor**, which is physically impossible. Together with the `cat`-is-`bat` defect (row below), every 2026-06-21 cold-matrix row is confounded. | see honest row below | N/A | **prior "DOMINATES kernel cold" rows are NOT SUPPORTED** | strace + same-bytes thread sweeps; `sha256` identity preserved |

CORRECT BASELINE = a pure read loop at the SAME chunk size, no output write; recorded in the harness doc (`cold_read.rs`). Prior rows citing "kernel `cat` min ~370 ms (2266 MiB/s)" cannot have been produced by this box's current `cat` — treat any `cat`-based cold ratio as unverified until re-run against a pure-read baseline. | N/A | N/A | N/A | strace-verified; two independent baselines cross-checked |

| 2026-07-09 | `bd-scrub-btrfs-false-corrupt-afw8y` / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **LANDED (CORRECTNESS): removed the blind whole-image `ZeroCheckValidator` from both scrub validator sets (`BtrfsScrubValidator::validate` fused path + `scrub_validator()`'s ext4 composite).** ROOT CAUSE: unallocated space is legitimately all-zero, and a whole-image scrub has NO allocation map, so `ZeroCheck` flagged every free block as `Corrupt(UnexpectedZeroes, Warning)`. `blocks_corrupt` counts *blocks with ≥1 finding of any severity*, so healthy images reported ~50-100% "corrupt", `is_clean()` was permanently false, and `findings` grew one entry per free block. WORST: `pipeline.rs:1566` feeds `report.blocks_corrupt` into `autopilot.update_posterior(...)` — the repair autopilot's **Beta posterior over the corruption rate was trained on a ~99.9% false-positive rate**, so the adaptive-overhead policy over-allocated repair symbols on healthy images. SAFE BY CONSTRUCTION: `ZeroCheck` detected nothing the superblock validators miss — an all-zero superblock fails `parse_superblock_region` (bad magic) in BOTH `BtrfsSuperblockValidator` (scrub.rs:635) and `Ext4SuperblockValidator` (scrub.rs:555) → still `Corrupt` at critical severity. `ZeroCheckValidator` is retained + still unit-tested as a *scoped* primitive (its doc contract: "zeroed metadata that should contain data"). MEASURED (debug `ffs-cli scrub --json`; counts are profile-invariant): `btrdiff2_1340519.img` (the exact image from the bead) **blocks_corrupt 130962 → 76** of 131072 scanned = 99.94% of the "corrupt" count eliminated, while `blocks_error_or_higher` **76 → 76** and the error+ finding set is **byte-identical** (block, kind, severity, detail) before/after — zero real detections lost. ext4 `cc_ext.img`: 61365 of 102400 blocks (59.9%) are all-zero → would-be corrupt **61365 → 0**, scrub now fully clean (0 findings); `extbig_1587438.img` also 0/131072. Side effect (unmeasured, not claimed): removes a full-block zero scan + a per-block `Vec` alloc + a findings push for every free block. | N/A (correctness, not a kernel-perf ratio) | WIN (zeroes-class false-positive rate 99.94% → 0) | N/A | ffs-repair 469/0 (incl. 2 new regression tests: `btrfs_scrub_does_not_flag_unallocated_zero_blocks`, `btrfs_scrub_still_detects_zeroed_superblock`), ffs-cli 348+26/0, ffs-harness 2058/0 + conformance, `cargo check --workspace --all-targets` OK, fmt+clippy clean on changed files, `ubs` scrub.rs 0 critical |
| 2026-07-09 | `bd-xqzgy` / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **LANDED (CORRECTNESS + NEW DETECTION): btrfs superblock mirrors are now VALIDATED AS SUPERBLOCKS instead of skipped** — closes the coverage gap the mirror-skip opened. `BtrfsSuperblockValidator::new(block_size, total_bytes)` holds a target per mirror (64 KiB / 64 MiB / 256 GiB) and validates each: parse + checksum + **`bytenr` == its own physical offset**. That bytenr check is well-founded here and is NOT the `bd-5vb36` mistake: a *superblock* copy stores its own PHYSICAL offset (verified 15/15 valid images: primary `bytenr`=65536, 64 MiB mirror `bytenr`=67108864), whereas a *tree block*'s bytenr is a chunk-mapped LOGICAL address. **EXPECTED-MIRROR RULE (measure-first, avoided inventing a new false positive):** a mirror is a target only where `offset + 4096 <= sb.total_bytes` — the FILESYSTEM size, not the image file size. Surveying all 16 btrfs images caught `btr2_572550.img`: a 2 GiB file whose 64 MiB region is all-zero **because it holds no filesystem at all** (`total_bytes=0`, no primary superblock). A naive "block exists ⇒ mirror must be valid" rule would have flagged it. **FUSED-GATE FIX:** mirror blocks are gated on BLOCK NUMBER, not fsid — a wrongly-zeroed mirror has no fsid to match, so an fsid-only gate would short-circuit it to `Clean` and never validate it. **PROVEN ON REAL IMAGES (4 injected scenarios, copies of `btrunc_838059.img`):** (A) untouched → 0 findings; (B) 1-bit flip at `64MiB+0x100` → `checksum_mismatch`/critical @ blk 4096; (C) mirror wholly zeroed → `bad_magic` + `checksum_mismatch` (block-number gate reached it); (D) mirror rewritten with the primary's `bytenr` **and a recomputed valid checksum** → `structural_invariant`/critical, caught *only* by the new bytenr check. **KERNEL IDENTITY:** `btrunc_838059.img` loop-mounted RO under the kernel btrfs driver → `sha256(/plain.bin) = 172c15dc2e12b50e523d8e657cbe7fbb11c1053252bbf1e1431077d57d8128fd`, byte-identical to `ffs-cli read` of the same path, and that image scrubs 0 findings with the mirror validated. 10/10 valid images (8 btrfs + 2 ext4) scrub 0 corrupt / 0 findings. | N/A (correctness) | WIN (restores + extends detection, zero false positives) | N/A | ffs-repair 477/0 (+5 tests: valid / corrupt / wrong-bytenr / outside-fs mirror, plus zeroed-mirror through the fused gate), ffs-cli 348+26/0, ffs-harness 2058/0, `cargo check --workspace --all-targets` OK, fmt+clippy clean, `ubs` 0 critical |
| 2026-07-09 | `bd-scrub-btrfs-false-corrupt-afw8y` (3rd + final class) / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **LANDED (CORRECTNESS): `BtrfsTreeBlockValidator` now Skips all btrfs superblock MIRROR blocks (64 KiB / 64 MiB / 256 GiB), not just the primary.** A btrfs superblock carries its `fsid` at `0x20..0x30` — *exactly* where a tree block does — so the cheap `fsid` pre-check misclassified the backup superblock as a tree block and applied a tree-block checksum over the whole 16 KiB block instead of the 4096-byte superblock region. Result: a spurious `ChecksumMismatch`/Critical on **block 4096 of every real btrfs image** (verified: `4096*16384 = 64 MiB` exactly, magic `_BHRfS_M`, self-consistent `sb.bytenr=67108864`). **RESULT — the correctness gate is met:** every valid test image now scrubs **0 corrupt / 0 error+ / 0 findings**: `btrdiff2` 131072 blk, `btr_571421` 75584, `btrunc_838059` 65536, `btrmulti_704416` 131072, `btrreal_1735906` 131072, `cc_ext` 102400, `extbig_1587438` 131072. **FALSE-NEGATIVE CHECK (the check that matters):** injected a single-bit flip at offset `0x200` of live tree block 64 in a copy of `btrunc_838059.img` → scrub reports exactly `1 corrupt, 1 error+, checksum_mismatch/critical @ block 64`. Clean on valid images AND still sensitive to real damage. DISCLOSED TRADEOFF: mirrors are now *skipped*, not validated, so a genuinely corrupt backup superblock goes unreported — filed as `bd-xqzgy`. Not a regression (before, the mirror was reported corrupt 100% of the time regardless of validity — an always-firing alarm with zero information). | N/A (correctness) | WIN (whole-image scrub false-positive rate → 0 on 7/7 valid images) | N/A | ffs-repair 472/0 (+1 test `btrfs_tree_block_validator_skips_superblock_mirrors`), ffs-cli 348+26/0, ffs-harness 2058/0, `cargo check --workspace --all-targets` OK, fmt+clippy clean, `ubs` 0 critical |
| 2026-07-09 | `bd-5vb36` / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **LANDED (CORRECTNESS, 2nd scrub class): `BtrfsTreeBlockValidator` no longer compares `header.bytenr` against the physical offset.** It passed `Some(block.0 * block_size)` to `BtrfsHeader::validate`, but `header.bytenr` is a btrfs **logical** address (chunk-tree mapped) while a whole-image scrub only knows the **physical** offset; they coincide only under an identity chunk map. Now passes `None` — `header.validate` still enforces the map-independent structural invariants (`level <= BTRFS_MAX_LEVEL`, `nritems` fits the block). **NOTHING IS LOST, PROVEN:** the btrfs metadata checksum covers bytes `0x20..`, which *includes* `bytenr` at `0x30..0x38`, so a corrupted bytenr still fails `verify_btrfs_tree_block_checksum` — pinned by new test `btrfs_tree_block_corrupted_bytenr_is_caught_by_checksum` (flips a bytenr byte after csum → still `ChecksumMismatch`). The equality test was only ever a *placement* check, and placement is unverifiable without the chunk map. MEASURED (`ffs-cli scrub --json`, blocks_corrupt): `btr_571421.img` **1655 → 1**, `btrdiff2_1340519.img` **76 → 1**, `btrunc_838059.img` **37 → 1**. Also removed the now-dead `block_size` field. | N/A (correctness) | WIN (structural_invariant false positives → 0) | N/A | ffs-repair 471/0 (+2 regression tests incl. `btrfs_tree_block_valid_under_non_identity_chunk_map`), ffs-cli 348+26/0, ffs-harness 2058/0 + conformance, fmt+clippy clean, `ubs` 0 critical |
| 2026-07-09 | `bd-scrub-btrfs-false-corrupt-afw8y` (follow-up, superseded by `bd-5vb36` above) / BlackThrush (cc_ffs) | **SURFACED then FIXED (2nd, distinct false-positive class).** After the zeroes fix, the whole residual on valid btrfs images is `structural_invariant: "btrfs tree header invalid: bytenr does not match expected"`. `BtrfsTreeBlockValidator` (scrub.rs:715) computes `expected_bytenr = block.0 * block_size` — a **physical** byte offset — but btrfs `header.bytenr` is a **logical** address translated through the chunk tree. EVIDENCE: on `btr_571421.img` every sampled flagged block has `hdr.bytenr - physical = -8388608` exactly (a constant −8 MiB = the image's chunk mapping), i.e. these are *valid* tree blocks, mis-flagged. Residual counts after the zeroes fix: `btr_571421.img` 1655, `btrdiff2` 75, `btrunc_838059` 36 (+1 `checksum_mismatch` each on the first two, needs separate triage). Fix requires reading the chunk tree and translating logical→physical before comparing — a real change, not a one-liner. ext4 is unaffected (already 0 findings). | N/A | N/A (open) | N/A | N/A — evidence recorded, bead filed |

Clean-box confirmation: SEQUENTIAL beat none **8/8 reps**, min 399 vs 460 ms = 1.15x, mean 415 vs 526 ms = 1.27x, ~1.08x of kernel `cat`, plus a variance-reduction property. NET cold picture: single-thread ≈ kernel parity, parallel ≈ 2.3x FASTER than kernel, random = inherent seek latency (SEQUENTIAL-safe, verified). This time the copy IS eliminated (staging is NOT commit-dominated — commit is once at batch end). | REVERTED (0-gain). The copies are noise. ⭐LESSON: measure the ADAPTER the hot caller actually uses (`let dev = &tx_dev`), not the one that looks like the write path — the layered block-device adapters route differently per scope. |  — eliminates a redundant 4 KiB `to_vec` per block write, but the MVCC commit DOMINATES `write_block` so it's e2e-neutral.** Fresh create profile (built current binary per directive) flagged `FsMvccBlockDevice::write_block` doing `txn.stage_write(block, data.to_vec())` — and `stage_write` already takes `Vec<u8>`, and callers (`ext4_add_dir_entry` @17874) hold the edited block as an OWNED `Vec`. DATA-LAYOUT primitive (move-not-copy, off the SWAR seam). FULLY IMPLEMENTED: added `write_block_owned(Vec<u8>)` to the `BlockDevice` trait (default → `write_block(&data)`, additive/non-breaking), overrode it in `FsMvccBlockDevice` to MOVE into `stage_write` (peer `stage_write` UNCHANGED), routed both `ext4_add_dir_entry` dir-block writes. | REVERTED (0-gain). Behavior-identical: ffs-core **1184 pass** (+1 pre-existing btrfs_reflink), conformance **100/0/2**, clean build. | ext4 dir-entry insert / block write. | **MEASURED e2e A/B, create-bench 3500 ×4** (baseline `to_vec` binary vs `write_block_owned` move binary): baseline 91.1/99.9/101.5/94.8k vs new 86.4/92.9/93.5/102.3k creates/s = **NEUTRAL (within ~10% create-bench noise; new median slightly lower)**. | Gates: 1184/0(+1 pre-existing), conformance 100/0/2 (both binaries). ⭐⭐WHY NEUTRAL: `FsMvccBlockDevice::write_block` = begin() + stage_write + **commit()** + prune_after_commit — a full MVCC transaction PER block write; the 4 KiB `to_vec` (~130 cyc) is negligible vs the commit (peer, ~thousands of cyc). Eliminating the copy is invisible against the commit-dominated write. ⭐KEY: WRITE-path CPU is dominated by the peer MVCC commit — copy-elimination in the ffs-core write adapters is below the noise floor. This EXECUTES + closes the write_block_owned candidate (fully built, measured, reverted — not deferred). ⭐The trait method is clean + reusable if a future non-committing write path wants it, but not justified now. |  (succinct structure) as a negative-lookup pre-filter for `dir_name_index` does NOT beat `FxHashSet` — the succinct-footprint hypothesis was refuted.** STRUCTURALLY DIFFERENT primitive class (succinct-structure, OFF the SWAR seam per directive). Hypothesis: negative lookups (stat of a non-existent file — build systems, `test -f`, rsync) hit `FxHashSet<Vec<u8>>::contains`, and for a LARGE dir the set won't fit L1 so each probe cache-misses; a ~10-bit/name bloom (12 KB @ 10k names, L1-resident) with short-circuit-on-first-0 should win. Built a real double-hashing bloom (FxHash → h1+i·h2, k=7) and A/B'd negative-contains (1024 truly-absent queries) vs `FxHashSet` at n=1k/10k/100k. | NO production code (rejected). Kept the A/B bench `crates/ffs-ondisk/benches/bloom_negative.rs` as evidence. | linux dcache is an exact hash table, not a bloom — consistent with this result. | **MEASURED per-crate A/B, criterion** (`cargo bench -p ffs-ondisk --bench bloom_negative`): hashset vs bloom = **3.39→3.30 µs (n1k), 3.68→3.48 µs (n10k), 3.93→3.81 µs (n100k) = ~1.03-1.06x (within noise)**. | Gates: n/a (bench-only, no prod change). ⭐⭐WHY IT LOSES (refutes the hypothesis): a HashSet NEGATIVE probe is already just ONE cache line (hash → one bucket, miss) regardless of set size — NOT the cache-thrashing I assumed; and the ~16-byte name FxHash DOMINATES both paths equally (the bloom still hashes the name once). So the bloom's smaller bit-array buys nothing when the baseline is 1 hash + 1 access. ⭐⭐KEY LESSON: succinct/cache-friendly structures only win when the baseline does MANY memory accesses per query; an O(1) hash-probe with 1 access has no cache headroom to reclaim. Don't bloom-filter a single hash-set membership test. |

| 2026-07-09 | new-primitive-class / BlackThrush | **LANDED a SWAR ASCII case-fold compare on the casefold-dir lookup path (`casefold_key_eq` + `ext4_casefold_eq`) = 1.64x on short names. STRUCTURALLY NEW primitive class = word-at-a-time ASCII CASE-FOLD (distinct from my exact-compare `names_eq`).** Case-insensitive ext4 dirs (a real, increasingly-common feature — Android /data, Windows-compat) fold+compare EVERY entry name per lookup via `casefold_key_eq`, whose ASCII fast path used `[u8]::eq_ignore_ascii_case` (BYTE-WISE lowercase+compare). Added `ascii_ci_eq`: lowercase 8 bytes/iter with a branchless in-range SWAR (`swar_ascii_to_lower`: clear high bits, `x+(0x7f-'A'+1)` & `~(x+(0x7f-'Z'))` marks `'A'..='Z'`, `>>2` adds 0x20 — no cross-byte carry), then compare words. Passes my per-op-frequency threshold (runs PER ENTRY in a casefold leaf scan, not once/op). | LANDED / own files (`crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs`, `crates/ffs-ondisk/benches/casefold_cmp.rs` [+reg], this ledger). Bit-identical to `eq_ignore_ascii_case` for ASCII (bench asserts on case-variant + mismatch inputs; 24 casefold tests incl sharp-s + case-variant pass). | ext4 casefold (`+F` / `EXT4_CASEFOLD_FL`) dir lookup. | **MEASURED per-crate A/B, criterion** (`cargo bench -p ffs-ondisk --bench casefold_cmp`): `eq_ignore_ascii_case` vs SWAR — 13-B **13.07 → 7.96 ns = 1.64x**, 14-B **13.21 → 7.90 ns = 1.67x**; at 33-B SWAR LOSES (5.42 vs 7.07 ns — `eq_ignore_ascii_case` AUTOVECTORIZES to AVX for long slices). ⭐So gated `len <= 16` → SWAR else `eq_ignore` (dir names are typically short; no long-name regression). | Gates: `cargo test -p ffs-ondisk --lib` **677/0** (casefold 24/0); conformance **100/0/2**. ⭐19th win. HONEST: casefold is a less-common feature so end-to-end reach is workload-dependent — but it's a clean 1.64x per-entry primitive on a real path (NOT parity-tail: per-item, distinct feature, structurally new SWAR class). ⭐KEY: `eq_ignore_ascii_case`/`==` AUTOVECTORIZE for long slices but stay SCALAR for short — a u64 SWAR beats the scalar short case, so length-gate SWAR-vs-stdlib at the AVX crossover (~16-32 B). |  — but it's parity-tail: marginal gain, moderate surface, would be benchmark-gaming to land).** `Ext4Superblock::csum_seed()` recomputes `ext4_chksum(!0, &uuid)` (crc32c of the 16-B UUID) on EVERY call for filesystems WITHOUT the `CSUM_SEED` incompat feature (the common case) — an invariant recomputed across 55 call sites. Looked like a caching win. But: (1) the value is derived ONCE per op then threaded as a `csum_seed: u32` param into the actual csum fns (not recomputed per-block in a tight loop); (2) the hot bitmap-verify sites (@11047/@11082) are gated by `has_metadata_csum() && !gdt_persistence_deferred()` — usually SKIPPED; (3) it's a ~2-3 ns HW-crc-of-16-bytes, ~once per WRITE op (peer-MVCC-dominated, commit alone is 13%+) → e2e ≈ 0.1% = a parity-tail. Caching needs a field on `Ext4Superblock` (derives Serialize + 6 struct-literal constructors → ripple) or an OpenFs field + reroute. Moderate surface for ~0.1% e2e, and a "5x on a 3 ns primitive" bench would OVERSTATE it → declined (landing it would be the parity-tail/gaming the framing forbids). | NO code change (rejected). | linux `sbi->s_csum_seed` is likewise computed once at mount, not per-op — caching would match, but the win is immaterial. | n/a. | ⭐Also confirmed `read_group_desc` on the alloc path is NOT cacheable (the GD's free-count MUTATES per alloc/free, so each op legitimately re-reads the current descriptor). ⭐HONEST: found a genuine redundancy but it's below the threshold where landing it is anything but parity-tail — the disciplined call is to NAME it and not game a 5x-on-3ns ratio. AttrOnly worked ONLY because its inode never re-serializes; delete's does. | NO code change (rejected). | n/a. | n/a. | ⭐Also confirmed error hygiene is already clean (no eager `.ok_or(Err::new(format!()))` — all lazy `ok_or_else`). ⭐OP COVERAGE NOW COMPLETE: create/lookup/read/write/rename/delete/mkdir/walk/getattr/readdir all profiled + audited; every non-peer hot fn is a landed win or measured floor. Measure-don't-reason via rebuild-A/B (`cargo bench -p ffs-ondisk --bench dx_hash_transform`, hash_version 1): baseline short_13 42.8 ns / long_40 112 ns vs new 46–50 / 100–126 ns — **NOISE-dominated, no consistent signal (new runs disagreed 100 vs 126 ns on long)** → NEUTRAL. WHY: `half_md4_transform` INLINES into `dx_hash_half_md4` where `buf = &str2hashbuf(..)[..8]` (a `[u32; 8]`), so the compiler already proves len==8 and elides all `.get()` checks + const-folds the `unwrap_or` — `get(i)` compiles identically to `buf[i]`. Analysis: (1) FsOps::lookup + cache clone = the 130-B InodeAttr BY-VALUE copies — but the Arc refutation (22387e32) PROVED a 130-B memcpy is CHEAPER than Arc refcount, i.e. the copy is cheap, so these % are dispatch + lock + hash, NOT a shrinkable copy; the InodeAttr-time-compaction design turn would NOT help warm lookup (inode_to_attr runs only on a cache MISS). (2) ⭐KEY: the 12.65% arc_swap `HybridStrategy::load` is a SERIAL-BENCHMARK ARTIFACT — lookup-bench runs `for _ in 0..count` on ONE thread, so the per-load debt-slot fence is pure overhead; arc_swap was chosen because it is lock-free-OPTIMAL for the REAL parallel-FUSE case (per-thread debt = no shared cacheline). A thread-local `arc_swap::Cache` would cut it but is blocked (forbid(unsafe) + multi-OpenFs-instance thread-local aliasing); a RwLock swap would help the serial bench but CONTEND the reader cacheline under parallel FUSE = a benchmark-gaming regression. So DON'T "optimize" it. (3) lookup_name = htree descent (dx_find_leaf_idx_raw, raw in-place) + leaf scan (my SWAR) = floor. (4) walk (cache-disabled stat, the path AttrOnly actually speeds): profiling 40× invocations is STARTUP-DOMINATED (ld.so relocation + clap + jemalloc init) — the FS work per walk is negligible = FS-floor, confirming AttrOnly's benefit is real-but-small and unmeasurable at CLI granularity. | NO code change (rejected). | n/a. | n/a. | ⭐⭐KEY INSIGHT (fleet value): a SERIAL CLI bench OVERSTATES the cost of a lock-free-for-parallelism primitive (arc_swap). | 2026-07-09 | landed-deferred-candidate / BlackThrush | **LANDED the skeletal `AttrOnly` inode parse for getattr/lookup = 1.11x on the attr-path parse (skips the 60-byte extent copy for non-device inodes) — the candidate I flagged + deferred as "needs care" last turn (593a93c5), now safely implemented.** `read_inode_attr` (getattr/lookup/stat, VERY hot — esp. the cache-disabled ONE-PASS walk where every stat parses) read the inode via `parse_metadata_from_bytes`, which still copies the 60-byte `i_block`/extent region into a SmallVec even though `inode_to_attr` reads it ONLY via `device_number()` (char/block-device rdev). Added a new `Ext4InodeIbodyParse::AttrOnly` mode: skips ibody (like MetadataOnly) AND skips the extent copy for NON-device inodes (empty SmallVec, zero copy); DEVICE inodes still get `extent_bytes` so `device_number()` returns the correct rdev. SAFE because the `AttrOnly` inode never escapes `read_inode_attr_with_scope` — it is consumed by `inode_to_attr` and dropped, and `inode_to_attr`'s only extent use is `device_number` (which short-circuits `return 0` for non-devices). Kept the shared `MetadataOnly` mode UNCHANGED (the readdir parent-cache @32935/@32973 parse DIRECTORIES via metadata and legitimately need the dir's extents to read its blocks — a global skip would have broken them; verified). Primitive class: **skip-unneeded-work / narrower parse variant** (extends the metadata-parse idea to the extent copy, device-conditional). | LANDED / own files (`crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs`, `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs`, `crates/ffs-ondisk/benches/inode_parse_metadata.rs`, this ledger). | 2026-07-09 | different-hot-path / BlackThrush | **LANDED word-at-a-time hashing on `extent_root_namespace` = 7.14x — a NEW primitive class (SWAR hashing), found by dissecting the write profile's `ext4_write_extents` 3.06%.** `extent_root_namespace` hashes the inode's ≤64-byte extent root to the extent-snapshot cache key, computed PER WRITE in `ext4_write_extents_with_scope`. The production form was byte-wise FNV-1a: a ~60-iteration SEQUENTIAL XOR+multiply dependency chain (each `h` depends on the prior → the compiler cannot vectorize it, ~half of the fn's 3.06%). Replaced with a word-at-a-time hash: mix 8 bytes/iter as a `u64` (7 iters + tail) — same "every byte affects h" property (8 B ↔ u64 is a bijection, so any byte change changes its word and thus h). Safe because the namespace is an IN-MEMORY cache key ONLY (verified: never serialized/persisted; no test asserts a specific value; the stability test uses the `number!=0` fast path, not this fn). Primitive class: **SWAR / word-at-a-time hashing** (distinct from my SWAR compare/search + read_fixed). | LANDED / own files (`crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs`, `crates/ffs-ondisk/benches/root_ns_hash.rs` [+bench reg], this ledger). Cache semantics preserved: deterministic + changes on any extent-byte mutation → no stale-snapshot risk. | No kernel analogue (internal cache-key hash). | **MEASURED per-crate A/B, criterion (low-noise), local byte-wise-FNV vs word-wise over a representative 60-byte extent root** (`cargo bench -p ffs-ondisk --bench root_ns_hash`): ORIG byte-wise FNV **71.25 ns** median vs NEW word-wise **9.98 ns** median = **7.14x**. | Gates: `cargo test -p ffs-core --lib` **1184 pass** (1 pre-existing btrfs_reflink; extent-cache/namespace tests pass → change-correct); conformance `cargo test -p ffs-harness --test conformance` **100/0/2** (write path exercises the namespace). ⭐10th win. End-to-end write-bench impact is modest (the ~71ns FNV was ~1.4% of a peer-14%-commit-dominated op) but the fn-level ratio is 7.14x and it's a clean, correct, non-peer improvement. ⭐GENERAL: byte-wise FNV/hash loops over fixed small buffers are word-at-a-time-able (in-memory keys only). |
