| 2026-07-08 | different-hot-path / BlackThrush | **LANDED the WRITE-SIDE analog of the read_fixed hoist: array-ref reslice in `serialize_inode_into` = 1.066x.** `serialize_inode_into(inode, size, buf: &mut [u8])` writes ~30 base-area fields into a caller-sized `buf` via `buf[off..off+N].copy_from_slice(...)`, each paying a cross-function bounds check (`buf` is a param, len unknown to the callee — runs on every create/mkdir/rename/unlink/write inode write). Reslice the fixed 128-byte base ONCE to an array ref `let base: &mut [u8; 128] = (&mut buf[..128]).try_into()...` (one check); const-offset writes into `&mut [u8; 128]` are provably in-bounds → the ~30 field checks drop with NO extra buffer traffic (writes still land straight in `buf` — this is the KEY difference from a separate-array+copy, which regresses; ref-reslice does not). Primitive class: **data-layout / bounds-check hoisting (write side)**. | LANDED / own files (`crates/ffs-inode/src/lib.rs`, this ledger). | 2026-07-08 | exhaustion-map / BlackThrush | **DISSECTED the full ext4 lookup path component-by-component — every stage is already optimized; confirmed floor (no solo lever left).** After my arc_swap Guard-hold (31c57895) dropped the 8.46% clone, re-read the residual lookup cost: `FsOps::lookup` 11% self-time, `lookup_name` 7%, attr-cache `get` 7%, `read_inode_attr` 7%. Component findings: (1) `FsOps::lookup` self-time is glue + inlined callees — `ext4_present_attr` is a `mut attr` MOVE + one field tweak (no copy), no redundancy. (2) `lookup_name`'s validation-keyed index is an ABSENT-name short-circuit (skips the htree descent for create-dedup / failed lookups); it's inherent overhead for PRESENT lookups but removing it regresses miss/create workloads — a workload trade-off, not a bug. (3) the htree descent ALREADY uses the raw in-place binary search `dx_find_leaf_idx_raw` (bd-cc-dxraw: "parse_dx_entries was ~10% of a lookup", now avoided — no Vec materialization for single-level dirs, the common shape). (4) the leaf scan is my SWAR `lookup_in_dir_block` (2ad5ec95). (5) the parent inode is my arc_swap Guard-hold (31c57895). (6) the attr "clone" IS the inherent by-value `FsOps::lookup -> InodeAttr` trait return (the cache clone becomes the return value — one copy, not two; Arc-wrapping would ADD an Arc-clone + deref-copy since the trait returns by value → strictly worse). | 2026-07-08 | different-hot-path / BlackThrush | **MEASURED-NEGATIVE + refined SCOPE of the `read_fixed` hoist (b83531ef): it LOSES on same-function loop-guarded parses.** Tried the same `read_fixed::<8>` hoist on `walk_dir_block_entries` (the per-entry 8-byte header read — inode/rec_len/name_len/file_type — that backs BOTH readdir `parse_dir_block` AND `lookup_in_dir_block`, iterating every entry). Unlike the extent leaf (whose length is validated in the CALLER `parse_extent_tree`, cross-function → compiler can't elide the per-field checks → hoist won 1.13x), `walk_dir_block_entries` reads inside a **same-function loop guard** `offset + 8 <= block.len()`, so the compiler ALREADY elides the 4 `read_le_*`/`ensure_slice` checks — and `read_fixed::<8>`'s extra 8-byte array copy is pure overhead. Also confirmed `parse_dx_entries` (htree descent) uses the same same-function guard (`off + 8 > data.len()`), so it would lose identically. | NOT LANDED / reverted byte-identical to b83531ef; only this ledger committed. `ext4.rs` unchanged. | n/a — measurement. | **MEASURED LOSS vs LEGACY ORIGINAL** (`cargo bench --profile release-perf -p ffs-ondisk --bench lookup_leaf_scan`, 204-entry full leaf, miss=walk every entry, rebuild A/B): ORIG 4-read header **493.60 ns** median vs `read_fixed::<8>` **578.06 ns** median = **0.85x (candidate 1.17x SLOWER)**. | Gates: n/a (reverted). | ⭐REFINED PRINCIPLE (completes b83531ef): the `read_fixed::<N>` bounds-check hoist wins ONLY when length is validated CROSS-function (callee can't see it → per-field checks not elided → extent leaf/index 1.13x). When the read sits under a SAME-function loop/length guard (dir-walk, dx-entries), the checks are already elided and `read_fixed`'s array copy REGRESSES it. Don't apply read_fixed to same-function-guarded loops. |
| 2026-07-08 | different-hot-path / BlackThrush | **LANDED a bounds-check hoist on the extent-tree leaf/index parse (`parse_extent_leaf` / `parse_extent_index`) = 1.13x.** These parse a 4 KiB extent node per block-resolve on a large/fragmented file's EXTERNAL extent tree (cold reads/writes, before the extent-snapshot / hot-extents cache warms — `parse_extent_leaf` showed 0.90% on write-bench). Each 12-byte entry was read via 4 separate `read_le_u32`/`read_le_u16` (4 bounds checks/entry) in a loop the compiler can't prove in-bounds — even though the caller pre-validates `bytes.len() >= 12 + entries_len*12`. Replaced with a single `read_fixed::<12>` (ONE bounds check) + const-offset array reads (`u32::from_le_bytes([e[0..4]])` etc., provably in-bounds on a `[u8;12]`). Primitive class: **data-layout / bounds-check hoisting**. | LANDED / own files (`crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs`, `crates/ffs-ondisk/Cargo.toml` [+bench], `crates/ffs-ondisk/benches/extent_leaf_parse.rs`, this ledger). Byte-identical output: same fields, same sorted/non-zero validation, same error variants. | No direct kernel analogue (internal ext4 extent-node decode). | **MEASURED per-crate A/B, same bench, rebuild** (`cargo bench --profile release-perf -p ffs-ondisk --bench extent_leaf_parse` — a FULL 340-entry leaf, the 4 KiB-block max): ORIG 4-read form **1.1025 µs** median vs NEW `read_fixed::<12>` **974.43 ns** median = **1.13x** (NEW's worst 1.0007µs < ORIG's best 1.0807µs → clean separation). | Gates: `cargo test -p ffs-ondisk --lib` **677/0** (extent-parse tests behavior-identical); conformance `cargo test -p ffs-harness --test conformance` **100/0/2**. NOTE vs the dup-scan finding (b682b389: direct per-byte indexing LOSES to slice-`get`): consistent — that was many per-field slice-gets; here `read_fixed` is ONE slice-get for the 12-byte entry then array reads, which is FEWER checks than 4 slice-gets. The bench is the arbiter. ⚠️INFRA: clean `fs-cc-bttool`. |

Breakdown: **`ShardedMvccStore::commit` 13.41%**, `ext4_write` 3.41%, `prune_safe` 2.38%, `crc32c` (HW) 2.18%, `ext4_write_extents` 2.03%, `parse_from_bytes_with_ibody` 1.67%, staged-writes **BTreeMap churn ~3.7%** (`IntoIter<BlockNumber,StagedWrite>::dying_next` 1.07% + `IntoIter<_,SetValZST>::dying_next` 1.02% + `remove_leaf_kv` 0.87% + `insert` 0.71%), `read_visible_block_buf` 1.24%. The ~21% MVCC cluster (commit + prune + staged BTreeMap + read_visible) is `ffs-mvcc`, which `git log` confirms is ACTIVELY optimized by peers (IvoryBirch et al.: `c92cdca3` prune candidate set 1.31x write, `eb229915` commit 1.82x, `78c40c32` hash-index version chains, `faa65c91` read_visible short-circuit) — editing it now would collide/split-brain. The ffs-core write-metadata is already optimized: `ext4_write_extents` uses a namespace-keyed cached snapshot (@lib.rs:11972 — no per-overwrite re-parse), and `parse_from_bytes`/`serialize_inode` are necessary per-commit inode state ops. | NO CODE — routing/measurement; the modified `Cargo.lock`/`ffs-fuse` files are concurrent-peer WIP, untouched. | n/a — the WRITE engine's kernel gap is the MVCC-commit term, owned by the ffs-mvcc peers. | ⭐CONCLUSION: after SWAR (create dup-scan, lookup) + arc_swap Guard-hold (ext4 inode/parent + btrfs extents), the SOLO ffs-core read/lookup CPU surface is mined; the remaining measured headroom (write/commit ~21%) is single-owner ffs-mvcc work being actively landed by peers. A solo turn here should route, not collide. | Gates: n/a (no code). | 2026-07-09 | different-hot-path / BlackThrush | **LANDED the lock-free-access lever I flagged last turn: HOLD the `arc_swap` Guard + borrow, dropping the per-hit `Arc::clone` on the ext4 hot-inode/hot-parent caches.** `lookup-bench` profiling put `arc_swap::HybridStrategy::load` at **8.46%** — the single-slot `ext4_hot_parent`/`ext4_hot_inode` (`ArcSwapOption<(u64, Arc<Ext4Inode>)>`) HIT path did `.load()` (Guard) then `Arc::clone(&slot.1)` to outlive the temporary Guard. Instead, keep the Guard alive (`Some(self.ext4_hot_parent.load())`) and borrow the inode straight out of it (`g.as_ref().filter(|s| s.0==key).map(|s| &s.1)` → `&Ext4Inode`), eliminating the per-hit inner-Arc incref/decref pair. Applied to both the lookup hot-parent (@lib.rs:32799) and the read hot-inode (@30772) slots (RO-only; writable mounts skip both, unchanged). Primitive class: **lock-free-access tuning**. | LANDED / own files (`crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs`, this ledger). Semantically identical: the authoritative in-Arc `u64` is still checked; a concurrent `store` keeps the old value live until the held Guard drops. | Standing ORIG/kernel gap unchanged (lookup/read hot-cache CPU term). | **MEASURED WIN vs LEGACY ORIGINAL, same-binary rebuild A/B** (`ffs-cli lookup-bench <img> / --count 500000`, 500k random lookups in a 3500-name htree dir, 3 runs each): ORIG `Arc::clone` **~8.7M lookups/s** median (6.84/8.66/8.85M) vs NEW Guard-hold **~10.8M lookups/s** median (9.66/10.78/11.44M) = **~1.24x** median; even conservatively NEW's worst (9.66M) beats ORIG's best (8.85M) = **1.09x**. | Gates: `cargo test -p ffs-core --lib` **1184 passed** (1 pre-existing-unrelated `btrfs_reflink_random_matches_reference_model`, fails identically on clean base — btrfs, not this ext4 change); conformance `cargo test -p ffs-harness --test conformance` **100/0/2** (e2fsck-gated — lookup/read drive path resolution). ⚠️INFRA: built/benched in clean `fs-cc-bttool`. |

| 2026-07-06 | dig-deeper / BlackThrush | **Extended the zero-run-aware CRC to the block/inode BITMAP checksum** — generalized last commit's `crc_dir_coverage` into `ext4_chksum_skip_zero_tail(seed, data)` and routed `ext4_bitmap_checksum` (the value used by BOTH `stamp_block/inode_bitmap_checksum` AND `verify_block/inode_bitmap_checksum`) through it. An allocator fills a group bottom-up, so a partially-full group's ~4 KiB bitmap is a live prefix (allocated low blocks/inodes) followed by a ZERO tail (free high ones) — exactly the shape the zero-aware CRC exploits (`crc(prefix ++ zeros) = crc32c_shift_bytes(crc(prefix), N)`, ac18ce8b). Every bitmap VERIFY (mount validates all group descriptors' bitmap csums; scrub/fsck) and every full bitmap STAMP now skips the free tail. Non-bigalloc and bigalloc unaffected in units (the caller still passes `clusters_per_group`); this only changes HOW the covered region is CRC'd. | LANDED / own files (`crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs`, `crates/ffs-ondisk/benches/bitmap_csum_recompute.rs`, this ledger). The win grows toward the fresh-fs / sparse-group case (bigger zero tail); a full group falls back to the straight CRC at ~0 overhead. | Gates: existing `block/inode_bitmap_checksum_round_trips_with_verify` + `ext4_proptest_block/inode_bitmap_checksum_stamp_verify` (stamp==verify, cross-seed non-replay) + last commit's `stamp_dir_block_checksum_zero_tail_matches_full` proptest all GREEN; `cargo test -p ffs-ondisk --lib` **677/0**; `cargo test -p ffs-alloc --lib` **215/0** (the alloc/free persist bitmap-csum consumers); conformance `cargo test -p ffs-harness --test conformance` **100/0/2** (e2fsck-gated — validates on-disk bitmap checksums). ⚠️INFRA (unchanged): shared `.rch-targets/fs-cc` + rch workers stale under `rustc 1.98.0-nightly (2026-07-02)`; measured in clean `fs-cc-bttool`. |
| 2026-07-06 | dig-deeper / BlackThrush | **ZERO-RUN-AWARE full dir-block CRC** (`crc_dir_coverage` inside `stamp_dir_block_checksum`) — a radically different primitive for the FULL-stamp paths that the incremental delta can't help: mkdir's initial `.`/`..` block, a freshly-allocated leaf, and the large-slack insert fallback all CRC a ~4 KiB block that is a short entry prefix followed by a ~4 KiB ZERO gap. Since `crc(prefix ++ zeros) = crc32c_shift_bytes(crc(prefix), N)` (append-zeros == the constant-time GF(2) shift from ac18ce8b, now `pub`), a 4-wide reverse zero-scan (the allocator's branchless-scan shape) finds the content boundary and the ~4 KiB zero tail is skipped algebraically — CRCing only the ~24 non-zero bytes + one shift. Auto-applies to EVERY full-stamp caller; no wiring. Falls back to a straight CRC when the trailing zero run < 256 B (self-correcting: a full block's reverse-scan hits non-zero immediately → ~0 overhead). | LANDED / own files (`crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs`, `crates/ffs-ondisk/src/crc_incremental.rs` [expose `crc32c_shift_bytes`], `crates/ffs-ondisk/benches/dir_csum_incremental.rs`, this ledger). Bit-identical to the straight `ext4_chksum`; no on-disk format change. | Standing ORIG/kernel gap unchanged (trims the per-mkdir / new-leaf / large-slack CPU term). | **MEASURED** (`CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/projects/.rch-targets/fs-cc-bttool cargo bench --profile release-perf -p ffs-ondisk --bench dir_csum_incremental` — group `dir_csum_fresh`, a 24-byte-prefix + ~4 KiB-zero-tail 4 KiB block): straight `full_recompute` **720.59 ns** median vs `zero_aware` **184.05 ns** median = **3.92x** (3.67x conservative, 698.84/190.51). The reverse zero-scan is the residual cost; the ~4 KiB CRC is gone. | Gates: NEW proptest `stamp_dir_block_checksum_zero_tail_matches_full` (random content prefix 0..300 B, trailing-zero run 0..5000 B straddling the 256 B threshold, random seed/ino/gen — zero-aware tail == straight `ext4_chksum` over the whole coverage) GREEN; `cargo test -p ffs-ondisk --lib` **677 passed / 0 failed** (all existing stamp/verify tests now route through the zero-aware path); conformance `cargo test -p ffs-harness --test conformance` **100 passed / 0 failed / 2 ignored** (e2fsck-gated — mkdir exercises the initial-block stamp). ⚠️INFRA (unchanged): shared `.rch-targets/fs-cc` + rch workers stale under `rustc 1.98.0-nightly (2026-07-02)`; measured in clean `fs-cc-bttool`. |
| 2026-07-06 | dig-deeper / BlackThrush | **WIRED the incremental dir-block `metadata_csum` into the two htree LEAF-SPLIT add sites** (`ext4_split_htree_leaf_and_add` @18254 + the node-leaf-split @18496) — the last hot add paths still doing a full ~4 KiB CRC recompute. On a create/rename into a FULL htree leaf, the split packs both halves and stamps them, then adds the one new entry to the owning half and (previously) re-stamped that whole leaf from scratch. | 2026-07-05 | dig-deeper / BlackThrush | **CONSTANT-TIME GF(2) suffix shift in the incremental-checksum primitive** (`ffs_ondisk::crc_incremental::crc32c_shift_bytes`) — a space-time-tradeoff primitive from the coding-theory playbook replacing the log-step *bit-decomposition* shift (which did `popcount(8·suffix)` GF matrix-vector products, **1..≈15, avg ~8** for a change at a random offset in a 4 KiB block — the dominant term after last commit's hardware-crc delta win). A change lands at an ARBITRARY offset (dir entries / bitmap runs are anywhere), so the suffix length and thus the shift cost varied per op. New base-256 factoring: precompute `lo[j]` = shift by `j` zero bytes and `hi[k]` = shift by `k·256` zero bytes (two 256-entry operator tables, ~64 KiB, built once); any `suffix < 65536` = `hi[s>>8] · lo[s&0xFF]` → **exactly TWO** matrix-vector products regardless of offset. Feeds `crc32c_update_region`, so it speeds every incremental consumer: the landed dir-block create (4976d6d7) + remove (c9e12ff4) csum and the peer bitmap-csum wiring. | LANDED / own files (`crates/ffs-ondisk/src/crc_incremental.rs`, `crates/ffs-ondisk/benches/crc_incremental.rs`, this ledger). Bit-identical to the log-step shift (same GF(2) operator, different factoring); no format change. | Standing ORIG/kernel gap unchanged (shrinks the CPU term of the already-landed dir-csum wins). | **MEASURED per-crate, clean-dir same-environment A/B** (`CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/projects/.rch-targets/fs-cc-bttool cargo bench --profile release-perf -p ffs-ondisk --bench crc_incremental`): production-representative **`incremental_avg_offsets`** (sum over 64 mixed-popcount suffixes 1..~4000) bit-shift **3.8189 µs** vs byte-shift **1.8955 µs** = **2.01x** median; **`incremental_worstcase`** (high-popcount suffix) **60.14 ns** vs **26.46 ns** = **2.27x**; `incremental_128b` **82 ns → 47.87 ns** (1.71x, on top of the prior hardware-crc win). The 8 B / low-popcount-offset arm is unchanged (already ~2 products). | Gates: primitive `incremental_matches_full_recompute_cases` + `_prop` (random deltas 1..=300 B at random offsets) — byte-shift result == `crc32c::crc32c` of the full buffer; `cargo test -p ffs-ondisk --lib` **676/0**; `cargo test -p ffs-dir --lib` **92/0** (dir-csum consumers); conformance `cargo test -p ffs-harness --test conformance` **100 passed / 0 failed / 2 ignored** (e2fsck-gated). ⚠️INFRA (unchanged): shared `.rch-targets/fs-cc` + rch workers stale under `rustc 1.98.0-nightly (2026-07-02)`; measured in clean isolated dir `fs-cc-bttool`. |
| 2026-07-05 | dig-deeper / BlackThrush | **HARDWARE-CRC32C the delta CRC inside the incremental-checksum primitive** (`ffs_ondisk::crc_incremental::raw_crc32c`) — a radically different primitive (SSE4.2 `CRC32` instruction, ~8 B/instr) replacing the software byte-wise table (one lookup/byte) that computed the changed-region CRC feeding `crc32c_update_region`. This is the delta term of BOTH landed dir-csum wins (create 4976d6d7 + remove c9e12ff4), which pass deltas up to the 256 B span gate; the byte-table was the bottleneck for larger deltas. Uses CRC linearity to convert the `crc32c` crate's *standard* result (init all-ones, inverted) to the raw form the affine combine needs: `R0(d) = std(d) XOR 0xFFFF_FFFF XOR shift(0xFFFF_FFFF, 8·len)` — the one init-constant shift is O(log len) and delta-independent. Hybrid: byte-table kept for deltas ≤ `HW_RAW_CRC_MIN_LEN` (16 B, where the crate-call + init-shift overhead would lose), hardware above. | LANDED / own files (`crates/ffs-ondisk/src/crc_incremental.rs`, this ledger). Bit-identical to the software raw CRC; no format change; benefits every consumer of the primitive (dir-block create/remove csum + the peer bitmap-csum wiring). | Standing ORIG/kernel gap unchanged (this shrinks the CPU term of the already-landed dir-csum wins; not a new mounted-kernel run). | **MEASURED per-crate, clean-dir same-environment A/B** (`CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/projects/.rch-targets/fs-cc-bttool cargo bench --profile release-perf -p ffs-ondisk --bench crc_incremental`): 128 B delta byte-table (ORIG, threshold forced high) **317.54 ns** median (`[307.70, 325.74]`) vs hardware **82.32 ns** median (`[79.95, 84.81]`) = **3.86x** median, **3.63x conservative** (307.70/84.81); the 128 B incremental now runs **8.05x** vs the full 663 ns recompute (was 2.09x). 8 B delta unchanged **~27 ns** (stays on the byte-table by design). `dir_csum_incremental` 28 B change ~66 ns (shift-dominated at that size; the win scales with delta length toward the 256 B gate). | Gates: primitive correctness `incremental_matches_full_recompute_cases` + `_prop` (widened to random deltas 1..=300 B straddling the crossover + explicit 15/16/17/200/256 B cases) — incremental == `crc32c::crc32c` of the full buffer; `cargo test -p ffs-ondisk --lib` **676 passed / 0 failed**; `cargo test -p ffs-dir --lib` **92/0** (dir-csum wiring consumers); conformance `cargo test -p ffs-harness --test conformance` **100 passed / 0 failed / 2 ignored** (e2fsck-gated). ⚠️INFRA (unchanged from prior row): shared `.rch-targets/fs-cc` + rch workers stale under `rustc 1.98.0-nightly (2026-07-02)`; measured in a clean isolated dir `fs-cc-bttool`. |

| 2026-07-05 | dig-deeper / SilverPine | ⭐WIN DEEP DIG + LANDED DESIGN-GUARD: lock-free FAA-style per-group atomic range lease added to the existing `ffs-core` `ext4_alloc_lock_convoy` bench. This is deliberately not another global-lock implementation swap and not the previously landed Flat-Combining microbatch: each disjoint group takes one atomic range lease, replays the group-local accounting in order, then publishes the final padded atomic words. The primitive maps to `/alien-graveyard` §14.6 FAA progress plus FrankenFS lock-free metadata guidance, and the ledger review avoided rejected/no-ship families: RaptorQ row caches/LRC tiling, btrfs staged-node micro-updates, CRC quick wins, free-path scans, and global-lock batching repeats. | LANDED in the benchmark commit / own files (`crates/ffs-core/benches/ext4_alloc_lock_convoy.rs`, this ledger). No production filesystem behavior changed; this is a bounded design guard for the measured bd-bhh0i convoy, not the full `Ext4AllocState` per-group refactor. It refines the dedicated refactor target: per-group ownership plus atomic range leasing can beat microbatching and a per-group mutex, but production sharding still must preserve bitmap read-your-writes, inode-table RMW order, FCW behavior, and e2fsck-clean counts. | Standing ORIG/kernel gap unchanged: FrankenFS deferred create 16t **46,048/s** vs kernel ext4 **170,976/s** = **0.269x kernel** / **3.71x slower**; independent-process ceiling remains **~489k/s** vs current single-image 8t **~54k/s** = **~9x recoverable**. Same-run context: prior microbatch guard **632.15 us** median, so the range lease is **1.90x** faster than the quick-win guard on this worker; sharded mutex was **891.47 us** median. | No direct-kernel claim. Isomorphism: the bench asserts the range-lease model produces the same synthetic per-group accounting result as the one-lock-per-op ORIG model. Ordering within each group is preserved by local replay; cross-group ordering is irrelevant to the synthetic per-group counters; FP/RNG are N/A. Production fallback remains the current `RwLock` until a real allocator refactor has loom/conformance proof. | Gates: benchmark command above GREEN. | 2026-07-04 | perf / BlackThrush | ⭐WIN (verified enabler LANDED, step 2 of the incremental-crc lever) + IMPORTANT GDT-defer nuance. Added `ffs_ondisk::ext4::bitmap_checksum_incremental(old_csum, start_bit, count_bits, units_per_group, desc_size)` — applies the crc32c primitive (ff222d17) to the ext4 bitmap descriptor checksum: alloc/free flip the range's bits so the CRC delta is exactly the range MASK (no before/after byte capture needed). Returns None (→ caller full-recomputes) for desc_size<64 (only 16 bits stored), out-of-region, or a large touched span (bit-wise delta CRC loses to SIMD full past ~1/32 of the bitmap). | LANDED / own files (`crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs` + proptest). Speed is the ff222d17 11.6x (this is a thin wrapper). | vs ORIG: ⚠️NUANCE found this turn — GDT persistence is DEFERRED by default (bd-cc-gdt-defer): the per-op GD *write* is batched to flush, but `persist_group_desc_force` still RECOMPUTES the csum per-op into the in-memory GD (before the deferral check). So the ~648 ns/alloc recompute IS happening per-op (wasteful if only the flush value is written). TWO fixes: (a) wire `bitmap_checksum_incremental` (11x, my area, this building block enables it), or (b) DEFER the recompute to the flush pass too (skip per-op entirely, ∞ better but owner-lane bd-cc-gdt-defer — SilverPine). (a) is the safe solo path. | Gates: proptest 400 cases == full; lib 674/0. |

I'd written that the undo-tracking free path "keeps per-bit — the undo Vec is inherently O(count)". WRONG: `free_blocks_persist`'s `rollback_set_bits` Vec is LOCAL to the fn, and each freed segment is a CONTIGUOUS range fully-set before the clear (guaranteed by the double-free validation) — so the segment's own `rel_start`/`count` IS the rollback undo. Range-ified the whole free path: double-free validation → `bitmap_find_free_range` (first free bit = first double-free, my 4-wide scan), clear → `bitmap_clear_range`, undo → re-`bitmap_set_range` the segment on rollback; DROPPED the per-bit undo Vec entirely. | LANDED / own files (`crates/ffs-alloc/src/lib.rs` free_blocks_persist + bench `free_range_undo`). | **MEASURED** (`CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/projects/.rch-targets/fs-cc rch exec -- cargo bench --profile release-perf -p ffs-alloc --bench free_range_undo`, iter_batched): count=256 old per-bit-validate+clear+undo-push **608 ns** → new range **84 ns = 7.2x**; count=4096 **6964 → 263 ns = 26.5x**. Bit-identical (equivalence assert old==new bitmap). | vs ORIG: large-file unlink/truncate frees big contiguous extents on the 3.7x create/write serial floor (bd-bhh0i) — was O(N) validate + O(N) clear + O(N) undo-push, now O(N/8) throughout. SAFE + conformance-preserving. | Gates: ffs-alloc **214/214** incl `proptest_multi_block_alloc_free_roundtrip`, `proptest_interleaved_alloc_free_consistent`, `proptest_free_blocks_cross_boundary_splits_segments` (rollback + segment-split correctness). ⭐measure-don't-reason applied to my OWN prior "inherently O(count)" claim — the undo was LOCAL + range-shaped. |

| 2026-07-04 | perf / BlackThrush | ⭐⭐NEW LEVER CLASS (scan vein exhausted → algorithmic O(E)→O(log E)). ext4 extent tree LEAF + INDEX search (`walk_extent_tree` + `walk_extent_tree_cached`, ffs-ondisk ext4.rs) was a LINEAR `for ext in extents` scan, RE-RUN per resolved block (the child-cache stores the child block, NOT the resolved extent → not amortized, UNLIKE btrfs leaf-search which the parsed-node cache amortized to ~0-gain). Result-identical for sorted non-overlapping extents (parse validates order; equivalence assert). | vs ORIG: the ext4 READ path resolves an extent per block; for a fragmented file (many extents/leaf, aged FS) the per-block resolve was O(E) → now O(log E), free on typical files. | 2026-07-04 | perf / BlackThrush | ✅WIN LANDED (skip the redundant readdir-internal inode-table PREFETCH for `readdirplus` — couples with the getattr-fan-out batching ee8d5208). `OpenFs::readdir` fires `prefetch_ext4_readdir_inode_table_blocks` (a rayon fan-out warming the page's inode-table blocks) for EVERY readdir. | 2026-07-04 | perf / BlackThrush | ✅WIN LANDED (bound the `readdirplus` getattr fan-out — first NON-xattr lever this session; a materialise-all-then-partially-use). `readdirplus` (ffs-fuse lib.rs:5971) fanned out parallel `getattr` over the ENTIRE `ops.readdir` page (capped 512) but the consumer `reply.add` loop `break`s the instant the client reply buffer fills (~150-200 entries) — so ~300 of the 512 getattrs/page were computed then DISCARDED, and because each readdirplus cookie only advances by the entries that fit, a full `ls -l` re-fetched that discarded window every page (≈512·N/F getattrs vs the ideal N). FIX: fan out in bounded batches (128) in entry order, stop the moment the reply buffer is full → wasted getattrs bounded to < 1 batch instead of 512−F; parallelism/I-O-overlap preserved within each batch. | LANDED / own file (`crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs` readdirplus). | **~2.8x FEWER getattrs on a large-dir `ls -l` enumeration** (structural: old ≈ 512·N/F ≈ per-page 512, new ≈ N ≈ per-page F+≤128; F≈150-200 fit per buffer → per-page ~512→~300 = 1.7x, full-enumeration ~512/F ≈ 2.8x). Each eliminated getattr is a real inode-table read+parse (cold) or shard-lock cache get + `to_file_attr` alloc (warm). ⚠️SCOPE: this is a getattr-work-COUNT reduction (behaviour-verified), NOT a full-mount wall-clock microbench — a FUSE-handler wall-clock needs a live mount + large dir + readdirplus(2) syscalls, not a per-crate `cargo bench`; the count reduction is a structural fact and getattr dominates readdirplus cost. | vs ext4/btrfs: cheapens `ls -l`/`find -ls`/`getdents+stat` on large directories (both fs — readdirplus is fs-agnostic dispatch). | Gates: ffs-fuse **lib tests 571/571 passed** (incl readdirplus dispatch tests via mock FsOps). Behaviour-identical: same entries/attrs/order, same skip-on-getattr-error, same break-on-full; only the discarded-getattr side-effect (cache warm) is bounded. ⭐continues the projection meta beyond xattr: producer (512-wide fan-out) materialised data the consumer (buffer-bounded `reply.add`) discarded. ⚠️Explore also flagged `Ext4FsOps::readdir` O(N²/p) tail-clone @16304 (RO image adapter, cap missing) — deferred: only pays if that adapter is hot (OpenFs is primary). |

| 2026-07-04 | perf / BlackThrush | ⭐WIN A/B-CONFIRMED + LANDED (WORK-HOIST on the ext4 READ path — a fresh uncontended crate, `ffs-ondisk`, found after standing down from a peer collision on scrub). `Ext4ImageReader::resolve_extent` (ext4.rs:4801) called `parse_inode_extent_tree` — a header decode **plus a heap-allocating `Vec<Ext4Extent>` build** (per-extent `from_le_bytes` + sort/overlap validation) — on EVERY call, and the three hottest read paths (`read_inode_data` 4991, `read_dir` 5028, `lookup` 5052) call it once per logical block in a loop. The parsed tree is INVARIANT across all blocks of one inode, so a 128 KiB read (32×4 KiB) re-parsed + re-allocated the identical tree 32×. FIX: add `resolve_extent_with_tree`, `parse_inode_extent_tree` ONCE before each loop, reuse per block (guarded on empty read / 0 dir-blocks to preserve exact error behaviour). | LANDED / own files (`crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs` resolve_extent+read_inode_data+read_dir+lookup, `crates/ffs-ondisk/benches/extent_resolve_hoist.rs`, `crates/ffs-ondisk/Cargo.toml`) | **MEASURED ~9.7x** on the per-block resolve loop for a 32-block read (`CARGO_TARGET_DIR=.rch-targets/blackthrush-dig rch exec -- cargo bench --profile release-perf -p ffs-ondisk --bench extent_resolve_hoist`, worker hz1): old `parse_per_block` **895.5 ns** vs new `parse_once_hoisted` **92.0 ns** = **9.73x**. Behaviour-IDENTICAL: bench equivalence assert (both arms resolve all 32 blocks to the same phys) + expected-sum assert both pass. NOTE: this is the resolve-loop COMPONENT; end-to-end `read_inode_data` gain is smaller (the per-block `read_block`+`copy_from_slice` data-copy is additive and unchanged) — but for depth-0 (inline) inodes, the common case, resolve was the dominant per-block CPU (data fetch is a zero-copy slice). | vs ext4: cheapens ext4 file-read, readdir, and path-lookup per-block extent resolution (removes a parse+heap-alloc per block); flows to every FUSE read/readdir/lookup. No direct kernel rerun; ratio is internal against the original per-block re-parse. | Gates: ffs-ondisk **lib tests 668/668 passed** (exercise read_inode_data/read_dir/lookup/resolve_extent/extent-parse) + 2 bench asserts; behaviour-identical by construction (parse-once yields the same tree the per-block parse produced). ⭐PATTERN: WORK-HOIST — a per-ITEM loop recomputing a loop-invariant (here a parse+alloc, not just a division) that the compiler can't lift across the call; hoist to before the loop. Bigger ratio than the scrub division-hoist because the invariant here includes a **heap allocation**, not just arithmetic. |

| 2026-07-04 | perf / BlackThrush | ⭐WIN A/B-CONFIRMED + LANDED (NEW, non-covered per-crate lever, benched via rch). `BtrfsTreeBlockValidator::validate` (ffs-repair scrub.rs) called `BtrfsHeader::parse_from_block` — copies 64B (csum[32]+fsid[16]+chunk_tree_uuid[16]) + ~8 int-field reads — on EVERY scanned block, only to reject on fsid mismatch. | 2026-07-03 | perf / BlackThrush | SURFACE (BtrfsTreeItem.data is `Arc<[u8]>` — closes the last btrfs-COW-churn question with a read/write tradeoff) — verified the type behind the ~22% btrfs-write COW churn (`drop_glue` + `extend_trusted` item copies): `BtrfsTreeItem { key: BtrfsKey, data: Arc<[u8]> }` (lib.rs:2783). So a COW leaf copy is N *single Arc-refcount atomics* (not N data copies), and the retire-drop is N refcount decrements. | SURFACE / no code | Not a lever — it's a deliberate read-vs-write TRADEOFF, and the current choice is right for read-heavy btrfs: (a) `Arc<[u8]>` clone = 1 atomic + a tiny item struct (key + ptr) → GOOD read/traversal cache density + cheap COW-sharing (the new leaf shares unchanged items' data, no data copy); (b) inline `SmallVec` data would make the small-item clone a cheap memcpy (no atomic) BUT bloat every item struct → worse leaf density on the hot READ/lookup path (the attr-Arc lesson: bigger inline structs hurt read-heavy density); (c) structural sharing (persistent vector, O(log M) COW) would slow the Vec-direct-index reads btrfs lookups depend on. | vs ext4/btrfs: the ~22% COW item churn is inherent to btrfs's copy-on-write B-tree (the kernel copies leaf blocks too); ext4's htree pays crc/RMW instead. | Definitively closes the btrfs-write COW analysis: every hot op optimized (insert bd-btrcow2, delete-leaf landed, split split_off), rare ops not-worth (merge refuted), and the inherent per-item Arc atomic churn is the accepted cost of the read-optimized design (all three alternatives — inline data, persistent vector, and the buffer-pool already-REFUTED — trade away the read-heavy fast path). btrfs writes are at their COW floor with the mechanism now fully understood, not just asserted. Sole large lever: fs-wide bd-bhh0i (~13×). |

| 2026-07-03 | perf / BlackThrush | REFUTED (incremental `dir_csum` — PROVEN correct but ~10× SLOWER than HW re-crc) — the rename-profile row flagged incremental-crc as a ~5-6% lever (compute the new 4 KiB dir-block crc from the old crc + the ~48-B edit delta, skipping the 4 KiB re-crc). Built it SAFELY in a bench (`ffs-inode::crc_incremental`, no FS change): implemented `crc32c_combine` (GF(2) matrix, CRC-32C poly) + the incremental dir-edit formula (`crc_new = crc_old ^ advance(M(delta), suffix)`, `M(x)=crc32c(x)^crc32c(0^len)`). | REFUTED / no prod code (kept the proof bench) | **Correctness PROVEN**: both asserts pass on random inputs — `crc32c_combine(crc(A),crc(B),len B) == crc(A++B)` (64 seeds) AND incremental-dir-edit == full re-crc (32 seeds). **But SPEED is a 10× LOSS**: `full_recrc` (SSE4.2 HW `crc32c` of 4 KiB) **888 ns** vs `incremental` (software GF(2) combine advancing a crc through the ~4 KiB suffix) **9.50 µs**. | n/a | Measure-don't-reason, decisively: my ~5-6% estimate was WRONG — I costed the GF(2) combine as cheap, but the hardware crc streams 4 KiB (~0.9 µs) far faster than a scalar polynomial combine (~9.5 µs). The HW crc is the reason the whole crc category is at its floor: it's too fast to beat with algebraic tricks. Proving it in a bench BEFORE recommending a corruption-risky `dir_csum` wiring saved a 10×-slower change. `crc32c_combine` is correct+kept as evidence, but incremental-crc STAYS UNBUILT. Removes the last flagged single-turn lever. |

The copy is LOAD-BEARING for round-trip preservation, not wasted. | n/a | Traced ALL consumers before touching it: the xattr-read consumer is robust to empty, but the write-back consumer (serialize) needs the raw bytes. HYPOTHESIS: construct inline (stack array + `.as_slice().into()` = `From<&[u8]>` copy) to drop the per-create heap alloc, matching parse + str2hashbuf/serialize pattern. TESTED with a new `prepare_inode_extent_bytes` criterion bench (kept). | REVERTED / no code change (comment + bench added) | **Criterion REFUTES it: `vec_into_spill` 35.4ns vs `array_slice_into_inline` 61.1ns — the inline copy is SLOWER.** jemalloc's small-alloc + Vec-buffer-reuse (from_vec just moves the pointer, no copy) beats zeroing a stack array and copying 60B inline. The heap "alloc" I hoped to remove is cheaper than the inline copy that replaces it. | n/a (reverted) | The str2hashbuf/serialize discipline ("measure, don't reason") cutting the OTHER way: I reasoned "inline removes an alloc → faster" and it was WRONG — the current `vec.into()` spill is the faster construction. Setting `CFLAGS=-march=x86-64-v3` for them would NOT help: both RUNTIME-DISPATCH their SIMD (libzstd detects CPU features and picks the best decode routine at run time; jemalloc is pointer-arithmetic + locks with minimal SIMD). That is precisely WHY `target-cpu` helped frankenfs (its scalar loops don't runtime-dispatch, so the compiler must be told to vectorize) but would be a no-op on these libs (they already use AVX2 at run time when present). | SURFACE / no code | No ratio — reasoned no-op (not worth a build cycle for a confidently-null result). | 2026-07-03 | perf / BlackThrush | CORRECTION (to the sibling MVCC-copy row) — verified the "~2 full-block copies per write" claim by reading the commit path, and it is WRONG for the common SINGLE-WRITER case. `merged_write_bytes_locked` (sharded.rs:523) has a fast path: `let observed = latest_commit_seq_in_shard(...); if observed <= snapshot.high { return bytes; }` — with no concurrent write to the block it returns the staged `bytes` by MOVE (no copy), and the merge's `latest.to_vec()` (ffs-mvcc:305) runs ONLY under a concurrent same-block modification. The commit-merge copy is concurrency-only; the MVCC already installs staged bytes by move on the no-conflict fast path. | 2026-07-03 | perf / BlackThrush | SURFACE (SECOND architectural lever identified — MVCC full-block copy) — the `__memmove` that sits at ~10–15% of EVERY write op (create/mkdir/rename/unlink/write, both eager and registered paths) is the MVCC version copy. Located precisely: `FsMvccBlockDevice::write_block` (fs_mvcc_store.rs:429) does `txn.stage_write(block, data.to_vec())` — a FULL 4 KiB heap alloc+copy per block write — because the `BlockDevice::write_block(&self, cx, block, data: &[u8])` trait borrows the data. The commit/merge path copies again: `let mut merged = latest.to_vec()` then range-patches (ffs-mvcc/src/lib.rs:305). So a metadata write that changes ~40 bytes pays ~2 full-block copies. | SURFACE / no code | No ratio. The clean fix is a DELTA/range-based staging API (callers pass changed ranges, not the full block; the `merge_proof` machinery in ffs-mvcc ALREADY tracks changed ranges — line 290/317 — so the read/merge side is half-built) OR a `write_block_owned(Vec<u8>)` path so callers hand off ownership instead of `to_vec`-copying. Both are PERVASIVE: the `BlockDevice` trait signature + every impl + every write-path caller. And per the alloc-refutation (0778ddfc) the pure-alloc portion may not fully translate — the win is the 4 KiB memmove (genuine work), not the malloc. | n/a | Completes the big-levers map: TWO architectural levers remain, both owner-lane/multi-turn — (1) bd-bhh0i parallel-create serialization (proven ~7x, `docs/bd-bhh0i-parallel-create-plan.md`); (2) this MVCC full-block-copy (~10–15% of every single write, fix = delta staging API). Everything else (all ops) is at its floor. No single-turn frankenfs-crate lever remains. |

| 2026-07-03 | perf / BlackThrush | SURFACE (btrfs lookup at floor; explains the ext4↔btrfs 5x gap) — profiled `lookup-bench` on btr3 (30000 entries): **btrfs 23.9M lookups/s vs ext4 4.74M = 5x**. btrfs profile: `FsOps::lookup` 40.8% (self — the tree query), `ShardedCache::get` 18.4% (the SAME shared attr cache as ext4), `btrfs_read_inode_attr` 15% — and notably ZERO arc_swap. Both lookups exceed the real kernel-round-trip FUSE ceiling by orders of magnitude. | 0/0/1: btrfs lookup is FASTER than the equivalent kernel-cached path here; not a gap. | Confirms btrfs lookup at floor and pinpoints the ext4↔btrfs 5x lookup gap as an inherent on-disk-format difference (dir-in-inode-blocks vs dir-in-tree), not an optimizable lever. Either alone is insufficient. | n/a | vs kernel: kernel parallel-create is ~8.3x faster @8t (prior bd-bhh0i note); the independent-process ~7x ceiling shows frankenfs's per-op WORK is competitive — the entire gap is in-process shared-state serialization, recoverable by shard+spread. Methodology note: the independent-process test is a reusable, unsafe-free way to measure a parallelization ceiling (isolates shared-state serialization from inherent per-op cost); WARM it first (a cold first run measured a misleading 1.7x from page-cache/binary warmup). | 2026-07-03 | perf / BlackThrush | SURFACE (SHARPENS bd-bhh0i — parallel create root cause) — fresh `create-bench --threads` into `/` NEGATIVE-scales: 1t **99.7k** → 2t 110k → 4t 70k → 8t 63.5k → 16t **59.4k** creates/s (16t is SLOWER than 1t). Traced the true serializer chain: `create_inode` = `prepare_inode` (`ffs_alloc::alloc_inode_persist` persists the inode BITMAP + group desc) THEN `write_inode` (persists inode CONTENT) — bitmap-before-content, so moving `write_inode` outside `alloc_mutex` would be crash-consistency-NEUTRAL (the crash window already exists). BUT `write_inode` does a read-modify-write of the shared inode-table BLOCK, and a same-parent create storm packs new inodes into ADJACENT slots of the SAME inode-table block → concurrent content writes conflict on that one block no matter the lock strategy. | SURFACE / no code | No ratio. **KEY CORRECTION to the standing bd-bhh0i plan**: per-group `alloc_mutex` sharding ALONE is INSUFFICIENT for the common same-parent/same-group storm — even with per-group locks, inodes cluster in one group's adjacent inode-table blocks and serialize on shared-block RMW (the exact coherence failure that killed the 2 prior attempts, now shown to generalize from the GDT block to inode-table blocks). The fix needs ALSO inode-allocation SPREADING (disjoint inode-table blocks per concurrent writer) or a kernel-style journaled batch-commit that coalesces adjacent-slot writes — NOT just lock sharding. | n/a | Refines the memory's "shard alloc_mutex per-group" into "shard + spread inode allocation so concurrent creates touch disjoint inode-table blocks." Still owner-lane / loom-gated / multi-turn (the throwaway-A/B ceiling would need the unsafe outside-lock write, which conflicts heavily on the shared inode-table block for a same-parent bench → confirms the shared-block RMW is the wall). This is the sole substantive remaining lever; everything else (single-node reads/writes, compression decode, fsck, per-request Cx) is at floor or dependency/owner-owned. |

The per-request-Cx cost is an ASUPERSYNC-owned lever (cheaper request context / pooled inner). The memmove is inherent (the read API + FUSE reply both need the bytes copied). | n/a | Important cross-cutting finding: the per-request `Cx::for_request()` allocation cost (7.70% here) is on the hot path of EVERY FUSE operation (read/write/lookup/getattr/…), so an asupersync-side lighter request-context (or a lock-free observability path) would lift the whole FUSE throughput ceiling. Recorded for the asupersync owners; not a frankenfs-crate change. |

| 2026-07-02 | perf / BlackThrush | LANDED — eager per-block commit for `ext4_unlink_impl` (unlink + rmdir), realizing the candidate lever from the sibling SURFACE/throughput rows. Then implemented it SAFELY: keep the bd-cc-unlink-1pass single hash-leaf descent, but the entry removal now only mutates the parent dir block IN MEMORY (`pending_dir_write`); the eager destructive persist is DEFERRED until AFTER every fallible check passes (is-dir/rmdir-empty/immutable-append AND the link-count-underflow corruption check), so a rejected op persists nothing — same observable rollback as the old drop-the-txn path. Dir-entry removal is persisted BEFORE the inode is freed → an interrupted op leaves at worst an orphan inode (e2fsck-reclaimable), never a dangling entry. | KEEP / landed on `main` | Interleaved A/B, `release-perf` on fresh `cc_ext.img` copies, 8000 ops into `/`: delbench (unlink) OLD ~68k/s vs NEW ~195k/s = **~2.85x** (5/5); rdbench (rmdir) OLD ~34k/s vs NEW ~55k/s = **~1.6x** (3/3). Every rejection returns before the deferred flush, so a rejected op is observably identical to the old txn-drop. Atomicity for a mid-write I/O error now matches create's eager path (orphan-inode-safe ordering; e2fsck-reclaimable, not corruption). Gates all green (above). | 
| 2026-07-02 | perf / BlackThrush | SURFACE (candidate lever, measured baseline, no code) — single-threaded metadata-op throughput baseline (8000 ops into `/` on `cc_ext.img`, `release-perf`): create **125k/s**, rename 86k, unlink 64k, mkdir 44k, rmdir 34k. Create is 1.5–3.6x FASTER than every remove/rename op. VERIFIED structural difference: `ext4_create` writes through the EAGER per-block adapter (`self.block_device_adapter()`, no txn) whereas `ext4_unlink_impl` (lib.rs:19011) stages into a `TransactionBlockAdapter` over one txn and commits via the HEAVY `self.mvcc_store.commit(txn)` (lib.rs:19251) — the same heavy path my sibling REFUTED row measured ~2x slower than eager for create. | SURFACE / no code change | Throughput table above (single run each; interleaved A/B not run — this is a scouting baseline). ⚠️ATTRIBUTION was initially unverified (unlink/rmdir do strictly MORE work than create: positive-entry removal + `delete_inode` free of inode+bitmap + parent link/time update + is-dir/empty validation) — BUT a cross-check STRENGTHENS it: the sibling REFUTED row's create-BATCHED throughput ≈ **59k/s** and unlink (batched) ≈ **64k/s** are nearly EQUAL, and both are ~half of eager create **125k/s**. Since create-batched lands at the same ~60k as unlink despite unlink doing more work, the **batched-commit path — not the extra op work — is what caps throughput at ~60k**. So the commit mode is well-supported as the dominant gap (a controlled unlink-eager A/B would confirm). | `n/a`. | `0/0/0`. | CANDIDATE LEVER (for a focused, gated effort — NOT rush-landed): convert unlink/rmdir to the eager per-block commit path like create. BLOCKER: the batched txn is load-bearing for atomicity — the one-pass removal (bd-cc-unlink-1pass) REMOVES the entry then validates (is-dir/rmdir-empty) and rolls back by dropping the uncommitted txn (lib.rs:19052-19055). Going eager requires reintroducing a validate-BEFORE-remove ordering (a lookup to learn the child inode first — the second htree descent the one-pass eliminated), so it's a correctness-sensitive restructure that must be e2fsck-gated + A/B'd to confirm the eager win exceeds the re-added lookup cost. Recorded as a candidate, not asserted as a win. |
| 2026-07-02 | `bd-bhh0i` / BlackThrush | REFUTED (measured, reverted) — the "batch create's per-block commits into one txn" premise is WRONG single-threaded. IMPLEMENTED the safe, bounded slice: wrapped `ffs_inode::create_inode`'s writes (inode-table + inode-bitmap + GDT, all through its single `dev` arg) in a `TransactionBlockAdapter` over one `mvcc_store.begin()` txn and committed once (~3 per-block commits → 1). Correctness PASSED (create-bench 2000 → re-open walk sees all + `e2fsck -fn` CLEAN, 2014 files 0% non-contig — so it is NOT the corruption the prior parallel op_batch hit). But it MEASURED **~2x SLOWER** single-threaded. | REVERT / no code change (working tree restored) | Interleaved A/B, `release-perf` `create-bench 4000 --threads 1` on fresh `cc_ext.img` copies: OLD (eager per-block) median ~132k creates/s vs NEW (batched) ~59k = **0.45x (2.2x SLOWER)**, 6/6. ROOT CAUSE (mechanism): the eager `FsMvccBlockDevice` runs in **Unregistered** mode, whose per-block `write_block` (fs_mvcc_store.rs:421) was deliberately made CHEAP by prior bd-bhh0i work (`block_device_adapter`, 8757b5c7-era: "construct Unregistered directly … zero global-lock ops" — no `register/release_snapshot`, no publication-gate round-trip). My batched path used the STANDARD `mvcc_store.begin()`/`commit()` = the HEAVY registered-commit path (register_snapshot + global `CommitPublicationGate` + `prune_after_commit_if_due`). Heavy-commit-once ≫ light-commit-3×. | `1/0/0`: create-bench 2000 + e2fsck-clean (correctness); ffs-core `create` (`41 passed`), `ext4_create` (`2 passed`). | `0/0/1`: n/a. | ⭐CORRECTS the bd-bhh0i framing (incl. my own earlier SURFACE rows): the single-threaded per-block commits are NOT the bottleneck — they are already a cheap Unregistered fast-path — so REDUCING COMMIT COUNT is a net LOSS single-threaded. The real bd-bhh0i cost is PURELY the PARALLEL serialization: N threads' cheap-but-in-order commits convoy on the ONE global `CommitPublicationGate` + `active_snapshots` lock. The fix must reduce PARALLEL contention on those globals (finer-grained / per-shard publication), NOT batch commits. Any batching MUST use a light Unregistered-equivalent commit, else it regresses even before contention. Reverted; working tree clean. |
| 2026-07-02 | `bd-bhh0i` / BlackThrush | SURFACE (sharpened blocker, no code) — single-threaded ext4 create path profiled to characterize the one remaining ext4/btrfs lever. `perf` of `create-bench --threads 1 --count 20000` is syscall-bound: kernel `pread`/`pwrite`/page-cache (`0xffff…`) ≈ 40%, plus rayon `steal`/`wait_until_cold` ≈ 3% (pool spinning even at t=1) and a residual `getenv` 1.0% (dependency-internal: jemalloc/asupersync — both frankenfs create-path getenvs are already `OnceLock`-memoized, `408cb413`). The syscall storm is the bd-bhh0i root cause, and this pass PINS it to code: `FsMvccBlockDevice::write_block` (crates/ffs-core/src/fs_mvcc_store.rs:421) does `store.begin()` + `stage_write(ONE block)` + `commit(txn)` PER BLOCK, so a create (~3-4 metadata blocks: inode, inode-bitmap, shared GDT, dir-block) = 3-4 full begin/commit/publish cycles, each through the global commit-publication gate + `register/release_snapshot`. | SURFACE / no code change (owner-lane) | No A/B (characterization). NEW over the bead's prior "route through commit_request_scope" note: the batching infrastructure ALREADY EXISTS and is simply not wired for create — `RequestScope.tx` (a per-op MVCC txn, `begin_request_scope` lib.rs:36159), `commit_request_scope` (lib.rs:36235, commits the scope's whole write-set ONCE), and `TransactionBlockAdapter` (lib.rs:2158, stages block writes into a `&mut Transaction` instead of committing). So the fix is a WIRING change, not new infra: point create's metadata block writes at a `TransactionBlockAdapter` over the scope's `tx` and commit once via `commit_request_scope`, collapsing 3-4 commits/create → 1 (cuts the global-gate + snapshot-register hits ~3-4x even single-threaded, and is the prerequisite for parallel scaling). | `n/a`: no change. | `0/0/0`: no behavior change. | BLOCKER / why not landed this turn: the correctness crux is the SHARED GDT block (block 1 holds all 4 group descriptors) — batching + any concurrency must keep its read-modify-write coherent, which is exactly where the two prior attempts died (op_batch `931cb6d8` = a parallel 2nd batching path that split-brained on block 1, e2fsck free_blocks/bitmap-csum, reverted `c4222300`; convoy prototype = stale committed-bitmap read across the deferred commit, first-committer-wins reject). The safe path is single-threaded first (batch one create's writes into scope.tx, gate on `create-bench 2000 → e2fsck CLEAN`), then parallel under loom — a focused owner-lane effort, not a rush-land. Read-only levers are exhausted (lookup/walk/read at floors; 4 walk wins `3e21ec5f`/`058cb671`/`38019cf5`/`61c9e052` this session), so this is the next real target. |
| 2026-07-02 | perf / BlackThrush | REJECTION (no lever) — ext4 file-read path (`rand-read`, the read-engine bench; `read`-to-stdout is write()-syscall-bound and not a clean read bench). Profiled `rand-read` on a 128MB contiguous ext4 file at two sizes to look for redundant FS-side work. | REJECT / no code change | 4KiB reads (200000): dominated by kernel `pread`/page-cache symbols (`0xffff…` ≈ 30%+) + `__memmove_avx` 4.3% + `__memset_avx2` 1.3%; FS-side is small — `read_file_data` 2.2%, `read_into` 1.65%, `ext4_ensure_hot_extents` 1.0%. 64KiB reads (20000): even more I/O/copy-bound; ALL FS-side symbols <1.2% — `resolve_extent` 1.15%, `read_file_data` 0.59%, `read_contiguous_into_with_scope` 0.30%, `ext4_ensure_hot_extents` 0.13%. No redundant-work hotspot: extent resolution is already hot-cached, the per-read `statx` (~0.72% at 64KiB) is the intentional bd-wvdrd truncation-safety re-check (must stay), and the `memset`/`memmove` are the read buffer fill/copy (meta-lesson: alloc/copy tuning measured neutral, e.g. e5bb9991). Confirms + quantifies the prior "ext4 rand-read is I/O-bound, beats kernel" note. | `n/a`: no change. | `0/0/0`: read output unaffected. | No isomorphism risk (no change). REMAINING GAP (surfaced, not a profile-and-fix lever): the read-only lookup / walk / read paths are all at their I/O/parse floors — this session's 4 walk/readdir wins (`3e21ec5f` 1.38x, `058cb671` 2.4x, `38019cf5` 1.46x, `61c9e052` 1.6x) exhausted the walk vein. The one substantive ext4/btrfs gap left is bd-bhh0i parallel-metadata-WRITE negative scaling (8.3x slower than kernel at 8t), which is owner-lane — it needs per-group inode-table/bitmap partitioning so disjoint creates touch disjoint blocks (the committed-bitmap-read serialization the two prior refuted attempts hit), not a redundant-work elimination. |
| 2026-07-02 | perf / BlackThrush | `ffs-core` ONE-PASS walk cache skip — btrfs (extends `38019cf5`, which wired the `readonly_lookup_cache_disabled` flag for ext4 only, to the two btrfs read-only repeat-access caches). A btrfs `walk` is one-pass just like ext4: it getattrs by inode and never resolves a name via `lookup`, so both caches only ever miss — the readdir name->child map (`btrfs_dir_entry_cache`, win #23, cloning every name into an `FxHashMap` per readdir, lib.rs:8178) and the shared `ext4_inode_attr_cache` insert in `btrfs_read_inode_attr` (win #22, lib.rs:7851). Gated both on the existing flag (`walk_cmd` already sets it for all walks); default path (lookups) unchanged → win #22/#23 preserved. | KEEP / landed on `main` | Two-binary interleaved A/B, OLD=`38019cf5` (ext4 gates only; btrfs walk still builds both caches) vs NEW=this change, `release-perf` FULL `walk` (with getattr) on persisted `btr3_572980.img` (30002 entries / 30001 stats, read-only). OLD median ~12.6ms vs NEW ~7.9ms = **~1.6x** faster (10/10 paired, every NEW << OLD) — again larger than the ~2.6% summed self-time (`btrfs_read_inode_attr` ~1.3% + the name-map build buried in ~10% memmove/malloc churn) because it removes ~30000 name-clone allocs + attr-cache inserts. Walk output byte-identical (30002 entries / 30001 stats). btrfs `lookup-bench` NOT regressed (V4≈V5 ~26M lookups/s — lookup workloads don't set the flag → both caches still built). | `1/0/0`: `rch exec -- cargo test -p ffs-core --lib` `btrfs_lookup` (`3 passed / 1 ignored`), `btrfs_readdir` (`9 passed`); `rch exec -- cargo test -p ffs-harness --test conformance` (`100 passed / 0 failed / 2 ignored`). | `0/0/1`: no fresh mounted-kernel comparator rerun; kernel-visible getattr/readdir output unchanged — both are private accelerators; skipping them means a following lookup descends the DIR_ITEM fs-tree and a re-getattr re-reads the inode (fresh, identical result). | Isomorphism: `btrfs_read_inode_attr` still reads the inode from the read-plan index / on-disk INODE_ITEM and builds the same attr; the name->child map is consulted ONLY by `btrfs_lookup_child`, unrelated to readdir's returned rows (which come from the deduped `rows`). Flag defaults `false` → every non-walk btrfs caller unchanged. Gates: btrfs_lookup/btrfs_readdir + conformance 100/0/2 pass; walk output identical; lookup-bench non-regressed; edited regions fmt-clean. |
| 2026-07-02 | perf / BlackThrush | `ffs-core` ONE-PASS walk cache skip (extends the `058cb671` present-index gate to the FULL stat walk + the per-inode attr cache). A `walk` (`find | xargs stat`) is one-pass in every mode: it getattrs each entry BY INODE, never resolves a name via `lookup`, and visits each inode once — so BOTH read-only *repeat-access* caches only ever miss: the readdir present-index (name->dirent, win #19) and the `ext4_inode_attr_cache` (`ShardedCache<u64,InodeAttr>`, win #20). `058cb671` skipped the present-index only for `--no-stat` (tied to the prefetch flag); a FULL stat walk still built the present-index (~2% self) AND grew the attr cache to 30000 entries (`RawTable<(u64,InodeAttr)>::reserve_rehash` ~2.1% + get ~1.1% + inserts, all pure miss). Added a dedicated `readonly_lookup_cache_disabled` flag (decoupled from the prefetch flag, which stays ON to warm the stat walk's getattr); `walk_cmd` sets it in ALL modes; gates the present-index build in readdir and the attr-cache get+insert in `read_inode_attr_with_scope`. | KEEP / landed on `main` | Two-binary interleaved A/B, OLD=`058cb671` (present-index gated only for --no-stat) vs NEW=this change, `release-perf` FULL `walk` (with getattr) on persisted read-only ext4 flat dir (`ext_flat.img`, 30004 entries, 30003 stats). OLD median ~37ms vs NEW ~25.4ms = **~1.46x** faster (10/10 paired, every NEW << OLD) — larger than the summed self-times because it also removes ~60000 present-index name-clone allocs + the attr-cache rehash churn. `--no-stat` still ~6ms (the ~2.4x from `058cb671` preserved via the new flag). Walk output byte-identical (30004 entries / 30003 stats). `lookup-bench` NOT regressed (lookup workloads don't set the flag → both caches still built; V4 ≈ V3, peaked ~1.99M lookups/s). ext4-focused (present-index + ext4 attr-cache path). | `1/0/0`: `rch exec -- cargo test -p ffs-core --lib` `getattr` (`8 passed`), `inode_attr` (`2 passed`), `lookup_name`/`present`/`readdir` (unchanged, `2`/`2`/`24 passed`); `rch exec -- cargo test -p ffs-harness --test conformance` (`100 passed / 0 failed / 2 ignored`). | `0/0/1`: no fresh mounted-kernel comparator rerun; kernel-visible getattr/readdir output unchanged — both caches are private accelerators, skipping them only means a repeated getattr / following lookup takes the uncached inode-read / htree path. | Isomorphism: getattr still reads+parses the inode fresh (`read_inode_metadata_with_scope` → `inode_to_attr`), so the returned attr is identical whether or not it was cached; the flag defaults `false` so every non-walk caller (FUSE, lookup benches, conformance) is byte-for-byte unchanged. Gates: getattr/inode_attr/lookup/present/readdir + conformance 100/0/2 pass; full-walk + --no-stat output identical; lookup-bench non-regressed; edited regions fmt-clean (pre-existing `nightly`-bump drift at unrelated lines only). |

A readdir-ONLY consumer (`ls -f`, getdents/name-enumeration, `walk --no-stat`) never issues that lookup, so on a large read-only ext4 dir it is pure dead work (`perf` of a 30004-entry `walk --no-stat` showed the `FxHashMap::from_iter::<readdir closure>` at ~4.4% self-time plus its ~60000 name-clone allocs across malloc/memmove). | 2026-06-21 | `bd-29an3` (CrimsonFox cc/opus) | `ffs-core` ext4 extent read par_iter (`lib.rs` ~11606) effective-parallelism cap via per-call `.with_min_len(jobs.len()/24)` — group contiguous 128 KiB chunks per rayon task so the read uses ~24-way instead of the full 64-thread pool. Motivated by a perf flamegraph: warm 200 MiB read self-time = 32.78% kernel `pread` copy but **6.89% crossbeam `Stealer::steal` + 2.09% epoch `try_advance` + 1.05% `wait_until_cold`** rayon coordination tax over ~1600 tiny jobs. | **REJECT / not landed — the win was a thread-SPAWN artifact, and `with_min_len` does not capture it** | `RAYON_NUM_THREADS` sweep (global-cap proxy) warm 200 MiB: 16t `22.2 ms` / 24t `22.3 ms` / 64t `26.5 ms` = `1.19x`; cold (drop_caches) 16t `59 ms` / 64t `65 ms` = `1.10x`. **But the gap is a fixed ~3-4 ms independent of read size — 800 MiB warm 16t `68 ms` vs 64t `71 ms` is only `1.04x` (gap did NOT scale with bytes)** = one-time 64-vs-24 rayon thread spawn paid *inside* the measured one-shot CLI read; it amortizes to ~0 in a long-running FUSE mount (pool spawned once). The production-viable per-call mechanism `with_min_len` (keeps all 64 threads alive, only groups jobs) measured NEUTRAL: `FFS_READ_PAR_TARGET` sweep warm 200 MiB target=8/16/24/32/48/64/off → 35.5/28.3/28.3/26.1/26.5/27.3/27.0 ms — no value beats OFF beyond noise. | `0/0/1`: `with_min_len` neutral-to-noise (best target=32 `26.1 ms` vs OFF `27.0 ms` ≈ `1.03x`, within ±2 ms sigma); reverted (worktree removed, never landed). | `1/0/0` (re-confirm, NOT a new lever): the same 800 MiB warm read DOMINATES true materialize — frankenfs default **74 ms / 11.3 GB/s** vs kernel `dd bs=1M` **238 ms / 3.5 GB/s = 3.2x** and python `f.read()` **400 ms / 2.1 GB/s = 5.4x**; only `cat>/dev/null` `35 ms` (24 GB/s, above DRAM bw = streaming/splice idealization, not materialize) is faster. sha256 byte-identical ON/OFF/kernel (`7b6b1f9a…`). | Prototyped + measured in an isolated git worktree off HEAD `a8b9ef20` (clean ffs-core, no race with cod-b's dirty e2compr WIP); built `release-perf -p ffs-cli` via RCH. Worktree removed; **zero production source changed**. Lesson: a `RAYON_NUM_THREADS`/global-cap proxy measures thread-spawn + pool-size effects a per-call `with_min_len` cannot reproduce — verify the win SCALES with the workload (constant gap ⇒ fixed cost ⇒ artifact) and on the amortized-pool path before treating a thread-cap as a real lever. Echoes `bd-xmh5g.414` and `bd-xmh5g.409` artifacts. |

| 2026-06-21 | `bd-eflng` (CrimsonFox cc/opus) — **NEW GAP, biggest of the campaign** | RANDOM read head-to-head (the previously unmeasured FS axis) — new `ffs-cli rand-read` harness (`fa8108d8`) vs a C random-`pread` loop (`/tmp/krandread.c`, same SplitMix64 offsets). 200 MiB ext4 `/big.bin`, 100k × 4 KiB random reads, warm. | **SERIAL: PARITY. PARALLEL: ~88x LOSS (negative scaling) — a real, root-caused contention gap** | SERIAL: frankenfs **1642 MiB/s** (237.9 ms, 420k IOPS) vs kernel **1651 MiB/s** (236.6 ms, 423k IOPS) = **1.0x parity**. PARALLEL: frankenfs `--parallel` **364 MiB/s** (1070 ms, 93k IOPS) = **4.5x SLOWER than its own serial**; kernel 64-thread **32284 MiB/s** (12.1 ms, 8.26M IOPS) = **~88x faster**; kernel 16-thread 23984 MiB/s. Kernel scales ~20x with threads; frankenfs DE-scales. | `0/1/0` (frankenfs parallel vs frankenfs serial = 0.22x) — adding threads makes it WORSE. | `1/0/0` serial parity + `0/1/0` parallel: **frankenfs parallel random read loses ~88x to the kernel** — the largest direct-kernel gap measured in the campaign, and it is a clean isolated contention (serial is at kernel parity). | ROOT-CAUSED (perf): per-read `OpenFs::with_latest_scope` does `self.mvcc_store.read().current_snapshot()` — a shared acquire of one `Arc<RwLock<MvccStore>>` (lib.rs:887/27499) on EVERY `read_into`; `current_snapshot()` just reads a `u64`. 64 threads × 100k reads = ~6.4M acquisitions of one lock word → cache-line ping-pong (`lock_shared_slow` 2.42% + `WordLock::lock_slow` 3.65% + futex). FIX = publish `next_commit` as a sibling `Arc<AtomicU64>` for a lock-free snapshot read (ffs-mvcc CLEAN/ownable; the `with_latest_scope` caller is a 1-line ffs-core change, currently cod-b WIP-dirty → coordinate). Filed **bd-eflng** (P1) with the full recipe. Harness landed so the swarm can reproduce + verify the fix. |
| 2026-06-21 | `bd-eflng` (CrimsonFox cc/opus) — thread-knee + btrfs contrast | Follow-up characterization of the parallel random-read gap (same `ffs-cli rand-read` harness, 100k × 4 KiB, warm): (a) ext4 `RAYON_NUM_THREADS` sweep on `--parallel`; (b) btrfs random read on `/data/tmp/btrc_1268087.img:/c.bin` (150 MB). | **CONFIRMS contended-lock signature on ext4; reveals a SEPARATE, larger per-read-descent gap on btrfs** | **ext4 thread sweep (median us): 1t 236110 / 2t 150697 / 4t `99468` (best, 2.4x) / 8t 100347 / 16t 197046 / 32t 482586 / 64t 949176.** Scales to 4 threads then INVERTS — the textbook single-contended-lock curve (pure overhead would plateau, not collapse); the `--parallel` default (64t) is **9.5x worse than its own 4-thread optimum**. **btrfs random read: serial `17.9 s` (21 MiB/s, ~180 µs/read = 75x heavier than ext4's 2.4 µs/read); parallel 4t `3.1 s`, 64t `458 ms`** — here more threads HELP (compute-bound), the OPPOSITE of ext4. | ext4 `0/1/0` (64t is 0.25x of serial; 4t is 2.4x); btrfs `0/1/0` (serial catastrophic). | ext4 confirms bd-eflng (MVCC RwLock contention saturates at ~4 threads). btrfs random read is a DISTINCT, larger gap: each random read pays a full B-tree extent descent with no cross-read extent-map caching (the `bd-xmh5g.421` per-descent pathology surfacing in single-file random reads), 75x heavier per read than ext4 — this dwarfs the MVCC lock, so threads still help. | ext4 fix = bd-eflng lock-free snapshot (lets it scale past 4t toward the kernel's 12 ms/64t). btrfs needs a separate per-read extent-resolution cache/memo for random access (related to but distinct from bd-xmh5g.421's prewarm, which covers the multi-file walk, not single-file random `read_into`). Both via the landed `rand-read` harness. |

Same knee SHAPE as random read, but **perf attributes it differently**: 2.98% `crossbeam Stealer::steal` + `MvccStore::register/release_snapshot` ~0.04%, while `lock_shared_slow`/`lock_exclusive_slow` are **<0.03%** (vs 2.42%+0.96% in the random-read profile). | `0/1/0` (64t is 2.5x worse than the 8t optimum on a real workload). | The already-winning many-files read is run at a **sub-optimal default (64t)**; ~8t is 2.5x faster. But this is **NOT the bd-eflng RwLock** — it's rayon work-steal coordination, most likely **nested parallelism** (the across-file parallel walk launches per-file `read_into`, which itself runs a rayon `par_iter`, over-subscribing the one 64-thread pool). | Honest correction of my own in-flight hypothesis (profiling refuted "same lock"). Distinct lever: cap or flatten the nested parallelism in `walk --read-data --parallel` (per-file reads inside a parallel walk should not each fan out on the full pool). Needs its own profile/bead before any change; recorded here so it is not conflated with bd-eflng. |
| 2026-06-21 | `bd-eflng` (CrimsonFox cc/opus) — **prototyped fix MEASURED INERT; root cause CORRECTED** | Implemented the proposed lock-free MVCC snapshot fix (publish `next_commit` as `Arc<AtomicU64>`, read it in `with_latest_scope` without the `mvcc_store.read()` acquire, gated to read-only), built it (release-perf, byte-identical sha `7b6b1f9a`), and re-ran the parallel random-read thread sweep. | **REJECT / not landed — the `with_latest_scope` lock was NOT the dominant contention; the fix is INERT** | Thread sweep WITH the fix (median us): 1t 224629 / 4t 90421 / 8t 90523 / 16t 195584 / 32t 445709 / 64t 900645 — **statistically identical to without (1t 236110 … 64t 949176); the 4t-optimum-then-collapse is unchanged.** Re-profile shows the lock persists: the dominant contention is `read_inode_with_scope`→`read_contiguous_into_with_scope` taking an **RwLock exclusive (7%)** + the `ShardedCache` per-shard `Mutex` (`WordLock::lock_slow` 3.79%, because every read of one file hits the SAME inode-table block → SAME shard) + secondary mvcc-overlay / group-desc read locks. `with_latest_scope`'s shared acquire was a minor contributor, so removing it changed nothing. | `0/0/1`: fix byte-identical but perf-neutral (within noise at every thread count); worktree discarded, **not landed**. | The 88x parallel-random-read gap stands (unchanged) but is **MULTI-LOCK**, not the single `with_latest_scope` lock. | **Corrects bd-eflng's recipe** (my original single-lock root cause was incomplete — like the bd-29an3 spawn artifact, prototyping + measuring caught it before shipping a wrong fix). Real target: the per-read **exclusive RwLock in the inode/block read path** (likely a read-through cache or adapter `.write()`) + the `ShardedCache` shard `Mutex` recency contention on the hot inode-table block (same family as bd-xmh5g.382). Needs a deeper, coordinated ffs-core/ffs-mvcc fix, not a one-liner. |
| 2026-06-21 | `bd-eflng` (CrimsonFox cc/opus) — **FIXED + SHIPPED `9376f4d6`** | After pinpointing the exclusive lock to per-read snapshot register/release: `block_device_adapter` creates a fresh `MvccBlockDevice` per `read_into`; `MvccBlockDevice::new` took the store WRITE lock to `register_snapshot` (+ `release` in Drop), and `with_read_your_writes` made `read_snapshot` take a per-block `store.read()`. Fix: `MvccBlockDevice::new_unregistered` (`SnapshotOwnership::Unregistered`: no register, Drop no-op, `read_your_writes=false`), used in `block_device_adapter` when `!is_writable()` — provably safe (a read-only FS never writes → never prunes versions → the active-snapshot ref-count is pure overhead). | **WIN — 5x at the default, collapse fixed, byte-identical, conformance green** | Parallel random read (100k × 4 KiB, 200 MiB ext4, warm, 64-core): **64t default `949 ms` → `170 ms` = 5.6x**; the 4t-optimum-then-collapse becomes positive scaling to 16t (`197 ms` → `52 ms` = 3.8x; 32t `483`→`171`; 64t `949`→`188`). frankenfs best (16t) **7054 MiB/s** vs kernel-best **23299 MiB/s** = **3.3x slower (was ~88x)** — the catastrophic negative-scaling gap is closed. | `1/0/0`: parallel now scales (16t = 4.3x over serial, was a collapse to ~1x). | `1/0/0` (gap `88x → 3.3x`): the campaign's biggest direct-kernel loss reduced ~27x. Still 3.3x off parity (residual = `ShardedCache` shard-`Mutex` on the hot inode block + the per-read overlay `store.read()`), but no longer catastrophic. | sha256 byte-identical to kernel (`7b6b1f9a`); `cargo test -p ffs-harness --test conformance` **100 passed / 0 failed / 2 ignored** (rch, release-perf). Writable path unchanged (registered). Landed ffs-mvcc (new variant + constructor) + the single `block_device_adapter` ffs-core hunk via `git apply --cached` (cod-b's e2compr WIP left unstaged/untouched). **Regression-guard (the `new_unregistered` adapter is now used for ALL read-only reads, not just random): sequential 200 MiB read `~26 ms` byte-identical (`7b6b1f9a`) + metadata walk 30k `~22 ms` both in their normal warm range — no regression on the headline read/metadata wins** (the change only removes locks; with `read_your_writes=false` the sequential path also drops its per-block `store.read()` snapshot re-resolution). Lesson cycle: hypothesis (with_latest_scope) → prototype → measured INERT → re-profile → real cause (snapshot register/release) → prototype → **measured 5x** → shipped. |
| 2026-06-21 | `bd-eflng` (CrimsonFox cc/opus) — **2nd win + corrects e4e45c53** | The same `9376f4d6` fix is flavor- and workload-agnostic (every read-only `read_into` uses `block_device_adapter`). Re-measured `walk --read-data --parallel` (the real `grep -r`/`tar`/build-source-read multi-file workload, ext4 smallfiles 700 MB) before/after. | **WIN — 4.4x at 64t; the multi-file read knee was substantially the MVCC register/release lock after all (not just rayon, as I'd claimed)** | `duration_us` before → after: 1t `170341`→`~110000` (**1.55x**, even serial — proves the per-file register/release *overhead* mattered, not only contention); 8t `57332`→`23699` (2.4x); 16t `65710`→`21752` (3.0x); **64t `145340`→`~33500` (4.4x)** — the 64t collapse is gone. | `1/0/0`: real multi-file parallel read 4.4x faster at the default. | `1/0/0`: this **widens** the existing many-files-read kernel domination (was 14–24x; the per-file cost just dropped further). | **Honest correction of my row `e4e45c53`**, which attributed the `walk --read-data --parallel` knee to "rayon coordination, NOT the MVCC lock" off a profile that under-weighted the per-file *exclusive* register/release lock. The fix proves it was substantially the MVCC snapshot register/release. Conformance 100/0/2 (reads are thread-count-independent + byte-identical), so the parallel speedup is pure win. The bd-eflng fix's impact is broader than random read: every read-only parallel read workload benefits. |
| 2026-06-21 | `bd-eflng` (CrimsonFox cc/opus) — **flips a default-config LOSS to a WIN vs kernel** | Fresh vs-kernel head-to-head of the multi-file read at the `--parallel` DEFAULT (64t), post-fix (ext4 smallfiles, 1000 files/700 MB, warm, RO loop mount). frankenfs `walk --read-data --parallel` vs kernel `find -type f -exec cat {} +` (materializing). | **WIN — `~1.4x` FASTER, and the default no longer collapses** | frankenfs **~34 ms** (32.4/32.7/34.9/35.5/35.6) vs kernel `find|cat` **~47 ms** (`0.04–0.05 s`, 10 ms `%e` resolution) = **~1.4x faster**. PRE-FIX the 64t default was `145 ms` = **2.9x SLOWER** than the same kernel baseline, so the fix flipped the out-of-box default from a `2.9x` loss to a `~1.4x` win. | N/A (measurement) | **`1/0/0`: the `--parallel` default multi-file read now BEATS kernel `find|cat` ~1.4x** (was a 2.9x loss because the 64t default hit the register/release collapse). | The practical impact: before, a user running the default `--parallel` many-files read LOST to the kernel; after `9376f4d6` they win, because the fix removed the per-file snapshot register/release that made the all-cores default the worst point. Materializing apples-to-apples (both deliver bytes to userspace). |

So random read follows the universal frankenfs pattern: warm = CPU/lock-bound (3.3x slower after the fix, was 88x), COLD/I/O-bound = dominates. | N/A (measurement) | **`1/0/0` cold**: frankenfs cold random read beats the kernel ~14x — the realistic on-disk regime is a win, the warm 3.3x is the cache-resident worst case. | **CAVEAT (honest):** the kernel baseline is loop-mounted, and the loop device adds significant cold-random overhead (64 MiB/s is loop-pathological), so this overstates the pure-ext4 delta — a real ext4-on-NVMe kernel would be faster than loop. Still, reading an ext4 IMAGE cold-random, the userspace-direct path beats kernel-via-loop by a wide margin (the loop layer is the real cost of mounting an image to read it). My fix is warm-specific (lock-bound); cold is I/O-bound where frankenfs already dominates architecturally. |
| 2026-06-21 | (gap, CrimsonFox cc/opus — minor, statelessness-caveated) | Pure **readdir** throughput (directory iteration, names only, NO stat) — isolated from the metadata walk. frankenfs `walk --no-stat` vs kernel `ls -fU` (getdents, no stat), 30k-entry htree dir, warm. | **LOSS warm `~2x+` — but it's a CLI-statelessness artifact, not a fundamental gap** | frankenfs **~20 ms** (19.2/19.9/20.1/20.8) vs kernel `ls -fU` **<10 ms** (`0.00 s`, 10 ms `%e` floor) = frankenfs at least `~2x` slower on pure warm readdir. | N/A (measurement) | **`0/1/0` warm pure-readdir** — but see caveat. | **CAVEAT (key):** the frankenfs CLI re-opens the image and re-parses the htree directory blocks on EVERY invocation (no dcache), while the kernel hits its warm dentry cache. A long-running FrankenFS FUSE mount caches parsed dirents (the readahead/dirent cache, `bd-xmh5g.312/.314`), so this gap is the readdir analogue of the sequential-read "loses warm to a cache-resident kernel" pattern — frankenfs does real parse work, the kernel returns cached dentries. The COMMON metadata case (readdir + stat: `ls -l`/`find`/`du`) already WINS 3.9x (bulk inode parse vs 30k getattr syscalls); pure no-stat readdir is the uncommon case. Not pursued — a per-invocation CLI is the wrong thing to optimize for dcache-style caching; the FUSE path already caches. |
| 2026-06-21 | `bd-n5w92` (CrimsonFox cc/opus) — **FIXED + SHIPPED `b4b9f331`** | btrfs single-file random read re-descended the on-disk fs tree per `read_into` (~57 µs/read tree descent). Added a per-inode read-only extent cache: resolve the inode's FULL extent list once (`btrfs_load_inode_all_extents`) into a `ShardedCache` keyed by inode objectid, filter the window in-memory per read. New branch in `btrfs_read_file_into` gated on read-only + no pending tree log (on-disk tree = complete immutable source); writable COW path unchanged. | **WIN — `~28x` on uncompressed (→ ext4 parity); 1.47x on compressed (decompression-bound)** | UNCOMPRESSED btrfs random read (btrfs-convert `big.bin`): **~2.1 µs/read serial (1857 MiB/s)** = **~ext4 parity** — the ~57 µs/read descent eliminated (derived ~28x; the descent cost is measured as the compressed fixture's `17.9s→12.2s` = 5.7s/100k = 57 µs/read delta). COMPRESSED `c.bin`: serial `17.9s→12.2s` (**1.47x**), 64t `436→387 ms` (flat) — because random 4 KiB reads decompress the whole 128 KiB extent per read, so decompression dominates there, not the descent. | `1/0/0` (uncompressed ~28x; compressed 1.47x). | `1/0/0`: uncompressed btrfs single-file random read now ~ext4 parity (was the 75x-heavier gap). | Byte-identical (sha = kernel on BOTH compressed + uncompressed reads), conformance **100/0/2** (rch release-perf). Landed ffs-core only (4 hunks at 989/3329/8639/8787) via `git apply --cached`; cod-b's e2compr WIP left unstaged/untouched. RESIDUAL on compressed random read = per-read decompression (decompress 128 KiB to extract 4 KiB), a separate workload-specific cost, not the descent. The descent-per-read pathology is RESOLVED. **Regression-guard (the new branch serves ALL read-only btrfs reads, not just random): sequential btrfs read unchanged — uncompressed `big.bin` 128 MB `~21 ms` (6 GB/s), compressed `c.bin` 150 MB `~24 ms`, byte-identical (`b6cfaf9d`) — no regression** (a sequential read's single large `read_into` window covers the whole file, so the fetch-all-extents adds no cost; the cache build is amortized). |
| 2026-06-21 | (bd-eflng residual, CrimsonFox cc/opus) | ext4 read-only **parsed-inode cache** — `read_inode_raw_with_scope` re-reads + re-parses the same inode on every `read_into` (random read of one file: 100k re-parses + parallel readers contend on the inode-table-block cache shard mutex). Added a per-inode `ShardedCache<u64, Arc<Ext4Inode>>` populated for `!is_writable()`, returning a clone on hit. Built it (byte-identical sha `7b6b1f9a`) and re-ran the ext4 parallel random-read sweep. | **REJECT / not landed — NEUTRAL within load noise, not a clean win** | 64t `170→160 ms` (~6%, partly noise); **16t (the optimum) `52→~55 ms`** — re-measured 6 rounds `[56.1, 47.2, 48.8, 54.2, 58.2, 58.8] ms` (min BELOW the prior `51.7 ms` median) under load 9.59 = neutral within noise, NOT a clean win. The per-hit `Ext4Inode` clone (struct + `xattr_ibody` Vec) largely offsets the parse it saves; only the 64t Mutex-contention relief is marginally positive. | `0/0/1`: byte-identical but perf neutral-to-marginal; worktree discarded, **not landed**. | The ext4 random-read residual is genuinely distributed (no single ≥10% lever); a parsed-inode cache on this high-blast-radius central path (every read/lookup/getattr) is not justified by a noise-swamped ~6%-at-64t / neutral-at-optimum result. Confirms (with data) the earlier profile assessment: the residual 3.3x is distributed overhead + harness artifact, not a single fixable lever. Recorded so it is not retried without a clean-box A/B showing a material, clone-offset-beating win. |
| 2026-06-21 | (scoping, CrimsonFox cc/opus — NOT pursued) | Compressed btrfs random read residual: a decompressed-extent cache (key by extent bytenr → `Arc<[u8]>`) so repeated 4 KiB random reads of the same 128 KiB compressed extent skip re-decompression (the dominant cost: 100k reads = 12.2s, ~122 µs/read mostly zstd of the whole extent per 4 KiB read). | **DEFER — a risky deep-swing that fights an intentional memory design, not a clean lever** | Estimated ~30x on the uniform-random benchmark IF the cache holds the working set (~83 hits/extent over ~1200 extents), and real value for clustered DB workloads. BUT the btrfs read path (`lib.rs:9047`) **deliberately drops each decompressed `Vec`** ("so a large compressed read no longer retains every decompressed Vec") — a memory-conscious design. A decompressed-extent cache re-retains extents and would **risk memory bloat on sequential compressed read** (the exact regression that design avoids), and the win is on a niche workload (compressed-btrfs random read = a DB on compressed btrfs). | N/A (not built). | N/A. | Deferred: it requires a bounded LRU that captures random-read locality without bloating the sequential path, inside the parallel decompress job, with byte-identity across codecs — a focused deep-swing, not a surgical add like `bd-n5w92`. Scoped + recorded so it isn't naively added (which would regress the deliberate decompressed-Vec-drop memory design). zstd cannot partial-decompress a 128 KiB blob, so there is no cheaper per-read alternative. |
