### 2026-06-26 DISCIPLINED read A/B (reliable): frankenfs read ≈ kernel dd parity on this box (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Applying the discipline lesson, redid the read measurement CLEANLY: real-data file (100MiB urandom, not zeros), warm both sides first, interleaved, 3 rounds, 64-core box, like-for-like (frankenfs `read --discard` = read+discard vs kernel `dd of=/dev/null` = read+discard):

| round | frankenfs | kernel dd |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | 1.88 GiB/s | 2.1 GB/s |
| 2 | 2.37 GiB/s | 2.2 GB/s |
| 3 | 2.17 GiB/s | 2.2 GB/s |

frankenfs ~2.25 GB/s ≈ kernel dd ~2.2 GB/s = PARITY-to-slightly-faster (reliable ratio ~1.0-1.05x). This is a trustworthy this-box number (clean interleaved A/B), in contrast to this session's two retracted single-run claims (dc9ec858, 4527c940).

It COMPLEMENTS, not contradicts, the campaign headline (read 1.66x vs dd, 5.3 vs 3.2 GB/s): that was a larger file (128MiB+) likely on an unloaded box; mine is 100MiB with both sides equally load-depressed (~2.2 vs the headline's ~3.2 absolute), so the load cancels in the RATIO — and the ratio here is parity-to-slightly-faster, not 1.66x. The read win is therefore file-size/load-dependent: reliably competitive (>= parity) everywhere measured, reaching 1.66x only on the larger-file/unloaded setup. NET: no gap (read is >= kernel parity), no new lever; a reliable, disciplined data point that tempers the headline's magnitude while confirming the read is never a loss. The non-conflicting lever surface remains exhausted; this is the disciplined-verification value in action.

### 2026-06-26 METHODOLOGICAL CAUTION: python os.stat baselines inflate the kernel ~3x; walk ratio inconclusive on flat-16k (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Clean warm interleaved walk A/B (flat 16k-entry dir, frankenfs walk vs kernel) exposed a measurement-methodology issue worth recording:
- frankenfs walk: 17.9 ms (16002 entries) = RELIABLE ~1.1 us/entry (bulk inode parse).
- kernel via python `os.scandir`+`os.stat`: 57 ms (3.2x slower than frankenfs).
- kernel via C `find -type f`: ~10 ms (but /usr/bin/time 0.01s resolution is too coarse).

The python and C baselines DISAGREE by ~5.7x (57 vs 10 ms) for IDENTICAL work — the python `os.stat` per-call interpreter overhead (~2.9 us x 16002 = ~47 ms) inflates the "kernel" number. CONCRETE CAUTION: any vs-kernel ratio measured against a PYTHON baseline (e.g. the campaign's "python read 400ms" comparisons) is inflated ~3x and makes frankenfs look better than a C baseline would. Prefer C baselines (dd, find, a compiled microbench) with precise (sub-ms) timing.

WALK RATIO: inconclusive here. Against coarse C `find` (~10ms) frankenfs (17.9ms) looks ~1.7x SLOWER; against python (57ms) ~3.2x faster. Neither is reliable (find too coarse, python overhead-laden), AND this flat-16k fixture differs from the headline's 30k bigdir (where the bulk-parse-vs-30k-getattr-syscalls advantage was measured). So the headline walk 2.03x is neither confirmed nor refuted by this run. To reliably re-validate the walk (and read 1.66x) headlines, a PRECISE COMPILED C baseline (scandir+stat / pread loop, us-resolution) on the MATCHED fixture is required — the python/find baselines used opportunistically this session are insufficient. The reliable frankenfs-side number stands: walk ~1.1us/entry. This is a measurement-discipline contribution, not a perf claim.

### 2026-06-26 RELIABLE WIN CONFIRMED (precise C baseline): metadata walk 2.5x FASTER than kernel — resolves prior inconclusive (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Built the precise compiled C baseline recommended last turn (/tmp/kwalk.c: opendir+readdir+lstat every entry, CLOCK_MONOTONIC) and ran a clean warm interleaved A/B (flat 16k-entry ext4 dir, 4 rounds):

| round | frankenfs walk | C kernel scandir+lstat | ff/k |
|-------|----------------|------------------------|------|
| 1 | 15.4 ms | 47.2 ms | 0.33x |
| 2 | 17.7 ms | 47.7 ms | 0.37x |
| 3 | 19.0 ms | 44.7 ms | 0.43x |
| 4 | 19.5 ms | 47.6 ms | 0.41x |

frankenfs walk = 2.5x FASTER than the kernel (like-for-like stat-every-entry). RESOLVES the prior-turn inconclusive (1c87f1f4) and CORRECTS the misleading `find` reading: `find -type f` uses the dirent `d_type` (NO stat per entry, ~10ms) so it is NOT a fair walk baseline — frankenfs `walk` stats every entry, so the like-for-like kernel baseline is `scandir+lstat` (16002 lstat syscalls = 47ms, ~2.9us/syscall). frankenfs's bulk inode parse (17.9ms = ~1.1us/entry) beats the kernel's per-entry syscall storm 2.5x. The python baseline (57ms, prior turn) was also fair-in-kind (stats each) but interpreter-inflated; the C baseline (47ms) is the clean number; both >> find's d_type-only 10ms.

This RELIABLY CONFIRMS the campaign headline (walk 2.03x faster) — exceeding it here (2.5x) on this fixture. METHODOLOGY ESTABLISHED: the fair walk baseline is stat-every-entry (compiled C scandir+lstat), NOT find -type f (d_type). /tmp/kwalk.c is a reusable us-resolution C baseline; an analogous compiled C pread-loop should re-validate the read 1.66x headline (the read A/B's `dd` baseline is fair but the read win is file-size/load-dependent per 32666a5c). A confirmed, reliable, measured WIN via disciplined like-for-like measurement.

### 2026-06-26 RELIABLE re-validation (C pthreads baseline): parallel random read ~1.4x slower than kernel — UPDATES the historical 3.3x residual (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Built /tmp/krpread.c (C pthreads parallel random-pread baseline: NT threads x random 4KiB preads, CLOCK_MONOTONIC) and ran a warm A/B vs `ffs-cli rand-read --parallel` (64t, 200k reads, 100MiB real-data file, 7 total rounds incl. kernel pre-warm):

- frankenfs: STABLE ~6.6M IOPS (6.47-6.88M across all rounds).
- kernel C pthreads pread (warm): VARIABLE 5.5-10.6M IOPS (thread-scheduling + cache noise), ~9.5M typical warm.
- Ratio: frankenfs ~1.4x slower at warm steady-state (1.38-1.46x in the stable rounds).

This UPDATES the campaign's historical parallel-random-read residual (memory: 88x pre-fix -> 3.3x post-new_unregistered-fix) to ~1.4x NOW — substantially narrowed (the landed FsMvccStore wiring + the RO-snapshot/read-path fixes brought it much closer to kernel). The residual ~1.4x is the per-random-read FS overhead (extent-map lookup + MVCC read_visible overlay-miss + ShardedCache shard lookup) vs the kernel's direct pread; the fix for the remainder is ffs-core/ffs-mvcc (the peers' lane). NOTABLE: frankenfs is markedly MORE STABLE than the kernel baseline (6.6M +/-3% vs kernel 5.5-10.6M) — predictable tail latency. NET: a reliable, modest, C-baselined gap (~1.4x), down from 3.3x; the parallel random read is now near kernel parity. /tmp/krpread.c + /tmp/kwalk.c are reusable us-resolution C baselines for disciplined re-validation.

### 2026-06-26 RELIABLE read re-validation (fair parallel C baseline) + LOOP-DEVICE nuance (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Built /tmp/kparead.c (C pthreads parallel chunked read) to compare LIKE-for-LIKE (frankenfs parallel vs kernel parallel, not vs single-thread dd). Warm interleaved, 100MiB real-data, 3 rounds:

| | throughput |
|--|-----------|
| frankenfs read (parallel) | ~5.1 GiB/s (4.91-5.26) |
| kernel dd (1 thread) | ~2.4 GB/s |
| kernel C parallel-64 read | ~2.4 GiB/s (2.33-2.47) |

TWO reliable findings: (1) frankenfs read is ~2.1x faster than the kernel here — confirming the read win's DIRECTION (headline 1.66x). (2) The kernel's image read DOES NOT SCALE: 64-thread parallel C read (~2.4) ≈ single-thread dd (~2.4). The bottleneck is the LOOP DEVICE's single request queue (the kernel can only read a file-backed image via loop-mount, which serializes); frankenfs reads the raw image DIRECTLY (no block device) so it scales across cores (5.1).

NUANCE (important for interpreting ALL image-based vs-kernel ratios, incl. walk 2.5x / 13ff3116): these compare frankenfs-direct-image-parse vs kernel-loop-mounted-image. That is FAIR for the image-reading use case (reading/inspecting a disk image without a real block device — the kernel MUST loop-mount). But on a REAL multi-queue block device (NVMe), the kernel WOULD scale, so frankenfs's parallel advantage would SHRINK (the 2.1x is partly the loop's single-queue non-scaling, not pure frankenfs speed). LOAD-dependence also applies: frankenfs scales with free cores (5.1 unloaded; dropped to ~2.2 = parity when the box was loaded, 32666a5c).

NET: the read win is real + reliable for the image use case (~2.1x, frankenfs scales where the loop-mounted kernel can't), but is loop-specific and load-dependent — not a blanket "2.1x faster than the kernel FS on any device." Disciplined like-for-like + honest scope. /tmp/kparead.c reusable.

### 2026-06-26 FAIR non-loop read re-validation (ramdisk): read win is REAL (1.6-2.0x), corrects the loop hypothesis (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Put the image on /dev/ram0 (real block device, brd — NO loop) and re-ran the read A/B to test the f031d545 loop hypothesis:

| | throughput |
|--|-----------|
| frankenfs read | ~4.7 GiB/s (4.37-4.97) |
| kernel-ramdisk dd (1t) | ~3.0 GB/s (2.6-3.3) |
| kernel-ramdisk parallel-64 C | ~2.3 GiB/s (2.22-2.32) |

CORRECTION of f031d545: the kernel's read does NOT scale with threads EVEN on a real block device (parallel-64 2.3 ≈ single-thread dd 3.0). So the non-scaling is NOT the loop device's single request queue (my prior hypothesis — WRONG); it is memory-bandwidth-bound memcpy (64 threads copying a single warm file's cache pages saturate ~3 GiB/s single-socket). RETRACT the f031d545 "loop-specific, shrinks on a real device" caveat — the win PERSISTS on a real block device.

CONFIRMED: the read win is REAL, not loop-confounded — frankenfs (4.7 GiB/s) beats the kernel 1.6x (vs dd-1t 3.0) to 2.0x (vs parallel-64 2.3) on a real ramdisk block device. frankenfs scales (parallel chunked, hits 4.7 > the kernel's memcpy-bound 3.0) where the kernel's single-file read cannot. REMAINING CAVEAT: frankenfs `read --discard` vs dd materialize — if --discard skips the final user-buffer copy it is partly idealization; treat the conservative ~1.6x (vs dd-1t materialize) as the fair figure. NET: read win confirmed real + non-loop (1.6-2.0x); kernel single-file read is memcpy-bandwidth-bound (doesn't scale); my loop hypothesis corrected via the ramdisk test. The disciplined process (hypothesis -> ramdisk test -> correction) upgraded f031d545's "loop-specific" to "real, non-loop".

### 2026-06-26 FAIR parallel create (ramdisk, both durable): the BIGGEST claimed gap (5.5x) is actually PARITY (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Built /tmp/kcr.c (C pthreads parallel create: open O_CREAT|O_EXCL + close, one syncfs at end — matches frankenfs create-bench's create-N + one-flush). Fresh ext4 on /dev/ram0 (REAL block device, no loop), 16000 files, both durable:

| threads | frankenfs | kernel-ramdisk | ratio |
|---------|-----------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | 33.7k | 35.2k | 1.04x |
| 8 | 34.0k | 37.1k | 1.09x |

PARITY (frankenfs only 1.04-1.09x slower). KEY: the kernel create in ONE directory does NOT scale either (8t 37k ≈ 1t 35k) — ext4's directory `i_rwsem` serializes concurrent creates into a single dir, exactly as frankenfs is one-dir-bound. So for the create-bench's single-dir pattern, BOTH are dir-lock-bound → parity.

RE-SCOPES the campaign's biggest "gap": the historical ~5.5x (kernel 159k@8t vs frankenfs ~30k) is a BASELINE MISMATCH — to reach 159k@8t the kernel baseline must have used MULTI-dir creates (per-dir locks → scales) and/or been non-durable (no sync), compared against frankenfs's single-dir durable create-bench. That is apples-vs-oranges. The FAIR matched comparison (same one-dir pattern, both durable, real block device) is PARITY.

CAVEAT (honest scope): for a MULTI-dir parallel create the kernel WOULD scale (independent per-dir locks) while frankenfs's create path (shared allocator + MVCC commit) might not — that scenario could still show a real gap, which is exactly what the peers' allocator-sharding lane targets. So "parity" holds for single-dir create-heavy workloads (tar-extract into one dir, maildir, etc.); multi-dir scaling is the open question. NET: the parallel create is NOT a blanket 5.5x gap — it is parity for the matched single-dir durable comparison; the 5.5x reflected a mismatched baseline. /tmp/kcr.c reusable. This is the most consequential re-scoping of the session: the headline "biggest gap" was measurement mismatch, not a real 5.5x deficit.

### 2026-06-26 CORRECTION of 52745cd2: parallel create gap IS REAL (~6.2x) — my "parity" was a single-vs-multi-dir fixture mismatch (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

RETRACT 52745cd2's "create is parity" claim — it was my error. frankenfs `create-bench --threads` creates each thread's files in its OWN SUBDIR (multi-dir, per its --help), but my kcr kernel baseline was SINGLE-dir (all threads in one dir → ext4 dir i_rwsem serializes → artificially slow at ~37k, non-scaling). That mismatched frankenfs-multi-dir against kernel-single-dir.

FAIR comparison with a MULTI-dir kernel baseline (/tmp/kcrm.c — each thread its own subdir), ramdisk, both durable, 32000 files:

| | 1t | 8t | scaling |
|--|----|----|---------|
| kernel multi-dir | 35.3k | 203.9k | 5.8x (SCALES) |
| frankenfs create-bench | ~33k | 32.0k | ~1x (FLAT) |

FAIR 8t ratio: frankenfs 6.2x SLOWER (31975 vs 198492). The ~5.5x historical gap is REAL and CONFIRMED — NOT a measurement artifact. The kernel scales multi-dir creates (independent per-dir locks + per-cpu inode/block allocators); frankenfs does NOT scale even with per-thread subdirs (the shared allocator + the single MVCC commit lock / publication gate serialize commits regardless of dir isolation). This is the campaign's biggest gap, REAL — exactly the target of the peers' allocator-sharding + sharded-MVCC lane (the create needs BOTH sharded, as established 0ca4bd30/0216ab3b).

LESSON (3rd measurement-discipline catch this session, after dc9ec858 + 4527c940): MATCH THE FIXTURE PATTERN. create-bench is multi-dir, so the kernel baseline MUST be multi-dir; a single-dir baseline is dir-lock-bound and fabricates false parity. /tmp/kcrm.c (multi-dir) is the correct create baseline; /tmp/kcr.c (single-dir) was wrong for this comparison. The single-dir result (parity) is still valid for single-dir-create workloads, but the create-bench/campaign scenario is multi-dir where the real 6.2x gap stands.

### 2026-06-26 PROFILED the parallel-create bottleneck: it's the existence-check lookup's RAYON fan-out, NOT the allocator (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

perf -g on `create-bench --count 64000 --threads 8` (the confirmed 6.2x gap). Hotspots:
- 20.18% rayon job execution under `OpenFs::lookup_name_with_scope::{closure}` mapping over `DrainProducer<BlockNumber>` -> i.e. the create's EXISTENCE CHECK parallelizes a `parse_dir_block` readdir scan across the rayon pool, PER CREATE.
- 4.71% `parse_dir_block`, 2.17% `ShardedMvccStore::read_visible` (overlay), 5.47% hashbrown name-index insert (+ 0.91% mutex lock_contended).
- The ALLOCATOR does NOT appear prominently.

ANALYSIS (redirects the create-gap cause): the per-create existence check (`lookup_name_with_scope`, confirming the new name's absence) does a `parse_dir_block` dir scan FANNED OUT via rayon. With 8 create threads EACH fanning out, the rayon pool is OVERSUBSCRIBED (nested parallelism) -> the create doesn't scale (8t ≈ 1t). The scan is also O(dir-size) per create (to confirm absence) -> O(N^2) as the dir grows. A name index (hashbrown) exists (5.47% insert) but the existence-check LOOKUP path isn't using it for O(1).

LEVER (for the peers, ffs-core create/lookup path): (1) the create existence check should use the NAME INDEX (O(1) hash lookup) instead of an O(dir) `parse_dir_block` scan; (2) it must NOT fan out via rayon — nested parallelism oversubscribes when N create threads each fan out; use a serial scan (or the index) for the small per-create lookup. This targets ~25% of the create profile + the non-scaling.

IMPORTANT REDIRECT: the Sharded MVCC `read_visible` is only 2.2% (the wiring is NOT the create bottleneck), and the ALLOCATOR is not prominent — so the create gap is dominated by the existence-check lookup's rayon-scan, NOT primarily the allocator/MVCC. The peers' allocator-sharding may be necessary for the WRITE/alloc path but is NOT what's throttling the create-bench's parallel scaling; the existence-check lookup (index + no-rayon-fan-out) is the higher-leverage create fix per this profile. Caveat: profile self-time; the peers should verify before re-prioritizing. /tmp/kcrm.c is the correct multi-dir create baseline.

### 2026-06-26 DOWNGRADE a9fa95da (existence-check-fan-out UNCONFIRMED) + SOLID: parallel create NEGATIVE-scales (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Tried to confirm a9fa95da's claim (the create bottleneck is the existence-check lookup's rayon fan-out) via RAYON_NUM_THREADS=1. CONFOUNDED: create-bench `--threads` itself uses rayon, so RAYON_NUM_THREADS=1 serializes the OUTER parallelism too (1t 35k->20k, 8t 29k->13k, both slower) — it does NOT isolate the inner existence-check fan-out. So a9fa95da's "existence-check fan-out is the bottleneck" is UNCONFIRMED — the 20% rayon in that on-cpu profile may be create-bench's OWN --threads rayon (the outer parallelism), not a nested fan-out. DOWNGRADE a9fa95da from "the bottleneck" to "a candidate, unverified."

SOLID facts (these stand): (1) parallel create is ~6.2x slower than the kernel (multi-dir, fair, 05b2ecd4). (2) the create NEGATIVE-SCALES: 8t (29k) < 1t (35k) — parallelism is COUNTERPRODUCTIVE, indicating a CONTENTION point (a shared lock that threads block on), not mere non-scaling. The kernel scales 5.8x on the same workload; frankenfs gets WORSE with threads.

WHY I can't isolate the lock: the on-cpu perf profile (a9fa95da) shows rayon overhead + parse_dir_block + a name-index HashMap insert with 0.91% lock_contended + read_visible — but NEGATIVE scaling is an OFF-CPU/blocking phenomenon (threads parked on a contended lock), which an on-cpu profile does NOT capture. Isolating it needs off-cpu / lock-contention profiling (perf lock / sched), which I have not reliably done. Candidate locks: the shared name-index mutex (the on-cpu lock_contended hint), the rayon pool, or an allocator lock — UNVERIFIED which dominates.

HONEST META: this is my 4th measurement walk-back this session (after dc9ec858, 4527c940, 52745cd2). The create-bottleneck isolation is beyond what I've reliably measured solo; I am stopping solo create-bottleneck CLAIMS. The SOLID handoff for the peers (whose lane the create path is, with their existing create-heavy-rayon guards + allocator/MVCC sharding): the parallel create NEGATIVE-scales (contention), 6.2x vs kernel — isolate the contended lock via off-cpu/lock profiling before fixing. The vs-kernel WIN measurements (walk 2.5x, read 1.6-2.0x, random-read 1.4x) remain reliable + correctly C-baselined.

### 2026-06-26 CREATE dig closed: the existence-check fan-out guard is ALREADY implemented; remaining negative-scaling is a deeper contention (peers' lane) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Resolution of the create whipsaw (a9fa95da fan-out claim -> f982dd89 downgrade). Read lookup_name_with_scope (lib.rs:12451): it tries htree first, then a linear-scan fallback that is ALREADY GUARDED (12539-12542): `if planned.len() < current_num_threads().max(2) || rayon::current_thread_index().is_some() { serial } else { par_iter }` — with a comment confirming the peers already fixed exactly this ("it was ~90% of a create-heavy workload's time ... Serialize unless ... not already in a rayon worker"). So a9fa95da's lever (guard the existence-check fan-out) is ALREADY IMPLEMENTED. f982dd89's downgrade was correct: the inner fan-out is NOT the create bottleneck (it's guarded to serial for the create-from-rayon-worker case).

So the off-cpu sched_yield (13.75%) is NOT the inner fan-out — it is the OUTER create-bench `--threads` rayon (the 8 create workers themselves), and/or workers parking on a shared-lock contention in the create path (the create NEGATIVE-scales, so a contended lock is blocking workers -> rayon yields). The off-cpu profile shows the yield but I could NOT cleanly attribute it to a specific lock (name-index HashMap insert 14% appears as CPU work, not lock-blocking; no single futex/mutex dominated). 

CONCLUSION (honest closure of my create dig): the obvious create lever (existence-check fan-out guard) is DONE by the peers; the remaining ~6.2x gap + negative-scaling is a deeper contention I could not reliably isolate solo (needs careful lock-contention attribution — perf lock with kernel lock events, beyond my reliable reach here). This is squarely the peers' create-path lane (their allocator/MVCC sharding + the existing rayon guards). DIRECTIVE for them: do NOT re-fix the fan-out (already guarded); isolate the shared-lock contention causing the negative-scaling (the worker-yield). My reliable contributions stand: the vs-kernel WIN measurements (walk 2.5x, read 1.6-2.0x non-loop, random-read 1.4x) + the SOLID create facts (6.2x vs kernel, NEGATIVE-scaling = contention). I am closing the create-bottleneck dig — repeated solo attribution attempts have whipsawed; the reliable facts are recorded, the exact lock is the peers' to isolate.

### 2026-06-26 CONSOLIDATED vs-kernel SCORECARD (disciplined C baselines, confounds corrected) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Authoritative summary of this session's reliable, like-for-like, compiled-C-baseline measurements on a 64-core box. Supersedes noisy historical/single-run numbers. All warm, interleaved, multi-round; ramdisk (real block device) where a kernel block device is needed.

| op | frankenfs vs kernel | baseline | notes |
|----|---------------------|----------|-------|
| metadata walk (stat every entry) | **2.5x FASTER** | C scandir+lstat (/tmp/kwalk.c) | bulk inode parse vs per-entry syscalls; ~1.1us/entry |
| sequential read | **1.6-2.0x FASTER** | dd + C parallel pread (/tmp/kparead.c) | frankenfs scales; kernel single-file read is memcpy-bandwidth-bound (~3 GiB/s, doesn't scale); load-dependent (parity when box loaded) |
| parallel random read | 1.4x slower | C pthreads pread (/tmp/krpread.c) | down from historical 3.3x; FS per-read overhead (extent resolve + read_visible miss + ShardedCache); frankenfs MORE stable than kernel |
| single-thread create | ~parity (1.04x) | C create+syncfs (/tmp/kcr.c, single-dir) | kernel single-dir is dir-i_rwsem-bound too |
| parallel create (multi-dir) | **6.2x slower + NEGATIVE-scaling** | C multi-dir create+syncfs (/tmp/kcrm.c) | kernel scales 5.8x; frankenfs flat/worse with threads = a CONTENTION lock (the real biggest gap) |
| single-thread delete | 42k unlinks/s (frankenfs) | (delbench has no --threads; parallel unmeasurable) | kernel A/B not run |

METHODOLOGY (corrected confounds — apply to ALL vs-kernel ratios): (1) use COMPILED C baselines, NOT python (os.stat inflates the kernel ~3x) NOR `find -type f` (uses dirent d_type, no stat — not a walk baseline). (2) use a REAL block device (ramdisk), NOT loop-mount (though the kernel's single-file read doesn't scale either way — memcpy-bound, not the loop). (3) MATCH THE FIXTURE PATTERN (multi-dir vs single-dir create differ ~6x; create-bench is multi-dir). (4) interleaved multi-round (single-run cross-process ratios are noise).

THE ONE REAL GAP: parallel create 6.2x + negative-scaling = a userspace-lock CONTENTION in the create path (off-cpu sched_yield; the existence-check fan-out is already guarded at lib.rs:12539, so it's a DEEPER lock — name-index/MVCC/allocator, unisolated; perf lock is kernel-only, BPF mode unbuilt). PEERS' LANE: isolate the contended userspace lock (futex tracing / their create-path knowledge), don't re-fix the guarded fan-out. Reusable C baselines: /tmp/{kwalk,kparead,krpread,kcr,kcrm}.c. SESSION CORRECTIONS (ledger kept honest): dc9ec858 (memory over-claim), 4527c940 (lookup noise), 52745cd2 (create single-vs-multi-dir mismatch), a9fa95da/f982dd89 (create fan-out whipsaw). Net: frankenfs WINS reads + metadata-walk vs kernel; LOSES parallel create (contention) and parallel random read (modest); the create contention is the one actionable gap, owned by the peers.

### 2026-06-26 FUTEX-traced the create contention: it's RAYON-POOL coordination (distributed), NOT a single FS lock — refines the 6.2x (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Used futex tracing (`perf record -e syscalls:sys_enter_futex -g` — the RIGHT tool for userspace locks, vs perf lock which is kernel-only). RESULT (parallel create, 8t): the contended futexes are DISTRIBUTED across many distinct per-worker addresses (~2.2% each, balanced FUTEX_WAIT/FUTEX_WAKE pairs) — there is NO single dominant lock. This is the signature of RAYON POOL coordination (each worker parks/unparks on its own futex), NOT a single contended FS lock (which would show one hot uaddr).

REFINES the create gap (scorecard ac64f9d0): the negative-scaling is rayon-pool coordination overhead (park/unpark/steal), consistent with the codebase's "create-heavy workload's ~90% rayon overhead" note (lib.rs:12532) and the off-cpu sched_yield (13.75%) — NOT a single userspace FS lock to fix. My earlier "userspace-lock contention" framing was imprecise: it's pool coordination, distributed, not one lock.

HARNESS CAVEAT (tempers the 6.2x): create-bench `--threads` uses RAYON for the outer parallelism (confirmed: RAYON_NUM_THREADS=1 collapses it). A REAL parallel-create workload (tar -p, parallel git-checkout) uses std threads/processes, which do NOT share a rayon pool for the outer parallelism. So part of the create-bench's 6.2x is its OWN outer-rayon pool overhead — a HARNESS ARTIFACT a real workload wouldn't pay. The FS's INTRINSIC parallel-create gap (std-thread creators, only the FS's guarded inner rayon) is UNMEASURED — no std-thread/FUSE frankenfs create harness exists; create-bench is rayon-based. So 6.2x is an UPPER BOUND (FS create cost + harness rayon-pool overhead), not purely the FS deficit. INFERENCE (not measured): a std-thread harness would show a smaller gap.

FOR THE PEERS: the create's negative-scaling is rayon-pool coordination (the harness's outer rayon + the FS's guarded inner rayon sharing one pool), not a single FS lock. To measure the FS's true parallel-create scaling, build a std-thread/process (or FUSE-mount) create harness that doesn't impose a shared rayon pool — that isolates the FS create's intrinsic lock behavior from the rayon-pool overhead. Until then the 6.2x is harness-inflated to an unknown degree. /tmp/fx.data approach (futex sampling) is the reusable userspace-lock tool.

### 2026-06-26 RESOLVED: the create NEGATIVE-scaling is a RAYON-HARNESS artifact — a std-thread (FUSE) harness POSITIVE-scales (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Built the std-thread create harness I called for (bed823a2): writable FUSE mount (`ffs-cli mount --rw`, root for write perms) + /tmp/kcrm.c (pthreads multi-dir create) on the FUSE mountpoint. REAL creates verified (find -type f = 16000 files each run):

| threads | FUSE frankenfs create | scaling |
|---------|----------------------|---------|
| 1 | 798 creates/s | — |
| 8 | 8535 creates/s | ~10.7x (POSITIVE) |

This is the OPPOSITE of create-bench's rayon `--threads` negative-scaling (8t 29k < 1t 35k). CONCLUSION: the create-bench negative-scaling is a RAYON-POOL HARNESS ARTIFACT (the rayon `--threads` pool coordination, confirmed by futex tracing bed823a2 + this std-thread re-test), NOT an intrinsic FS property. With real std-thread concurrency (FUSE), frankenfs's create POSITIVE-scales. The alarming "negative-scaling" part of the 6.2x create "gap" (05b2ecd4) is therefore a benchmark-harness artifact, not an FS deficit.

CAVEAT (honest): the FUSE ABSOLUTE throughput is FUSE-round-trip-overhead-dominated (1t 798 = 1.25ms/create, ~45x the create-bench's direct-image 28us/create), so (a) the FUSE absolute is NOT a clean FS-speed number, and (b) the 10.7x FUSE scaling partly reflects the kernel-FUSE multi-thread dispatch amortizing its overhead, not purely the FS create logic. So I do NOT claim a clean intrinsic create-vs-kernel ratio — BOTH harnesses are confounded (create-bench = rayon-pool-inflated; FUSE = round-trip-overhead-dominated). What IS reliable: the create does NOT intrinsically negative-scale (a std-thread harness positive-scales), so the negative-scaling alarm is a rayon-harness artifact.

NET correction to the scorecard (ac64f9d0): the parallel-create "6.2x + negative-scaling" is harness-confounded on BOTH sides; the negative-scaling specifically is a rayon-`--threads` artifact (FUSE std-thread positive-scales 10.7x). The clean intrinsic FS-create-vs-kernel number remains unmeasured (needs a direct-image std-thread harness, which doesn't exist — create-bench is rayon, FUSE has round-trip overhead). The peers should NOT treat the create as an intrinsic negative-scaling lock-contention bug — it scales with real concurrency. /tmp/kcrm.c on a `--rw` FUSE mount (root) is the reusable std-thread create probe.

### 2026-06-26 REAL-APP create cost (via FUSE) ~1ms/create = ~24x kernel — FUSE-path-dominated, NOT a per-create flush (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

The FUSE std-thread create test (c08ebbba) also exposed the REAL deployment cost: a real app creates files via the kernel->FUSE->frankenfs path, not the direct-image create-bench. Measured (writable FUSE mount, root, real files verified):
- frankenfs FUSE create: ~1ms/create (1t 798/s, 8t 8535/s).
- kernel ext4 create (std-thread, ramdisk, /tmp/kcrm): ~204k/s @8t.
- Real-app ratio: frankenfs FUSE ~24x slower than kernel ext4 (8535 vs 204k @8t).

The ~1ms/create is ~36x the create-bench's DIRECT-image path (28us/create) — i.e. the FUSE layer adds ~972us/create. ROOT (grep-confirmed): the FUSE create/mknod handlers (ffs-fuse/lib.rs) call `commit_request_scope` (the IN-MEMORY MVCC commit) per op — there is NO per-create durable `flush_to_device` in the create path. So the ~1ms is NOT a per-create durability flush; it's the FUSE-path per-op overhead: the kernel<->userspace FUSE round-trip + the in-memory commit + the managed-mount lifecycle + the writeback-batch machinery (commit_writeback_batch_for_key).

CONFOUND: measured in `--runtime-mode managed` (background lifecycle + metrics) — the standard (blocking) mode may have less per-op overhead; the ~1ms is not isolated to a single factor. LEVER (ffs-fuse, currently clean): profile the FUSE create-path per-op breakdown (round-trip vs commit vs managed-lifecycle vs writeback) and cut the ~972us FUSE overhead — this is the REAL-deployment metadata-write cost, far larger than the direct-image create-bench suggests.

NET completion of the create picture: (1) create-bench direct-image = 6.2x vs kernel, rayon-`--threads`-harness-inflated (05b2ecd4/bed823a2); (2) negative-scaling = rayon-harness artifact, RESOLVED (FUSE std-thread positive-scales 10.7x, c08ebbba); (3) REAL-APP via FUSE = ~24x vs kernel, FUSE-path-overhead-dominated (~1ms/create), the actual deployment gap. The biggest REAL metadata-write gap is the FUSE per-op overhead (~972us/create), not an intrinsic FS lock — a different, larger lever than the direct-image create-bench implied.

### 2026-06-26 CONFIRMED: real-app FUSE create ~1ms is INHERENT (standard ≈ managed), not managed-mode (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Isolated the managed-mode confound (0fcf314e caveat): re-measured the FUSE create in STANDARD mode (blocking, no managed lifecycle/metrics):
- standard: 1t 1008/s, 8t 8437/s.
- managed: 1t 798/s, 8t 8535/s.

Essentially IDENTICAL. So the managed-mode lifecycle is NOT the ~972us overhead — the ~1ms/create is INHERENT to the FUSE create path (the kernel<->userspace round-trip + the in-memory MVCC commit + the writeback-batch machinery). The real-app create gap (~24x vs kernel ext4, 0fcf314e) is therefore the inherent FUSE-path per-op overhead, confound-free (not a measurement artifact, not a per-create flush [grep-confirmed], not managed-mode).

The ~1ms breakdown (round-trip vs in-memory-commit vs writeback components) needs FUSE-handler-process profiling (perf -p <mount-pid> during create) — attempted but the FUSE mount/perf-attach timing is finicky here; this is the peers' ffs-fuse-expertise lane to pinpoint. LEVER (ffs-fuse, currently clean): cut the inherent FUSE create-path per-op overhead — this is the BIGGEST REAL metadata-write gap (~972us/create added by the FUSE path, ~24x kernel for real apps), far larger and more actionable than the direct-image create-bench's rayon-inflated 6.2x.

CREATE THREAD COMPLETE (full picture, all confounds resolved): (1) direct create-bench 6.2x = rayon-`--threads`-harness-inflated; (2) negative-scaling = rayon-harness artifact, RESOLVED (std-thread FUSE positive-scales 10.7x); (3) real-app FUSE ~24x = inherent FUSE-path overhead (~1ms/create, standard≈managed, not a flush) = the actual deployment metadata-write gap + the real lever. The campaign's "biggest gap" is the FUSE per-op overhead, not an FS lock.

### 2026-06-26 CREATE FULLY CLOSED: real-app ~1ms is the FUSE layer + deliberately-GATED writeback-cache, NOT frankenfs's FS logic (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Final framing of the real-app FUSE create gap (~24x), from two SOLID facts (the userspace handler profile via `perf -p <mount-pid>` came back empty — likely a sudo-nested-attach failure, so INCONCLUSIVE, not used as evidence):

1. frankenfs's FS create LOGIC is FAST: the direct-image create-bench is 28us/create. So the ~1ms real-app cost is NOT the FS create logic.
2. The FUSE mount runs `writeback_cache=FALSE` (confirmed in the mount log; it is the V1.x DEFAULT, gated off pending the audit gate + ordering oracle + crash/replay oracle + runtime guard per `ffs-cli mount --help`). With writeback-cache off, FUSE writes/creates are SYNCHRONOUS per op.

CONCLUSION: the real-app create slowness (~1ms/create, ~24x kernel) is (a) the inherent kernel-FUSE round-trips (LOOKUP+CREATE+GETATTR per create) + (b) the SYNCHRONOUS path because writeback-cache is deliberately OFF in V1.x — NOT a frankenfs FS-create deficit (the FS create is fast). The obvious FUSE-create speedup (writeback-cache) is GATED behind safety audits (ordering/crash-replay oracles, runtime guard) — a deliberate V1.x correctness hold, not a missing optimization to "dig". So there is no FS-create lever here for me to ship: the FS create is fast, the FUSE-layer overhead is inherent + the cache is intentionally gated.

CREATE THREAD FULLY CLOSED (complete, confound-free): (1) direct create-bench 6.2x = rayon-harness-inflated; (2) negative-scaling = rayon-harness artifact (resolved, std-thread FUSE +10.7x); (3) real-app FUSE ~24x = FUSE round-trips + synchronous writeback-cache-off (V1.x gated), NOT FS logic (FS create = 28us, fast). The campaign's "biggest gap" decomposes to: a benchmark-harness artifact (create-bench rayon) + the inherent/gated FUSE write path — frankenfs's FS create itself is competitive. No solo lever remains on create; the writeback-cache enablement is the gated-audit owners' lane.

### 2026-06-26 MEASURED + PROFILED: single-thread DELETE is 2.22x slower than kernel — the first clean FS-LOGIC gap (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Clean single-thread delete A/B (direct-image FS logic, both durable, ramdisk for kernel):
- frankenfs delbench: 39134 unlinks/s (25.6us/unlink).
- kernel ext4: 86889 unlinks/s (11.5us/unlink).
- frankenfs 2.22x SLOWER.

Unlike the create (single-thread ~parity, 52745cd2-corrected), the delete is a REAL FS-LOGIC gap — direct-image (no FUSE, no rayon harness). PROFILE (perf -g on delbench 64k): `ShardedMvccStore::commit` 10.71% (5.63% self — the per-delete MVCC commit, the biggest single component) + `read_visible` 3.62% (overlay reads for the positive lookup) + `ffs_alloc::persist_group_desc_with_bitmap_overrides` (the inode/bitmap free) + heavy jemalloc (`_rjem_je_edata_heap_remove_first`). So the 2.2x is the cumulative per-delete FS-logic cost: positive lookup (read_visible) + dir-entry removal + inode/bitmap free (alloc GDT/bitmap persist) + MVCC commit (dominant). The delete does MORE than the create (positive lookup + removal + free vs the create's negative-lookup + alloc), and each component costs ~2x the kernel.

LEVER (ffs-core/ffs-mvcc/ffs-alloc — peers' lanes): reduce the per-delete cost — the MVCC commit (10.71%, the peers' sharded-store optimization target), the overlay read_visible (the positive-lookup path), and the alloc bitmap/GDT persist. This is the FIRST clean FS-LOGIC gap I've found this session: reads/walk are WINS, create is FUSE/harness-confounded (FS-create is fast 28us), random-read is FS-overhead 1.4x, and DELETE is FS-logic 2.22x — a genuine metadata-delete deficit in the FS logic itself, not a harness/FUSE artifact. Reusable baseline: /tmp/kdel2.c. Adds delete=2.22x (FS-logic) to the scorecard.

### 2026-06-26 BIGGEST FS-LOGIC GAP: rename on a LARGE single dir is ~200x slower — O(dir) new-name insert (htree not used) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Clean rename A/B (direct-image, durable). On a LARGE single directory (32000 files, all in one dir):
- frankenfs rename-bench: 172 renames/s (5.8 ms/rename).
- kernel ext4 (htree): 34344 renames/s (29 us/rename).
- frankenfs ~200x SLOWER.

O(dir)/O(N^2) CONFIRMED (the per-rename time grows with the dir size): 4000-dir = 256us/rename (~3900/s); 32000-dir = 5.8ms/rename (~172/s) — 8x more files -> ~22x slower per op (super-linear, the rename-bench is O(N^2) overall, which is why --count 32000 took ~minutes). The kernel's htree keeps rename O(log N) -> flat ~34k/s regardless of dir size.

KEY CONTRAST localizing the culprit: the DELETE on the same 32000-file dir was only 2.22x (25.6us/op, NOT O(dir)) — and a delete does lookup + dir-entry REMOVE + free (no insert). The RENAME does lookup + remove + dir-entry INSERT (new name) + commit. The ~200x (rename) vs 2.22x (delete) gap isolates the O(dir) cost to the NEW-NAME DIR-ENTRY INSERT on a large directory: frankenfs's dir-entry insert is O(dir) (linear slot scan / htree not maintained-or-used for the insert) while the kernel inserts via the htree index (O(log N)).

LEVER (ffs-core/ffs-dir — peers' lane, HIGH VALUE): use/maintain the htree name index for dir-entry INSERT on large directories (O(log N) instead of O(dir) linear). This is the BIGGEST measured FS-logic gap (~200x on a 32k-entry dir) and is the same htree-staleness root as the create existence-check / lookup, but most acute for insert-heavy ops (rename) on big dirs. CAVEAT: small dirs (the create-bench's multi-dir subdirs) don't expose it (O(dir) is small); this is a LARGE-single-dir scaling cliff (mv into a big dir, save-rename in a large directory). Reusable baseline /tmp/kren.c. Scorecard: rename(large-dir)=~200x, the dominant FS-logic gap; lookup/htree-index for inserts is the highest-value FS lever.

### 2026-06-26 PINPOINT: the rename ~200x is the dir-entry INSERT's linear block scan (lib.rs:17051), not htree-guided (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Located the O(dir) insert mechanism behind the rename ~200x gap (f98d9e86). The dir-entry insert path in ffs-core SCANS the directory's data blocks linearly looking for a free slot:
- lib.rs:17051 `for block in Self::extent_phys_blocks(ext) { ... ffs_dir::add_entry(&mut data, ...) }` — iterate every dir block, try `add_entry` per block until one has a free slot = O(dir blocks).
- `ffs_dir::add_entry` itself "inserts into the first free slot without scanning other [blocks]" (lib.rs:5113) — it is per-BLOCK; the O(dir) is the CALLER iterating all blocks.
- There is htree awareness (lib.rs:17644 "For an htree dir, logical block 0 is the dx_root") but the insert still resolves the target by walking blocks, not by descending the dx_root to the hash-target leaf.

So the insert is O(dir) (linear block walk) rather than htree-guided O(log N). On a 32000-entry dir that is ~hundreds of blocks scanned per insert -> the ~200x rename gap (the kernel descends the htree dx_root to the one target leaf, O(log N)). The DELETE was only 2.22x because remove doesn't need a free-slot search (it finds+clears the existing entry); the INSERT's free-slot block walk is the O(dir) cost.

LEVER (ffs-core insert path @17051 + ffs-dir htree primitives — peers' lane, HIGHEST-VALUE FS lever found): make the dir-entry insert htree-GUIDED — for an htree dir, use the dx_root/dx_node (htree_find_leaf, already in ffs-dir, partition_point O(log N)) to descend to the hash-target leaf block and `add_entry` there, instead of the linear `for block in extent_phys_blocks` walk. This turns insert (and thus rename, and large-dir create) from O(dir) into O(log N) — the biggest measured FS-logic win available (~200x on a 32k-entry dir; grows with dir size). The htree primitives exist (ffs-dir); the gap is the ffs-core insert ORCHESTRATION at 17051 not using them. Precise, actionable, peers' lane.

### 2026-06-26 ROOT CAUSE of rename ~200x: frankenfs's htree is REBUILD-based (doubling-amortized), O(N^2) for rename (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Confirmed the precise mechanism behind the biggest gap (rename ~200x, fe7634e4). frankenfs's htree dir insert is REBUILD-based, not incremental:
- When linear blocks fill, the dir converts to htree via `ext4_rebuild_htree_dir` (lib.rs:17107 / fn 17310), and "further growth uses the O(log N) htree path with LOOSE-PACK DOUBLING REBUILDS (bd-xmh5g.111), so mass-create is O(N) rather than O(N^2)" (comment 17099-17102).
- So the htree insert is a FULL O(dir) rebuild, AMORTIZED to O(N) for mass-CREATE by capacity doubling (like a dynamic array: rebuilds get geometrically rarer as the dir grows).

WHY RENAME IS O(N^2) (~200x): rename = insert new name + remove old name -> NET COUNT CONSTANT. With no monotonic growth, the doubling NEVER amortizes — the dir sits at/near capacity, so each rename's insert triggers another full O(dir) rebuild (the doubling only helps when N keeps growing). 32000 renames x O(32000) rebuild = O(N^2) -> 172 ren/s (5.8ms/op) vs kernel 34344 ren/s. The DELETE is only 2.22x because remove is in-place (no rebuild). The kernel's htree is INCREMENTAL (descend dx_root to the hash-target leaf, add there, split the leaf + update the index only when that leaf is full) -> O(log N) per insert, no rebuild, so rename is O(N log N) on the kernel.

LEVER (ffs-core/ffs-dir — the biggest FS-perf lever in the codebase): replace the rebuild-based htree insert with an INCREMENTAL insert — descend dx_root/dx_node (htree_find_leaf, already in ffs-dir, O(log N)) to the hash-target leaf, add_entry there, and split only that leaf (updating the dx index) when full. This makes insert O(log N) per op regardless of growth pattern, fixing rename (and any churn/constant-count insert workload) from O(N^2) to O(N log N) — ~200x on a 32k-entry dir. The doubling-rebuild design (bd-xmh5g.111) optimized mass-create but left rename/churn at O(N^2); incremental split is the standard ext4-htree approach. Precise root cause + lever for the dominant measured gap; peers' lane (correctness-sensitive htree split logic). /tmp/kren.c reusable.

### 2026-06-26 PRIORITIZED metadata-write lever handoff (capstone of the create/delete/rename investigation) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Consolidated, ranked, actionable handoff to the ffs-core/ffs-dir/ffs-mvcc/ffs-alloc owners — the metadata-WRITE levers by win potential (all measured/profiled/root-caused this session; reusable C baselines /tmp/{kcr,kcrm,kdel2,kren}.c):

1. **RENAME ~200x on large dirs — HIGHEST VALUE.** Root cause: the htree is REBUILD-based (`ext4_rebuild_htree_dir`, lib.rs:17107/17310, "loose-pack doubling rebuilds" bd-xmh5g.111). Doubling amortizes mass-CREATE to O(N), but RENAME (insert+remove, net count constant -> no growth -> no amortization) rebuilds the whole htree per op -> O(N^2). LEVER: incremental htree insert (descend dx_root via `htree_find_leaf` already in ffs-dir -> hash-target leaf -> add_entry -> split only that leaf when full), replacing the per-op full rebuild. POTENTIAL: ~200x -> kernel parity (172 -> ~34k ren/s). Correctness-sensitive (htree split); the standard ext4 approach. Same root also caps large-dir create/lookup.

2. **DELETE 2.22x — per-op cost.** Profile: ShardedMvccStore::commit 10.71% + read_visible 3.62% (positive lookup) + alloc bitmap/GDT persist + jemalloc. LEVER: the per-op MVCC commit cost (the peers' sharded-store optimization) + the read_visible owned-Vec alloc (borrowed-read, 0216ab3b). POTENTIAL: ~2.2x -> parity.

3. **CREATE real-app ~24x via FUSE — gated.** FS-create logic is FAST (28us direct); the ~1ms/create real-app cost is inherent FUSE round-trips + synchronous writeback-cache-OFF (V1.x default, deliberately gated behind audit/ordering/crash-replay oracles). LEVER: enable writeback-cache (gated-audit owners' lane) — NOT a missing optimization, a safety hold.

SHARED ROOT across delete/rename/create: the per-op MVCC commit (the peers' active sharded-store optimization) is a common factor in every metadata write. The single biggest discrete FS win is #1 (incremental htree insert). frankenfs's metadata READS win (walk 2.5x, lookup competitive); the WRITES are commit-bound + (for rename) htree-rebuild-bound. This is the complete, root-caused metadata picture — the implementation levers are the owners' lanes; my disciplined measurement located + quantified + ranked them.

### 2026-06-26 PRECISE #1 lever: htree insert already descends O(log N); the O(N^2) is the FULL-LEAF rebuild — fix is LEAF SPLIT (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Read the htree-dir insert path and REFINED the #1 lever (rename ~200x, 5f712241/0371ed0c). Correction to "make the insert incremental": the descend+add ALREADY EXISTS and is O(log N):
- lib.rs:16966 `htree_target_leaf_block(hash_seed, name, ...)` — descends the dx_root/dx_node to the hash-target leaf block (O(log N)).
- 16981 resolve target leaf phys; 16991 read it; 16992 `add_entry` to the target leaf -> Ok -> stamp + write + update parent -> DONE. O(log N), NO rebuild.

So the common-case htree insert is already O(log N). The O(N^2)/~200x is ONLY the FULL-LEAF case: when `add_entry` returns NoSpace (target leaf full), the insert falls through to the full-htree REBUILD (`ext4_rebuild_htree_dir`, O(dir), loose-pack doubling) instead of SPLITTING just that one full leaf (O(log N)). For RENAME the dir sits at capacity (insert+remove, no net growth) so EVERY insert's target leaf is full -> rebuild per op -> O(N^2). (Mass-create grows into non-full leaves, so it mostly hits the O(log N) descend+add path + occasional doubling — hence create is O(N).)

PRECISE LEVER (smaller + sharper than a full insert rewrite): implement htree LEAF SPLIT for the full-leaf branch (16992 Err NoSpace) — allocate a new leaf block, redistribute the full leaf's entries by hash midpoint, add ONE dx-index entry pointing at the new leaf (recursively split the dx node if it is itself full), then retry add_entry — replacing the call to ext4_rebuild_htree_dir. ONLY the full-leaf branch changes; the descend+add (16966-16995) is already correct + O(log N). This is exactly what the Linux ext4 htree does (leaf split on full). Turns rename from O(N^2) to O(log N) (~200x -> kernel parity). Biggest FS win, now scoped to a single branch (the leaf split), ffs-core/ffs-dir, peers' lane (correctness-sensitive: dx-node split + index consistency). Reusable baseline /tmp/kren.c.

### 2026-06-26 FEASIBILITY capstone for the #1 lever (htree leaf split): building blocks exist, contained change (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Confirmed the #1 lever (rename ~200x -> O(log N) via htree LEAF SPLIT at the lib.rs:16992 full-leaf branch, 38fc72f7) is a CONTAINED, feasible change — the primitives already exist:
- dx descent: `ffs_ondisk::htree_target_leaf_block` (used at 16966) finds the hash-target leaf O(log N).
- leaf add: `ffs_dir::add_entry` (16992) inserts into a leaf block.
- redistribute/dx reference: `ext4_rebuild_htree_dir` (17310) already collects leaf entries (`htree_leaf_logical_blocks`/`parse_dx_root`) + redistributes + builds the dx index — the LOCAL split reuses this hash-redistribute logic for ONE leaf.

NEW code needed (only the 16992 `Err(NoSpace)` branch): (1) alloc a new leaf block; (2) redistribute the full leaf's entries across old+new by hash midpoint (reuse the rebuild's hash-keyed redistribute, scoped to one leaf); (3) insert ONE dx-index entry for the new leaf (`htree_insert` into the dx node); (4) recursively split the dx NODE if it is itself full (the one deep edge case — for a 2-level htree); (5) retry `add_entry`. Replaces the call to `ext4_rebuild_htree_dir` in that branch. Everything else (descend+add for non-full leaves, 16966-16995) is already correct + O(log N).

RECOMMENDATION (honest terminus of my solo contribution): this is CORRECTNESS-CRITICAL (htree/dx invariants, dx-node-split, hash-bucket consistency, large-dir 2-level case) — a silent-corruption risk if rushed. It is the ffs-core/ffs-dir OWNERS' careful-review lane, NOT a loop-pressured solo attempt by an error-prone process (I retracted 4+ over-claims this session; an FS index data-structure change must not be rushed). I will VERIFY it the moment it lands: re-measure rename at 32000 -> confirm O(log N) -> ~200x to kernel parity (/tmp/kren.c ready). This completes my handoff of the dominant FS lever: located (16992) + root-caused (full-leaf rebuild vs split) + feasibility (primitives exist, contained 5-step delta) + verification plan. The measurement/root-cause campaign is COMPLETE; the implementation is the owners' lane.

### 2026-06-26 MEASURED btrfs WIN: frankenfs btrfs metadata walk ~4x FASTER than kernel-btrfs (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Extended the campaign to the 2nd filesystem (btrfs). Clean warm walk A/B, compiled-C baseline (no python inflation):
- btrfs-convert the 32k-file image (cp lk.img -> btrfs-convert); frankenfs auto-detects btrfs.
- frankenfs btrfs walk: ~9-12ms (3 dirs + 32002 files = 32005 entries, 0.31 us/entry, serial).
- kernel btrfs (C nftw FTW_PHYS = lstat each): ~42-44ms.
- frankenfs 3.74x / 4.27x / 4.71x FASTER (3 warm rounds).

This is BIGGER than the ext4 walk win (2.5x): the kernel's btrfs metadata access is heavier (per-inode btrfs B-tree descent) than ext4's contiguous inode table, while frankenfs's bulk btrfs-tree parse is efficient on both. So frankenfs WINS the metadata walk on BOTH filesystems — ext4 2.5x, btrfs ~4x.

CAVEAT: kernel mounted via loop (btrfs-convert image; a native-ramdisk btrfs is setup-heavy/dcg-tricky); measured WARM so the loop's per-read overhead is page-cache-amortized -> the ~4x is the warm metadata ratio (the python-baseline first pass showed 4-5x vs inflated python os.stat; the C nftw baseline corrects to a still-decisive ~4x). Reusable: `cp lk.img x; btrfs-convert x` + /tmp/kwalk2.c (C nftw). Scorecard adds btrfs-walk = ~4x (WIN), confirming the metadata-READ advantage generalizes across filesystems (frankenfs's batch on-disk parse beats per-inode kernel syscalls; the gaps are all on the metadata-WRITE side — delete/rename/create).

### 2026-06-26 MEASURED btrfs data read: frankenfs ~1.37x faster than kernel-btrfs (warm) — btrfs coverage complete (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Completed the btrfs coverage with the DATA read (after the walk ~4x). Fair A/B (both read the same btrfs-converted 128MiB-file image; C kparead baseline, 64 threads):
- frankenfs btrfs read: 3913-4279 MiB/s (29.9-32.7ms / 128MiB).
- kernel-btrfs parallel pread: ~3010 MiB/s.
- frankenfs 1.30x / 1.42x / 1.37x FASTER (3 warm rounds).

CAVEAT (honest): the kernel reads via a loop mount while frankenfs reads the image file directly — the loop layer penalizes the kernel side, so ~1.37x is an UPPER bound; the fair native-ramdisk-btrfs ratio is likely lower (~parity-to-modest-win, consistent with the prior scorecard's ~parity-1.74x range). Both read the same scattered converted layout, so the convert-scatter affects both equally (ratio fair w.r.t. layout). A native-ramdisk btrfs (the btrfs format tool is dcg-blocked) would remove the loop asymmetry.

btrfs COVERAGE COMPLETE: walk ~4x (WIN, decisive), data read ~1.37x (WIN, loop-caveated). The metadata-READ AND data-READ advantage holds across BOTH filesystems (ext4: walk 2.5x, read 1.6-2.0x; btrfs: walk ~4x, read ~1.37x) — frankenfs's batch on-disk parse + parallel chunked read beat per-inode/loop kernel paths. All measured GAPS remain on the metadata-WRITE side (delete 2.22x, rename ~200x = htree full-leaf-rebuild lever, create FS-fast/FUSE-gated). Unified campaign conclusion: READS win on ext4+btrfs; WRITES are the lever surface (htree leaf-split #1, per-op commit #2). /tmp/kparead.c + btrfs-convert reusable.

### 2026-06-26 btrfs WRITE status: incomplete (create fails after write-state init) — bounds campaign coverage (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Probed the last untested surface (btrfs writes). frankenfs btrfs WRITE is INCOMPLETE:
- create-bench on a btrfs image: mount + write-state init SUCCEED (btrfs chunk map expanded bootstrap_chunks=1->11, mount resolved root objectid=5 dirid=256, "btrfs write state initialized" nodesize=16384 generation=12 fs_tree_items=64034 extent_tree_items=500 next_objectid=16268).
- but the FIRST create FAILS: `command_failed ... error=failed to create cb_00000000` (after 760ms of init).

So frankenfs is btrfs-READ-capable (walk ~4x, data read ~1.37x — both WINS) but btrfs-WRITE-incomplete (the COW create path errors on the first op). 

CAMPAIGN COVERAGE NOW BOUNDED + COMPLETE: measured perf surface = ext4 (reads AND writes) + btrfs (reads ONLY). The metadata-WRITE gaps (delete 2.22x, rename ~200x htree-leaf-rebuild, create FS-fast/FUSE-gated) are EXT4-only; on btrfs the write path is not yet functional (a FEATURE-COMPLETION lane — implement btrfs COW create — distinct from the perf-lever lanes). Final unified map: READS win on both filesystems (ext4 walk 2.5x/read 1.6-2.0x; btrfs walk ~4x/read ~1.37x); ext4 WRITES are the perf-lever surface (htree leaf-split #1 @lib.rs:16992, per-op MVCC commit #2); btrfs WRITES are a correctness/feature gap (create fails), not a perf measurement. The disciplined vs-kernel campaign is comprehensive across every functional path.

### 2026-06-26 STUDY-BACKED final characterization of #1 lever: leaf-split needs a NEW ffs-ondisk incremental-dx primitive (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Studied ext4_rebuild_htree_dir + the full ffs-ondisk dx API to determine exactly what the leaf-split (rename ~200x -> O(log N)) requires to implement:
- The rebuild collects ALL entries (parse_dir_block per leaf) -> `build_htree_directory_for_rebuild` (full rebuild, ~50% loose-pack slack, doubling schedule -> O(N) amortized for monotonic growth; O(N^2) for rename/churn).
- ffs-ondisk dx API = LOOKUP (htree_find_entry, htree_target_leaf_block, parse_dx_root, dx_hash, dx_root_entry_limit, dx_node_entry_limit) + FULL-REBUILD (build_htree_directory_for_rebuild). There is NO incremental insert/split primitive.

So the #1 lever requires NEW ffs-ondisk code: an incremental "split one full leaf by hash midpoint + insert one dx_entry into the dx_root (sorted, respecting dx_root_entry_limit; promote to / recurse a 2-level index via dx_node_entry_limit when the dx_root is full)" primitive, wired at lib.rs:16992 (the full-leaf Err(NoSpace) branch) to replace the build_htree_directory_for_rebuild call. The single-level case (dx_root has room) covers the rename-bench (~160 leaves, dx_root not full) and could fall back to the existing full rebuild for the rare dx_root-full case.

CONCLUSION (responsible terminus): this is correctness-critical on-disk dx-FORMAT manipulation (dx_root/dx_node sorted-entry arrays, hash-bucket boundaries, entry limits, 2-level promotion) — the ffs-ondisk/ffs-dir OWNERS' format-expertise lane, NOT a loop-rushed solo addition by a process that retracted 4+ over-claims this session. I will NOT rush a new on-disk-index primitive (silent-corruption + data-loss risk). The #1 lever is now characterized to the implementation level: NEW ffs-ondisk incremental-dx-insert primitive + 16992 wiring + targeted split tests + kernel e2fsck verify; reuses dx_hash/dx_*_entry_limit; single-level first, rebuild-fallback for dx_root-full. I verify (re-measure rename -> O(log N), /tmp/kren.c) when landed. The vs-kernel measurement campaign is COMPREHENSIVE + COMPLETE (every functional path, both filesystems, reads+writes); the perf-lever surface is the owners' write-path lanes.

### 2026-06-26 e2fsck on write-bench image: writes are OVERLAY-oriented — write A/Bs are conservative (overlay-vs-durable); htree gate must use read-back (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

While validating an independent correctness gate for the in-flight htree leaf-split work, ran `e2fsck -fn` on a frankenfs create-bench image (mke2fs 128m ext4 -> create-bench --count 2000 -> e2fsck):
- e2fsck NOT clean: "Free blocks count wrong (26567, counted=26551)", "Free inodes count wrong (32756, counted=30756 => 2012 used)", but Pass 2 found only "12/32768 files" linked.
- So: 2000 inode-bitmap allocations are flushed to the image, but the 2000 directory links + the superblock/GDT free-counts are NOT — the dir state lives in the in-memory MVCC OVERLAY, not durably linked. Confirms the memory's "write-bench is overlay-only fast-path; durable write = overlay + disk-bound sync".

IMPLICATION 1 (corrects the htree leaf-split gate): e2fsck is NOT a valid correctness gate for the overlay write path (the bench doesn't produce a complete durable on-disk image). The leaf-split verification MUST use frankenfs's OWN overlay read-back (lookup + walk every entry, rename-churn re-verify) — which is what the dispatched implementation agent's gate does — NOT kernel e2fsck. (Messaged/noted for the in-flight htree work.)

IMPLICATION 2 (fairness caveat on the write-side A/Bs): the create/delete A/Bs compared frankenfs's OVERLAY (in-memory dir, partial durability) write to the kernel's DURABLE (syncfs) write. So the measured write gaps are CONSERVATIVE lower bounds — a fully-durable frankenfs write (with the disk-bound sync) would be SLOWER, widening the gaps:
  - delete 2.22x: frankenfs's in-memory overlay delete is already 2.22x slower than the kernel's DURABLE delete -> durable frankenfs would be worse. Gap real + conservative.
  - create ~parity/6.2x: overlay-vs-durable -> the durable create gap is >= measured.
  - rename ~200x: O(N^2) in-memory htree rebuild -> CPU-bound, holds regardless of durability (and still 200x slower than the kernel's DURABLE rename).
This STRENGTHENS the campaign conclusion: the metadata-WRITE gaps are real and the measured ratios understate the durable-vs-durable gap. The READ wins (walk/data, both FS) are unaffected (reads are read-only, no overlay/durability asymmetry).

### 2026-06-26 CORRECTION of f6a505c8: durable write IS correct + kernel-compatible; only the FREE-COUNTS are stale (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Retract my prior-entry over-conclusion ("write-bench overlay-only / dir not durably linked / write A/Bs conservative"). VERIFIED empirically after `create-bench --count 2000`:
- frankenfs re-open walk: 2 dirs + 2000 files (frankenfs sees them).
- kernel loop mount: `find` = 2000 files, 2 dirs (THE KERNEL SEES THEM TOO).
So the dir entries + inodes + bitmaps ARE durably persisted and kernel-compatible — the create-bench is genuinely durable (`sync_all_to_device`, as its --help states). My "overlay-only" reading was WRONG.

CONSEQUENCE: the write-side A/Bs were FAIR durable-vs-durable, NOT optimistic-overlay. RETRACT the "conservative lower bound" caveat — create (~parity single-dir / 6.2x multi-dir) and delete (2.22x) stand as fair durable measurements; rename ~200x (in-memory O(N^2) htree rebuild, then flushed) is fair durable too.

THE REAL e2fsck FINDING (minor, genuine): only the SUPERBLOCK/GDT FREE-COUNTS are stale — "Free blocks count wrong (26567 vs counted 26551)", "Free inodes count wrong (32756 vs counted 30756)". The free-inode/free-block COUNT fields are not decremented for the allocations, though the bitmaps + dir entries + inodes are all correct and kernel-readable. e2fsck's "12 files" summary is computed FROM the stale free-count (32768-32756=12), not from the reachable set. So frankenfs's durable write is DATA-correct + kernel-compatible but leaves an e2fsck-dirty free-count (an `e2fsck -fy` would reconcile it). This is a small write-path SPEC-COMPLIANCE lever (maintain superblock/GDT free-inode/free-block counts on alloc/free) for the owners (ffs-core/ffs-alloc) — distinct from the perf levers.

LESSON: the e2fsck "N files" summary reflects the (possibly stale) superblock count, not the actual reachable inode set — always cross-check with an independent walk. Here frankenfs AND the kernel both enumerated 2000, proving the data is intact; only the bookkeeping count is off. (Good catch on my own over-claim — same discipline as the 4 earlier retractions this session.)

### 2026-06-26 Connected spec-compliance finding: write leaves stale free-counts AND fsck doesn't catch them (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Followed the stale-free-count thread (d13fde95) to its root — TWO connected gaps, both CORRECTNESS/spec-compliance (distinct from the perf levers):

1. WRITE path: frankenfs's durable write does not maintain the superblock/GDT free-inode/free-block COUNT fields on alloc/free (ffs-alloc grep shows no decrement of free_inodes_count/free_blocks_count). After create-bench the data is correct + kernel-visible, but e2fsck flags "Free blocks count wrong / Free inodes count wrong".

2. FSCK path: frankenfs's OWN fsck does NOT catch it. VERIFIED: `ffs-cli fsck <post-write-image>` reports "outcome: Clean" (it validates superblock parse + GD parse + checksum scrub, but does NOT verify free-count-vs-bitmap consistency) — MORE LENIENT than kernel e2fsck, which flags the same image. So frankenfs-fsck-Clean != e2fsck-clean.

Plus a test gap: the only fsck test (cli_fsck_ext4_clean_image, crates/ffs-cli/tests/cli_e2e.rs:273) checks a freshly-created CLEAN image, never a post-frankenfs-write image — so neither the stale-count nor the fsck-leniency is gated.

FIX (owners' lane, ffs-alloc + ffs-repair/ffs-cli-fsck): (a) decrement/increment superblock + bg_free_inodes_count/bg_free_blocks_count on inode/block alloc+free; (b) add a free-count-vs-bitmap consistency check to `ffs-cli fsck`; (c) add a post-write fsck gate (create-bench -> ffs fsck AND e2fsck -fn -> assert clean). These are spec-compliance correctness levers: frankenfs's writes are data-correct + kernel-readable but not yet e2fsck-clean, and its fsck doesn't detect the difference. LESSON: cross-check frankenfs fsck "Clean" against kernel e2fsck — they disagree here. (Non-perf finding surfaced while validating the gate for the in-flight htree leaf-split work.)

### 2026-06-26 ext4 random-read A/B (careful): --parallel mode is harness-confounded; serial number needs fixture reconciliation (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Re-validated the read-side random-read (clean A/B, 4K x 50000 reads, fresh contiguous 128MiB file /tmp/btr.img, C krpread baseline):
- frankenfs serial: 115877 IOPS (8.6 us/read).
- frankenfs --parallel: 12430 IOPS (9.3x SLOWER than its own serial = negative scaling).
- kernel serial: 597314 IOPS (1.67 us/read). kernel parallel 64t: 3,622,795 IOPS (scales 6x from serial).

FINDING 1 (solid, methodology): the `rand-read --parallel` mode NEGATIVE-scales (12430 < serial 115877) -> it issues reads across the rayon pool as ~one task per 4K read, so per-read task/coordination overhead dominates for tiny reads (the same rayon-harness artifact family as create-bench --threads and the .414 coordination notes). It is NOT a clean FS-parallel measure; the apparent 291x parallel gap is inflated by this harness shape (kernel krpread uses thread-LOOPS, no per-read task). A fair parallel comparison needs a thread-loop harness, not rayon-task-per-read.

FINDING 2 (FLAGGED, not a claim): the SERIAL number here (115877 IOPS / 8.6 us/read = 5.15x slower than this kernel baseline's 597314) is HIGHER than the memory's recorded ext4 random-read ~parity. Do NOT treat as a confirmed regression — this fixture (fresh mke2fs 400m + 128MiB contiguous urandom file) and kernel baseline (krpread 597k) differ from the memory's; the prior ~parity used a different image/baseline. NEEDS same-fixture reconciliation before claiming a serial gap. On a CONTIGUOUS file the extent lookup is trivial (1 extent), so any serial overhead would be the per-read MVCC read_visible + overlay + ShardedCache machinery (~7us above the kernel's 1.67us page-cache pread) -> the read-path lever IF the gap reconciles as real (owners' ffs-mvcc/ffs-core lane). LESSON (anti-over-claim): flagged rather than asserted, given the discrepancy with well-studied prior data + this session's earlier retractions.

### 2026-06-26 RESOLUTION of f0a4449b flagged random-read gap: it was BUILD CONTENTION, not a real gap (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

The f0a4449b serial random-read "5.15x slower" was FLAGGED (not asserted) pending reconciliation. RESOLVED: it is a build-contention artifact, not a real gap. Evidence — a size-sweep made the measurements obviously unreliable:
- 4K = 16.37 us/read, but 16K = 2.75 us/read and 64K = 2.82 us/read (IMPLAUSIBLE: a 4K read cannot legitimately be ~6x slower per-op than a 16K read).
- the 4K serial varied 8.6 us (f0a4449b) <-> 16.37 us (this run) = ~2x run-to-run variance.
CAUSE: the background htree-leaf-split implementation agent's builds were ACTIVE during BOTH measurements (`ps` showed ~67s cargo + rustc + clippy running) -> cores contended -> the rand-read A/Bs were noisy/slow. So the "5.15x" is contention, NOT a real serial gap — consistent with the memory's well-studied ext4 random-read ~parity (measured on an idle box).

STILL STANDS: f0a4449b Finding 1 (the `rand-read --parallel` mode is structurally harness-confounded — rayon-task-per-read negative-scaling) is unaffected by contention (it's a structural property, reproduced as serial>>parallel regardless).

LESSON (methodology, important): NEVER run perf A/Bs while a background build competes for cores — `ps -eo etimes,comm | grep -E 'cargo|rustc'` FIRST; require an idle box. This session I correctly FLAGGED the suspicious serial number instead of asserting it (anti-over-claim discipline), and the flag resolved as contention. FOLLOW-UP: re-measure the serial random-read on an idle box (after the htree agent completes) to positively confirm ~parity; until then the memory's ~parity stands and the "5.15x" is retracted as a contention artifact.

### 2026-06-26 REFINEMENT of c4bb5890: the stale free-counts are the SUPERBLOCK GLOBAL counts (per-group GDT is maintained) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Pinpointed the stale-free-count gap (c4bb5890) to its precise location (code-read, non-perf, while the box is build-contended):
- PER-GROUP GDT free-counts ARE maintained: `persist_group_desc_with_bitmap_overrides` (ffs-alloc/src/lib.rs:1535) patches `free_blocks_count`/`free_inodes_count` from `AllocStats` (lines 1572-1573, and again 3726-3727). So per-group bookkeeping is correct.
- SUPERBLOCK GLOBAL free-counts are NOT updated: grep across ffs-alloc/ffs-core/ffs-ondisk found no update of the superblock-global s_free_blocks_count / s_free_inodes_count on alloc/free.
- The e2fsck message "Free blocks count wrong / Free inodes count wrong" (with NO "for group N" qualifier) is e2fsck's GLOBAL/superblock count check — matching exactly: the per-group GDT is fine, the superblock global is stale.

NARROW FIX (owners' lane, ffs-alloc + superblock persist): on inode/block alloc+free, also adjust the SUPERBLOCK GLOBAL s_free_inodes_count / s_free_blocks_count (sum the per-group deltas already computed for the GDT) and persist the superblock — the per-group GDT path is already correct, so this is a small additive change at the same site that calls persist_group_desc_with_bitmap_overrides. Plus the c4bb5890 fsck-check + post-write gate. This is a contained correctness/spec-compliance lever, distinct from the perf levers. (Non-perf code-read done deliberately because the htree-leaf-split agent's background builds contend for cores, making perf A/Bs unreliable per 961941e3.)

### 2026-06-26 CORRECTION + BUG: frankenfs btrfs walk OVER-COUNTS ~2x (DIR_ITEM+DIR_INDEX?) — confounds the d9e66fc6 walk win (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

A count cross-check (run to VALIDATE the btrfs walk win) caught a real over-enumeration bug:
- frankenfs walk (btw.img): 3 dirs + 32002 files (32006 stats).
- kernel `find /mnt`: 3 dirs + 16002 files. ~2x mismatch, SAME dir count.
- Ruled out subvolume artifact: the btrfs-convert ext2_saved subvolume (ID 256) holds only 1 file (the saved image, per kernel ls), NOT the ~16000 excess.

So frankenfs's btrfs walk over-enumerates the converted files ~2x. LIKELY CAUSE: btrfs stores TWO items per directory entry — a DIR_ITEM (keyed by name-hash, for lookup) and a DIR_INDEX (keyed by sequence, for readdir) — and frankenfs's walk appears to count BOTH, while the kernel's readdir enumerates only DIR_INDEX (1x). (Alternative: descending into the ext2_saved image-file as a nested fs — less likely given same dir count.) Root-cause is in the ffs-core btrfs walk/readdir path (owners' btrfs-read lane; NON-overlapping with the in-flight ext4-htree write work).

IMPACT on the d9e66fc6 btrfs walk win (~4x): CONFOUNDED — the time comparison was frankenfs(32002 entries, 10ms) vs kernel(16002, 42ms), NOT equal sets. The WIN DIRECTION still holds and is likely LARGER per-entry (frankenfs did ~2x the enumeration work and was still ~4x faster -> ~8x per-entry), but the headline "~4x" must be RESTATED on equal enumeration (re-measure after the double-count is fixed, or scope both to the same DIR_INDEX set). I am DOWNGRADING d9e66fc6's "~4x" to "win-direction-confirmed, ratio pending equal-count re-measure".

LESSON (re-confirmed, important): a perf "win" must enumerate the SAME set as the baseline — always cross-check COUNTS, not just times. The count check caught both a correctness bug AND a confounded headline ratio. (Same validation discipline that caught the overlay/contention/serial-gap issues earlier this session.)

### 2026-06-26 ROOT CAUSE of btrfs walk 2x over-count (cdaf7711): dir_items collects BOTH DIR_ITEM + DIR_INDEX (lib.rs:8788) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Root-caused the btrfs walk over-enumeration (cdaf7711) to one site:
- ffs-core lib.rs:8788-8792: `BTRFS_ITEM_DIR_ITEM | BTRFS_ITEM_DIR_INDEX => dir_items.entry(item.key.objectid).or_default().push((item.key, item.data))` — the bulk fs-tree load pushes BOTH key types into the per-directory `dir_items` map.
- In btrfs EVERY directory entry exists as TWO items: a DIR_ITEM (key type 84, keyed by name-hash, used for name LOOKUP) and a DIR_INDEX (key type 96, keyed by sequence, used for READDIR ordering) — both carry the same name + location. So `dir_items[dir]` holds 2 rows per file.
- The walk enumerates all of `dir_items` -> counts each file TWICE. The kernel's readdir enumerates only DIR_INDEX (1x). Hence the measured 32002 (frankenfs) vs 16002 (kernel).

FIX (ffs-core btrfs walk/readdir consumer of `dir_items`, owners' btrfs-read lane): for directory LISTING/walk, enumerate only DIR_INDEX rows (type 96) — keep DIR_ITEM (type 84) available for name LOOKUP (lookup is unaffected: it finds the name under either key). Equivalently, dedup by name. One-filter fix at the dir_items consumer.

SEVERITY should-check (NOT asserted): the owners should verify whether the FUSE readdir path shares this both-keys enumeration. If it does, FUSE readdir would return DUPLICATE dirents (user-visible: `ls` shows each file twice on a btrfs mount), escalating this from a benchmark-count artifact to a real correctness bug. The walk-command path is confirmed affected (the 2x measurement); the FUSE path is unverified here. CONFIRMS cdaf7711: d9e66fc6's "~4x" compared 2x-inflated frankenfs to 1x kernel -> win direction holds (~8x/entry) but re-measure on equal counts after the filter. Validation chain: count cross-check -> over-count bug -> this root cause (no perf measurement needed; contention-immune).

### 2026-06-26 RETRACT 41f0c43f: btrfs walk 2x is a SUBVOLUME-resolution bug (ext2_saved shows main-subvol files), NOT DIR_ITEM+DIR_INDEX (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

RETRACT 41f0c43f's root cause (DIR_ITEM+DIR_INDEX double-count). REFUTED by a FUSE name-set comparison: the FUSE mount's 32006 paths are ALL UNIQUE (no duplicate dirents) — a DIR_ITEM+DIR_INDEX double-count would produce DUPLICATE NAMES, which is not what happens. (The 8788 code does collect both key types, but that is evidently deduped/handled downstream for listing — it is NOT the cause of the count gap.)

REAL CAUSE (verified by `comm` of FUSE vs kernel name sets): the ~16000 extra entries frankenfs reports are ALL under `/ext2_saved/` — `/ext2_saved/big.dat`, `/ext2_saved/cb_00000000`, ... — i.e. the SAME filenames as the main subvolume. So frankenfs exposes the btrfs-convert `ext2_saved` SUBVOLUME's contents as a full copy of the MAIN subvolume's 16000 files, whereas the kernel shows ext2_saved as its actual single `image` file (kernel: /ext2_saved has 1 file; frankenfs: 16000). This is a SUBVOLUME-RESOLUTION bug — frankenfs resolves the non-default subvolume's tree to (what presents as) the main subvolume's file set, doubling the walk count on any btrfs-convert image. Owners' btrfs-read lane (subvolume root resolution); NON-overlapping with the in-flight ext4-htree work.

IMPACT on d9e66fc6 walk win: the btw.img fixture is CONTAMINATED by ext2_saved -> frankenfs walked 32002 (main 16000 + ext2_saved-as-main 16000) vs kernel 16002 (main 16000 + ext2_saved 1). The win still HOLDS and is LARGER per real entry (frankenfs did 2x work, still ~4x faster -> ~8x per main-subvol entry), but a clean ratio needs a btrfs fixture WITHOUT ext2_saved (native btrfs, or delete the subvolume).

LESSONS: (1) compare actual NAME SETS, not just counts, to root-cause an enumeration discrepancy — `comm` instantly localized it to /ext2_saved/*. (2) I asserted 41f0c43f from CODE-READING (8788 collects both keys) WITHOUT verifying it produced the predicted dup NAMES; the empirical check refuted it. Verify a root-cause hypothesis's prediction empirically BEFORE asserting. (7th self-correction this session; the validation discipline keeps catching them.)

### 2026-06-26 LANDED: ext4 htree rename O(N^2)->O(log N) — ~200x gap closed to ~3.3x vs kernel (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

LANDED the campaign's #1 lever (the dominant measured gap, rename ~200x). Implemented in an isolated agent worktree, independently verified by me, now on main.

MEASURED (rename-bench, 32000 files, single dir):
- before: 172-187 renames/s (O(N^2): per-op time grew with dir size).
- after: 10,340/s (release-perf) = ~3.3x vs kernel (34344/s); my independent release build reproduced 7,088/s (32000 in 5.66s) = ~41-60x over the old 172/s. O(N^2) ELIMINATED (near-flat per-op).

The ~200x had THREE O(N)-per-op costs (the agent traced all three; fixing the insert alone left it at 187/s):
1. Insert: full-htree rebuild on a full leaf -> incremental LEAF SPLIT (split one leaf + insert one dx_entry). New pure `ffs_ondisk::split_htree_leaf` (re-hashes with the lookup path's params, splits at the clean hash boundary, repacks both leaves, stamps). Declines (->rebuild) for multi-level index / full dx_root / no clean boundary / casefold.
2. Removal: linear O(dir) block scan -> htree descent to the hash-target leaf (O(log N)).
3. Negative lookup (the rename target-existence check): O(N) block scan + index rebuild every op -> authoritative dx-descent miss (the dx index always covers every entry; our writer never linearly appends, the kernel's invariant), non-casefold only.

INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION (not blind-trusting the agent): anti-corruption gate test PASSED (6000-file split + rename-churn: every name findable, exact enumeration, no dupes/losses); ffs-ondisk 666+21 tests green; ffs-core 1180 passed / 5 failed = the SAME 5 pre-existing clean-HEAD failures (btrfs_symlink x3, largest_contiguous_free_run, read_block_with_empty_scope), ZERO htree/dir/rename failures; e2fsck Pass 2 (directory structure) CLEAN (only the orthogonal pre-existing superblock free-count gap, c4bb5890); kernel RO mount enumerates exactly the renamed set. Diff: ffs-ondisk +390, ffs-core +560, new gate test; clean cp-land (main's ffs-core/ffs-ondisk were identical to the worktree base).

Files: crates/ffs-ondisk/src/{ext4.rs,lib.rs}, crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs, crates/ffs-core/tests/htree_leaf_split_gate.rs. Residual at 32000: mild sub-linear growth (per-op MVCC commit / collect_extents as the extent tree grows) — a separate, smaller lever. This closes the campaign's dominant gap.

### 2026-06-26 REFINE a8da59c0: btrfs subvolume bug = no tree-switch on ROOT_ITEM crossing; fix direction (mechanism verified, exact line not pinned) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Refined the btrfs subvolume-resolution bug (a8da59c0) — mechanism and fix direction, verified at the design level (NOT asserting a specific read-side line, having traced it partway):
- frankenfs resolves a SINGLE subvolume at MOUNT (`resolve_btrfs_mount_selection`, ffs-core ~3413-3543 → `subvol_root_dirid`); the walk/readdir then operates within that ONE fs tree.
- It parses the dir-child's `child_key_type` (`BtrfsDirItem`) and emits a ROOT_ITEM child (a subvolume) AS a directory, but does NOT switch to that child subvolume's own root tree for its CONTENTS. So `/ext2_saved` surfaces the MOUNTED (default/main) subvolume's files (verified: the FUSE `comm` showed `/ext2_saved/{big.dat,cb_*}` = the main subvol's names) instead of ext2_saved's actual single `image` file.

FIX DIRECTION (owners' btrfs-read lane): when a dir-child's `child_key_type == BTRFS_ITEM_ROOT_ITEM`, resolve its contents via that subvolume's ROOT_ITEM → its `root_dirid` tree — the same resolution `resolve_btrfs_mount_selection` already performs for the default subvolume at mount — instead of staying in the current tree.

CAVEAT (anti-over-claim): I did NOT pin the exact read-side traversal point. The ROOT_ITEM handling I found at lib.rs:25765 is the subvolume CREATION (write) path — a red herring for the read bug. The mechanism above is verified by the symptom (FUSE vs kernel name-set divergence isolated to /ext2_saved) + the single-subvol-at-mount design; the precise read-side fix site is the owners' to locate. (Held back from asserting a line, per this session's correction discipline.)

### 2026-06-26 Rename residual (post-land next gap): profile blocked by toolchain (libpanic_abort.rlib missing); residual = per-op MVCC commit / collect_extents (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

After landing the htree fix (5eb32cc6), the new biggest perf gap is the rename RESIDUAL (~3.3x vs kernel at 32000, mild sub-linear growth). Attempted an independent profile of the LANDED code but it was BLOCKED by a toolchain issue:
- release-perf build fails (PERFBUILD_EXIT=101): "failed to build archive from rlib ... libpanic_abort-*.rlib: No such file or directory". This is a TOOLCHAIN-component issue (nightly's libpanic_abort.rlib, needed by release-perf's panic=abort, is missing), NOT my code — the release build + ffs-core 1180 + ondisk 666+21 + the gate test all passed.
- Consequence: the perf profile that ran used the STALE pre-fix release-perf binary (the `[ -x ]` guard passed on the old one). It is INVALID for the residual — its top hotspot (`lookup_name_with_scope` HashSet `from_iter`, the per-op name-index REBUILD) is precisely the O(N) negative-lookup cost my landed fix ELIMINATED. So that profile reflects pre-5eb32cc6 behavior, not the residual.

RESIDUAL CHARACTERIZATION (from the implementation agent's working-build measurement): the ~3.3x residual is per-op MVCC commit / `collect_extents` as the extent tree grows. Two candidate levers: (a) per-op MVCC commit — the shared metadata-write root (delete/create too), the peers' ffs-mvcc sharded-store lane; (b) a `collect_extents`-skip for metadata-only rename (a rename moves a dir entry, not data — it should not need to collect the inode's extent tree) — a possible NEW lever, but UNCONFIRMED pending a valid profile.
