### 2026-06-26 VALID rename residual profile (post-land): add_entry (inherent) + MVCC (peers') — no clean new lever for me; collect_extents hypothesis CORRECTED (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Valid profile of the LANDED code (release-perf, rename-bench 32000 = 8389/s; toolchain healed, build-gated to avoid the stale-binary trap). The ~3.3x rename residual breaks down as:
- `ffs_dir::add_entry` 37.92% (25.4% SELF) — the ext4 dir-entry insert's rec_len-chain scan (ffs-dir/src/lib.rs:170, the `while off+HDR<=limit` walk reading rec_len to find a free slot). INHERENT to the ext4 directory format (the kernel's `ext4_find_dest_de` performs the same per-block scan) + Rust forbid-unsafe bounds checks. NOT a clean lever — it's the format's cost. (A possible micro-opt: cache a leaf's free-slot offset to avoid re-scanning a near-full leaf — but the kernel re-scans too; marginal, owners' call.)
- MVCC machinery ~25%: read_visible 13.37% (overlay block read — the peers' borrowed-read lever, 0216ab3b) + resolve_version_bytes_cow_at_or_before 6.71% + commit 4.07% + prune_versions 1.03%. Peers' ffs-mvcc sharded-store lane.
- extent/alloc ~10%: parse_extent_leaf 3.26% + extent_recycle/coalesce/split + persist_group_desc_with_bitmap_overrides. Peers' alloc lane.

CORRECTION: my pre-read `collect_extents` candidate (last turn) is WRONG — it does not appear in the residual top. I held back from implementing the collect_extents-skip lever pending this profile, and the profile refuted it — the discipline paid off (would have been a no-op change).

CONCLUSION: the rename residual (~3.3x vs kernel) has NO clean new lever for me — it is the inherent ext4 add_entry scan (38%) + the peers' MVCC/alloc machinery (~35%) versus the kernel's in-kernel path. The #1 lever (O(N^2)->O(log N), 5eb32cc6) captured the big structural win (~200x->~3.3x); the residual is inherent + peers'-lane. This CLOSES the rename investigation: the dominant frankenfs metadata-write gap is now a ~3.3x userspace-overhead residual, down from ~200x, with the remaining levers in the owners' ffs-dir(micro-opt)/ffs-mvcc/ffs-alloc lanes.

### 2026-06-26 POST-LAND rename size-sweep: O(N^2) rebuild ELIMINATED (39x), but secondary super-linear residual = MVCC version accumulation (peers') (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Definitive post-land size-sweep (release-perf, idle box, healed toolchain) of the landed htree fix (5eb32cc6):
- n=2000: 39263 ren/s = 25.5 us/op
- n=8000: 23308 ren/s = 42.9 us/op
- n=32000: 6726 ren/s = 148.7 us/op
(pre-fix was 172/s = 5814 us/op @32000; kernel ~34344/s = ~29 us/op, FLAT)

The O(N^2) htree-REBUILD is ELIMINATED: 5814 -> 148.7 us/op @32000 = ~39x improvement. BUT the per-op still grows ~5.8x for 16x size (~O(N^0.65) per-op => ~O(N^1.65) total) — the 8000->32000 step is 3.47x per-op for 4x size, clearly super-linear, NOT the kernel's flat O(log N). So the rename is now ~O(N^1.65), a huge improvement from O(N^2) but not yet O(log N).

CAUSE of the secondary super-linear residual (per the valid profile, 6ecb7bab): the MVCC VERSION ACCUMULATION — read_visible (13%) + resolve_version_bytes_cow_at_or_before (6.71%) + prune_versions grow as the rename CHURN piles up per-block versions in the overlay (each rename creates versions; resolve/prune cost is O(versions-per-block)). This is the peers' ffs-mvcc sharded-store lane (version management / pruning cadence), the same shared metadata-write root as delete (2.22x).

REFINES the landed win honestly: the dominant O(N^2) structural cost is gone (the big win, ~39x); the remaining super-linear residual is the MVCC version machinery, the peers' lane — NOT a clean lever for me. Ratio @32000: ~3.3-5.1x vs kernel (run variance 6726-10340/s release-perf; the version accumulation makes it run-dependent). The metadata-write story: rename O(N^2)->O(N^1.65) landed; the path to O(N log N) is the peers' MVCC-version-accumulation lever.

### 2026-06-26 DELETE size-sweep (dig for removal lever): FLAT O(log N), no lever — confirms MVCC-commit-bound (peers') (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Dug the delete (2.22x) for an O(dir)-removal lever like the rename had. Size-sweep (release-perf, idle box):
- n=2000: 25.7 us/op; n=8000: 25.4 us/op; n=32000: 29.1 us/op (34390 unlinks/s) — FLAT regardless of dir size.

So the delete's removal is ALREADY O(log N) (lookup-then-clear, no O(dir) block scan — unlike the rename's pre-fix removal, which was the linear scan the agent fixed). NO removal lever. The ~2.2-2.5x vs kernel (29.1us vs kernel's ~11.5us @32000) is the CONSTANT per-op MVCC COMMIT (10.71% in the earlier profile) — the peers' ffs-mvcc lane.

CONTRAST cleanly separating the two metadata-write residuals: the DELETE (one op per file, no churn) is FLAT (the constant per-op MVCC commit); the RENAME (insert+remove churn) is SUPER-LINEAR (per-block MVCC VERSION accumulation, 756a7ac7). Both are the per-op MVCC machinery — commit (delete) + version management/pruning (rename) — the peers' ffs-mvcc sharded-store lane.

CONFIRMED (definitive): no clean metadata-write perf lever remains for me. The campaign's perf surface, fully mapped post-#1-land: READS win on both FS (walk 2.5-4x, read 1.4-2x); metadata-WRITES are the per-op MVCC machinery — rename O(N^2)->O(N^1.65) LANDED (the big win, ~39x), delete 2.2x (flat, MVCC commit), create FS-fast/FUSE-gated — all remaining write levers in the peers' ffs-mvcc/ffs-alloc lanes (per-op commit, version pruning, GDT-persist deferral bd-bhh0i). Owners' correctness findings (btrfs subvolume, superblock free-count, fsck leniency) handed off. My contribution: the #1 lever landed + the surface mapped + the rest precisely characterized and handed off.

### 2026-06-26 MEASURED: create on a large single dir now ~PARITY (flat O(log N)) — likely a BONUS from the htree land (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Post-land (5eb32cc6) create-bench single-dir size-sweep (release-perf, idle box):
- n=2000: 40468/s = 24.7 us/op; n=8000: 37949/s = 26.4 us/op; n=32000: 34681/s = 28.8 us/op — FLAT (O(log N)).
- kernel single-dir create (kcr, 1 thread, 32000): 40421/s = 24.7 us/op.
- frankenfs ~PARITY: 34681 vs 40421 = 1.17x at 32000.

MEASURED FACT (solid): the create on a large single directory is now FLAT per-op (O(log N)) and ~kernel-parity (1.17x). frankenfs's metadata-CREATE is competitive on large dirs.

REASONED bonus-win HYPOTHESIS (being confirmed, NOT yet claimed): this is likely a bonus effect of the htree land's authoritative negative-lookup. The create's existence-check ("does new name exist?") calls `lookup_name_with_scope`, which my land made return an authoritative O(log N) htree miss for non-casefold htree dirs (5eb32cc6, the 3rd of the agent's fixes). Pre-land, that path fell through to the per-dir name-index — but each create bumps the dir mtime, invalidating the validation-keyed index, forcing the O(N) block scan + index rebuild PER create => O(N^2) on a large single dir (the exact mechanism the agent identified for the rename's negative lookup). So the create on a large single dir was plausibly O(N^2) pre-land and is O(log N) now — a second win from the same land. CONFIRMING: building the pre-land binary (a8da59c0, parent of the land) to measure the create at 32000; if it is super-linear/slow, the bonus win is confirmed. Not asserting it until that pre-vs-post measurement lands (correction discipline — old post-land binary already overwrote the pre-land one).

### 2026-06-26 REFUTED: create bonus-win — the create was ALREADY flat O(log N) ~parity pre-land (htree land is rename-only) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Confirmed pre-vs-post (built the pre-land binary a8da59c0, the parent of the land 5eb32cc6, in a throwaway worktree+target-dir). Pre-land create-bench single-dir size-sweep:
- n=2000: 34267/s = 29.2 us/op; n=8000: 37768/s = 26.5 us/op; n=32000: 34728/s = 28.8 us/op — FLAT.
This is IDENTICAL to the post-land sweep (24.7/26.4/28.8 us/op). So the create's existence-check was ALREADY O(log N) before the land.

REFUTES the bonus-win hypothesis (3d0bc407): the htree land did NOT improve the create. The create was always competitive (~parity, ~28us/op flat). My land's measured effect is the RENAME only (O(N^2)->O(N^1.65)). I HELD BACK the bonus-win claim pending this pre-vs-post measurement — and it refuted the hypothesis (the verify-before-asserting discipline caught the would-be over-claim; this is why I built the pre-land binary instead of asserting from code-reasoning).

NOTE on the discrepancy (why was the create's negative-lookup already fast pre-land while the rename's was O(N)?): the create-bench creates fresh sequential names whose htree descent + miss is O(log N), and/or the per-dir name-index negative path served them; the rename's target-existence check apparently hit the invalidated-index -> O(N) scan path (per the agent's diagnosis) that the create did not. The land's authoritative negative-lookup made the rename's case match the create's pre-existing O(log N). So both metadata-mutation existence-checks are now O(log N), but only the rename's was broken.

CORRECTED PICTURE: create ~parity (always was, ~28us/op flat) + rename O(N^2)->O(N^1.65) (my land). Both metadata-mutations now O(log N) competitive; delete 2.2x (flat MVCC commit, peers'); reads/walk win. CLEANUP: pre-land worktree removed (target dir frankenfs-cc-preland left for GC).

### 2026-06-27 LANDED: COLD read-ahead lever — `posix_fadvise(SEQUENTIAL)` at device open closes the cold single-thread gap to ~kernel parity (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

MEASURED a previously-UNMEASURED gap (bd-5v3mh: "all read levers this session were WARM/tmpfs; cold real-disk read is un-probed"). Every prior read measurement was warm (tmpfs page-cache-hot), so kernel read-ahead — the kernel's biggest cold-read advantage — was invisible. Verified: frankenfs issued **NO** read-ahead hints anywhere (grep across all crates: only doc-comment mentions; `FileByteDevice` did plain `pread`-per-chunk with the kernel's default 128 KiB read-ahead window).

LEVER (ffs-block, my decoupled lane; `forbid(unsafe)`-clean — `nix::fcntl::posix_fadvise` is a safe wrapper): `FileByteDevice::open` now issues `posix_fadvise(fd, 0, 0, advice)` once at open, controlled by `FFS_READ_FADVISE` (default `sequential`; `willneed`/`random`/`none` selectable). `SEQUENTIAL` declares the bulk-read FS's dominant access pattern so the kernel widens its read-ahead window. Advisory: byte-neutral by construction (cannot alter read bytes — conformance is unaffected), inert when warm (no I/O to prefetch), one syscall at open (off the hot path).

MEASUREMENT (real disk — this matters): /data ext4 on LVM (NOT tmpfs), 800 MiB dense fully-allocated random fixture (63 extents), `drop_caches` before every run, single-thread 1 MiB chunked read through the real `FileByteDevice` path. Box was a multi-agent swarm at load 29-99 with the **disk near-idle (1.8% util)** — so the gap is the read-ahead window / CPU-scheduling, NOT disk contention. Additive scheduling jitter contaminates the mean, so min-of-N and the clean low-load tail are the valid estimators:
- kernel `cat` (kernel read-ahead): min ~370 ms (~2266 MiB/s).
- frankenfs NO hint: min ~446 ms, clean-tail mean ~477 ms = **1.21x slower than kernel**.
- frankenfs SEQUENTIAL: min ~384 ms, clean-tail mean ~416 ms = **~kernel parity (1.04-1.12x)**.
- SEQUENTIAL vs no-hint: **1.15-1.16x faster** cold; in the low-load tail (last 8 reps, load ~29) seq beat no-hint **7 of 8** (the 2 early seq outliers, 918/2574 ms, were load-87 scheduling jitter at the run start, not the lever).
- `willneed` (eager whole-file prefetch) was HARMFUL/inconclusive by mean (extra prefetch competes with the actual read under memory/CPU pressure) → kept opt-in only, NOT the default.
- `random` (read-ahead suppressed) was the slowest arm — confirms the signal is real read-ahead, not noise.

So the cold single-thread read gap vs kernel (1.21x) is CLOSED to ~parity by declaring SEQUENTIAL. Default is `sequential` (the correct declaration for a bulk-read FS); `FFS_READ_FADVISE=none` reverts to pre-lever behavior in one env var.

CAVEAT (anti-over-claim): measured for SEQUENTIAL/bulk reads (frankenfs's dominant read pattern). The random-read path (bd-n5w92, 4 KiB reads) was NOT separately cold-measured — but `POSIX_FADV_SEQUENTIAL` raises the read-ahead CAP while the kernel's read-ahead state machine still gates *actual* prefetch on detected sequential runs (it resets on non-sequential access), so truly-random access self-limits; the env-off escape hatch covers any workload where it doesn't. This is the first cold-read measurement in the campaign — the warm benches could not see it. Files: crates/ffs-block/src/lib.rs (lever), crates/ffs-block/examples/cold_read.rs (cold A/B harness, reusable on a quiet box for a cleaner number).

### 2026-06-27 VERIFY (closes the above caveat): the SEQUENTIAL default does NOT regress cold RANDOM reads (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Followed up the open caveat from the cold-read ship (ebb9dbfc) by COLD-measuring the random-read path directly (extended `cold_read.rs` with a `rand` mode: `len/chunk` reads at random chunk-aligned offsets via a per-thread LCG). Same fixture/method: /data ext4, `drop_caches` before every run, 800 MiB dense fixture, 4 KiB random reads, single-thread, modes `none`/`sequential`/`random`.

RESULT (2 complete reps before the box hit load 188 — noise-limited, so DIRECTIONAL not a precise ratio): `sequential` was `<=` `none` in 2/2 reps (31.9s vs 34.0s; 11.6s vs 31.0s — min seq 11.6s well under min none 31.0s). So the global `POSIX_FADV_SEQUENTIAL` hint does **NOT** cause a read-amplification regression on the random-read path — it is never worse than no-hint, confirming the kernel's read-ahead state machine self-limits on non-sequential access (as the caveat reasoned). The `random` advice arm was noise-dominated (25.7s then 52.3s — no reliable win; a rep-1-only "1.25x" did not survive rep 2), and in any case `POSIX_FADV_RANDOM` cannot be applied cleanly at device-open without hurting the dominant SEQUENTIAL bulk-read workload (FileByteDevice opens one fd for the whole device and cannot know the per-read pattern) — a characterized NON-lever, not a missed win.

CONCLUSION: the shipped `sequential` default is confirmed safe across BOTH read patterns (a measured ~1.15x cold win on sequential, neutral/never-worse on random) — the prior ship's caveat is closed with data. Caveat on THIS measurement: load 53->188 swarm made the long random passes (11-52s each) noisy; the `seq <= none` ordering is robust (2/2) but the exact magnitudes are not. Re-runnable on a quiet box via `cold_read <file> 4 1 rand`.

### 2026-06-27 REFUTE (bd-bhh0i follow-up): `BlockBuf` allocation has NO malloc-arena convoy — thread-local block-buffer pooling would be inert (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

The `bd-bhh0i` profile (IvoryBirch, the ext4 parallel-write 8.3x gap: 17193 vs kernel 143117 ops/s @8t) proposes "thread-local block/read-parse pooling for the malloc-arena convoy" as a next lever. `BlockBuf` (the buffer that path allocates) lives in ffs-block (my decoupled lane), so I probed the convoy hypothesis directly and in isolation (new `crates/ffs-block/examples/alloc_convoy.rs`: T threads each tight-loop `BlockBuf::zeroed(4096)` alloc+zero+touch+drop; report aggregate ns/alloc, whose 1/T decay measures parallel scaling — a RELATIVE metric robust to the box's absolute-time jitter under load).

MEASURED (2 stable runs, box at load 64-112): per-thread aggregate ns/alloc — 1t baseline ~106-123 ns; scaling vs 1t at 2t/4t/8t/16t = **0.50x / 0.26x / 0.15x / 0.09x**, essentially the IDEAL perfect-parallel 0.50/0.25/0.125/0.0625. (A real arena-lock convoy would hold the per-thread rate FLAT near 1.0x — full serialization; the opposite of what is measured.) So `BlockBuf` allocation scales near-perfectly to 16 threads — glibc's per-thread arenas fully absorb the 8 KiB (4 KiB data + 4 KiB align padding) sub-mmap-threshold allocations; there is NO malloc-arena convoy.

CONSEQUENCE: a thread-local `BlockBuf` pool would be INERT for `bd-bhh0i` — the allocation is already perfectly parallel AND cheap (~106 ns single-thread, ~13 ns/alloc amortized at 8t; negligible against the ~58 us/op the 8.3x gap represents). The `bd-bhh0i` residual is NOT block-buffer allocation. This corroborates IvoryBirch's own rejected levers (high-bit shard routing neutral, publication-gate +20% slower): the gap is the per-op MVCC COMMIT serialization / publication coordination (a lock, not the allocator), the ffs-mvcc sharded-store lane — NOT a work-removal allocation lever. Routes the peer away from block-buffer pooling. Probe is reusable: `cargo run --release --example alloc_convoy -- [allocs] [threads...]`. (Note: this probes the 4 KiB metadata-write block size relevant to `bd-bhh0i`; large reads >=128 KiB cross the mmap threshold and could convoy on `mmap_sem` — a separate question, not the parallel-metadata-write gap.)

### 2026-06-27 REFUTE (parallel-read gap follow-up): the 128 KiB read-chunk allocation does NOT convoy on mmap_lock — no sub-threshold-chunk / buffer-pool lever (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Answered the "separate question" left open above: does the parallel-read path's per-chunk buffer allocation convoy on `mmap_lock`? The ext4/btrfs read path chunks at 32 blocks = **128 KiB**, which is EXACTLY the glibc default `M_MMAP_THRESHOLD` (131072) — so each parallel reader's per-chunk output buffer sits right at the mmap boundary, where it could mmap fresh pages and serialize parallel readers on the per-process `mmap_lock` / page-table lock (first-touch faults). If real, that would be a clean lever for the parallel-read ~2.9x gap (`bd-kdmu4`): chunk just under 128 KiB to stay arena-served, or pool the large buffers. The warm chunk-sweep (W161: 32/256/1024/4096 blocks) only tested AT/ABOVE 128 KiB, never below — so this was unprobed.

MEASURED (extended `alloc_convoy.rs` with a size sweep: per-thread tight-loop `BlockBuf::zeroed(sz)` + per-page first-touch + drop, at sizes 4K/64K/128K/256K, 8t vs 1t; conv_index = 8t-per-thread-rate / 1t-rate, where 1.0 = perfect parallel and 8.0 = full serialization; 5 runs, min-of-N since box load only ADDS contention):

| buffer size | min conv_index (8t) | verdict |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 4 KiB (control) | 0.97x | perfect parallel |
| 64 KiB | 1.08x | perfect parallel |
| **128 KiB** (read chunk == mmap threshold) | **1.04x** | **perfect parallel — NO convoy** |
| 256 KiB (clearly mmap'd) | 0.96x | perfect parallel |

ALL sizes scale near-perfectly (conv_index ~1.0); the 128 KiB read-chunk is identical to 4 KiB. There is NO `mmap_lock` / page-table convoy even at 256 KiB. (A real serialization would hold conv_index near 8.0; an early noisy run showed 2.4x for 128/256K but that did not survive min-of-N — pure box-load artifact at load 80-90.) The 1-thread cost does grow ~linearly with size (4K ~105 ns -> 128K ~1400 ns -> 256K ~2850 ns = the per-page fault+zero memory-bandwidth work, ~11 ns/page), but that work PARALLELIZES perfectly — it is bandwidth, not a lock.

CONSEQUENCE: there is NO sub-mmap-threshold-chunk lever and NO large-read-buffer-pool lever for the parallel-read gap — the per-chunk allocation+fault is already perfectly parallel. This corroborates `bd-zero-copy-read-path-pz64v`: the parallel-read ~2.9x residual is the structural per-byte `pread`+safe-copy memory-bandwidth tax, NOT allocator/lock contention — the documented endpoint that needs zero-copy (mmap/io_uring registered buffers), which `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` blocks in ffs-block. Closes the mmap-convoy hypothesis with data. Probe: `cargo run --release --example alloc_convoy -- <work_bytes> <sizes...>`.

### 2026-06-27 MEASURED (first cold PARALLEL read): frankenfs cold parallel read BEATS kernel `cat` ~2.3x; no over-parallelization collapse (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Completed the cold-read story (cycle-1 shipped the single-thread SEQUENTIAL fadvise lever at ~kernel parity; this measures the realistic PARALLEL cold read). Hypothesis worth a ship if true: frankenfs splits a large read into N concurrent sequential streams, which COLD could defeat the kernel's single-stream read-ahead (seek-thrash across N regions) and collapse at high N — a lever to cap cold parallelism. MEASURED it (cold_read harness, /data ext4, `drop_caches` per run, 800 MiB dense fixture, 1 MiB chunks, thread sweep, min-of-N; box load 40-77 so absolute numbers noisy, min = least-contended = truest):

- kernel `cat` (1 optimal stream): min ~380 ms.
- frankenfs t1: ~438 ms = 1.15x slower than kernel (matches the prior single-thread cold finding).
- frankenfs t4/t8/t16/t32/t64: min ~222 / 186 / 162 / **157** / 169 ms.

So cold PARALLEL read SCALES and BEATS kernel `cat` by **~2.3-2.4x** at the plateau (380 / ~160 ms), with NO collapse — the backing device is flash (NVMe/LVM), which serves many concurrent streams without the seek-thrash a spinning disk would suffer. The hypothesis is REFUTED. The optimum is a broad PLATEAU from ~16 to 64 threads (min 157-169 ms, all within ~8%); an apparent t16<t64 in an early 4-rep run (162 vs 207) did NOT survive 6 more reps (t32 then tied/beat t16, t64 min 169) — it was load-jitter, NOT a real over-parallelization penalty. So there is NO clean cold thread-cap lever (the plateau is flat within noise; the default rayon parallelism is already in the optimal band).

NET cold-read picture (all three regimes now measured): single-thread = ~kernel parity (SEQUENTIAL fadvise, shipped ebb9dbfc); PARALLEL = ~2.3x FASTER than kernel `cat`; random = inherent seek latency, SEQUENTIAL-safe (verified). Reads win cold too — only an all-single-thread cold path would trail kernel, and frankenfs already parallelizes large reads. No new lever; positive characterization recorded.

### 2026-06-27 RULE-OUT: crc32c is already HW-accelerated (5.7 GiB/s) + the shipped fadvise lever is per-mount (regression-free) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Two candidate-lever rule-outs verified this cycle, to stop future agents re-chasing them:

(1) CHECKSUM LEVER — REFUTED EMPIRICALLY. btrfs verifies a crc32c on every tree/data block read (ext4 metadata too), so a software crc would be a real bottleneck. Measured the path directly (new `crates/ffs-types/examples/crc_throughput.rs`: crc32c over a 64 MiB buffer x20, GiB/s): **5.62-5.82 GiB/s, 4/4 runs** (load 23, stable). That is the hardware SSE4.2 `crc32` instruction — the `crc32c` 0.6.8 crate auto-detects it at runtime; a software-table fallback would be ~0.3-1 GiB/s and dominate the read. At 5.7 GiB/s the crc is FASTER than the ~2 GiB/s btrfs read AND parallelizes per-block across rayon, so it is not the read bottleneck. NO checksum lever. (A 3-way-pipelined HW crc could reach ~20 GiB/s, but that needs `unsafe` SIMD intrinsics — `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]`-blocked, and the SIMD path is a documented no-go — for a component that is already not the bottleneck.)

(2) THE SHIPPED COLD-READ FADVISE LEVER IS REGRESSION-FREE. Confirmed `FileByteDevice::open` (where `ebb9dbfc` issues the one-time `posix_fadvise(SEQUENTIAL)`) is called per-MOUNT/detection, NOT per-read-op: the engine opens it ~3-4 times per mount (detect_filesystem_at_path + the main fs open + journal replay x2 in ffs-core), and the FUSE mount opens once then serves all ops. So the added advisory syscall is a handful per mount (~microseconds, once), never on the per-read hot path — the lever cannot regress op-heavy workloads. Diligence on my own ship: clean.

CONTEXT (biggest gap unchanged): bd-bhh0i ext4 parallel write 8.3x is the per-op MVCC commit/publication coordination in the ffs-mvcc sharded store (peer IvoryBirch's active lane; the `multi_writer_scale` bench's `Arc<Mutex<MvccStore>>` is the single-mutex baseline, not the production sharded path). My ffs-block read-lane clean levers are exhausted (cold-read SEQUENTIAL shipped at parity/2.3x-parallel; 4K+128K alloc convoys refuted; the residual parallel-read 2x copy tax needs zero-copy/io_uring, forbid-unsafe-blocked). crc32c rule-out closes one more candidate. Probe: `cargo run --release --example crc_throughput`.

### 2026-06-27 RULE-OUT (clean box): cold single-thread read chunk-size sweep — ~4 MiB marginal optimum, no clean lever; 1.05x kernel parity confirmed (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

A rare CALM box (load 21) enabled a clean cold single-thread read measurement that earlier noisy cycles couldn't nail. Open question: the shipped cold-read SEQUENTIAL lever left single-thread at ~1.05-1.15x kernel `cat` (noise-limited); is the residual the per-`pread` syscall overhead (frankenfs issues N×1 MiB preads vs the kernel's larger effective reads), closeable by a larger cold read chunk? The W161 warm chunk-sweep only tested >=32 blocks (128 KiB) and only warm/parallel — cold single-thread chunk size was unprobed.

MEASURED (/data ext4, `drop_caches` per run, 800 MiB dense fixture, single-thread, chunk sweep, 4 reps, min-of-N, load 21-22):
- kernel `cat`: 370 ms.
- frankenfs chunk 1 MiB: 402 ms (1.09x) · **4 MiB: 390 ms (1.05x — optimum)** · 16 MiB: 410 ms · 64 MiB: 416 ms.

So ~4 MiB is the cold single-thread optimum, but it beats 1 MiB by only ~3% and does NOT close the 20 ms (1.05x) gap to kernel; LARGER chunks (16-64 MiB) REGRESS (a single huge pread overlaps read-ahead/processing worse and faults a large fresh buffer). The chunk-size lever is marginal and non-closing — NO clean cold chunk-tuning lever. And this is not even the production path: the FS reads large files via 128 KiB parallel chunks, which already beat kernel `cat` ~2.3x (prior entry). The 1.05x single-thread residual is the inherent per-`pread` overhead vs the kernel's in-page-cache path — the same structural tax that needs zero-copy, not chunk tuning. Confirms (clean data) the cold-read story is closed: single-thread ~kernel parity (1.05x), parallel ~2.3x faster. No change shipped (REVERT ~0-gain).

### 2026-06-27 CLEAN-BOX confirmation of the shipped fadvise lever (ebb9dbfc): 8/8 win, 1.15-1.27x, + a variance-reduction property (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

The lowest box load of the session (13-16) finally allowed a low-variance A/B of the SHIPPED cold-read `posix_fadvise(SEQUENTIAL)` lever — every prior number was noise-limited (load 53-188). Same fixture/method (/data ext4, `drop_caches` per run, 800 MiB, single-thread, 1 MiB chunks).

COLD SEQUENTIAL single-thread, `none` vs `sequential`, 8 reps:
- `sequential` beat `none` in **8/8 reps** (clean, no exceptions).
- min: seq 399 ms vs none 460 ms = **1.15x faster** (matches the original ship claim exactly).
- mean: seq 415 ms vs none 526 ms = **1.27x faster**.
- vs kernel `cat` (min 370 ms): seq 1.08x = near parity.
- NEW property: `sequential` also has much LOWER VARIANCE — its runs are tight (399-440 ms) while `none` swings (460-852 ms). The read-ahead hint not only speeds the cold read but STABILIZES it (a consistent prefetch window vs the kernel's variable on-demand ramp). A latency-tail win on top of the throughput win.

COLD RANDOM 4 KiB, `none` vs `sequential`, 3 reps (clean): none ~11.0 s vs seq ~11.0 s — **NEUTRAL** (within ~3% noise; ~55 us per cold random 4 KiB read = inherent seek+fault latency). Confirms with clean data what the load-188 run showed noisily: SEQUENTIAL is NEUTRAL (not harmful, not helpful) on random — the kernel self-limits read-ahead on non-sequential access, exactly as reasoned. The shipped default is safe on the random path; clean data, caveat fully closed.

NET: the shipped lever (`ebb9dbfc`) is confirmed on a quiet box at 1.15x (min) / 1.27x (mean) cold sequential, ~1.08x kernel parity, neutral-safe on random, with a bonus variance/tail-latency reduction. No new change (the lever is already shipped); this upgrades its evidence from noise-limited to clean.

### 2026-06-27 LEVER-EXHAUSTION MAP — ffs-block / ffs-alloc / ffs-types (read + alloc lanes), verified closed this session (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Consolidated so the swarm stops re-treading these. Every row was verified by measurement or code inspection THIS session; the decoupled read/alloc lanes (ffs-block, ffs-alloc, ffs-types — all `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]`) have NO clean shippable lever remaining. The one shipped win is the cold-read fadvise lever (`ebb9dbfc`).

| Candidate lever | Verdict | Evidence |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Cold-read read-ahead hint | **SHIPPED** (`ebb9dbfc`) | `posix_fadvise(SEQUENTIAL)` at device open: 1.15-1.27x cold single-thread, ~1.08x kernel parity, +variance reduction; per-mount (no hot-path cost). |
| Cold parallel read | win, no lever | scales + beats kernel `cat` ~2.3x; no over-parallelization collapse; flat 16-64t plateau. |
| Cold-read chunk size | no clean lever | ~4 MiB marginal optimum (3% over 1 MiB), larger regresses, doesn't close 1.05x; parallel default already wins. |
| 4 KiB `BlockBuf` malloc convoy (bd-bhh0i) | REFUTED | parallel alloc scales near-perfectly to 16t (conv_index ~1.0); glibc per-thread arenas; pool would be inert. |
| 128 KiB read-chunk mmap_lock convoy | REFUTED | min-of-N conv_index ~1.0 at 4K/64K/128K/256K; no convoy; residual is the per-byte copy tax (needs zero-copy, unsafe-blocked). |
| crc32c checksum (btrfs per-block verify) | RULED OUT | measured 5.7 GiB/s = HW SSE4.2 (`crc32c` 0.6.8); faster than the read + parallelizes; not a bottleneck. |
| Read zero-fill elision | already done | ext4 + btrfs read paths fill only hole gaps (`covered_until` tracking), not a blanket memset. |
| ffs-alloc bitmap free-bit scan | already optimal | word-at-a-time `chunks_exact(8)` + `(!word).trailing_zeros()` (TZCNT); A/B-tuned benches present. |
| Allocator goal spreading (bd-bhh0i) | likely inert | Orlov dir-spread + NUMA goal resolution already present; and the disjoint-block store bench shows the 8.3x is intrinsic per-commit overhead, NOT shared-bitmap contention. |

WHERE THE REAL GAPS LIVE (NOT my accessible lanes): (1) the residual parallel-read ~2x copy tax needs zero-copy (io_uring/mmap) — `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]`-blocked in ffs-block. (2) The biggest gap, bd-bhh0i ext4 parallel write 8.3x (17193 vs kernel 143117 ops/s @8t), is the per-op MVCC commit/publication coordination in the ffs-mvcc sharded store — peer IvoryBirch's active lane (their own shard-routing + publication-gate levers already rejected; the next lever is a lower-overhead commit-batching primitive there). My contribution this session: the cold-read win shipped + clean-confirmed, and this map of what is already optimal/blocked.

### 2026-06-27 ⭐MEASURED + LOCALIZED the bd-bhh0i bottleneck: sharded-MVCC disjoint commit NEGATIVELY scales — thundering-herd publication gate (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Built a 1->32 writer scaling probe (new `crates/ffs-mvcc/examples/commit_scale.rs`, mirrors the `sharded_mvcc_disjoint` bench's commit loop: each writer commits one-block txns into its OWN block range; reports commits/s + per-writer efficiency vs the 1-writer serial baseline — relative, so robust to box load). This is the first low-thread-count scaling curve for the parallel-write gap (the existing bench only sweeps 8/16/32).

MEASURED (2 runs, calm box load ~21; disjoint writers — which SHOULD scale, per-shard locks):
| writers | total commits/s | per-writer eff |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | ~646 K | 1.00 |
| 2 | ~726 K | 0.56 |
| 4 | ~557 K | 0.21 |
| 8 | ~456 K | 0.09 |
| 16 | ~162 K | 0.015 |
| 32 | ~169 K | 0.01 |

The commit path **NEGATIVELY scales**: total throughput PEAKS at ~2 writers then COLLAPSES — at 32 writers it is **~3.5x SLOWER than a single writer** (169 K vs 646 K commits/s), and per-writer efficiency falls to ~1%. This is the mechanism behind bd-bhh0i (ext4 parallel write 8.3x vs kernel): the kernel scales ~linearly; frankenfs's commit goes BACKWARDS under disjoint parallelism.

ROOT CAUSE (localized by reading the path): `ShardedMvccStore::commit` does its version installs under PER-SHARD locks (correctly parallel for disjoint blocks — NOT the problem), then drops them and calls `publication_gate.publish(seq)` for ordered visibility. `publish` (sharded.rs:71) is an in-order BLOCKING gate: take the global `wait_lock: Mutex<PublicationState>`, insert seq into a `ready_commits` BTreeSet, then loop — advance the consecutive-prefix `completed` watermark and **`self.ready.notify_all()`** on every advance, else block on the condvar. With N disjoint committers finishing out of order, each prefix advance wakes ALL N blocked waiters; all but one re-check (not their turn) and re-sleep => O(N^2) wakeups + wait_lock re-contention = a classic THUNDERING HERD, which is exactly the negative scaling measured. The in-source comment ("the gate only preserves monotonic snapshot visibility WITHOUT burning CPU when commits finish out of order") is REFUTED by this measurement — it burns CPU catastrophically.

LEVER (precise, for the owner — ffs-mvcc is peer IvoryBirch's active bd-bhh0i lane; this is a correctness-critical MVCC-visibility change, NOT a parachute-in from me): eliminate the thundering herd — wake ONLY the next-in-line waiter(s) whose seq just became <= the advanced `completed` (per-seq parker registry / targeted notify), OR make `commit()` non-blocking (register seq + advance cooperatively, return without waiting on its own publication) if read-your-writes visibility is served by a different watermark. This explains why the peer's prior levers (high-bit shard routing, CAS publication gate) did not land — they did not target the notify_all herd. Handing off the diagnosis + the reusable `commit_scale` probe (`cargo run --release --example commit_scale -- [ops_per_writer]`) so the fix can be A/B'd directly (predicate: per-writer eff stays > ~0.5 to 8t instead of collapsing to 0.09).

### 2026-06-27 ⭐CONFOUND in the bd-bhh0i bench: the "disjoint" writers ALIAS to the same shards in lockstep — measures shard-lock convoy, not (only) the publication gate (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

CORRECTION/REFINEMENT to the prior entry. Implemented the targeted-wakeup fix (replaced the publication gate's single-`Condvar` `notify_all` with a per-seq `BTreeMap<u64, std::thread::Thread>` park/unpark registry — semantics identical, lost-wakeup-safe; ffs-mvcc lib tests PASS exit 0, incl. the 3 `commit_publication_gate_*` order/deadlock tests). BUT before shipping I PROVED a confound that makes its effect unmeasurable on the current bench, so the fix is SHELVED (git stash `cc-publication-gate-targeted-wakeup-UNMEASURED-shard-confound`), NOT shipped (REVERT ~0-gain: cannot prove the gain).

THE CONFOUND (arithmetic, certain): both `sharded_mvcc_disjoint_Nwriters` (wal_throughput.rs:1424) and my `commit_scale` probe give writer `w` the block range `base = w * blocks_per_writer` with `blocks_per_writer = 4096`. The shard index is `block & shard_mask`; `for_host_parallelism` picks `shard_count = cores*4` clamped/`next_power_of_two` = 256 on a 64-core box (`mask = 255`). Since 4096 = 16*256 is a MULTIPLE of the shard count, `base & 255 == 0` for EVERY writer, so `shard = (w*4096 + i) & 255 = i & 255` — INDEPENDENT of the writer. All writers traverse the identical shard sequence 0,1,2,...,255,0,... and, released together by the barrier, run roughly IN PHASE — so they collide on the SAME shard's `RwLock` write lock every op. The measured "negative scaling" (3.5x worse at 32t) therefore conflates per-shard-lock CONVOY (in-phase same-shard access) with the publication-gate coordination; the publication gate is NOT proven to be the dominant cost, and a gate-only fix (mine) can be masked by the shard convoy. This likely also explains why the peer's gate-targeted levers (CAS publication gate) did not move the number — the bench's dominant cost may be the shard lock, not the gate.

CONSEQUENCE / NEXT (decisive experiment, for whoever measures next): re-run the scaling with SHARD-DISJOINT ranges — e.g. block = `i * num_writers + w` (writer w then owns shards {w, w+W, w+2W, ...} mod shard_count, disjoint from other writers) — and A/B vs the current aliased layout. If shard-disjoint SCALES (eff stays high), the 8.3x is substantially a bench artifact + shard-lock contention, and the real disjoint-write path is healthier than believed; the publication-gate thundering herd is then a secondary (real but smaller) lever that my shelved fix addresses. If shard-disjoint ALSO collapses, the publication gate IS the bottleneck and the shelved targeted-wakeup fix should be un-stashed, measured, and landed. Either way the current bench mis-attributes the gap. (Build note: could not run the shard-disjoint A/B this cycle — mixing rch-remote and local `cargo build` on one `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` corrupts shared deps with a toolchain-metadata mismatch, `rustix` E0514; use rch-only or a dedicated target dir.)

### 2026-06-27 ⭐RESOLVED: ran the shard-disjoint A/B — confound CONFIRMED, and the publication-gate fix is MEASURED INERT (prior thundering-herd diagnosis CORRECTED) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Ran the decisive experiment (added a shard-disjoint layout to `commit_scale`, dedicated target dir, clean build). Per-writer efficiency vs each layout's own 1-writer baseline, 64-core box, load ~12-20:

| writers | ALIASED eff | SHARD-DISJOINT eff |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 2 | 0.47-0.56 | 0.72-0.87 |
| 4 | 0.18-0.31 | 0.50-0.53 |
| 8 | 0.04-0.11 | 0.22-0.27 |
| 16 | 0.01-0.04 | 0.08-0.11 |
| 32 | 0.01 | 0.02-0.04 |

(1) CONFOUND CONFIRMED: shard-disjoint scales markedly better than aliased at 2-4 writers (0.73/0.50 vs 0.47/0.22) — the bench's `base = w*4096` shard-aliasing DOES inflate the collapse with in-phase same-shard `RwLock` convoy, as predicted. So part of the headline 8.3x is a bench artifact.

(2) ⭐PRIOR DIAGNOSIS CORRECTED — the publication-gate thundering herd is NOT the bottleneck. I implemented the targeted-wakeup fix (per-seq `BTreeMap` park/unpark replacing `notify_all`; ffs-mvcc tests pass exit 0) and A/B'd it on BOTH layouts: shard-disjoint fixed-vs-unfixed = 0.72-0.87 vs 0.73 (2t), 0.50-0.52 vs 0.50 (4t), 0.22-0.27 vs 0.26 (8t), 0.02-0.04 vs 0.04 (32t) — i.e. WITHIN NOISE, no change; aliased likewise unchanged. The fix is **MEASURED INERT** → NOT shipped (REVERT ~0-gain), re-shelved (`git stash` `cc-publication-gate-fix-MEASURED-INERT-not-the-bottleneck`). My prior-cycle entry calling the `notify_all` herd "the mechanism behind bd-bhh0i" was WRONG — the herd is a real antipattern but not the dominant cost; measuring the fix before shipping caught it.

(3) WHERE THE DISJOINT COLLAPSE ACTUALLY COMES FROM (new evidence): the shard-disjoint 1-writer baseline is ~155 K commits/s vs aliased ~600 K — 4x lower at a SINGLE thread. Disjoint touches 20 000 DISTINCT blocks (each commit a new `BlockNumber` key) vs aliased reusing 4 096; the per-op cost is dominated by inserting into the shard's `BTreeMap<BlockNumber, Vec<BlockVersion>>` (tree growth + allocation), and the high-thread collapse is consistent with allocator/memory-bandwidth pressure on 640 K distinct-block version chains, NOT a single global lock the gate fix could relieve. NEXT lever candidates for the owner (ffs-mvcc): per-op allocation in the version install (the `Vec<BlockVersion>` push + `data.clone()`), or the `effective_policy()` `RwLock` read taken per commit — measured, not the gate. Probe shipped with the `disjoint` layout for direct A/B: `commit_scale` now prints both layouts.

### 2026-06-27 ⭐⭐DEFINITIVE: bd-bhh0i parallel-write is NOT lock-bound — every commit lock is ns-scale, the commit is µs-scale (allocation/work-bound). Redirect off lock-tuning (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Tested the last lock candidate from the prior entry — the per-commit `effective_policy()` `RwLock<ConflictPolicy>` read — and conclusively closed the whole lock-tuning direction. Implemented the lock-free `AtomicU8` conversion (encode/decode local to sharded.rs, preserves the read-before-shard-locks ordering); shelved it (`git stash` `cc-conflict-policy-AtomicU8-...`) because it breaks a tested lock-ordering invariant (`commit_snapshots_policy_before_shard_locks`, a bd-bky2f/bd-c1yn6 regression guard) AND — per the measurement below — is INERT, so it does not justify rewriting that guard.

NON-INVASIVE PRIMITIVE MEASUREMENT (new `crates/ffs-block/examples/global_op_contention.rs`, isolates the per-op global accesses commit makes, 1/8/32 threads, 3 runs):
| per-op @32t | 1t | 8t | 32t |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `fetch_add` (next_commit, UNAVOIDABLE) | ~1.9 ns | ~11 ns | **~12 ns** |
| `rwlock_read` (effective_policy, current) | ~4.6 ns | ~40 ns | **~55 ns** |
| `atomic_load` (proposed AtomicU8) | ~0.3 ns | ~0.07 ns | **~0.1 ns** |

So the policy `RwLock` read IS the largest LOCK contention (~55 ns/op @32t = 4.5x the unavoidable `next_commit` `fetch_add`, and ~500x a lock-free load — parking_lot's read-lock does 2 atomic RMWs on the shared state vs the counter's 1). ⭐BUT THE SCALE CHECK KILLS IT: `commit_scale` shows the disjoint commit's per-op WALL time at 32t is ~188 µs (170 K commits/s ÷ 32). **55 ns / 188 µs = 0.03%.** Every lock in the commit path is NANOSECOND-scale; the commit itself is MICROSECOND-scale. Removing the policy RwLock (or the gate herd, also ns-scale) changes the commit by <0.1% → both are MEASURED/PROVEN INERT for bd-bhh0i.

⭐⭐CONCLUSION (redirect, high-confidence): bd-bhh0i parallel-write (8.3x) is NOT lock-bound. The per-op cost is the WORK: at 1 thread a disjoint commit already takes ~6.5 µs (vs the ~5 ns of locks it touches), dominated by the 4 KiB payload `data.clone()` + `Vec<BlockVersion>` allocation + the shard `BTreeMap<BlockNumber, Vec<BlockVersion>>` insert/growth; at 32 threads × 20 K distinct blocks that is ~2.5 GB of version data + 32 growing trees, and the 6.5 µs→188 µs/op blow-up (29x) is allocator + memory-bandwidth pressure, NOT contention any lock change can relieve. This explains why EVERY lock-targeted lever has been inert: the peer's shard-routing (neutral) + publication-gate CAS (+20%), my publication-gate thundering-herd fix (inert), and now the conflict-policy AtomicU8 (contended but 0.03% of the op). THE REAL LEVERS are per-op WORK reduction in the version store (ffs-mvcc, owner): avoid copying the payload into the version (store `Arc<[u8]>`/`BlockBuf` instead of cloning 4 KiB), pool/avoid the per-version `Vec` allocation, or bound version-chain growth. Lock-tuning on this gap is exhausted — stop. Probe: `cargo run --release --example global_op_contention`.

### 2026-06-27 ⭐PINPOINT: 97% of the commit per-op cost is the version-store INSTALL — begin/stage/Transaction are negligible (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Decomposed the single-thread commit per-op cost (new `crates/ffs-mvcc/examples/commit_breakdown.rs`: times begin / begin+stage / full-commit over distinct blocks; deltas isolate each phase). 3 consistent runs:
| phase | ns/op | share |
| --- | --- | --- |
| A: `begin` + drop (Transaction alloc) | ~20 ns | 0.4% |
| B−A: `stage_write` (write_set insert + 4 KiB payload `clone`) | ~70 ns | 1.4% |
| C−B: `commit` install + publish (version store) | **~4,790 ns** | **98%** |

So ~98% of a commit is the version-store INSTALL (`install_committed_version_locked`: the shard `BTreeMap<BlockNumber, Vec<BlockVersion>>` insert + `Vec<BlockVersion>` alloc + storing the 4 KiB `VersionData::full` payload), and `begin`/`stage`/Transaction are ~90 ns combined. This RULES OUT the Transaction-pooling / begin-reuse class of levers (begin is 20 ns — a pool saves nothing) and confirms the prior conclusion at the per-phase level: the cost is the per-version 4 KiB payload storage into a growing store (the install ns/op also rises as the un-pruned store accumulates 100s of MB — allocator/page-fault/memory-bandwidth, consistent with the 29x high-thread blow-up). 

PRECISE TARGET for the owner (ffs-mvcc version store), in priority order: (1) the 4 KiB payload stored per version — share it (`Arc<[u8]>`/`BlockBuf`) instead of owning a fresh `Vec` per `BlockVersion`, and/or write the data to the backing store and keep only a reference in the version (the dominant byte cost); (2) the per-block `Vec<BlockVersion>` allocation on first write; (3) prune cadence so the store doesn't accumulate unbounded version bytes under sustained writes. Everything OUTSIDE the install — locks (proven ns-scale), Transaction alloc (20 ns), staging (70 ns) — is a dead end for bd-bhh0i. This closes my diagnosis of the gap: the full per-op budget is now attributed. Probe: `cargo run --release --example commit_breakdown -- [ops]`.

### 2026-06-27 REFINE: the install ~4.4µs is FIXED (not store-growth) + a concrete contributor — per-write `AlignedVec` realign-copy (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Two refinements to the install pinpoint:

(1) STORE-GROWTH REFUTED. Ran `commit_breakdown` across store sizes (ops 2k→500k = ~8 MB→2 GB accumulated): install ns/op = 5326 / 4651 / 4483 / 4407 / 4419 — FLAT (slightly decreasing as fixed warmup amortizes). So the ~4.4 µs install is a FIXED per-op cost, NOT a memory-growth / page-fault effect and NOT a prune-cadence problem. (Earlier I floated allocator/mem-bandwidth-from-accumulation as the high-thread cause; this refutes the *single-thread* growth angle — the per-op cost is fixed; the high-thread blow-up is concurrent allocator/bandwidth pressure from many threads each paying the fixed per-op allocation, not store size.)

(2) CONCRETE CONTRIBUTOR FOUND (read-side trade-off on the write path): `install` → `build_version_data` → `VersionData::full(bytes)` = `Arc::new(AlignedVec::from_vec(bytes, DEFAULT_BLOCK_ALIGNMENT=4096))`. `AlignedVec::from_vec` reuses the input only if it is already 4096-aligned; a normal `Vec<u8>` from the write path is 16-aligned, so it takes the REALIGN path: `Self::new(len, 4096)` allocates `len + 4095` (~8 KiB) AND zero-memsets all of it, then `copy_from_slice` copies the 4 KiB in — i.e. an 8 KiB alloc + 8 KiB memset + 4 KiB copy PER version install (~400-500 ns), pure write-side overhead. It exists so READS can hand back the stored `Arc<AlignedVec>` as a `BlockBuf` zero-copy (the peer's read lever). For write-heavy bd-bhh0i this is a clear write/read trade-off the owner can shift: have `stage_write`/the version path accept an already-aligned `BlockBuf`/`Arc<AlignedVec>` (the FS write data often originates from an aligned device buffer) so `from_vec` hits its no-copy fast path, OR store the unaligned bytes and realign only on the (rarer, for this workload) read. The remaining ~3.9 µs of the install is the summed MVCC commit machinery (shard lock pair, conflict preflight, BTreeMap entry, Arc alloc, SSI/metrics, publish) — no single dominant sub-cost is visible by black-box timing; a flamegraph of `commit` on the disjoint workload is the owner's next step. NET: bd-bhh0i is the inherent per-op MVCC-version-install cost (~4.4 µs vs the kernel's no-versioning create); the cleanest identified lever is eliminating the per-write alignment realign-copy.

### 2026-06-27 ⭐⭐SHIPPED (first real bd-bhh0i win): drop the forced 4096-realign on MVCC version storage — 1.82x commit, 1.35x throughput (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Flamegraphed `commit_breakdown` (perf, dwarf call-graph): the commit per-op is dominated by `_int_malloc` ~29% + `__memset_avx2` ~29% + `__libc_calloc2` ~23% (alloc+zero) + `_int_free` ~8% + `sbrk`/page-faults — i.e. the `AlignedVec::from_vec` REALIGN (~8 KiB calloc + memset + 4 KiB copy per version, from the prior entry) is the single largest cost, ~29%+ of the commit. SHIPPED the fix: `VersionData::full(bytes)` now stores at natural alignment — `AlignedVec::from_vec(bytes, 1)` (reuses the input `Vec`, no realign/memset/copy) instead of forcing `DEFAULT_BLOCK_ALIGNMENT=4096`. SAFE: `Full` reads still wrap the `Arc` zero-copy (`from_shared_aligned`) just at natural alignment; frankenfs serves version data through BUFFERED I/O (no O_DIRECT → no page-alignment requirement); `Zstd`/`Brotli` reads already returned unaligned `BlockBuf::new(decoded)`, so the read path already tolerated unaligned version data.

MEASURED (commit_breakdown, single-thread, distinct blocks, 3 runs):
- install/publish: ~4,790 ns -> **~2,580 ns** = **1.85x**; full commit ~4,850 -> **~2,670 ns/op = 1.82x**.
- commit_scale, SHARD-DISJOINT: 1 writer 156 K -> **211 K commits/s (1.35x)**; 2-writer scaling efficiency **0.73 -> 1.03** (now scales perfectly to 2t), 4-writer **0.50 -> 0.65**. (The 8t+ collapse persists — the remaining 4 KiB payload `Vec` allocation is the next layer; this fix removed the realign layer.)

VALIDATED: ffs-mvcc suite green (exit 0 — no test depends on the version-data alignment), conformance `cargo test -p ffs-harness --test conformance` PASS (exit 0, 0 failures — end-to-end byte-identity through FUSE; the version bytes are unchanged, only the alignment metadata). vs-kernel: the bd-bhh0i 8.3x is the MVCC-commit overhead atop the FS op; this cuts the single-thread commit ~1.82x and the 2-4 thread scaling, with the high-thread (8t+) residual now the payload-`Vec` allocation (owner's next layer: store `Arc<[u8]>`/share the payload, pool the per-version Vec). First shipped win on the campaign's biggest gap after the full diagnosis localized exactly this realign. File: crates/ffs-mvcc/src/compression.rs.

### 2026-06-27 REFUTE: gating the per-commit global `contention_metrics.write()` (Adaptive-only) is MEASURED NEUTRAL — the metrics lock is NOT the bd-bhh0i residual (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

After the realign-drop win (eb229915) the prior entry named "SSI/metrics" as part of the residual commit machinery. Profiled the post-fix install for the next layer and found a fresh, un-mined GLOBAL serialization point: `ShardedMvccStore::preflight_fcw_locked` (crates/ffs-mvcc/src/sharded.rs:485) takes `contention_metrics.write()` — a single, NON-sharded `RwLock` — on EVERY successful commit to update the adaptive-policy EMA. But that EMA is only consulted by `select_policy`, which `effective_policy` invokes *solely* under `ConflictPolicy::Adaptive`. The default policy is the fixed `SafeMerge`, so for `sharded_mvcc_disjoint_{8,16,32}writers` every parallel writer funnels through one global write lock per commit purely to bump a counter no decision reads — a textbook negative-scaling suspect that none of the gate / realign / malloc-pool / shard-routing digs had touched.

CANDIDATE (branch `cc-bhh0i-metrics`): gate that write on `effective == Adaptive || had_conflict` — elide only the hot conflict-free fixed-policy path; preserves Adaptive selection and all conflict/merge/abort observability (every `adaptive_*` test sets Adaptive first → unaffected by construction).

MEASURED NEUTRAL. First a methodology trap: the cross-worker `rch` A/B was confounded — the candidate built/ran on a slower worker and read a uniform ~1.6x "slower" across all three counts (a pure machine artifact; a change that only removes work cannot be 1.6x slower). Rebuilt BOTH bench binaries locally (identical toolchain/flags) and ran an INTERLEAVED same-machine A/B (alternating base/cand, 8 rounds, min-of-N — the robust estimator under swarm load):

| writers | base min / med (ms) | cand min / med (ms) | speedup min / med |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 8  | 2.46 / 3.57  | 2.15 / 3.99  | 1.14x / 0.89x |
| 16 | 6.90 / 11.07 | 5.31 / 11.28 | 1.30x / 0.98x |
| 32 | 16.85 / 21.93 | 15.24 / 24.43 | 1.11x / 0.90x |

CONTRADICTORY: min-of-N favors the candidate at all three counts, but the median favors the baseline at all three. A genuine win moves BOTH estimators the same way; a change that only deletes work cannot inflate variance. The split means the ns-scale lock-acquisition saving is buried under the ~4.4µs install cost + ~2x swarm-load jitter — there is NO robust, reproducible win. This extends IvoryBirch's "NOT lock-bound (commit locks are ns-scale, the op is µs-scale)" conclusion to this SPECIFIC global metrics lock: it is real per-commit overhead but immeasurable against the install. REVERTED (kept off main; candidate preserved on branch `cc-bhh0i-metrics`). The bd-bhh0i residual remains the per-version 4 KiB payload `Vec` allocation/copy (the owner's next layer) — NOT any lock. File touched (reverted, NOT landed): crates/ffs-mvcc/src/sharded.rs.

### 2026-06-27 ⭐PARALLEL PROFILE + REFUTE allocator lever: bd-bhh0i 8t+ collapse is 76% KERNEL (futex-park + version-store page-faults), NOT a glibc-arena artifact — jemalloc in the bench is MEASURED NEUTRAL-to-WORSE (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Every prior bd-bhh0i profile was single-thread (`commit_breakdown`). Ran the FIRST *parallel* perf profile (flat, `-F 1999`, `sharded_mvcc_disjoint_32writers`, current main incl. ivory's hash-index chains `78c40c32`). DSO split: **76% kernel, 16% userspace MVCC, 6.6% libc**. Kernel hotspots resolved against live kallsyms (`kptr_restrict=0`, Python addr→symbol map — the recorded data was kptr-masked): syscall entry/exit + AMD `srso_alias_return_thunk`/`srso_alias_safe_ret` SRSO mitigation (~9% combined — a per-syscall tax on this box), `clear_page_erms`+`memset` (fresh-page zeroing), `native_queued_spin_lock_slowpath` + `futex_wait_setup` (lock park/wake), `update_curr`/`update_load_avg`/`native_sched_clock` (scheduler churn from parking). So the 8t+ collapse is KERNEL-bound on syscalls from lock-parking + page-commit — the userspace MVCC logic is only 16% (the single-thread commit_breakdown could not surface this).

HYPOTHESIS (then tested): the `wal_throughput` bench is a separate binary with NO `#[global_allocator]`, so it measures **glibc malloc** — while production `ffs-cli` uses **jemalloc** (in-tree comment at crates/ffs-cli/src/main.rs: "~1.6x single-thread, ~2.2x parallel on alloc-heavy paths"). If the 76% kernel were glibc arena-lock + mmap/munmap churn, matching production's allocator would close it.

REFUTED. Added `#[global_allocator] = tikv_jemallocator::Jemalloc` to the bench (matching production), built locally, ran an interleaved same-machine A/B (6 rounds, min-of-N):

| writers | glibc min / med (ms) | jemalloc min / med (ms) | jem speedup min / med |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 8  | 2.21 / 2.30  | 2.27 / 2.53  | 0.97x / 0.91x |
| 16 | 5.53 / 6.85  | 5.81 / 6.91  | 0.95x / 0.99x |
| 32 | 14.13 / 16.72 | 15.85 / 17.54 | 0.89x / 0.95x |

jemalloc is NEUTRAL-to-SLIGHTLY-WORSE at every count (min AND median agree — a real signal, not jitter). So bd-bhh0i's parallel collapse is NOT an allocator artifact; a thread-caching allocator does not close it. The kernel time is FUTEX (shard-lock + publication-gate park/wake) plus GENUINE page-faults from the unbounded-growing version store — each commit commits fresh version bytes → `clear_page` that NO allocator avoids (it is real new memory, not freelist-recyclable arena churn). This is consistent with the campaign conclusion that bd-bhh0i's high-thread collapse has no clean lever in the current architecture: it is lock-park + memory-commit bound, and the realistic levers are structural (prune cadence to cap version-store growth, or finer publication ordering to cut futex park/wake), not allocator/lock-micro-tuning. REVERTED (bench jemalloc change kept on branch `cc-bhh0i-jemalloc-bench`, NOT landed — landing it would only make the bench measure production's allocator, which is ~neutral-to-worse on this micro-bench). Files touched (reverted, NOT landed): crates/ffs-mvcc/benches/wal_throughput.rs, crates/ffs-mvcc/Cargo.toml.

### 2026-06-27 ⭐REFRAME pz64v + BLOCKER: the warm-read residual is the KERNEL copy_to_user penalty (~14x, SRSO-amplified), NOT userspace copy bandwidth — and zero-copy is BLOCKED by forbid(unsafe) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

The pz64v bead (my own 2026-06-22 evidence) framed the ~2x read gap vs kernel-splice as "purely the per-byte copy memory-bandwidth tax of a forbid(unsafe) FS." A direct measurement REFUTES that framing. Single-thread, warm (page-cache-resident), this box (AMD w/ SRSO mitigations active — see the parallel profile above where `srso_alias_*` dominate):

| op (1 MiB warm) | time | rate |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `pread` into dst (the read path's copy) | 333 µs | **3.2 GB/s** |
| pure userspace `memcpy` of the same MiB | 23.6 µs | **44.4 GB/s** |
| `pread` 4 KiB (per-syscall fixed floor) | 1073 ns/syscall | — |

So the userspace copy is only **~7%** of the read; the kernel `copy_to_user` path is **~14x slower than userspace memcpy** (3.2 vs 44 GB/s). The residual is NOT memory bandwidth (memcpy is fast) — it is the **kernel copy + syscall path, heavily amplified by the CPU's speculation mitigations** (SRSO return-thunks tax every kernel entry/copy on this box; this is exactly what the bd-bhh0i parallel profile showed at ~9% syscall/SRSO). The production parallel-chunked read hits ~5.8 GB/s by overlapping many `copy_to_user` calls across 64 cores, so the realized end-to-end prize of zero-copy at scale is smaller than 14x but still large (5.8 GB/s → DRAM-bound tens of GB/s).

PRIZE = letting the consumer read page-cache pages directly (userspace ~44 GB/s) instead of paying kernel `copy_to_user` (~3.2 GB/s). BLOCKER (the decision is the owner's, not resolvable in a safe per-crate slot): capturing it needs **mmap** (`memmap2::Mmap::map` is an `unsafe fn` — the backing file can mutate under the mapping; there is no safe-Rust mmap, unlike `posix_fadvise` which `nix` wraps safely) or **io_uring registered buffers** (also `unsafe`). ffs-block is `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` at line 1, AND the read API copies by construction (`io_engine::IoOp::Read{buf}` → `IoCompletion::Read(Vec)`; `read_into(dst)` fills a caller buffer) — so even the scaffolded io_uring engine would not close it without a borrow-returning API change (high blast radius: every read caller + preserve-on-error contract + conformance byte-identity). NET: the read campaign's last ~2-14x is gated on an explicit architectural decision — carve a vetted `unsafe` zero-copy read module (mmap-backed `ByteDevice` or io_uring fixed buffers) with documented safety invariants + a borrow read API, or accept the kernel-copy penalty as the permanent safe-Rust floor. No code change this turn (measurement only; harness left in /tmp).

### 2026-06-27 ⭐MEASURE rename O(N²) on current main (bd-8ch29 / bd-wurxc): kernel PARITY at 2k → 2.94x at 20k; per-op cost LINEAR in dir size, memmove-dominated (NOT rayon) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Re-measured the rename gap on current main (post all landed htree work) with `ffs-cli rename-bench` (in-process create-then-rename N files in an ext4 root dir, no FUSE) against an apples-to-apples in-process kernel baseline (`os.rename` loop on real `/data` ext4 — NOT a shell `mv` loop, which is fork/exec-bound at ~500/s and grossly unfair to the kernel). Both warm, single-thread:

| N (dir entries) | frankenfs µs/op | kernel ext4 µs/op | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2,000  | 28.9 | 27.8 | **1.04x (parity)** |
| 5,000  | 41.6 | 29.7 | 1.40x |
| 10,000 | 57.7 | 31.2 | 1.85x |
| 20,000 | 90.3 | 30.7 | **2.94x** |

frankenfs per-op cost grows ~LINEARLY with directory size (28.9→90.3 µs/op as N: 2k→20k, ≈+3.4 µs per 1000 entries) = **O(N²) total**; the kernel is FLAT ~30 µs/op (htree O(log N)). So frankenfs rename is at kernel PARITY for small dirs and diverges only past ~2k entries. This UPDATES the bead's stale "~200x at 20k": the landed htree work cut it to ~2.9x, but the super-linearity remains.

PROFILE (`perf`, rename-bench N=20000, single-thread): DSO 72.7% ffs-cli / 16.2% libc / 10.7% kernel. Top self-time: **`__memmove_avx_unaligned_erms` = 14.8%** (the single largest cost) + a tight ffs-cli function cluster at ~0x3e3xxx (~15% summed = ONE hot routine) + `__memcmp_avx2` 0.7% (name compare) + `__memset` 0.5%. **NOTABLY ABSENT: any rayon steal/yield/epoch symbols** — so on the current single-thread CLI path the cost is NOT the "~30% rayon coordination" bd-wurxc reported (that was a parallel-fixture profile); it is **per-op data shuffling (`memmove`) that scales with dir size**, consistent with a dir-structure rewrite (htree leaf rebuild / in-block entry shifting) copying O(dir) bytes per rename. The exact hot function needs an unstripped (`release-perf`) build to symbolize — the shipped `release` profile strips, so addr2line/nm return `??`; the ~0x3e3xxx cluster + 14.8% `memmove` is the precise lever target. LEVER (owner, ffs-core, high blast radius): eliminate the per-op O(dir) `memmove` — incremental in-place entry insert/remove without rewriting the leaf/htree, and/or reclaim emptied leaves on removal so the dir stops bloating (bd-wurxc root cause). No code change this turn (measurement + profile only). Harness: `ffs-cli rename-bench <img> / --count N` vs an in-process `os.rename` kernel loop on real ext4.

### 2026-06-27 ⭐PINPOINT rename O(N²) hot function (unstripped `release-perf` profile): `ffs_dir::add_entry` 23% self, called O(N)×/rename via rebuild-on-bloat; lookups ARE htree O(log N) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Built `ffs-cli --profile release-perf` (unstripped — the shipped `release` strips, so addr2line returned `??` last entry) and re-profiled `rename-bench` N=20000. Resolved self-time:
- **`ffs_dir::add_entry` = 23.3%** — THE hot function (the 14.8% `memmove` of the stripped profile lives in/under it). add_entry is a *correct* O(entries-per-block) in-block slot scan, so 23%-and-growing-with-N proves it is **called O(N) times per rename**.
- Trailing: `parse_dir_block` 2.9%, `parse_extent_leaf` 1.6%, `validate_name` 1.2%, `write_entry` 1.1%, `lookup_name_with_scope` 1.0%, `ext4_preflight_dir_entry_insert` 0.8%. Under add_entry's subtree: `ShardedMvccStore::read_visible` + `resolve_version_bytes_cow_at_or_before` + `__memmove_avx` + jemalloc churn (`do_rallocx`/`extent_recycle`/`_rjem_je_*`) — the O(N) per-rename block reads each go through MVCC and COPY the 4 KiB version bytes.

DIAGNOSIS: `dx_hash_half_md4` + `parse_dx_entries` appear (0.27 / 0.36%), so LOOKUPS are htree O(log N) — the bd-gauub authoritative-descent guard works; lookup is NOT the gap. The gap is the ADD path. Over a rename burst the dir BLOATS: each add can split a full leaf (new leaf appended) while each remove leaves a gap that is **never reclaimed** (bd-wurxc); leaf count climbs until the single-level dx_root fills (~508 dx_entries) → `ext4_split_htree_leaf_and_add` declines (would need multi-level) → falls back to `ext4_rebuild_htree_dir`, which re-adds all N entries through `add_entry` = O(N)/rename = O(N²). PRECISE LEVER (owner, ffs-core htree maintenance, must stay e2fsck-clean): **reclaim emptied leaves on removal** so the dir never bloats and the dx_root never overflows to multi-level → the existing O(log N) split fast-path stays engaged and the full rebuild never fires. Secondary: give the rename loop a read-your-writes dir-block cache so the per-op block reads stop re-copying 4 KiB MVCC version bytes (`resolve_version_bytes_cow`). No code change this turn (function-level profiling; the fix is high-blast-radius htree maintenance, not a safe per-crate slot — the incremental-split fast path bd-gauub already exists and is correct; the missing piece is leaf reclamation, not the split).

### 2026-06-27 RULE-OUT "optimize the rebuild" + CONVERGENCE note: `ext4_rebuild_htree_dir` is already O(N); rename O(N²) is purely rebuild FIRING-FREQUENCY under churn → leaf-reclamation is the SOLE fix (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Read `ext4_rebuild_htree_dir` (ffs-core lib.rs:17708) to check whether the rebuild itself is quadratic (which would be a contained, per-crate-safe optimization). It is NOT: it collects all entries once (O(N) reads + `parse_dir_block`), rebuilds via `build_htree_directory_for_rebuild` (re-packs from the entry list into fresh leaves with ~50% loose_pack slack — not a quadratic `add_entry`-rescan loop), and writes back O(N) blocks. So the rebuild is correctly O(N) per call. This **rules out "optimize the rebuild" as a lever** and confirms the rename O(N²) is purely the rebuild's FIRING FREQUENCY: the loose_pack doubling schedule makes a *growing* directory O(N)-amortized, but rename CHURN (remove+add, no net growth) defeats it — the dir stays pinned at the dx_root overflow boundary so the full rebuild re-fires repeatedly. Therefore **leaf-reclamation-on-removal is the SOLE fix** (keep the dx_root from overflowing → the existing O(log N) split fast-path stays engaged → rebuild never fires); it is e2fsck-validated htree surgery (free emptied leaf block + remove its dx_entry + handle dx_node underflow), correctly NOT attempted in a 60-minute per-crate slot.

CONVERGENCE (campaign frontier as of this session): no stranded measured win exists in any `.scratch`/`.worktrees` worktree (all recent commits across the swarm are docs(perf) refutations/measurements), and every measured gap vs the kernel is now mapped to an architectural floor that exceeds a safe per-crate 60-minute slot: **(1) parallel metadata writes bd-bhh0i** — futex park/wake + version-store page-commit, owned-structural (IvoryBirch); allocator (jemalloc) and lock-micro levers measured neutral. **(2) warm reads pz64v** — SRSO-amplified kernel `copy_to_user` (~14x vs userspace memcpy), blocked by `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` (mmap/io_uring both require `unsafe`) + a copy-by-construction read API; needs an explicit unsafe-exception decision. **(3) rename O(N²) bd-8ch29/bd-wurxc** — htree leaf-reclamation-on-removal, e2fsck-validated surgery. The cheap-and-safe lever space is exhausted; remaining wins require a structural/policy decision or a focused multi-hour htree effort, not micro-tuning.

### 2026-06-27 ⭐CORRECT + DATA-CONFIRM the rename fix: it is a FULL-DX_ROOT → two-level dx promotion (kernel-matching), NOT leaf reclamation; my own measured curve pins the ~508-leaf threshold (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Read `ext4_split_htree_leaf_and_add` (ffs-core 17462) → `ffs_ondisk::split_htree_leaf`. The single-level split adds ONE dx_entry to the dx_root; it declines (→ Ok(None) → full rebuild) on "multi-level, **full dx_root**, no clean boundary, unparseable." The **full-dx_root** decline is the O(N²) source, and my own rename-bench curve PINS it: a 4 KiB dx_root holds ~508 dx_entries (≈508 leaves; ~85 entries/leaf ⇒ ~43k entries before the root fills *without* churn). At **N=2,000** the dir is ~24 leaves — even with churn-bloat it never nears 508 → split always succeeds, rebuild never fires → **PARITY (28.9 vs kernel 27.8 µs)**. At **N=20,000** the rename burst bloats the live leaf count *past 508 mid-run* (each add can split a leaf, removes never reclaim) → dx_root full → `split_htree_leaf` declines → full `ext4_rebuild_htree_dir` fires per op for the back half → **2.94x**. This parity-then-divergence is the signature of crossing the dx_root capacity, not of a per-op constant-factor gap.

CORRECTION to my prior two rename entries (which said "leaf reclamation"): the kernel baseline is **FLAT ~30 µs/op to N=40,000** — Linux ext4 dirs ALSO never shrink/reclaim, yet stay O(log N). The kernel achieves this by promoting the dx_root to a **second index level** (a dx_node split: allocate an interior dx_node, move the dx_root's dx_entries down, set `indirect_levels=1`, the root now indexes dx_nodes) — and then keeps splitting at the leaf level into whichever dx_node has room. It NEVER rebuilds. So the correct, kernel-matching fix is **implement the full-dx_root → two-level promotion (dx_node split) in `ffs_ondisk::split_htree_leaf`** so the fast path stops declining and the full rebuild never fires; **leaf reclamation is NOT required** (the kernel proves it). This is well-defined but intricate (interior dx_node layout, dx checksums, `indirect_levels`, e2fsck validity) — an ffs-ondisk + ffs-core focused effort with the existing `rename-bench` (vs in-process `os.rename`) as the win predicate, not a safe 60-minute slot. No code change this turn (the diagnosis is now complete and data-confirmed; this supersedes the leaf-reclamation direction).

### 2026-06-27 REFUTE the htree dense-pack lever: packing rebuild leaves to 75% (vs 50%) makes rename 0.86x SLOWER — split-frequency dominates dx_root-overflow; 50% slack is near-optimal; multi-level split is the ONLY fix (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Tested the one remaining SAFE, per-crate, correctness-neutral rename lever derived from the dx_root-threshold analysis: pack the rebuild's leaves denser (`build_htree_directory` `fill_limit` from `usable/2` → `usable*3/4`) so fewer leaves per N entries push the ~508-leaf dx_root-overflow threshold higher (~21k → ~32k entries) and keep rename on the O(log N) split fast path longer. Built `ffs-cli` with the change, A/B at N=20000 (same fixture/machine vs current-main 50%-pack binary):

| op (N=20,000, µs/op) | baseline (50% pack) | candidate (75% pack) | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| rename | 90.2 | **105.1** | **0.86x (LOSS)** |
| create | 33.2 | 32.7 | 1.02x (neutral) |
| create N=40,000 | 33.1 | 36.0 | 0.92x (slight loss) |

REFUTED, instructively: denser packing made rename WORSE, not better. A freshly-rebuilt 75%-full leaf has only ~21 slots of headroom (vs ~42 at 50%), so rename churn triggers a leaf SPLIT roughly twice as often — and the per-split O(block) `memmove` + dx_entry insert cost outweighs the reduced full-rebuild frequency from the higher overflow threshold. So the existing **50% loose_pack slack is near-optimal**: it balances split-frequency (wants more slack) against leaf-count/dx_root-overflow (wants less slack) — you cannot tune the packing to win rename. This **confirms the multi-level dx_node split (kernel-matching, prior entry) is the SOLE fix**: only eliminating the full rebuild entirely (not shifting when it fires) closes the gap. REVERTED (candidate kept on branch `cc-htree-densepack`, NOT landed). File touched (reverted): crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs.

### 2026-06-27 ⭐SHIPPED (small win): borrow leaf-entry names in `split_htree_leaf` — eliminates ~N allocs/free per htree split; rename 1.045x, create neutral, byte-identical (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

The dense-pack refutation (prior entry) proved leaf SPLIT frequency dominates rename/create churn, which makes the per-split cost the lever (the only safe per-crate one left — the rebuild-frequency fix needs multi-level dx surgery). The unstripped rename profile showed jemalloc churn (`do_rallocx`/`extent_recycle`/`_rjem_je_*`) under the split path. `split_htree_leaf` collected the full leaf's entries via `parse_dir_block`, which allocates an **owned `Vec<u8>` per entry** (~N names/leaf) — but those names only feed `dx_hash` + the borrowed `(ino, ft, &[u8])` pack refs, all within the function while `target_leaf` is live. SHIPPED: collect via `DirBlockIter` (borrows names from `target_leaf`) into `Vec<(u32,u32,u8,&[u8])>` instead of owned `Vec<u8>` — strictly fewer allocations, identical entries (the iterator already skips inode-0 + csum tail; a parse error still → rebuild fallback).

VALIDATED byte-identical: the 3 leaf-split correctness tests pass (`htree_leaf_split_keeps_dir_navigable_and_checksummed_bd_gauub` + invariants + declines-unsupported), conformance **100 passed / 0 failed / 2 ignored**. MEASURED (interleaved same-machine A/B, 8 rounds rename + 4 create, min-of-N, vs the current-main 50%-pack baseline binary): **rename 88.22 → 84.44 µs/op = 1.045x** (median also favors candidate ~1.01x; both estimators agree, candidate also lower-variance); **create 32.34 → 32.33 µs/op = 1.000x (neutral, no regression)**. A small but consistent, zero-risk efficiency win on the hot dir-split path (helps every leaf split = rename + create-into-large-dir); the structural ~2.9x rename gap still needs the multi-level dx split. File: crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs (`split_htree_leaf`).

### 2026-06-27 RULE-OUT delete gap + metadata-op decomposition: `delbench` is FLAT ~27 µs/op (≤ kernel parity); isolates the rename O(N²) entirely to the ADD-side rebuild (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Measured the previously-unmeasured `delbench` (delete = positive lookup + dir-entry removal + inode/bitmap free + MVCC commit) to check for a hidden gap: **N=5,000 → 26.2 µs/op, N=10,000 → 26.6, N=20,000 → 27.2 µs/op — FLAT** (≤ the kernel metadata-op baseline ~30 µs/op from the rename ORIG measurement). No gap; delete is O(log N) and healthy (no add → no leaf split → no rebuild). This completes the serial ext4 metadata-op picture and pins where the cost lives: **delete 27 µs < create 33 µs < rename 90 µs/op @ N=20k**. The ladder isolates the rename gap entirely to the **ADD-side rebuild-on-bloat**: delete (no add) is clean; create (add, but the dir GROWS so rebuilds amortize on the loose_pack doubling schedule) is near-parity; only rename CHURNS at fixed size (remove+add, no growth to amortize) so the dx_root-overflow rebuild re-fires → O(N²). Confirms the multi-level dx split is the one structural fix worth the focused effort, and that create/delete need no work. No code change (measurement only).

### 2026-06-27 ⭐⭐SHIPPED (big win): cache the per-group reserved-block set — mkdir 2.77x, all block-allocation ops (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

NEW gap found by measuring the unmeasured `mkdir-bench`/`rdbench`: **mkdir 234 µs/op and rmdir 200 µs/op — ~7-8x the analogous file create (33) / delete (27)** (flat, not O(N²)). mkdir allocates a dir DATA block per op (file create allocates only an inode → no block), so the cost is **block allocation**. Unstripped `perf` of mkdir-bench: **`core::slice::sort quicksort<u32>` = 42% (call-graph) + `ffs_alloc::reserved_blocks_in_group` 8.3% self + `alloc_blocks_persist` 5.2%**. `reserved_blocks_in_group` rebuilt + sort_unstable'd the group's reserved-metadata offsets — with **flex_bg** this pulls ~16 groups' worth of inode-table blocks (~thousands of u32s) — on EVERY block allocation, yet the set is a pure function of the fixed mkfs metadata layout, **invariant for the FS lifetime**.

SHIPPED: memoize it in a `OnceLock<Arc<[u32]>>` field on `GroupStats` (compute once per group, reuse; `OnceLock` is `Sync`+`Clone`+`Debug` and fills through `&self`, so the immutable-`&[GroupStats]` allocator callers populate it; first-writer-wins under concurrency). No caller signature changes (still returns `Vec<u32>` via `cached.to_vec()`).

VALIDATED: ffs-alloc reserved/alloc tests **13 passed / 0 failed** (incl. the `is_reserved` binary-search tests — the cached set is byte-identical to the recompute), conformance **100 passed / 0 failed / 2 ignored**. MEASURED (interleaved same-machine A/B, 3 rounds, vs current-main baseline binary): **mkdir 197.9 → 71.5 µs/op = 2.77x** (tight variance both sides, all rounds consistent). The lever is the per-allocation quicksort, so it speeds **every block-allocating op** — mkdir, file writes that grow, directory growth, fallocate, rmdir. File: crates/ffs-alloc/src/lib.rs (`reserved_blocks_in_group` + `GroupStats::reserved_cache`).

BREADTH CONFIRMATION (same landed commit `37cdf5f8`): re-ran `rdbench` (rmdir) on the cache binary vs the pre-cache baseline — **rmdir 200 → 53.7 µs/op = 3.8x** (N=5k 51.6, N=20k 53.7; now ~2x file-delete 27 µs ≈ kernel parity), so the dir-removal path also hit the per-alloc reserved-sort (via the block-free / group-desc persist). Net landed impact: **mkdir 2.77x + rmdir 3.8x**, not mkdir-only. The post-cache *residual* mkdir (~79 µs vs file-create 33) is the inherent extra work of a directory (dir-block alloc + write + `.`/`..` init + parent link-count + a block-bitmap read/write that file-create skips); IvoryBirch separately tried + REJECTED a reserved-Vec→`Arc` borrow follow-up as neutral (`30117f1a`), so the `to_vec` clone is not the residual and there is no further cheap lever there. METHOD GOTCHA: a re-profile showed the old 42% quicksort because the `release-perf` binary was STALE (an rch rebuild that no-op'd, mtime predated the landed commit) — always check the profiled binary's mtime against the commit before trusting a residual profile.

### 2026-06-27 RULE-OUT create gap + CONSOLIDATED serial ext4 metadata-op final state (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Profiled `create-bench` (the most common metadata op) for an invariant-recompute lever like the reserved-cache: NONE. create self-time = `ShardedMvccStore::commit` 5.4% (the bd-bhh0i per-op MVCC commit, owned) + `__memmove_avx` 8.3% (the version-store install copy = the bd-bhh0i 4 KiB payload copy, owned) + crc32c ~6.6% (metadata_csum stamping, correctness-required + HW-accelerated) + `parse_dir_block` 3.5%. No cheap lever — create is commit/crc/copy-bound and at kernel parity (~33 µs). Also confirmed `lookup_in_dir_block` (the htree leaf name-search in EVERY metadata op) is ALREADY lean — it `walk_dir_block_entries` in place and allocates only the one matching entry (not a Vec of all), so the split-borrow pattern is already applied there. And a sweep of `sort_unstable`/`sort_by` in prod code across ffs-{alloc,core,ondisk,inode,extent,btree} found NO other per-op INVARIANT-input sort (the remaining sorts are on per-op DATA — htree build, btrfs key order — not cacheable); `reserved_blocks_in_group` was the only one.

CONSOLIDATED serial ext4 metadata-op state (vs in-process `os.*` kernel baseline ~30 µs/op, N=20k, after this session's fixes): **delete 27 (parity) · create 33 (parity, commit/crc/copy-bound) · rmdir 53 (was 200, FIXED 3.8x) · mkdir 71-79 (was 234, FIXED 2.77x) · rename 90 (2.9x, UNFIXED)**. lookup = htree O(log N) authoritative, lean. The cheap-and-safe metadata-op lever space is now HARVESTED; the only remaining metadata gaps are (1) rename 2.9x = the add-side full-rebuild firing at the dx_root-overflow threshold, fix = two-level dx_node split in `ffs_ondisk::split_htree_leaf` (intricate, e2fsck-validated, not a 60-min slot) and (2) the per-op MVCC commit/version-copy cost shared by create/write/all = bd-bhh0i, IvoryBirch's owned lane. No code change (profiling + sweep only).

### 2026-06-27 ⭐⭐SHIPPED (big, universal win): htree leaf-search borrow — lookup 2.5x, helps EVERY metadata op (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Profiled the previously-unmeasured `lookup-bench` (random name resolves in a 20k-entry htree dir): frankenfs **5.8 µs/op**. Perf: **`parse_dir_block` = 21.6% self** + ~18% jemalloc churn (`sdallocx`/`malloc`/`ptr_array_flush`/`slab_alloc`) + memmove 4.8% — ~40% of every lookup. CORRECTION to my prior "lookup is lean" claim: the htree leaf NAME-SEARCH (`ffs_ondisk::htree_find_entry_inner`, the dominant resolver hit by EVERY metadata op's lookup) did `parse_dir_block(leaf).into_iter().find(name == target)` — materializing a `Vec<Ext4DirEntry>` with an owned name `Vec` per entry (~85 allocs/leaf) just to find ONE. (`lookup_in_dir_block` was already lean, but the htree path didn't use it.)

SHIPPED: replace it with the existing lean `lookup_in_dir_block` / `lookup_in_dir_block_casefold` (walk the leaf in place, allocate only the one matching entry; both validate the whole block identically → `IndexInvalid` accept/reject unchanged). VALIDATED: `htree_find` test passes, conformance **100 passed / 0 failed / 2 ignored** (lookups run in every conformance op). MEASURED (interleaved same-machine A/B, 3 rounds, vs the current-main cache binary): **lookup 5.40 → 2.14 µs/op = 2.52x** (tight variance, all rounds consistent). Because lookup is on the path of EVERY metadata op (create/rename/delete/mkdir/getattr/open), this is a broad win, not just `lookup-bench`. File: crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs (`htree_find_entry_inner`).

FOLLOW-UP SWEEP (harvest-complete check for the "materialize a Vec to extract one item" pattern): swept all prod `parse_dir_block` callers — the htree leaf-search (just fixed) was the ONLY clean drop-in. The two remaining prod ffs-ondisk callers (`Ext4ImageReader::read_dir` / `read_dir_block`) legitimately read ALL entries (tooling/image-reader API, not the hot FUSE path) — justified; the ffs-core linear-scan fallback (`lookup_name_with_scope`) only fires for non-htree dirs (small, rare).
