NEXT lookup residual assessed + DEFERRED: `parse_dx_entries` (the dx-index descent materializes a `Vec<Ext4DxEntry>` per lookup, was ~2.25%) is NOT a clean drop-in — `dx_find_leaf_idx` + the collision-chain frame advance hold `Vec<Ext4DxEntry>`, so eliminating the parse needs a raw-bytes dx accessor + a frame-representation change (no lean helper exists, unlike the leaf case), for a much smaller win; an mtime-keyed parsed-dx_root cache (like the existing `dir_name_index`) is the alternative — both are involved, not a 60-min clean drop-in. CONCLUSION: the clean drop-in metadata borrow/cache vein is HARVESTED this session (reserved-block cache `37cdf5f8` mkdir 2.77x + rmdir 3.8x; htree split-borrow `18e9f0ea` rename 1.045x; htree leaf-search borrow `d52eb62e` lookup 2.52x). Remaining metadata work is structural (rename two-level dx split) or owned (bd-bhh0i MVCC commit). No code change (sweep + assessment).

UPDATE 2026-06-27 (late): the deferred `parse_dx_entries` residual is now ACTIVELY IN PROGRESS by a peer (uncommitted `crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs` WIP introducing `dx_find_leaf_idx_in_data` + a raw-bytes `Ext4DxFrame<B>` replacing the per-descent `Vec<Ext4DxEntry>`) — exactly the raw-bytes dx accessor + frame-representation change I scoped above, so it is owned, not abandoned; I did not touch it. My three metadata wins are verified intact on origin/main (`37cdf5f8`, `18e9f0ea`, `d52eb62e`). For my lane this session is CONVERGED: the clean drop-in borrow/cache levers are harvested + landed, the next residual is peer-owned, and the remaining gaps are structural (rename dx split) / owned (bd-bhh0i). METHOD NOTE for future loops: this is a shared machine running many projects' agents and the `sbh` disk-defense daemon wipes `.rch-targets` build artifacts mid-session under disk pressure — budget a fresh per-crate build immediately before each measurement (a binary from an earlier turn will likely be gone) and do build→measure→commit in one tight sequence.

BTRFS-SIDE MAP (extending the harvest check beyond ext4): `btrfs.rs` `parse_leaf_items` (runs on every `walk_tree`, returns `(BtrfsHeader, Vec<BtrfsItem>)`) is the SAME materialize-all pattern, but — like `parse_dx_entries`, NOT like the ext4 leaf — it has NO lean drop-in helper: a btrfs key lookup binary-searches the sorted items, so eliminating the Vec needs a raw-bytes item/key accessor + reworking callers (involved), and the item structs are small (no per-item owned `Vec`, unlike `Ext4DirEntry`'s names) so the win is smaller than the ext4 leaf case. ALSO `crates/ffs-ondisk/src/btrfs.rs` + `benches/btrfs_leaf_parse.rs` have been peer WIP (dirty working tree) all session — btrfs is peer-territory. NET FRONTIER for the metadata/parse lane: every remaining accessible lever is peer-owned (`parse_dx_entries` ext4, `parse_leaf_items` btrfs) or structural (rename two-level dx split) or owned (bd-bhh0i MVCC commit); the clean drop-in borrow/cache wins are landed (3 this session). No code change.

NEXT ffs-alloc RESIDUAL (precise handoff, the second lever after the landed reserved-Vec cache `37cdf5f8`): `try_alloc_safe` still iterates the full reserved set per block allocation — `for &r in &reserved { bitmap_set_with_clear_undo(&mut bitmap, r, &mut rollback_clear_bits) }` — i.e. ~N (≈8000 with flex_bg) `bitmap_get` checks on EVERY block alloc to mark reserved bits before the free search. The cache removed the per-alloc SORT but not this O(N) marking loop (a provable ~10-15 µs / ~15% of the post-cache mkdir ~71 µs, on EVERY block-allocating op). FIX SKETCH: cache a reserved BITMASK (`OnceLock<Arc<[u64]>>` on `GroupStats`, same pattern as `reserved_cache`) and bulk-check per u64 word — `missing = mask[w] & !bitmap_word[w]`; skip the word when `missing == 0` (the common case: mkfs already marks metadata blocks), only set+undo the rare missing bits — turning O(8000) into O(words≈128). (A mask bit-test also makes `is_reserved` O(1) vs O(log N).) NOT DONE here because: (a) it is fiddly + correctness-critical (u64 words over a `&[u8]` bitmap; endianness; tail bits; the per-bit `rollback_clear_bits` undo must stay exact on the alloc-bitmap critical path — a bug = on-disk corruption), and (b) it needs a clean post-cache A/B that the `sbh` artifact-wipe currently constrains (best done env-gated: one build, toggle on/off). Deferred as a careful, measured follow-up, not a 60-min slot.

### 2026-06-28 ⭐SHIPPED (the above lever, simpler+safer variant): skip the per-alloc reserved-marking loop once a group is confirmed — mkdir 1.16x; + FIXED a pre-existing golden-test regression I had introduced (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Took the marking-loop lever above but via a SAFER mechanism than the bitmask bulk-check: the loop is a pure NO-OP once a group's on-disk bitmap already carries every reserved bit (the post-mkfs steady state — reserved metadata blocks are never freed; `free_blocks` validates not-reserved). So `GroupStats` gets a `reserved_confirmed: OnceLock<()>`; the first alloc whose marking loop sets nothing records the confirmation, and subsequent allocs skip the O(N≈8000) loop entirely. The post-find `is_reserved` check stays as a belt-and-suspenders guard, so correctness does not depend on the fast path. Env `FFS_ALLOC_FORCE_RESERVED_MARK` forces the old always-mark behaviour (escape hatch + A/B baseline). MEASURED (single-binary env A/B, 3 rounds, mkdir N=20000): FORCE(old) ~75.3 → SKIP(default) ~64.8 µs/op = **1.16x** (consistent, ~10 µs/op saved); helps EVERY block-allocating op. VALIDATED: ffs-alloc **213 passed / 0 failed**, conformance **100 / 0 / 2**.

ALSO FIXED a regression I had introduced: the earlier reserved-Vec cache (`37cdf5f8`) made `GroupStats`'s DERIVED `Debug` dump the populated cache, which silently broke the exact-string golden test `representative_allocator_diagnostics_exact_golden_contract` (it was RED on main — I had only run the `reserved` test filter + conformance then, not the full ffs-alloc suite). Replaced the derive with a manual `Debug` that renders the memoization caches (`reserved_cache`, `reserved_confirmed`) as a stable `OnceLock::new()` placeholder, so the diagnostic golden is now invariant under cache population/additions. File: crates/ffs-alloc/src/lib.rs. LESSON: run the FULL per-crate test suite (not a name-filtered subset) before landing a struct-field addition — a derived `Debug` golden will catch it, a filtered run won't.

NEXT (third ffs-alloc lever, deferred to a write-heavy bench): now that the marking-skip elides the per-alloc reserved-bit iteration, the cached reserved set's only remaining hot use is the single post-find `is_reserved` binary search — yet `reserved_blocks_in_group` still returns `cached.to_vec()`, a ~32 KiB clone (8000 u32) + malloc/free on EVERY block alloc (~0.8 µs ≈ 1% of post-fix mkdir 64.8 µs, but ~205 ms wasted per 1 GiB written = 256k block allocs — meaningful for write-heavy paths, marginal for mkdir). FIX: a `reserved_blocks_arc(...) -> Arc<[u32]>` accessor returning the cached `Arc` (refcount bump, no data copy); `try_alloc_safe` uses it (Arc derefs to `&[u32]` for `is_reserved`/`.len()`; the rare unconfirmed marking loop uses `.iter()`). Deferred here because the mkdir signal is ~1% (noise-prone) — it should be A/B'd on `write-bench` (multi-block allocs) where the clone-per-alloc dominates, which I could not set up cleanly this turn (needs a pre-extended file). Post-4-wins, the block-alloc path is otherwise MVCC-commit (bd-bhh0i, owned) + crc32c + bitmap/dir/inode write bound — inherent. No code change.

ALLOC-LANE CLOSEOUT (verification, no lever found): (1) the to_vec lever above has NO clean measurable workload — mkdir is ~1% (noise-prone), and `write-bench` *overwrites existing blocks* (whether it allocates new physical blocks is MVCC-COW-dependent, not a clean block-alloc-per-op isolation), so it stays a documented strictly-fewer-allocs follow-up, not a 60-min measured ship. (2) The INODE-alloc path (`try_alloc_inode_in_group_persist`, on every create/mkdir) is CLEAN — it has the same reserved-marking loop shape but over `reserved_inodes_in_group` which is only ~11 offsets (inodes have NO flex_bg cross-group reservation, unlike blocks' ~8000), and there is no per-alloc full-bitmap refresh — so the block-path lever class does NOT recur for inodes. NET: the ffs-alloc fast-path lever lane is fully harvested+verified this session (reserved-Vec cache `37cdf5f8` + reserved-marking skip `c5df77f0`); inode alloc is inherently cheap; the only residual is the marginal/unmeasurable to_vec clone. Remaining frontier unchanged: structural (rename two-level dx split) / owned (bd-bhh0i) / peer (`parse_dx_entries` peer-rejected-neutral, `parse_leaf_items` btrfs).

### 2026-06-28 CONSOLIDATED post-session metadata state (all 4 wins together) vs in-process `os.*` kernel ORIG (~30 µs/op flat), N=20000

Measured on a fresh all-wins `ffs-cli` build (single-thread, warm, ext4 root, per-op µs):

| op | now | start-of-session | cumulative | vs kernel ORIG |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **mkdir** | 60.5 | 234 | **3.9x faster** | ~2.0x |
| **rmdir** | 45.7 | 200 | **4.4x faster** | ~1.5x |
| create | 29.8 | 33 | 1.1x | ~parity |
| delete | 26.2 | 27 | — | **0.87x (beats kernel)** |
| rename | 86.0 | 90 | 1.05x | **2.9x (the one structural gap)** |

mkdir + rmdir, the two worst metadata ops at start (~7-8x kernel), are now ~1.5-2x via the reserved-block cache (`37cdf5f8`) + reserved-marking skip (`c5df77f0`) — a ~3.9-4.4x cumulative single-op win. create/delete are at/below kernel parity. lookup is 2.14 µs/op (`d52eb62e`, 2.5x). The ONLY remaining metadata gap is **rename 2.9x**: the lookup-borrow shaved ~4 µs (its two htree lookups) but the per-op cost is dominated by the full `ext4_rebuild_htree_dir` that fires at the dx_root-overflow threshold under churn — closing it needs the two-level dx_node split in `ffs_ondisk::split_htree_leaf` (intricate, e2fsck-validated, multi-hour, not a tight-turn slot), which is the single highest-value remaining ext4-metadata lever. No code change (consolidated measurement).

METADATA-SUBSYSTEM AUDIT COMPLETE (this turn): verified `statfs` is already optimized (bd-qsmav — writable fs sums cached in-memory `GroupStats`, O(group_count) field reads under one lock, NOT a per-call group-descriptor re-read/parse; only the RO fallback re-reads). Every ext4 metadata hot path is now accounted for: block-alloc (harvested, 2 wins), inode-alloc (verified inherently cheap — ~11 reserved inodes, no flex_bg), htree lookup + split (harvested, 2 wins), readdir (parses-all, justified), inode parse (allocation-free), statfs (cached). RECOMMENDATION for the loop: the ext4 metadata fast-path lever space is EXHAUSTED — the only remaining win (rename 2.9x via the two-level dx_node split) is a focused multi-hour, e2fsck-validated effort that does not fit a tight ship-every-turn cadence; it should be scheduled as a dedicated block, OR the current state (mkdir/rmdir now ~1.5-2x kernel from ~7-8x, create/delete/lookup at-or-beating parity) accepted as the near-term ext4 metadata endpoint. Other open fronts are owned (bd-bhh0i MVCC commit) or peer (btrfs).

RENAME TWO-LEVEL DX-SPLIT — IMPLEMENTATION ROADMAP (de-risking the dedicated effort; reusable primitives already exist in ffs-ondisk/ext4.rs): the building blocks are present — `write_dx_node` (interior-node block writer), `dx_node_entry_limit(block_size, csum)` (interior capacity), `write_dx_root`, and `build_htree_directory` already constructs multi-level via a `write_dx_node` + `interior_blocks` loop (the chunk-the-dx_entries pattern). So the work is NOT inventing the format, it is wiring incremental promotion into `split_htree_leaf` (which today returns `Err(DxRootFull)`/`MultiLevelIndex` → caller rebuilds). SCOPE (the two pieces are COUPLED — doing only the first just relocates the rebuild to the dx_node level): (1) **1→2-level promotion** when the dx_root is full: chunk the dx_root's dx_entries into ≤`dx_node_entry_limit` groups, `write_dx_node` each (the caller allocs+maps the new interior blocks), rebuild the dx_root as `indirect_levels=1` pointing at them, then split the target leaf and route its new dx_entry to the correct dx_node; (2) **2-level leaf-split**: on `MultiLevelIndex`, descend to the covering dx_node, add the split's dx_entry there, and split the dx_node itself (→ 3-level, bounded by `max_indirect_levels`) when it is full. RETURN-TYPE CHANGE: `HtreeLeafSplit` must carry the extra interior block(s) so `ext4_split_htree_leaf_and_add` allocates+maps them (mirrors how the leaf-split already returns `new_leaf`). VALIDATION: existing `htree_leaf_split_keeps_dir_navigable_and_checksummed` + a new ≥508-leaf fixture that forces promotion, e2fsck-clean, then `ffs-cli rename-bench` as the win predicate (target: per-op flat instead of climbing at the dx_root threshold → rename 2.9x→~parity). Estimate: a focused multi-hour session, NOT a tight-turn slot — but the format/primitive risk is now retired.

DEFINITIVE CONVERGENCE (both proven win-patterns verified harvested codebase-wide): (A) materialize-a-Vec-to-find-one — the in-place "find without materializing the rest" variant is already used at EVERY hot find site: `lookup_in_dir_block`/`_casefold` (dir-block name search), `find_ibody_xattr_by_name`/`find_xattr_block_value_by_name` (getxattr, bd-abu3z), and now the htree leaf search (`d52eb62e`, my last clean instance). (B) per-op invariant recompute — `reserved_blocks_in_group` sort (cached `37cdf5f8`) + marking loop (skipped `c5df77f0`) were the only flex_bg-sized ones; `reserved_inodes_in_group` is ~11 (inherent), `FsGeometry` is computed once and passed as `&FsGeometry` into the hot paths (not recomputed per-op), `statfs` sums cached `GroupStats` (bd-qsmav). NET: the two patterns that produced all four of my wins this session are now exhausted in the ext4 fast path — every remaining find is in-place and every per-op invariant is cached/passed. The ONLY remaining ext4-metadata lever is the rename two-level dx-split (roadmapped above); other fronts are owned (bd-bhh0i) or peer (btrfs). This is the audited endpoint of the ext4-metadata fast-path optimization lane.

RENAME DX-SPLIT — SCOPING GOTCHA (do NOT reuse build_htree_directory's chunking directly): I scoped the promotion against `build_htree_directory`'s interior-node construction (the `while children.len() > root_limit { chunk into node_limit groups; write_dx_node; parent = (chunk[0].hash, node_logical) }` loop). That loop packs each dx_node FULL (`node_limit` per chunk) because it is a one-time bottom-up BUILD. Reusing it verbatim for incremental split-time promotion is WRONG: packing ~508 dx_root entries into full nodes leaves ZERO room for the very split entry that triggered the promotion → the next add immediately re-overflows the dx_node. The incremental 1→2 promotion must chunk into ~HALF-full nodes (split the entries at a clean hash boundary into 2 dx_nodes of ~254, mirroring `choose_htree_leaf_split`'s boundary logic), which is also why piece (2) — splitting a full dx_node on later adds — is unavoidable. So the dedicated effort: write a slack-aware `promote_dx_root_to_two_level` (hash-boundary split into 2 half-full dx_nodes, NOT the full-pack build loop) + the dx_node-split path; reuse `write_dx_node`/`write_dx_root`/`dx_node_entry_limit` for the byte layout only. Primitive AND chunking-strategy risk now both retired.

READ-PATH ALSO VERIFIED OPTIMIZED + CONCRETE BLOCKER: checked `resolve_extent` (every file read's logical→physical map) — already a lock-free hot-extent fast path (`ext4_hot_extents` arc-swap, zero-lock in-memory for the hot single-file stream) → `ExtentCache` shard → `partition_point` binary search; indirect-mapped files use `resolve_indirect_block`'s own chain cache. So the READ fast path joins the metadata fast path as exhaustively optimized. **CONCRETE BLOCKER (process/scope, not a code primitive):** across this session I have audited every accessible ext4 hot path — block-alloc, inode-alloc, htree lookup+split, readdir, inode parse, statfs, getxattr, geometry, extent resolution — and they are all either optimized (4 wins shipped + many pre-existing) or inherent. There is NO tight-turn-shippable lever left in my accessible lane (ext4 read + metadata fast paths). The single remaining win, rename 2.9x via the two-level dx-split, is a multi-hour focused implementation (roadmapped + primitive-scoped + chunking-gotcha-identified above = near-implementation-ready) that is structurally incompatible with a "ship a measured win every tight turn" cadence (it ships nothing until complete, and completing it in one turn is the very "giant turn" the churn guard forbids). **NEED:** either (a) a dedicated uninterrupted multi-hour block allocated to the rename dx-split, or (b) reassignment of this agent to a different product/subsystem with un-harvested fast-path levers. Continuing to fire tight metadata turns at this lane will only produce documentation, not wins.

### 2026-06-28 ⭐STARTED the rename dx-split — landed piece (1): `promote_dx_root_to_two_level` helper + routing unit test, ffs-ondisk 667/0 (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Rather than defer the multi-hour rename fix indefinitely, landed its first cleanly-separable, independently-testable piece as a tested building block (pure function, not yet wired — so it is correct-by-test and conformance-inert until the caller wiring lands). `promote_dx_root_to_two_level(dx_root_block, block_size, csum, large_dir, node_a_logical, node_b_logical, csum_seed, dir_ino, gen) -> Option<(new_root, node_a, node_b)>` takes a FULL single-level dx_root and splits its leaf-pointer entries at a clean hash boundary (`choose_htree_leaf_split`) into TWO ~half-full interior dx_nodes (`write_dx_node`), rebuilds the dx_root as `indirect_levels=1` (`write_dx_root`, preserving the `.`/`..` header at 0x00..0x18), and checksum-stamps all three blocks. UNIT TEST `promote_dx_root_to_two_level_routes_every_entry` is the read-half ORACLE: builds a 40-entry single-level root, promotes, and asserts every original leaf is reachable via the real `dx_find_leaf_idx` descent (root→dx_node→leaf) AND both dx_nodes are under-full (room for the triggering leaf-split). PASSES; full ffs-ondisk suite **667 + 21 + 21 / 0 failed** (purely additive, no regression). REMAINING for the rename win: piece (2) dx_node-split on later adds (→3-level), and wiring into `split_htree_leaf`'s `DxRootFull` arm + `ext4_split_htree_leaf_and_add` (alloc+map the two new logical blocks via the `HtreeLeafSplit` return) + `rename-bench` measurement. The 1→2 promotion algorithm + byte layout are now CODE, tested, on main — not just a roadmap. File: crates/ffs-ondisk/src/ext4.rs.

### 2026-06-28 ❌REFUTED (measured per-crate): reserved-set `to_vec`→`Arc` borrow in `try_alloc_safe` — `+3.5%` SLOWER on `batch_alloc/single_20x1`

Also FIXED a separate regression first: `c5df77f0` (reserved_confirmed field) broke `cargo bench -p ffs-alloc` — the `GroupStats` literals in benches/{batch_alloc,bitmap_ops,group_order}.rs (separate compile targets) lacked the new field (E0063). Added it (`c4f325c0`); benches compile again (baseline `single_20x1` 27.8 µs / ~1391 ns per alloc, `batch_20` ~121 ns/alloc). Same field-addition-misses-separate-targets class as the earlier golden-test break — LESSON: after adding a `GroupStats`/struct field, `cargo build -p <crate> --all-targets` (tests AND benches AND examples), not just a filtered test run.

Then implemented + MEASURED the long-deferred `to_vec`→`Arc` lever: new `reserved_blocks_arc` returning the cached `Arc<[u32]>` (refcount bump, no clone), `try_alloc_safe` borrows it as `&[u32]`. Criterion A/B on `single_20x1` (the `alloc_blocks_persist` path): **change +0.36% / +3.49% / +6.41% (p=0.03) — a real REGRESSION, not a win.** ROOT CAUSE: `batch_alloc` rebuilds fresh `GroupStats` per iteration, so `reserved_cache` is COLD every iter; the `Arc` path's cold branch does strictly MORE work than the old `to_vec` (it calls `reserved_blocks_in_group` to populate the cache, discards that Vec, then re-reads + clones the published Arc). The lever's only benefit is on the WARM steady state (production: thousands of warm allocs after first-touch elide the `to_vec`) — which a cold-per-iter microbench STRUCTURALLY cannot show, while it DOES pay the heavier cold path. Net on the only available per-crate bench: refuted. REVERTED (`git stash`, recoverable). CONCLUSION: the to_vec clone is a genuine-but-warm-only, ~1%-on-mkdir cost with no clean benchable win and a cold-path downside — abandon it as a lever (the reserved-cache `37cdf5f8` + marking-skip `c5df77f0` already captured the real allocation wins). LESSON: a warm-cache optimization measured on a cold-cache microbench will read as a regression — match the bench's cache state to the path you're optimizing, or the number lies.

PER-CRATE-BENCH AUDIT CLOSED (last benchable paths verified already-optimized): (1) **casefold lookup** — `lookup_in_dir_block_casefold` folds the target key ONCE then compares each entry via `casefold_key_eq`, which is ALLOCATION-FREE for ASCII names (`eq_ignore_ascii_case`, the common case); only non-ASCII names materialise a key. `ext4_casefold_key`'s own `Vec` is inherent (it returns owned bytes) and the hot lookup avoids it. (2) **bitmap ops** (`bitmap_ops` bench) — already carry A/B-proven optimized variants in-tree: `count_free` succinct O(1), `find_free` succinct O(log n), `bitmap_owned_move` move-into-inner (no copy), succinct `direct_word_scan`. Both harvested. With casefold + bitmap-ops optimized, the benchable ext4/alloc surface (`batch_alloc`, `bitmap_ops`, `group_order`, `dir_lookup`, `xattr_lookup`, `casefold_key`) holds no un-harvested per-crate lever; the to_vec lever above was the last candidate and it refuted. The ONLY remaining ext4 win is rename via the dx-split (piece 1 `promote_dx_root_to_two_level` landed+tested `f3966190`; the 2-level-split + `split_htree_leaf` wiring + `rename-bench` measurement remain — a focused multi-hour effort, the documented path forward).

### 2026-06-28 ⭐⭐BOLD-VERIFY end-to-end: WIRING DxRootFull→`promote_dx_root_to_two_level` REGRESSES (crashes at create #71788) + REFUTES the "rename O(N²) = dx_root overflow→rebuild" hypothesis for the practical range (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

End-to-end measured the rename dx-split (my piece-1 helper `promote_dx_root_to_two_level` wired into `ext4_split_htree_leaf_and_add`'s `DxRootFull` arm, delegating the leaf split to IvoryBirch's piece-2 `ext4_split_htree_dx_node_leaf_and_add`) vs current main, `ffs-cli rename-bench /` on fresh `mke2fs` ext4 (4096 block, `^64bit`), release-perf, single-thread. Built BOTH baseline (clean `origin/main` = piece-2 only) and candidate (my wiring) locally; A/B:

| N | base µs/op | cand µs/op | ratio | root dir structure (debugfs `htree_dump`) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2,000 | 20.8 | 19.3 | 1.08x | single-level |
| 20,000 | 60.7 | 54.9 | 1.11x | single-level (290 entries < 507 limit) |
| 40,000 | 112.2 | 103.2 | 1.09x | single-level (290 entries) |
| 80,000 | 260.4 | **CRASH @ create #71788** | — | baseline → two-level (via rebuild) |

TWO decisive findings:

**(1) REFUTE the long-standing rename hypothesis for N ≤ 40,000.** `debugfs htree_dump /` proves the root dir is `Indirect levels: 0` (single-level) with only **290 entries (limit 507)** at N=40,000 — so the dx_root NEVER fills and `DxRootFull`/the full rebuild NEVER fires in this range. With short names (`rb_00012345`, ~138 entries/leaf) the 507-leaf dx_root capacity is not reached until ≈70,000 entries. Therefore the measured O(N²)-shaped divergence (20.8→60.7→112 µs/op over 2k→20k→40k) is **NOT the htree rebuild** (prior entries, incl. my own 2026-06-27 "DATA-CONFIRM the ~508-leaf threshold at N=20000", were WRONG — the threshold is ~70k for these names, debugfs-confirmed). The real per-op cost that grows with N in this range is the single-level split fast-path + MVCC version-store growth (each rename's O(log N) block reads copy 4 KiB MVCC version bytes via `resolve_version_bytes_cow`, and the version store grows with cumulative writes). **The practical rename gap is not a dx-split problem.**

**(2) REGRESSION — the DxRootFull wiring crashes where baseline survives.** At N=80,000 baseline completes (260 µs/op) by crossing 507 leaves and going two-level **via `ext4_rebuild_htree_dir`** (`build_htree_directory_for_rebuild` emits a two-level index that the read-side full-enumeration `htree_leaf_logical_blocks` CAN parse). My candidate instead `promote`s to two-level at the threshold, then a later split declines → falls to `ext4_rebuild_htree_dir` → its leaf enumeration `htree_leaf_logical_blocks(...)` returns `None` → `UnsupportedFeature("htree rebuild for dir inode 2 with 1 indirect level(s): DX index unreadable")` (lib.rs:18058-18068). ROOT CAUSE: **`promote_dx_root_to_two_level` (piece-1) produces a two-level layout navigable by `dx_find_leaf_idx` (its only unit-test oracle, `promote_dx_root_to_two_level_routes_every_entry`) but NOT by the full-enumeration `htree_leaf_logical_blocks`** — a byte-level dx_node/dx_root layout discrepancy vs what the rebuild emits. Piece-1 was validated against the point-lookup descent, never against the enumerate-all-leaves descent that the rebuild fallback uses. **NOT shippable** — REFUTED/REVERTED (`git stash` `cc-dxrootfull-wiring-WIP`, recoverable; main tree clean; candidate worktree removed).

CONCLUSION + path forward: (a) the landed piece-1 helper must stay UNWIRED until its output is made byte-identical-readable by `htree_leaf_logical_blocks` (add a piece-1 unit test that asserts `htree_leaf_logical_blocks` enumerates every leaf of a promoted root, matching the rebuild's two-level output); (b) even when fixed, the dx-split only matters for huge dirs (>≈70k entries) — the practical rename gap (2–3.7x at N≤40k, single-level) needs a DIFFERENT lever (per-op MVCC version-bytes copy / single-level split cost), not the dx-split. METHOD: `debugfs htree_dump` is the authoritative structural oracle — it caught that the dir stays single-level long past where the per-crate overflow microbench (`htree_dx_node_leaf_overflow_8000x1k`, which CONSTRUCTS a two-level dir) shows 100x; a microbench that pre-builds the overflow state cannot tell you whether the real workload ever reaches it. Files (reverted, not landed): crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs (`ext4_promote_dx_root_and_split` + `ext4_map_new_dir_block` + DxRootFull arm).

### 2026-06-28 ⭐⭐⭐SHIPPED (the REAL rename O(N²) fix): preflight checks only the hash-target leaf, not all dir blocks — rename 5.57x at N=40k, per-op now FLAT (beats kernel) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

After REFUTING the dx-split hypothesis (prior entry: dir stays single-level <507 leaves through N=40k, debugfs-confirmed), profiled baseline `rename-bench` N=40000 with `perf` (release-perf, unstripped): self-time **`ffs_dir::add_entry` 33.6% + `__memmove/__memcpy` 23.3% (largely the per-block MVCC `read_block_vec` 4 KiB copy) ≈ 57% combined**; MVCC version-chain (`resolve_version_bytes_cow` 1.0%, `read_visible` 1.8%) was NOT the cost. ROOT CAUSE found in `OpenFs::ext4_preflight_dir_entry_insert` (ffs-core, called before every rename insert for atomicity): it **dry-ran `add_entry` over EVERY directory block** (`for ext in &extents { for block in ... { read_block_vec + add_entry } }`) and **discarded the per-block result** — O(dir_blocks) per insert = **O(N²) over a rename burst**. That single full-directory scan per op IS the rename gap (not the htree rebuild, not the dx-split).

FIX (per-crate ffs-core, mirrors `ext4_htree_remove_dir_entry`'s descent): for an htree non-casefold dir, dry-run `add_entry` ONLY on the block the real insert will touch — the hash-target leaf via `ffs_ondisk::htree_target_leaf_block` — instead of all N blocks. Preserves the atomicity guarantee (a corrupt/too-full target leaf is still caught BEFORE the source entry is removed) + the name-too-long empty-block dry-run + the casefold gate, at O(1). Casefold htree dirs and linear dirs keep the full scan.

MEASURED A/B (`ffs-cli rename-bench /`, fresh `mke2fs` ext4 4096/^64bit, release-perf, single-thread; baseline = current `origin/main`):

| N | baseline µs/op | candidate µs/op | speedup |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2,000 | 20.8 | 14.6 | 1.42x |
| 20,000 | 61.3 | 18.8 | **3.26x** |
| 40,000 | 108.5 | 19.5 | **5.57x** |

The candidate per-op is now **FLAT (~14.6→18.8→19.5 µs/op)** = O(N) total (was superlinear) and at ~19.5 µs/op **BEATS the kernel's flat ~30 µs/op rename** (vs ext4-kernel: was ~3.6x slower at N=40k → now ~1.5x FASTER). Correctness: the change writes nothing (read-only preflight), so the resulting image is structurally identical to baseline — `e2fsck -fn` reports the IDENTICAL pre-existing superblock-counter staleness on both baseline and candidate N=40k images (free inodes 200052/160052, free blocks 756140/755853, "12/200064 files, 30292/786432 blocks" — byte-identical output, NOT introduced here), and all 40000 renames apply correctly (debugfs: 40000 `rn_*`, 0 `rb_*`). File: crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs (`ext4_preflight_dir_entry_insert`).

### 2026-06-28 CONVERGENCE: full single-thread ext4 metadata-op N-curve sweep — after the rename preflight fix, ALL ops are flat/O(N); remaining gaps are constant-factor (mkdir/rmdir) or the contested parallel-write structural gap (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

After shipping the rename preflight fix (`51294142`), swept every `ffs-cli` metadata bench at N=2000 vs N=40000 (fresh `mke2fs` ext4 4096/^64bit, release-perf, single-thread) to find the next hidden O(N²). Result — every op is now FLAT (per-op constant in N = O(N) total), no remaining O(N²):

| op | µs/op @2k | µs/op @40k | vs ext4-kernel | shape |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| create | 24.3 | 25.7 | ~parity | flat |
| rename | 14.6 | 19.5 | **~1.5x FASTER** (post-fix) | flat |
| delete (unlink) | 20.0 | 27.8 | ~parity | flat (htree O(1) removal already in place, lib.rs:18573) |
| rmdir | 39.1 | 41.2 | ~1.4x slower | flat |
| mkdir | 53.7 | 47.5 | ~1.7x slower | flat |

The two residual single-thread gaps (mkdir ~1.7x, rmdir ~1.4x) are FLAT constant-factor, and `perf` shows BROAD profiles with no dominant hotspot (mkdir N=40k: memmove 10.6% = MVCC `read_block_vec` 4 KiB block copies, MVCC `commit` 5.5%, crc32c ~5.7%, jemalloc churn ~6%, kernel syscalls ~10%, `ext4_add_dir_entry` only 2.6%) — i.e. the inherent cost of a dir-creating op's two inode commits + dir-block init + checksums, not a single elidable lever. The recurring cross-cutting cost across EVERY metadata profile is the MVCC full-4 KiB-block copy on each `read_block_vec`/`read_visible` — eliminating it (borrow version bytes instead of copy) is the "payload-reference storage" structural change already scoped under bd-bhh0i (parallel writes, 8.32x — the biggest remaining gap), which is `forbid(unsafe)`-constrained and correctness-critical. CONCLUSION: the clean per-crate single-thread metadata levers are harvested; the rename O(N²) (preflight full-dir scan) was the last one. Remaining wins require the structural MVCC zero-copy/commit-batching work (bd-bhh0i) or the blocked zero-copy read path (pz64v), not micro-tuning.

### 2026-06-28 ⭐follow-up: extend the rename preflight target-leaf fast path to CASEFOLD htree dirs — closes the same O(N²) for case-insensitive directories (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

The rename preflight fix (`51294142`) routed only verbatim (non-casefold) htree dirs through the O(1) hash-target-leaf check; casefold htree dirs still fell to the O(N) full-block scan = O(N²) rename. Extended `ext4_preflight_dir_entry_insert` to also fast-path casefold htree dirs via `ffs_ondisk::htree_target_leaf_block_casefold` (the folded-name descent), mirroring the already-conformance-tested casefold branch the unlink/delete path uses (lib.rs ~18589). Safe because the function's top-of-body gate already rejects non-ASCII casefold names, so the ASCII fold is byte-exact to the kernel here. Verbatim path unchanged; linear dirs keep the full scan. Pure preflight (read-only dry-run) → no on-disk behavior change. Conformance GREEN. File: crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs.

### 2026-06-28 ⭐btrfs single-thread metadata N-curve sweep REFUTES the stale "btrfs metadata ~3.5-4x behind" gap — it is FLAT / at-parity (the 3.5-4x was a PARALLEL measure); mkdir is the lone ~1.7x outlier on BOTH fs = shared MVCC-commit cost (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Following the ext4 metadata convergence, ran the same N-curve sweep on a btrfs image fixture (`ffs-cli` create/rename/mkdir/delbench, release-perf, single-thread) to test whether the long-cited "btrfs metadata ~3.5-4x behind kernel" gap is a real single-thread lever:

| op | btrfs µs/op @2k | btrfs µs/op @~20k | shape | (cf ext4) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| create | 22.6 | 25.1 | flat | ~same as ext4 25.7 |
| rename | 13.3 | 17.5 | flat | ~same as ext4 19.5 |
| delete | 23.2 | 23.3 | flat | ~same as ext4 27.8 |
| mkdir | 50.2 | 53.9 (@10k) | flat | ~same as ext4 47.5 |

REFUTED for single-thread: btrfs metadata ops are FLAT (O(N) total) and within noise of ext4 — there is NO 3.5-4x single-thread btrfs metadata gap. That figure (IvoryBirch's bd-bhh0i notes) is a PARALLEL (multi-writer) measurement; the single-thread btrfs metadata path is already at ext4-parity. The only residual single-thread metadata gap on EITHER filesystem is **mkdir (~50-54 µs/op, ~1.7x kernel, identical on ext4 AND btrfs)** — because it is identical across both fs it is a SHARED `ffs-core` cost, not an fs-specific one: a `perf` profile is broad (MVCC `commit` + `read_block_vec` 4 KiB copies + crc32c + jemalloc, no single hotspot), and the structural driver is mkdir's second inode commit + the post-`create_inode` block-device-adapter re-acquisition (MVCC snapshot coherence, lib.rs:16715) — i.e. the same MVCC-commit/version-store cost that bounds bd-bhh0i parallel writes, NOT an isolated elidable lever. CONCLUSION: single-thread metadata is harvested on both ext4 and btrfs; the remaining real gap is the structural MVCC commit/version-store cost (bd-bhh0i, parallel + the mkdir constant factor), which needs the payload-reference/commit-batching work, not micro-tuning. No code change (measurement + refutation).

### 2026-06-28 ⭐⭐NEW BIGGEST SINGLE-THREAD GAP FOUND: single-file SEQUENTIAL write is O(N²) — each overwrite invalidates the write-extent snapshot → full parallel `collect_extents` re-walk of a growing extent tree (rayon-coordination-dominated). Lever target identified, structural fix deferred (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Swept the one un-measured metadata-adjacent path — single-file writes (`ffs-cli write-bench <img> /bigfile`, 4096 B blocks, release-perf, single-thread, fresh `mke2fs -d` ext4 with a 64 MiB file). MEASURED sequential-write per-op vs N:

| N (4 KiB writes) | µs/op | per-op growth |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 2,000 | 25.1 | — |
| 10,000 | 82.0 | 3.3x |
| 40,000 | 340.6 | 13.6x (for 20x N) |

Per-op grows ~linearly in N ⇒ **O(N²) total** — the biggest single-thread gap found this session (worse than mkdir; ext4 kernel `pwrite` is flat ~few µs). Tell-tale inversion: RANDOM writes are FASTER than sequential at the same N (53 vs 82 µs/op @10k) because random offsets re-hit already-touched blocks (less distinct extent-tree growth), while sequential always extends into fresh blocks.

ROOT CAUSE (perf `--call-graph dwarf`, N=20000): self-time is **rayon coordination — `crossbeam_deque::Stealer::steal` 30.4% + `crossbeam_epoch::try_advance` 7.1% + `rayon wait_until_cold` 5.4% ≈ 43%** — all under `OpenFs::collect_extents_recursive::{closure#2}` (the parallel extent-tree block read). `ext4_write_extents_with_scope` (lib.rs:11635) caches the collected extent list in `ext4_write_extent_snapshot` keyed by `extent_cache_namespace`, but **each write mutates the inode's extent tree (COW block remap), which invalidates that snapshot → the next write re-collects the WHOLE extent list in parallel**. As the tree fragments, E grows ~linearly, so the per-write `collect_extents` is O(E) and the run is O(N²); at the small-to-moderate E of this bench the rayon spawn/steal overhead per call dominates each collect.

LEVER TARGET (structural, write-path/COW — owner area, not a safe 60-min per-crate slot, esp. with agent-mail down): the write path should NOT re-collect the entire extent tree per write. Options: (1) incrementally update the cached extent snapshot on a single-block COW remap instead of invalidating+rebuilding it; (2) resolve only the target block's mapping via the sorted `partition_point` single-extent lookup the read path already uses (`collect_extents_from_with_scope`, bd-yd3a0), never materializing the whole tree; (3) avoid per-overwrite extent remapping (version block content in the MVCC store without changing the physical mapping). Secondary: gate `collect_extents_recursive`'s `par_iter` with `with_min_len` so small extent counts go serial (removes the ~43% rayon overhead on the common small-E call). Distinct from bd-bhh0i (parallel metadata writes) — this is a SINGLE-THREAD O(N²) in the data-write commit path. No code change this turn (measurement + profile + root-cause; the fix is structural COW/extent-cache work).

### 2026-06-28 ⚠️CORRECTION to the prior "sequential write O(N²)" entry: pure OVERWRITE of a mapped file is FLAT (~4-5 µs/op, kernel parity) — the O(N²) was a SPARSE-FIXTURE artifact; it is the sequential ALLOCATION (hole-fill / new-file) path, not overwrite (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

The prior entry measured `write-bench` on a 64 MiB file seeded from `/dev/zero` — which `mke2fs -d` stores SPARSE (all-zero blocks → holes). So every "write" was actually a hole-FILL = a fresh block allocation, not an overwrite. Re-ran on a `/dev/urandom`-seeded file (fully mapped: `dump_extents` shows a single 16384-block written extent, no holes). Sequential OVERWRITE of the mapped file:

| N (4 KiB overwrites) | µs/op |
| --- | --- |
| 2,000 | 4.5 |
| 10,000 | 5.2 |
| 40,000 | 3.0 |

FLAT and at/below kernel `pwrite` — **there is NO O(N²) in the overwrite path**; the prior headline was wrong (classic sparse-fixture artifact, cf bd-xmh5g.409). What IS O(N²) is the sequential ALLOCATION path (filling holes / writing a brand-new file block-by-block): each allocating write does `allocate_extent` → `invalidate_ext4_write_extent_snapshot` (lib.rs ~20125) → the next write re-collects the whole extent list (`collect_extents_recursive`, rayon), and because `ffs_btree::insert` is deliberately non-coalescing (lib.rs ~20018) a sequential run becomes E=O(blocks) distinct extents → O(E) re-collect per write → O(N²). This still matters for the tar-extract / file-copy / new-file-write workload (the kernel coalesces the contiguous run into one extent + delayed allocation → flat), but it is the ALLOCATION path, not overwrite, and is narrower than the prior entry claimed. LEVER (write-path/COW, structural — deferred): coalesce contiguous extents on the write allocation path so E stays O(1) for a sequential run (then the per-write re-collect is cheap), and/or incrementally update the cached snapshot instead of invalidate+rebuild. METHOD NOTE: always seed write fixtures with /dev/urandom (non-compressible → mke2fs stores it fully-mapped); a /dev/zero seed is stored sparse and turns an overwrite bench into an allocation bench.

### 2026-06-28 SCOPE the sequential-allocation O(N²): severity is governed by write GRANULARITY — fine-grained 4 KiB alloc is 45.5x slower than 64 KiB for identical bytes; large-chunk writers (tar/cp) are fast (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Quantified the (corrected) sequential-ALLOCATION O(N²) by write size, on a sparse (all-holes `truncate`-seeded) file so every write allocates. Same 40 MiB total:

| write size | writes (= extents, non-coalescing insert) | total | per-40MiB |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 4 KiB | 10240 | 774,808 µs | 1x (baseline) |
| 64 KiB | 640 | 17,018 µs | **45.5x faster** |

The cost scales ~(bytes / write_size)² because `ffs_btree::insert` is non-coalescing (each allocating `write()` adds one extent) and each allocating write re-collects the whole extent list (`ext4_write_extents_with_scope` snapshot is invalidated per `allocate_extent`, lib.rs:20124). So: realistic large-chunk writers (tar/cp/git use 64 KiB-512 KiB buffers) hit few extents → ~2.4 GB/s, FINE; only FINE-GRAINED 4 KiB allocation (sparse random fill, some DB page writers) is O(N²)-dominated. This scopes the lever's priority: the structural fix (coalesce physically-contiguous extents on the write-allocation path so a sequential run stays O(1) extents → the per-write re-collect is cheap) chiefly benefits the 4 KiB-allocation workload; the common large-chunk path is already near-kernel. Recorded to right-size the gap (the headline "sequential write O(N²)" two entries up was both mis-attributed (overwrite is flat) AND, for allocation, narrower than it sounded). No code change (measurement/scoping).

### 2026-06-28 RULE-OUT rayon-tuning for the 4KiB-alloc O(N²) + pin the SOLE fix = extent COALESCING (ffs_btree::insert is non-coalescing by design; collapse_range/insert_range/fallocate are well-tested → a coalescing variant is verifiable) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Closing out the fine-grained-allocation O(N²) (45.5x vs 64 KiB, prior entry): checked whether the ~43% rayon overhead in its profile is a fixable over-subscription. It is NOT — `collect_extents_recursive` (ffs-core lib.rs:11762) ALREADY gates the child-fetch par_iter (`survivors.len() < rayon::current_num_threads()` OR `rayon::current_thread_index().is_some()` ⇒ serial; the comment notes this already fixed a "create-heavy ~90% rayon overhead"). So the rayon cost in the 4 KiB-alloc case is the LEGITIMATE parallel read of a LARGE fragmented extent tree (E grows because each non-coalesced 4 KiB allocation adds an extent) — not a tunable artifact. This rules out the rayon-tuning red herring (cf the bd-29an3 with_min_len phantom) by code-confirmation.

SOLE FIX (pinned, implementation-ready spec): make extents COALESCE on the write-allocation path so a physically-contiguous sequential run stays O(1) extents (E small ⇒ the per-write `collect_extents` re-walk is cheap ⇒ O(N)). `ffs_btree::insert` (crates/ffs-btree/src/lib.rs:587) is deliberately non-coalescing "so collapse_range/insert_range bookkeeping is unaffected", and ffs-btree exposes no in-place update primitive (public API: search/walk/insert/delete_range/trim_extents). EXECUTION: add a coalescing variant (e.g. a `coalesce: bool` threaded through `insert`/`insert_descend`, or `insert_coalescing`) used ONLY by `ffs_extent::allocate_extent` (lib.rs:421); at the leaf insert point merge the new extent into the entry at `insert_pos-1` when it is exactly logical+physically contiguous, same written state, and combined `actual_len <= EXT_INIT_MAX_LEN` (32768); leave `collapse_range`/`insert_range` on the non-coalescing path. DE-RISKED: collapse_range/insert_range/fallocate are well-covered (conformance.rs ×29, ffs-extent ×46, fuse_e2e ×163), so a coalescing variant is validated by the existing suite. This is correctness-critical multi-hour B+tree work (leaf-merge × the depth-0 and descend paths; merging makes an insert add 0 entries, which simplifies split but needs separator-boundary care) — a dedicated cycle, not a safe blind 60-min change with agent-mail down. Single-thread perf surface is otherwise harvested (metadata both fs at parity, overwrite at kernel parity, large-chunk writes fast); this coalescing lever + bd-bhh0i (parallel) are the remaining real gaps.

### 2026-06-28 ⭐⭐⭐SHIPPED the extent-coalescing fix: fine-grained (4 KiB) sequential ALLOCATION 120x faster at N=40k, O(N²)→O(N) (per the spec two entries up) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Implemented the coalescing lever spec'd above. Added `ffs_btree::insert_coalescing` (a thin wrapper over a shared `insert_inner(.., coalesce)`; plain `insert` keeps `coalesce=false` so `collapse_range`/`insert_range` are byte-for-byte unchanged) + `try_merge_written`: a newly-allocated WRITTEN extent that is exactly logical- AND physically-contiguous with its leaf predecessor and whose combined `raw_len <= EXT_INIT_MAX_LEN` (32768) merges into it (ext4's standard adjacent-extent merge), at the depth-0 leaf site in both `insert_inner` and `insert_descend`. Added `ffs_extent::allocate_extent_coalescing` (delegates to a shared `allocate_extent_inner(.., coalesce)`; plain `allocate_extent` stays non-coalescing so its unit tests + any non-data caller are unchanged), and pointed ONLY `ext4_write`'s two data-block allocation sites (ffs-core lib.rs:20068/20089) at it. The returned `ExtentMapping.count` still reports only the newly-allocated blocks (i_blocks accounting unchanged). Unwritten extents (`raw_len > EXT_INIT_MAX_LEN`) never merge (mark_written/fallocate boundaries preserved); cross-leaf merge (predecessor in a prior leaf) is skipped → plain insert. Surgical: only the file data-write path coalesces.

MEASURED A/B (`write-bench` 4 KiB sequential allocation into a sparse file, release-perf, baseline = current main):

| N (4 KiB allocs) | baseline µs/op | coalescing µs/op | speedup |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2,000 | 28.7 | 10.8 | 2.6x |
| 10,000 | 75.6 | 8.9 | 8.5x |
| 40,000 | 991.3 | **8.2** | **120.4x** |

Coalescing makes per-op FLAT (~8-11 µs/op) = O(N) total (was O(N²)); the extent tree now holds one extent per ~32768-block run instead of one per block, so the per-write `collect_extents` re-walk stays cheap. CORRECTNESS: data is byte-identical — read-back sha256 of baseline vs coalescing after the same 10000×4 KiB workload MATCHES (`a6d72ac7…`); `e2fsck -fn` on the coalescing image is CLEAN (Pass 1-5, **0.0% non-contiguous** — the merge yields contiguous extents). Also fixed a pre-existing test-compile break (`crates/ffs-extent/src/lib.rs:1965` `GroupStats` literal missing `reserved_cache`/`reserved_confirmed` from c5df77f0). Files: crates/ffs-btree/src/lib.rs, crates/ffs-extent/src/lib.rs. This closes the last single-thread O(N²) (the fine-grained-allocation path); large-chunk writes were already fast and overwrite at kernel parity.

### 2026-06-28 ⭐extend extent coalescing to the mark_written (fallocate-fill) path — same O(N²) class as the shipped allocate coalescing, for the DB preallocate-then-write workload (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Follow-up to the write-allocation coalescing (2aa92946): the SIBLING primitive `ffs_extent::mark_written` (which converts unwritten→written when data is written into a fallocated region) inserted its split replacements via the non-coalescing `ffs_btree::insert` (lib.rs:1065), so sequentially filling a fallocated (unwritten) extent in small chunks fragments the tree to E=O(blocks) — one written extent per write, the SAME O(N²) mechanism (per-write `collect_extents` re-walk) fixed on the plain allocation path. Switched that insert to `insert_coalescing`: the newly-written replacement merges into a contiguous WRITTEN predecessor (e.g. block i's written extent merges with block i-1's), so a sequential fallocate-fill keeps O(1) written extents. `try_merge_written` only merges written↔written, so the unwritten sides of a partial split are never absorbed (mark_written/fallocate boundary semantics preserved); `mark_written` is called only from `ext4_write` (lib.rs:20165/20185), the data-write path. CORRECTNESS-ALIGNED: ext4 canonically merges contiguous written extents; the prior behavior produced valid-but-fragmented trees. Gated: ffs-btree+ffs-extent 156+154/0, conformance 100/0/2 (no regression; the single-split mark_written unit tests are unaffected since they have no adjacent prior-written extent to merge). MEASUREMENT NOTE: the direct fallocate-fill ratio is not measurable in this environment (ffs-cli has no fallocate subcommand and a kernel-fallocate fixture needs an RW loop mount whose umount is dcg-blocked); the win is established by the IDENTICAL mechanism on the plain-allocation path (120x at N=40k, 2aa92946) — `mark_written`'s fragmentation and fix are byte-for-byte the same coalescing. Files: crates/ffs-extent/src/lib.rs.

### 2026-06-28 PINPOINT the last single-thread mkdir gap (~1.7x) AND tie it to bd-bhh0i: `ext4_mkdir` re-acquires `block_device_adapter` 3x — each is a writable snapshot register/release (`store.write()`); a read-your-writes adapter would collapse it to 1 (two-for-one lever, MVCC-owner) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

After the single-thread metadata sweep left mkdir as the lone ~1.7x outlier on BOTH ext4 and btrfs (≈50 µs/op vs create ≈24 µs/op; broad profile, no single hotspot), traced the structural delta: `ext4_mkdir` (ffs-core) calls `block_device_adapter()` **three times** (lib.rs:16652 initial, 16715 after `create_inode`, 16762 before the extent-tree insert). For a writable fs each call is `FsMvccBlockDevice::new` → `ShardedMvccStore` snapshot **register + Drop release** under `store.write()` (the same per-op snapshot churn that is bd-bhh0i's parallel-write contention source, ffs-mvcc lib.rs:4079 `read_your_writes: false`). The re-acquires exist because the default adapter is snapshot-isolated (`read_your_writes=false`), so a fresh snapshot is needed to observe each prior sub-write (the new inode table entry; the dir-block write) — see the comment at lib.rs:16713.

LEVER (MVCC-owner, two-for-one): give `ext4_mkdir` (and the analogous multi-write metadata ops) a SINGLE `with_read_your_writes()` adapter (ffs-mvcc lib.rs:4119) for its internal write→read sequence, so it sees its own committed sub-writes without re-registering a snapshot — collapsing 3 register/release cycles to 1. This shaves the mkdir constant factor AND cuts per-op `store.write()` snapshot traffic, which is exactly the bd-bhh0i (8.32x parallel) bottleneck — the last single-thread gap and the biggest parallel gap share this mechanism. NOT prototyped here: switching an internal adapter from snapshot-isolated to read-your-writes is an MVCC isolation-semantics change (must confirm no mkdir sub-step relies on snapshot isolation for correctness), correctness-critical and in the contested ffs-mvcc/adapter lane (agent-mail down → cannot coordinate with the owner). Recorded as the precise, unifying lever. The clean per-crate single-thread levers are shipped (rename 5.57x `51294142`, write-alloc coalescing 120x `2aa92946`, fallocate-fill coalescing `475a0e31`); the remaining gaps (mkdir constant-factor + bd-bhh0i parallel) both reduce to this adapter/snapshot-churn primitive.

### 2026-06-28 btrfs data-write path is FLAT / at parity (no O(N²)) — completes the write-path map across both filesystems (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Measured the one un-swept primitive — btrfs single-file data writes (`write-bench /bigfile` on a btrfs-convert image, 4 KiB writes, release-perf, single-thread). Per-op is FLAT: N=2000 5.2 µs/op, N=10000 4.9, N=30000 5.1 (~700 MiB/s, 180k IOPS). No O(N²): btrfs's COW write path does not suffer the ext4 fine-grained-allocation extent-tree fragmentation that the coalescing fix (`2aa92946`) addressed — btrfs's own extent/COW machinery keeps per-write cost constant. So btrfs data write needs no lever. This completes the single-thread write-path coverage: ext4 overwrite (kernel parity), ext4 large-chunk alloc (fast), ext4 fine-grained alloc (FIXED, coalescing), btrfs write (flat/parity). Combined with the metadata sweeps (ext4 + btrfs all flat) and reads (parity), the single-thread perf surface is fully mapped; the only residual gaps (mkdir ~1.7x constant-factor + bd-bhh0i 8.32x parallel) both reduce to the MVCC adapter/snapshot-register primitive (`533bf004`), which is owner-lane parallel-isolation work. No code change (measurement).

### 2026-06-28 ⭐ROOT-CAUSE bd-bhh0i to the exact line: parallel-write negative scaling is the GLOBAL `active_snapshots.write()` refcount lock in `register_snapshot`/`release_snapshot` (sharded.rs:950/959), taken per-adapter per-op — NOT shard count, NOT payload copy (both already handled). Lever = shard/lock-free that refcount map, preserving the prune TOCTOU (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Ground-truthed bd-bhh0i with its per-crate bench `wal_throughput/sharded_mvcc_disjoint_Nwriters`: 8w 1.94 ms, 16w 3.21 ms, 32w 8.15 ms — super-linear (2.5x for 2x writers at the high end) = negative scaling under contention, confirmed. Traced the serialization precisely:

- NOT shard count: the bench hardcodes `ShardedMvccStore::new(8)` (sharded.rs:1734); production uses `host_parallelism_shard_count()` = `available_parallelism*4` (256 on this 64-core box). IvoryBirch's end-to-end 8.3x was at 8 threads with production (256-shard) sharding ⇒ disjoint writers DON'T collide on shard locks, so shard count is not the bottleneck.
- NOT payload copy: `VersionData::Full(Arc<AlignedVec>)` (compression.rs:38, bd-xmh5g.394) — reads already SHARE the Arc (no copy); payload-reference storage (one of IvoryBirch's three listed structural levers) is ALREADY DONE. The residual write-side `new_bytes.to_vec()` on commit is inherent to the `&[u8]` write API.
- ROOT CAUSE: `register_snapshot` (sharded.rs:950) takes `self.active_snapshots.write()` — a SINGLE GLOBAL write lock on the snapshot-refcount map — and `release_snapshot` (959, on Drop) takes it again. Every `block_device_adapter()` for a writable fs registers+releases a snapshot, so EVERY metadata op (mkdir does it 3x, `533bf004`) globally serializes against every other parallel writer here. This is the write-side analog of the 88x parallel-RANDOM-READ gap (same `store.write().register_snapshot` per-op serialization, which for reads was fixed by `new_unregistered` — writes can't use that, they need a real registered snapshot for isolation + prune-safety).

LEVER (owner-lane, ffs-mvcc, structural-but-targeted): make the `active_snapshots` refcount map register/release WITHOUT a global write lock — shard it by `snapshot.high` (register/release hit different shard locks), or use atomic per-seq counters / a lock-free concurrent map. CORRECTNESS CONSTRAINT (the hard part): preserve the prune-vs-register TOCTOU that `prune_safe_with_registry` (sharded.rs:1040) currently guarantees by holding the registry lock — a snapshot registered concurrently with a prune must be visible before the prune decides to reclaim its versions, else use-after-reclaim. A sharded/lock-free refcount needs an epoch or all-shard barrier at prune time to keep that ordering. NOT shippable blind: this is correctness-critical concurrent-GC code, conformance is single-threaded (can't validate the TOCTOU), and it's the contested ffs-mvcc lane (agent-mail down). Recorded as the precise, located bd-bhh0i lever + its safety invariant for the MVCC owner. This supersedes the vaguer "adapter snapshot-churn primitive" framing (`533bf004`) with the exact lock + line + constraint.

### 2026-06-28 ⭐SHIPPED (safe high-concurrency win): size the production MVCC store to host parallelism, not a fixed 8 shards — parallel writes 1.17x @16w / 1.29x @32w (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

While root-causing bd-bhh0i I found `FsMvccStore::sharded()` (the production parallel-write store, runtime-confirmed via `shard_count=8` log) hardcoded `ShardedMvccStore::new(8)` — even though `for_host_parallelism()` (= `available_parallelism * 4`, bounded, `next_power_of_two`) is the documented "default to prefer for high-core deployments." The per-crate bench `wal_throughput/sharded_mvcc_disjoint` already uses `for_host_parallelism`, so it did NOT expose this production gap. A/B on the bench (`new(8)` = production vs `for_host_parallelism` = 256 shards on this 64-core box):

| disjoint writers | new(8) (was prod) | for_host_parallelism | speedup |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 8 | 1.87 ms | 1.94 ms | ~neutral |
| 16 | 3.74 ms | 3.21 ms | **1.17x** |
| 32 | 10.53 ms | 8.15 ms | **1.29x** |

FIX: `sharded()` now uses `ShardedMvccStore::for_host_parallelism()`. CORRECTNESS-IDENTICAL — more shards, same semantics; no change to the snapshot/prune/lock-ordering machinery; the bench's own use of `for_host_parallelism` is already conformance/test-covered. This removes the shard-lock-contention COMPONENT of parallel writes (16+ writers); the RESIDUAL gap (bd-bhh0i, ~8x at 8 threads) is the GLOBAL `active_snapshots.write()` lock (`edc90b1b`), which shard count does not address (the bench already had 256 shards and still scaled negatively). 8-writer neutral, so no regression at low concurrency. File: crates/ffs-core/src/fs_mvcc_store.rs.

### 2026-06-28 ❌REVERT (~marginal + non-portable): bumping `FFS_CACHE_SHARDS` 16→64 = only 1.05x parallel read — the read-cache path is already near-optimal at 16 shards (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Applied the config-mismatch lesson (from the shipped MVCC shard win `a2807896`) to the read-side `ShardedCache` (7 block caches, `Mutex<BTreeMap>` shards keyed `% FFS_CACHE_SHARDS=16`, lib.rs:1294). A/B parallel random read (`ffs-cli rand-read --parallel`, 500000×4 KiB, warm, 64-core, median of 3):

| FFS_CACHE_SHARDS | parallel rand-read |
| --- | --- |
| 16 (current) | ~31,450 MiB/s |
| 64 | ~33,170 MiB/s |

Only **1.05x** (clean separation but marginal). REVERTED: (1) the gain is ~5%, near `~0-gain`; (2) `FFS_CACHE_SHARDS` is a `const` (used in `[bool; FFS_CACHE_SHARDS]` array sizes, lib.rs:1336), so a hardcoded 64 is NON-PORTABLE — it 4x's per-cache shard memory on small-core boxes for no benefit; a portable host-sized version would need de-const-ifying those arrays (a bigger change not justified by ~5%). The parallel-read path is already healthy (scales 11.6x, 31 GB/s warm — not bandwidth- or cache-shard-bound). Distinct from the MVCC store shard win (that used the existing `for_host_parallelism()` constructor, was 1.17-1.29x, and the version-store contention IS shard-sensitive; the read cache's brief `get()` critical section — BTreeMap lookup + Arc-clone — is not). NOTE: the comment at lib.rs:1290 already documents that Mutex-shards beat RwLock here (RwLock's reader-count atomic ping-pongs, measured 1.8-2.8x worse) — so RwLock-ifying the cache is also a known dead end. No production change.

### 2026-06-28 REFINE the bd-bhh0i lever (simpler than sharding): make `active_snapshots` register/release a READ-lock + atomic refcount; `prune`'s WRITE lock still excludes them (preserves lock-ordering) — same TOCTOU constraint, owner-lane (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Follow-up to the bd-bhh0i root-cause (`edc90b1b`: the global `active_snapshots.write()` at sharded.rs:950/960 serializes all parallel writers). A SIMPLER fix than sharding the map: keep `active_snapshots` a single `RwLock<BTreeMap<CommitSeq, _>>` but store `AtomicU64` (or similar) counts, so `register_snapshot`/`release_snapshot` take a SHARED READ lock + atomic inc/dec for an EXISTING seq (the common case — most adapters share `current_snapshot`); only first-registration of a new seq needs the WRITE lock. Concurrent register/release then DON'T serialize (read lock is shared), eliminating the per-adapter global write-lock that is the parallel-write bottleneck. This PRESERVES the existing lock-ordering hierarchy (conflict_policy → active_snapshots → shards → contention_metrics) — it's still one lock — unlike sharding, which would complicate that ordering. CORRECTNESS CONSTRAINT (unchanged, the hard part): `prune` must take the WRITE lock on `active_snapshots` while computing/acting on the reclaim watermark, so it mutually-excludes register/release (RwLock read↔write exclusion) — this is what preserves the prune-vs-register TOCTOU. The residual subtle case to validate: a `register` of a seq whose count just hit 0 racing a `prune` that decides to reclaim that seq's versions — must guarantee the registration is either blocked-until-after-prune-removes-the-seq (then it re-registers a fresh current snapshot) or visible-before-reclaim. Still NOT shippable blind: no loom infra in ffs-mvcc to exhaustively check this, single-threaded conformance can't catch it, contested lane (agent-mail down). Recorded as the PREFERRED (simpler, hierarchy-preserving) implementation path for the MVCC owner, refining the sharding suggestion in `edc90b1b`. No code change.

### 2026-06-28 ⚠️CORRECTION to the bd-bhh0i root-cause (edc90b1b/25e4ca94): the per-crate bench measures the PUBLICATION GATE, not `active_snapshots` — they are TWO distinct serialization points on DIFFERENT paths (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Re-reading the actual code paths, my prior bd-bhh0i root-cause conflated two separate serializations:

- The per-crate bench `wal_throughput/sharded_mvcc_disjoint_Nwriters` does `store.begin()` + `txn.stage_write` + `store.commit(txn)` directly. `begin()` = `current_snapshot()` = `self.publication_gate.completed()`; `commit()` ends in `self.publication_gate.publish(commit_seq)` (ORDERED publication — commits become visible in seq order). It does NOT call `register_snapshot`, so it NEVER touches `active_snapshots`. Therefore the bench's negative scaling (8w 1.94 / 16w 3.21 / 32w 8.15 ms) is the **publication gate** (in-order commit visibility), NOT the `active_snapshots.write()` lock I claimed in `edc90b1b`. Prior probes of the publication gate were measured INERT (stashes `cc-publication-gate-fix-MEASURED-INERT`, `cc-publication-gate-targeted-wakeup`), and in-order visibility is largely inherent.
- The `active_snapshots.write()` lock (sharded.rs:950/960) IS a real per-op global serialization, but on the **adapter/end-to-end path**: `block_device_adapter()` → `FsMvccBlockDevice::new` → `register_snapshot` + Drop `release_snapshot`. That path is what IvoryBirch's end-to-end bd-bhh0i (parallel create/write `17193` vs kernel `143117`) and the mkdir 3x-adapter-churn (`533bf004`) exercise — NOT the per-crate `sharded_mvcc_disjoint` bench.

CONSEQUENCE: bd-bhh0i has (at least) TWO distinct serialization points — (1) the publication gate on the commit path (per-crate-bench-measured, prior fixes inert, in-order-visibility largely inherent), and (2) the `active_snapshots` global lock on the adapter path (end-to-end only). The read-lock+atomic `active_snapshots` lever (`25e4ca94`) addresses (2) but CANNOT be validated by the `sharded_mvcc_disjoint` bench (which doesn't hit it) — it needs an end-to-end parallel-adapter (FUSE/CLI) write bench. Both remain owner-lane (publication-gate fixes are inert; active_snapshots is correctness-critical concurrent-GC). Corrects the over-claim that the bench confirmed `active_snapshots`. No code change.

### 2026-06-28 bd-bhh0i parallel-commit PROFILE (definitive): futex/synchronization-bound, NO userspace hotspot — confirms the gap is inherent ordered-publication + lock coordination, not a fixable code path (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Profiled the per-crate `sharded_mvcc_disjoint_32writers` bench (perf, the begin/commit path = publication gate + shard locks, per the `3269c956` correction). The profile is FLAT and KERNEL-FUTEX-DOMINATED: the top self-time frames are kernel futex/scheduler symbols (`[k] 0xffff…` ~4.45% + 2.05% + 1.77% + 1.40%×2 + … ≈ 15%+ summed — threads PARKED on synchronization), plus minor libc malloc/free churn (`_int_malloc`/`unlink_chunk`/`_int_free_*` ≈ 2.5%, per-commit allocation) and `__memmove` 1.7%. There is NO userspace hotspot to optimize — the parallel writers spend their time blocked/parked waiting on the in-order publication gate + per-shard commit coordination. This is conclusive evidence the bd-bhh0i parallel-commit gap is INHERENT synchronization cost (ordered commit visibility for snapshot isolation + lock handoff), which is exactly why the prior publication-gate fixes measured INERT (there is no userspace path to speed up — the cost is the parking itself). Reducing it requires a fundamentally different concurrency design (e.g., relaxed/batched publication that still preserves linearizable snapshot visibility, or wait-free commit sequencing) — a structural owner-lane redesign, not a tractable safe lever; the ~2.5% malloc churn is the only userspace component and is too small to matter. This profile is the definitive close of the per-crate bd-bhh0i dig (the adapter-path `active_snapshots` serialization, `25e4ca94`, remains separate and end-to-end-only). No code change.

### 2026-06-28 ⭐VERIFIED vs LIVE KERNEL: the rename fix (51294142) makes frankenfs rename 1.71x FASTER than a live ext4 mount at N=40000 (was ~3.5x slower pre-fix) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Closed the loop on the headline rename win with a direct LIVE-KERNEL head-to-head (prior A/B was only frankenfs-old vs frankenfs-new). Same workload both sides — create 40000 files in a dir (untimed), then time 40000 renames + a final sync — on matching 3 GiB ext4 (`mke2fs ^64bit -b 4096 -N 200000`):

| N=40000 rename + sync | µs/op | renames/s | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| live ext4 kernel (RW loop mount, `os.rename` loop) | 30.9 | 32,314 | 1.0x |
| frankenfs (`ffs-cli rename-bench`, current main) | **18.1** | **55,381** | **1.71x FASTER** |

So the preflight target-leaf fix (`51294142`, which turned the rename O(N²) into O(N) by checking only the hash-target leaf instead of all dir blocks) doesn't just beat old-frankenfs — it **beats the live Linux ext4 kernel by 1.71x** at N=40000 (pre-fix frankenfs was O(N²), ~108 µs/op ≈ 3.5x SLOWER than the kernel's flat ~31 µs/op). This is the definitive vs-kernel confirmation of the session's headline metadata win. Method: `sudo mount -o loop` (RW) the throwaway image for the kernel side; `ffs-cli rename-bench` on a separate fresh copy for frankenfs; both include the durability sync. No code change (verification).

### 2026-06-28 ⭐VERIFIED vs LIVE KERNEL: the extent-coalescing fix (2aa92946) closes 4 KiB sparse-fill from ~170x SLOWER to 1.52x vs a live ext4 mount; residual 1.52x is inherent MVCC write-path overhead (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Live-kernel head-to-head for the write-coalescing win (4 KiB sequential fill of a sparse/hole file, the fine-grained allocation path). Same workload both sides — sparse file, 40000 sequential 4 KiB writes (each allocates), + a final sync:

| 4 KiB sparse-fill N=40000 + sync | µs/op | MB/s | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| live ext4 kernel (RW loop mount, `pwrite` loop) | 5.8 | 703 | 1.0x |
| frankenfs current (coalescing) | 8.8 | 444 | 1.52x slower |
| frankenfs PRE-coalescing (`O(N^2)`) | ~991 | ~4 | ~170x slower |

So the coalescing fix (`2aa92946`, written-extent merge on the write-alloc path) took fine-grained sparse-fill from **~170x slower than the kernel to 1.52x** — frankenfs writes are now within a small constant of the live kernel. The residual 1.52x (≈3 µs/op) is the inherent frankenfs write-path overhead per 4 KiB block — the full MVCC commit (version install + publication + per-op adapter snapshot register/release) vs the kernel's direct page write + delayed allocation. That residual is the same MVCC commit/adapter machinery behind bd-bhh0i (publication gate + `active_snapshots`, profile-confirmed futex/inherent + owner-lane), not a separate safe lever. Confirms the second shipped win vs a live kernel. No code change (verification).

### 2026-06-28 ⭐VERIFIED vs LIVE KERNEL: create is 1.12x FASTER than ext4; mkdir is 1.31x slower (corrects the earlier "1.7x" — live-kernel mkdir is 39.4µs, not the stale baseline) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Live-kernel head-to-heads for the metadata create/mkdir paths (RW loop mount, `os.open`/`os.mkdir` loops + sync, vs `ffs-cli create-bench`/`mkdir-bench`, N=40000, matching 3 GiB ext4):

| op N=40000 + sync | kernel µs/op | frankenfs µs/op | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| create | 27.6 | 24.6 | **1.12x FASTER** |
| mkdir | 39.4 | 51.5 | 1.31x slower |

create BEATS the live kernel (1.12x). mkdir is **1.31x** slower than the LIVE kernel — this CORRECTS the earlier "~1.7x" mkdir figure (which compared to a stale/inferred kernel baseline; the real live-kernel mkdir is 39.4 µs/op, slower than assumed). The ~12 µs/op mkdir delta over create is the extra dir-block alloc+init (`.`/`..` + checksum) + the 2nd inode commit + the 3x `block_device_adapter` snapshot register/release churn (`533bf004`) — i.e. the MVCC-adjacent per-op overhead, the same `active_snapshots`-family cost that is owner-lane (read-lock+atomic refcount, `25e4ca94`). So mkdir's residual is bounded (1.31x vs live kernel) and tied to the known owner-lane primitive, not a fresh safe lever. (delete/rmdir vs-kernel not measured: `os.unlink`/`os.rmdir` are dcg-blocked in heredocs.) No code change (verification).

### 2026-06-28 ⚠️⚠️RETRACTION + real measurement: my "btrfs metadata flat/at-parity" (4f78205f) was measured on `btr.img` which is EXT4 (not btrfs) — REAL btrfs metadata is SLOWER and GROWS (a missed gap) (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

The fixture `/tmp/btr.img` I used for this session's "btrfs" sweeps is EXT4, not btrfs: it has ext4 magic `0xEF53` (`dumpe2fs`), the kernel mounts it `type ext4`, and frankenfs reads it via the ext4 path ("ext4 journal replay"). A real btrfs image (`/tmp/btrfs_test.img`, `/data/tmp/btrc_*.img`) makes frankenfs take the btrfs path ("btrfs chunk map expanded from chunk tree", `btrfs_mount_selection_resolved`). So **the btrfs claims in `4f78205f` (metadata flat/at-parity) and the btrfs-data-write-flat entry were actually EXT4 re-measurements — RETRACTED.**

REAL btrfs metadata (on genuine btrfs images, `create-bench`/`rename-bench`, current binary):

| op | N=2000 µs/op | N=20000 µs/op | shape | vs ext4 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| create | 27.5 | 49.9 | GROWS (1.81x for 10x N) | ext4 flat ~24 |
| rename | 86.4 | 144.3 | GROWS (1.67x for 10x N) | ext4 18 (post-fix), kernel ~31 |

So btrfs metadata is NOT flat/at-parity — btrfs create grows with N, and btrfs **rename is ~5-8x slower than ext4 rename and grows** (86-144 µs/op vs ext4's flat 18). This is a REAL, previously-missed btrfs metadata gap (I'd been measuring ext4 on a misnamed fixture all session). LESSON: verify the fixture's ACTUAL fs type (frankenfs log: "btrfs chunk map" vs "ext4 journal replay"; `dumpe2fs`/`btrfs filesystem show`) before labeling a measurement btrfs — a `btr*`-named image is not necessarily btrfs. The btrfs rename/create growth is the next lever target (under investigation).

### 2026-06-28 ⭐⭐NEW REAL GAP (was masked by the ext4-fixture error): btrfs rename is 4.6x SLOWER than the btrfs KERNEL — frankenfs re-COWs already-staged internal path nodes per op instead of mutating in-place. Lever: extend in-place COW to staged internal nodes (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

After discovering my "btrfs" sweep was ext4 (`03c2c815`), measured frankenfs btrfs rename vs the btrfs KERNEL (RW loop-mounted real btrfs image, `os.rename` loop + sync, N=20000, both create-then-rename):

| btrfs rename N=20000 +sync | µs/op | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- |
| btrfs kernel (RW loop mount) | 31.4 | 1.0x |
| frankenfs btrfs | 144.3 | **4.6x SLOWER** |

This is a REAL gap (the kernel also does COW, so it's not just a vs-ext4 design difference). PROFILE (perf, btrfs rename-bench N=20000): dominated by `InMemoryCowBtrfsTree` COW node churn — `insert_into` 8.5% + `alloc_internal_node` 7.7% + `drop_glue` 7.1% + `BtrfsCowNode::clone` 4.8% ≈ **28% on node alloc/clone/drop**; `try_insert_inplace` is only 0.5%. ROOT CAUSE: `try_insert_inplace` (lib.rs:3430) mutates in-place ONLY a LEAF allocated in the current batch (`staged_allocations.contains(&id)`, line 3455); but the general `insert_into` path COWs the ENTIRE root→leaf path on every op, re-cloning internal nodes that are ALREADY staged (COW'd) earlier in the same batch. The kernel COWs each node once per transaction then modifies it in-place for subsequent changes in that transaction; frankenfs re-COWs the upper path (root + internals, on every insert) — that is the `alloc_internal_node`/`clone`/`drop` churn and the source of the 4.6x.

LEVER (the real next-biggest gap vs btrfs-kernel): extend the in-place mutation to already-STAGED INTERNAL path nodes — during the COW descent in `insert`/`delete`, mutate a path node in place (update child pointer / key / item) when `staged_allocations.contains(node)`, and only COW (clone+alloc+retire) nodes NOT yet staged this batch. SAFETY: identical invariant to the existing leaf in-place path — a staged node is allocated in THIS uncommitted batch only, never referenced by a committed/snapshot generation, so in-place mutation cannot corrupt a durable/older version. VALIDATION: btrfs `crash_consistency.rs` + conformance btrfs paths + `rename-bench` win + btrfs scrub/read-back. NOT YET IMPLEMENTED — this is a careful btrfs-core COW-insert restructure (correctness-critical, btrfs-corruption risk) deserving a focused effort, not a rushed end-of-cycle change; recorded as the precise lever with root cause + profile + safety invariant. This is the largest fixable vs-kernel gap remaining (4.6x), previously masked because the session's btrfs measurements were accidentally ext4.

### 2026-06-28 REFINE the btrfs-rename-4.6x lever (19bd2cad): the in-place-staged-internals fix is INTRA-batch only (~1.16x rename, more on create/mkdir 3-insert batches) — the DOMINANT 4.6x is per-op COMMIT GRANULARITY (structural, owner-lane), not the local fix (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Deeper read of the COW path corrects the impact estimate in `19bd2cad`. `insert_many`/`insert_many_then_update` clear staging at the end of each call (`commit_allocated_nodes`, lib.rs:3419), and `try_insert_inplace` requires the node to be in `staged_allocations` for the CURRENT batch. So:
- A single create/mkdir batches its ~3 inserts (DIR_ITEM + DIR_INDEX + INODE_REF) into one `insert_many_then_update`; a rename's remove+insert share the upper path within one batch. Extending in-place to staged INTERNAL nodes would let the 2nd/3rd op in a batch reuse the staged upper path instead of re-COWing it — a real but MODEST win (~1.16x rename; the shared upper path is re-COW'd ~2x→1x per rename; create/mkdir benefit more from their 3-insert batch).
- But the DOMINANT 4.6x is CROSS-batch: each rename is a SEPARATE batch that re-COWs the whole root→leaf path FROM THE COMMITTED ROOT, because frankenfs commits per FsOp. The btrfs KERNEL keeps the b-tree dirty in memory and COWs each node ONCE PER TRANSACTION (flushing at sync), so its per-rename cost is an in-memory node touch, not a from-root re-COW. That gap is the per-op COMMIT GRANULARITY — the same structural "commit batching / metadata-write deferral" lever as bd-bhh0i (IvoryBirch's listed structural work), owner-lane.

CONCLUSION: the in-place-staged-internals lever (`19bd2cad`) is real but only ~1.16x (intra-batch), and it is an intricate, corruption-risky btrfs-core COW-descent restructure — LOW EV for the gain. The btrfs rename 4.6x vs kernel is mostly per-op commit granularity (structural, owner-lane), joining the ext4 write/mkdir residuals in the "inherent MVCC/commit per-op overhead" category. The fixture-error discovery (btrfs measurements were ext4) and the real 4.6x-vs-kernel measurement stand; the local lever is downgraded to modest/owner-lane. No code change (analysis refinement).

### 2026-06-28 SYNTHESIS: every remaining vs-kernel residual reduces to ONE root cause — per-op COMMIT GRANULARITY; the single structural lever (commit batching / write deferral) would address all of them (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Pulling the session's kernel-verified results together, the gaps split cleanly into SHIPPED-and-fixed (the O(N^2) cliffs) vs a single remaining structural root cause:

| path | vs live kernel | status |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ext4 rename | 1.71x FASTER | SHIPPED (`51294142`, O(N^2)→O(N)) |
| ext4 create | 1.12x FASTER | at/above kernel |
| ext4 read (seq/rand/parallel) | parity / faster | harvested |
| ext4 write (4 KiB sparse-fill) | 1.52x slower | per-op MVCC commit residual |
| ext4 mkdir | 1.31x slower | per-op MVCC commit + dir-setup residual |
| btrfs rename | 4.6x slower | per-op COW-from-committed-root residual |

The three remaining residuals (ext4 write 1.52x, ext4 mkdir 1.31x, btrfs rename 4.6x — btrfs worst because its COW b-tree re-COWs root→leaf from the committed root each op) are the SAME phenomenon: **FrankenFS commits per FsOp**, so every metadata/data op pays a full MVCC commit (version install + publication gate + per-op `active_snapshots` adapter register/release) and, on btrfs, a full from-root COW. The **kernel amortizes**: it keeps metadata dirty in memory, COWs/dirties each node once per TRANSACTION, and flushes once at `sync`. So the single structural lever that would close ALL three residuals (and bd-bhh0i parallel-write, profile-confirmed futex/inherent) is **commit batching / metadata-write deferral** — coalescing many FsOps into one transaction/flush, exactly IvoryBirch's listed bd-bhh0i structural direction. This is owner-lane (it changes the commit/transaction model — durability, crash-consistency, snapshot-visibility semantics; not a safe blind per-crate change). CONCLUSION of the vs-kernel sweep: FrankenFS is at/above the live kernel for rename+create+read; the remaining write/mkdir/btrfs-rename residuals are one structural commit-granularity gap, owner-lane. The safe-Rust per-op lever space is exhausted and kernel-verified.

### 2026-06-28 ⚠️CORRECTION to the synthesis (10cf435a): btrfs CREATE is 1.36x FASTER than the kernel — so residuals are NOT uniform "commit granularity"; the slow ops have OP-SPECIFIC costs (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Measured the missing btrfs create-vs-kernel head-to-head: frankenfs btrfs create **48.7 µs/op vs btrfs kernel 66.2 µs/op = 1.36x FASTER**. This DISPROVES the `10cf435a` synthesis that the residuals are a uniform per-op commit-granularity gap: btrfs create has the SAME per-op commit granularity as btrfs rename, yet create BEATS the kernel while rename is 4.6x slower. Likewise on ext4, create is 1.12x FASTER while write/mkdir are slower — same asymmetry. So the slow ops are NOT uniformly commit-bound; each has an OP-SPECIFIC extra cost:

| op | vs kernel | op-specific cost |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ext4 create | 1.12x FASTER | — |
| btrfs create | 1.36x FASTER | — |
| ext4 write 4 KiB | 1.52x slower | per-4 KiB-block MVCC version install (vs kernel page-cache + delayed alloc) |
| ext4 mkdir | 1.31x slower | dir-block alloc+init+CRC + 2nd inode commit + adapter churn |
| btrfs rename | 4.6x slower | touches ~2x leaves (remove old DIR_ITEM+DIR_INDEX + insert new into different hash-leaves + ref + parent inode) via `remove_many_then_insert_many_then_update` (lib.rs:26858) + `require_inode_ref_index` lookups — more COW footprint per op than create's `insert_many_then_update` |

So FrankenFS metadata CREATE beats the live kernel on BOTH filesystems — the per-op MVCC/COW commit path is competitive. The remaining slow ops each pay a distinct, op-specific cost (write: per-block version churn; mkdir: dir-setup; btrfs rename: 2x-leaf COW footprint). The btrfs rename lever is therefore to reduce its per-op COW footprint (fewer/coalesced leaf touches), not "batch commits" — though that's an intricate btrfs-core change for a single op. CORRECTS the over-general `10cf435a`; the per-op-granularity framing was wrong because create disproves it. No code change (measurement + correction).

### 2026-06-28 ⭐VERIFIED vs LIVE KERNEL (completes the btrfs picture): btrfs READ ~3.6x FASTER than kernel dd-materialize — FrankenFS btrfs is faster than the kernel for create+read, slower only for rename (CrimsonFox cc/opus)

Final vs-kernel datapoint — btrfs read on a REAL btrfs image (`/data/tmp/btrc_1268087.img`, frankenfs takes the btrfs path: "btrfs chunk map expanded"), file `c.bin` (150 MiB, compressed). Warm, materialize (both deliver bytes to userspace):

| btrfs read c.bin (150 MiB, warm) | throughput | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- |
| kernel `dd ... of=/dev/null bs=1M` | ~1.5 GB/s | 1.0x |
| frankenfs `read --discard` (engine, duration_us=27356) | ~5.5 GB/s | **~3.6x FASTER** |

Completes the btrfs vs-live-kernel picture:

| btrfs op | vs live kernel |
| --- | --- |
| create | 1.36x FASTER |
| read (compressed, materialize) | ~3.6x FASTER |
