Completing the matrix with the parallel-write row (8-way, +sync, real kernel ext4 loop mount, true multiprocess vs frankenfs `create-bench --threads 8`):

| ext4 create | 1 thread | 8-way | scaling |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| kernel | 35,070/s | 118,734/s | **3.4x UP** |
| frankenfs | 29,400/s | 21,441/s | **0.65x DOWN (negative)** |
| ratio (ffs/kernel) | 0.84x | **0.18x (kernel 5.5x faster)** | — |

This is the campaign's BIGGEST measured gap vs the kernel: at 8 parallel writers the kernel scales 3.4x while frankenfs NEGATIVELY scales (29.4k→21.4k), opening a **5.5x** deficit. Root cause is the bd-bhh0i commit-path convoy already diagnosed+measured this session: per-FsOp commits funnel through the GLOBAL in-order `CommitPublicationGate` mutex + the GLOBAL `active_snapshots` RwLock (register/release per adapter), serializing — and convoying — all parallel committers. Both safe-Rust attempts to fix the gate are REFUTED with measurement (retry-spin busy-spins; CAS-once regresses via failed-CAS cache-line contention); the only remaining lever is the owner-lane reduction of commits-per-FsOp (intra-op batching via `commit_request_scope` unification) which also relieves the gate frequency Nx. NET measured standing: frankenfs beats kernel on ext4 rename (1.61x), btrfs create (1.16x), btrfs mkdir (9.6x); trails on ext4 create/mkdir (single-thread, ~0.8x) and — by far the largest — PARALLEL ext4 create (0.18x / 5.5x). The 5.5x parallel-write gap is the single highest-value target and is squarely owner-lane.

### 2026-06-29 ⭐SHIPPED (MEASURED perf win): byte-wise inode-bitmap padding fill — create 1.14x / parallel 1.18x (CrimsonFox)

A `perf record` of parallel `create-bench` (8t, count=40000, release-perf symbolized) found `ffs_alloc::fill_inode_bitmap_padding_with_clear_undo` as the #1 frankenfs self-time function at **13.48%** — a regression from this session's own inode-bitmap-padding correctness fix (42c56abc): it iterated the contiguous padding region (`inodes_per_group`..end ≈ 7168 bits) ONE BIT AT A TIME with a per-bit `bitmap_get`, on EVERY inode alloc, even though after the first alloc the whole region is already `0xFF`.

FIX (ffs-alloc, byte-wise): skip whole already-`0xFF` bytes in O(1); only descend to bits in non-full bytes, recording just the NEWLY-set bits for rollback (so the rollback contract is byte-identical and the common already-padded case touches zero individual bits).

MEASURED (local build, create-bench, vs this session's pre-fix baseline same binary minus this function):
| | before (bit-wise) | after (byte-wise) | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| single-thread create | ~29,400/s | ~33,025/s | **1.14x** |
| 8-thread create | ~21,441/s | ~25,310/s | **1.18x** |

Matches the profile (removing ~13% self-time → ~1.15x). Conformance GREEN: ffs-alloc 213/0 (incl. the persist-rollback-restore test — byte-wise yields the same undo set), e2fsck 0 errors on both create- and mkdir-bench images (padding still correct). Helps every inode allocation (create + mkdir). This also narrows the measured vs-kernel ext4 gaps: single-thread create 0.84x→~0.94x, mkdir likewise improved.

### 2026-06-29 ⭐SHIPPED (MEASURED perf win): name-index checked before htree descent — single-thread create 1.13x (CrimsonFox)

Profiling create (post-padding-fix) showed `lookup_in_dir_block` still ~2.2% — the hash-target-leaf scan inside `htree_lookup_name_authoritative`, which `lookup_name_with_scope` ran BEFORE the O(1) bd-f8rd8 negative name-index. A new-name create existence check is always a negative lookup, so it paid the htree descent + leaf scan even though the validation-keyed name index could answer "absent" in O(1) (one hash-set membership test under a shard lock). FIX: try the name-index FIRST; only fall to the htree descent on index miss/invalid or a PRESENT name. Correctness unchanged — the index's "absent" answer is already authoritative when validation matches; everything else still descends.

MEASURED (local build, create-bench, vs the byte-wise-padding baseline = same binary minus this reorder):
| | padding-fix baseline | + index-first | this step | cumulative vs original |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| single-thread create | ~33,000/s | ~37,544/s | **1.13x** | **1.28x** (29.4k orig) |
| 8-thread create | ~25,300/s | ~25,850/s | ~1.02x (neutral; convoy-bound) | — |

The single-thread ext4 create now ~37.5k/s — at/above the kernel ext4 baseline (35.1k), flipping the earlier 0.84x deficit to ~parity-to-slightly-ahead. Parallel is convoy-bound (owner-lane) so the per-op lookup saving is masked there. Conformance GREEN: ffs-core lib 1185/0 (lookup/duplicate-detection correct), e2fsck 0 errors on create + mkdir images. Helps every create/mkdir/rename negative existence check on an indexed dir.

### 2026-06-29 DIG (profile sweep): metadata-write hot paths now flat post-2-wins; remaining levers owner-lane/covered/inherent (CrimsonFox)

After landing the byte-wise-padding (369f1493) and name-index-first (c47c9009) wins, re-profiled (release-perf, perf record -g, flat self-%) the metadata-write ops to find the next lever:
- **create (single-thread)**: `lookup_in_dir_block` GONE (reorder worked). Top now: memmove ~10% (kernel device-flush I/O, inherent), `ShardedMvccStore::commit` ~7% (owner-lane per-FsOp commit), crc32c ~4.5% (inherent metadata checksums), `persist_group_desc` 2.5% (inode-bitmap CRC, inherent — 1 bit changed but CRC is over the full block), `parse_extent_leaf` 2.3% (dir-extent resolve in add_entry; cacheable but ~1.02x for cache+invalidation risk). FLAT — no self-inflicted outlier.
- **mkdir / delete**: also flat — same memmove/commit/crc distribution, no op-specific outlier.
- **btrfs rename** (the 0.43x gap): NOT flat — `insert_into` 8.6% + `drop_glue::<InMemoryCowBtrfsTree>` 8.6% + `BtrfsCowNode::clone` 4.8% = ~22% in CoW node churn (Vec<BtrfsTreeItem> alloc/free + per-item Arc refcount inc/dec → jemalloc sdallocx/coalesce). But: item payloads are ALREADY `Arc<[u8]>` (no deep payload copy), the realloc double-copy is ALREADY fixed (bd-btrcow2 +1-capacity build), and CoW node-reuse was MEASURED-NEUTRAL (staged-internals 0adac5d6). The residual is the fundamental CoW model (new node version + retire old per op) — owner-lane (would need a node arena/pool or version-pinning-aware in-place mutation, both larger than a safe land and the reuse angle already refuted neutral).

CONCLUSION: the safe, profile-surfaced create/mkdir hot-path levers are now banked (2 wins this stretch → single-thread ext4 create 1.28x cumulative, at/above kernel parity). The remaining measured gaps are all owner-lane (parallel-commit convoy 5.5x; btrfs-rename CoW serialization) or inherent (device-flush memmove, metadata CRC). Profiling remains the right tool — it found 2 clean wins "safe-Rust ceiling" reasoning had missed — but the metadata-write paths are now profile-flat for safe levers.

### 2026-06-29 DIG (profile): the 5.5x parallel-create convoy is BLOCKING-bound, not CPU-bound — unifies the gate refutations (CrimsonFox)

`perf record` of parallel-8 create (release-perf) shows the SAME spread CPU profile as single-thread — memmove ~8%, `commit` ~4%, `persist_group_desc` ~3%, crc ~3%, `lookup_in_dir_block` ~2% — and crucially **NO hot lock/spin function** (`active_snapshots` RwLock, the gate mutex, `lock_shards` are all absent from the hot list). CPU sampling only sees RUNNING threads; the 5.5x parallel deficit (negative scaling, measured 4074ac87) is threads PARKED — blocked on the in-order `CommitPublicationGate` condvar and the global `active_snapshots` RwLock — which is OFF-CPU and invisible to `perf record`.

This UNIFIES the session's gate refutations: both lock-free gate fast-path forms regressed (retry-spin busy-spins; CAS-once cache-line-thrashes) precisely because the gate is NOT CPU-bound — it is a BLOCKING serialization point. You cannot CPU-optimize a barrier that threads sleep on; making each lock op faster does nothing when the cost is the serialization itself. The only lever that helps is reducing the NUMBER of serialization points per FsOp — i.e. intra-op batching (one commit/op instead of N → 1 gate trip + 1 register/release instead of N). That is the owner-lane `commit_request_scope`-unified write-site rewrite (corruption-prone in the thread-local form via GDT split-brain; needs the e2fsck oracle gate). NET: confirmed the parallel-write frontier is a blocking-serialization problem solvable only by commit-count reduction, not lock-primitive tuning — redirecting any future gate-primitive attempts (dead ends) to the batching lever.

### 2026-06-29 REFUTED (~0-gain): lookup_in_dir_block early-exit on match — measured neutral (CrimsonFox)

A release-perf profile of random positive lookups (lookup-bench, 40002-entry htree dir) showed `lookup_in_dir_block` at 31.75% — it walks/validates the ENTIRE leaf block even after finding the match (deliberate whole-block validation). Implemented an early-exit walker variant (`walk_dir_block_entries_until`, callback→ControlFlow; existing whole-block walker delegates unchanged) so lookup stops at the match like the kernel's `ext4_find_entry`. Built + clean same-binary A/B (local release, count=200000):

| | lookups/s (median) |
| --- | --- |
| baseline (whole-block walk) | 291,807 |
| early-exit on match | 284,704 |

Ratio ~0.98x — NEUTRAL (within run-to-run noise; not the ~1.15x the 31.75% self-time predicted). ROOT CAUSE / LESSON: the 31.75% was measured on the `release-perf` profile (debuginfo SUPPRESSES inlining), which OVERSTATES a small leaf-scan helper's self-time vs the fully-optimized `release` binary where it inlines and the real per-lookup cost is dominated by the inode read + group-desc read + cache lookups, not the (short, cache-resident, branch-predicted) leaf scan. Halving an already-cheap scan saves nothing measurable. ffs-ondisk 668/0 (the early-exit is correct — whole-block validation preserved for negative lookups and parse_dir_block). REVERTED (~0-gain; also a validation relaxation not worth a non-win). LESSON: cross-check release-perf profile self-% against an actual A/B on the optimized release binary before committing to a sub-5% function lever — debuginfo inlining differences can inflate it.

### 2026-06-29 ⭐SHIPPED (MEASURED perf win): drop the wasteful per-op snapshot register+release round-trip on the writable adapter — create 1.04x single / 1.06x parallel (CrimsonFox)

Reading the adapter constructors revealed a provably-wasteful round-trip: the writable no-tx `block_device_adapter()` did `FsMvccBlockDevice::new(..)` (which `register_snapshot` under the GLOBAL `active_snapshots` write lock) then `.with_read_your_writes()` — which IMMEDIATELY converts the adapter to `Unregistered` AND `release_snapshot`s the snapshot it just registered. So every FsOp paid TWO global `active_snapshots` lock acquisitions (register + release) that net to the same `Unregistered` end state. (The adapter reads at live `current_snapshot()` = the chain head, which prune never removes, so it never needed the registration.) FIX: construct `new_unregistered(..).with_read_your_writes()` directly — byte-identical end state, ZERO global-lock ops, removing a per-op serialization point from the write path (bd-bhh0i convoy's lock half — distinct from the gate half, which was refuted).

MEASURED (env-toggle same-binary A/B, `FFS_WRITABLE_ADAPTER_REGISTER` 0=new vs 1=old):
| | OLD (register+release) | NEW (unregistered) | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| single-thread create | ~36,170/s | ~37,630/s | ~1.04x |
| 8-thread create | ~23,926/s | ~25,268/s | ~1.06x (NEW won all 4 interleaved pairs) |

CORRECTNESS: provably identical end state (Unregistered + read_your_writes); the eliminated register+release net to nothing. e2fsck 0 errors (create + mkdir), ffs-core lib 1185/0. Helps every writable FsOp (create/mkdir/rename/delete). Env-revertible. This is the only safe-Rust dent in the parallel-convoy `active_snapshots` half (the gate half stays refuted/owner-lane); modest because the convoy is gate-dominated, but real and free.

### 2026-06-29 SCORECARD: cumulative measured impact of the 3 single-thread create-path wins vs kernel (CrimsonFox)

The three wins this stretch — byte-wise inode-bitmap padding (369f1493), name-index-before-htree (c47c9009), and drop-wasteful-snapshot-register+release (a2509ad7) — measured head-to-head vs a real ext4 kernel loop mount (single-thread, local release binary, best-of-3):

| op | kernel | FrankenFS BEFORE | FrankenFS AFTER (3 wins) | shift |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| create | ~35,070/s | ~29,400/s (0.84x) | ~37,842/s (**1.08x**) | **0.84x → 1.08x — flipped to BEATING kernel** |
| mkdir | ~21,844/s | ~15,819/s (0.72x) | ~18,076/s (0.83x) | 0.72x → 0.83x (improved; residual = inherent dir-block init + owner-lane commit) |
| rename | ~30,900/s | (already ahead) | ~45,528/s (**1.47x**) | strong (benefits from padding + adapter wins) |

So single-thread ext4 metadata is now create-AHEAD / rename-AHEAD / mkdir-0.83x. CODE-READ CONFIRMATION the create/mkdir hot paths are now lean (no remaining clean lever ≥ a few %): adapter creation is cheap post-register-fix (Arc clone + atomic, no lock); `current_snapshot()` is a single atomic load; the parent inode is read once and threaded (not re-read); mkdir's single dir-extent is stored INLINE in i_block (`ffs_btree::insert` allocates no separate tree block); mkdir's 3 adapter re-creations are redundant-but-cheap (read-your-writes reads live current, shared cache ref — not worth eliminating). The remaining measured gaps are owner-lane (the 5.5x PARALLEL convoy, gate-blocking; mkdir's per-op commit) — NOT safe-Rust per-op shaving, which is now measured-exhausted on this path.

### 2026-06-29 SURFACED BLOCKER: parallel random-read 3.3x lever scoped (ShardedCache Mutex→RwLock) but unmeasurable — rand-read data-fixture gap (CrimsonFox)

With the write path mined (3 wins, single-thread create now beats kernel) and the register+release round-trip lens exhausted (that pattern's only hot-path instance was already fixed in a2509ad7), the next-biggest measured gap is PARALLEL random read (~3.3x residual after the 88x→3.3x new_unregistered fix 9376f4d6). PRECISELY SCOPED the lever by reading `ShardedCache` (lib.rs:1373): `get()` is already lean (`shard.lock().get().cloned()`, no LRU bump), but the shards are `Mutex<BTreeMap>` — 64 concurrent readers of the SAME hot inode block all map to ONE shard and SERIALIZE on its exclusive Mutex. LEVER: `Mutex`→`RwLock` per shard so `get()` takes a SHARED read lock (concurrent gets on the same shard proceed in parallel); insert/remove take write(). parking_lot RwLock-read ≈ Mutex-lock uncontended, so the single-thread create path I just optimized should not regress — but that must be MEASURED, not assumed (per the lookup-early-exit lesson).

BLOCKER (why not landed this turn): cannot build a rand-read data fixture via the CLI to measure the actual parallel-read win. `rand-read`/`write-bench` require a PRE-SIZED file with data; `create-bench` makes only EMPTY files (`/t{t}/cb_{t}_{i:08}`), and `write-bench` refuses an empty/too-small file ("file 0 B smaller than write size") rather than extending it. The real-kernel route (mount + dd + umount) is umount-blocked. So the MEASURED ratio the campaign requires is unobtainable for this lever right now.

ACTIONABLE NEXT STEP (unblock then land): add a CLI affordance to create a sized data file (e.g. write-bench `--create`/`--extend`, or a `fallocate`+fill subcommand), OR identify a path inside an existing data-bearing image (e.g. /data/tmp/indfrag_*.img). Then env-toggle A/B the Mutex→RwLock ShardedCache change on parallel rand-read (target) + single-thread create (regression gate) + conformance/e2fsck. The remaining big lever beyond this stays the owner-lane parallel-WRITE convoy (gate-blocking).

### 2026-06-29 REFUTED (~0-gain, shelved): ShardedCache Mutex→RwLock for parallel reads; + LANDED write-bench --create fixture tooling (CrimsonFox)

Acting on last turn's surfaced blocker, added `write-bench --create` (create the file in its parent + pre-fill `count*size` bytes + `sync_all_to_device`) so a sized rand-read data fixture is buildable without a kernel mount — UNBLOCKING parallel random-read measurement. Then implemented the scoped lever: `ShardedCache` shards `Mutex<BTreeMap>` → `RwLock<BTreeMap>` (get/contains → shared `read()`, insert/remove/clear → `write()`) so concurrent gets on the same hot shard share instead of serializing.

MEASURED (env-built A/B, same fixture /bigfile 64MB, rand-read --parallel --seed 42 count=200000; create regression gate count=10000):
| | Mutex (baseline) | RwLock | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| parallel rand-read | ~7.44M IOPS | ~7.53M IOPS | ~1.01x (NEUTRAL, heavy overlap) |
| single-thread create | ~37,361/s | ~36,747/s | ~0.98x (slight regression) |

NEUTRAL → REVERTED (shelved stash `rwlock-cache-AB`, not dropped). ROOT CAUSE: a WARM fixture's parallel random read runs at ~7.5M IOPS — it is NOT Mutex-contention-bound; the Mutex hold (BTreeMap get + Arc clone) is too brief to contend even same-shard at that rate, and the per-inode extent cache means readers don't all hammer one inode-block shard. The 3.3x parallel-read residual (memory) was measured COLD (/data ext4 dense + drop_caches), which needs root to reproduce — not reproducible on a warm local fixture, so the RwLock benefit (if any) is unmeasurable here and the change is a measured no-win on the workload I CAN run. LESSON: the hot-shard-contention hypothesis didn't survive measurement once a real fixture existed — warm random read is throughput/overhead-bound, not lock-bound. KEPT: `write-bench --create` (e2fsck-clean fixture, valid fs) lands as reusable read-path measurement tooling that resolves the prior blocker.

### 2026-06-29 ⭐SHIPPED (MEASURED perf win): borrow the hot inode from its Arc instead of deep-cloning per read — single-thread random read 1.10x (CrimsonFox)

Profiling warm parallel random read (enabled by the new write-bench --create fixture) showed `Ext4Inode::clone` at ~6.6% + associated malloc/sdallocx churn. `read_into`'s lock-free hot-inode fast path served the parsed inode from an ArcSwap slot but then DEEP-CLONED the `Ext4Inode` out of the Arc on every read (`(*slot).clone()`), and the struct carries a heap `xattr_ibody: Vec<u8>` (never touched on the read path) — so each read paid a heap alloc+free for a field it ignores. The comment even mislabeled it a "cheap struct copy". FIX: on a hot hit, BORROW the inode straight out of the Arc (`slot.as_ref()`, `inode: &Ext4Inode`); on a miss, own the parsed inode locally. Every downstream use was already `&inode`.

MEASURED (clean same-binary A/B, fixture /bigfile 64MB, rand-read --seed 42 count=200000):
| | with per-read clone | borrow from Arc | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| single-thread random read | ~460,612 IOPS | ~507,541 IOPS | **1.10x** |
| parallel random read | ~7.88M IOPS | ~7.87M IOPS | ~1.00x (neutral; parallel is bandwidth/dispatch-bound, sublinear ~17x scaling, so per-read CPU savings don't move the shared-bottleneck aggregate) |

Single-thread is the clean per-read-latency signal and the win helps EVERY `read_into` (every FUSE read goes through this path), even where parallel aggregate is bw-bound (lower per-read CPU frees cores). Correctness: read-only inode, borrow vs clone is identical data; ffs-core lib 1185/0 (read path). RO-only fast path (writable mount unaffected).

### 2026-06-29 DIG: read path mined post inode-borrow win — single-thread rand-read now IO-bound; btrfs RO + read_file_data already optimal (CrimsonFox)

Re-profiled single-thread random read WITH the inode-borrow win (00a2bdb1) in: `Ext4Inode::clone` is GONE; the profile is now dominated by KERNEL (~27%: device read / page-cache / syscall) + memmove/memset (~6.7%, inherent data copy), with userspace FS minimal (read_file_data 2.0%, read_into 1.7%). Single-thread ext4 random read is now IO/kernel-bound — the inode-borrow was the last clean userspace lever on it. Checked the sibling read sub-paths for the same "heap struct cloned per read" pattern and found them already optimal:
- **btrfs RO read** (`btrfs_read_file_into` hot path, bd-n5w92): serves the cached extent list via `Arc::clone` (cheap refcount, not deep clone) and `btrfs_filter_window_extents` by ref — already the borrow form my ext4 fix introduced.
- **read_file_data**: zero-fills only HOLE segments; data-bearing segments read in place (zero-copy, "each read lands in place") — no redundant whole-buffer memset. The profile's memset is the BENCH's per-read `vec![0; size]` (harness, not FS).
- **parallel rand-read**: bandwidth/dispatch-bound (~7.88M IOPS, sublinear ~17x scaling) — per-read CPU levers (inode-borrow, RwLock) are all neutral there.

CONCLUSION: the read path's clean, safe, measurable FS levers are now mined (ext4 inode-borrow shipped; btrfs RO + read_file_data already optimal; single-thread IO-bound; parallel bw-bound). Remaining read costs are inherent (device IO, data copy). The only remaining read lever class is the WRITABLE-mount path (ext4 read skips the RO hot-inode slot; btrfs writable re-descends the COW tree per read) — both need write-invalidation-aware caching (harder, less-common scenario). The session's biggest remaining gap stays the owner-lane parallel-WRITE convoy.

### 2026-06-29 DIG: walk/stat already allocation-optimized — metadata read/write/walk hot paths all mined (CrimsonFox)

Profiled `walk` (recursive readdir + getattr-per-entry; 40002 stats) — top userspace fn `Ext4Inode::parse_from_bytes_with_ibody` 8.17%, then ShardedCache::get 2.63%, rest kernel/IO. Investigated the parse for the same heap-alloc-per-op pattern the inode-borrow win (00a2bdb1) exploited, and traced getattr → `read_live_inode_metadata` → reader `read_inode_metadata` → `parse_metadata_from_bytes` (`MetadataOnly`): it ALREADY skips the xattr_ibody `to_vec()` (the Full parser's per-inode alloc), and `extent_bytes: Ext4InodeBlockBytes = SmallVec<[u8;64]>` stores the 60-byte i_block INLINE (no heap alloc). So the metadata parse does ZERO heap allocations per stat — the 8.17% is inherent CPU field-decode of ~40 inode fields, not a redundancy. (Stat is ~0.72µs/entry: walk-with-stat 38.9ms vs walk --no-stat 10.2ms over 40k entries.)

A pure-attr parse decoding only the ~10 InodeAttr fields could shave maybe ~4% of walk (~1.04x) but needs a new parse variant usable only where extents/dir-state are never touched (not readdir), and the gain is release-perf-inflated — deferred (low-EV, maintenance cost).

CONCLUSION: with read (inode-borrow win + IO-bound), write (3 wins, create beats kernel), and walk/stat (already alloc-optimized) all confirmed mined, the clean safe-Rust per-op lever space across the measurable metadata workloads is exhausted. Session net: 4 measured wins (369f1493 padding, c47c9009 lookup-index, a2509ad7 snapshot-roundtrip, 00a2bdb1 inode-borrow) + write-bench --create tooling. The sole remaining large gap is the owner-lane parallel-WRITE convoy (gate-blocking serialization; intra-op batching).

### 2026-06-29 REFUTED (measured REGRESSION, shelved): batch ext4_create's inode-alloc writes into one MVCC txn — 0.92x single / 0.96x parallel (CrimsonFox)

Located the convoy lever's machinery: `ext4_add_dir_entry` ALREADY batches its writes via `mvcc_store.begin()` + `TransactionBlockAdapter` (stages writes, read-your-writes via `staged_write`) + one `commit`, but `ext4_create`'s `create_inode` call uses the per-block `block_device_adapter` (a commit per block write). Mirrored add_dir_entry's exact pattern to wrap create_inode in one txn (commit once on success, drop staged on error so create_inode's in-memory geo/groups rollback stays authoritative). e2fsck CLEAN on single AND 8-thread create (the TransactionBlockAdapter pattern is correct — unlike the prior thread-local op_batch that corrupted the GDT).

MEASURED (clean same-binary A/B, create-bench):
| | baseline (per-block) | create_inode batched | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| single-thread create | ~38,486/s | ~35,493/s | ~0.92x (REGRESSION) |
| 8-thread create | ~26,047/s | ~25,038/s | ~0.96x (REGRESSION) |

ROOT CAUSE / KEY CONVOY FINDING: the per-block commit path is LEAN (~700ns machinery, measured prior), and `create_inode` issues only a FEW block writes — so the `TransactionBlockAdapter` staging overhead (a parking_lot `Mutex<&mut Transaction>` lock + `BTreeMap` stage_write/staged_write on EVERY read and write, including the bitmap/GDT RMW reads) EXCEEDS the savings from collapsing those few commits into one. Corroborates the prior "in-memory MVCC batching = only 1.06x" decomposition (5dafad44): the parallel-create 5.5x convoy is NOT commit COUNT or per-commit COST — it is the in-order `CommitPublicationGate` BLOCKING (threads waiting for the prior seq to publish). Reducing commit count via the heavyweight staging adapter cannot beat the lean per-block path and does not relieve the blocking. REVERTED (shelved stash `create-inode-batch-AB`). The genuine convoy fix would need either a far leaner staging path (no per-op Mutex/BTreeMap) or out-of-order publication (a major MVCC snapshot-visibility redesign) — both deeper owner-lane than a per-op-adapter swap.

### 2026-06-29 DIG (convoy closure): the parallel-write 5.5x is fundamental in-order-publication blocking — gate already optimal, all per-op approaches measured-dead (CrimsonFox)

Read `CommitPublicationGate::publish` (sharded.rs:72) to look for a batch-drain/wakeup lever distinct from the twice-refuted CAS fast-paths. Found the gate is ALREADY optimal for its model: `advance_ready_prefix` (60) loops to publish EVERY contiguously-ready commit in one pass (already batch-drains), and `notify_all` (86) is REQUIRED — one prefix advance can make multiple waiters ready at once, so `notify_one` would strand them (deadlock/delay). So the gate's own machinery has no clean lever.

This closes the convoy investigation. The parallel-create 5.5x convoy is the FUNDAMENTAL in-order-publication wall-clock blocking: a committer with seq N+1 must wait for seq N to publish so the high-water-mark snapshot (`current_snapshot = completed contiguous seq`) stays gap-free. Everything per-op has now been measured/reasoned dead:
- gate CAS fast-path (retry-spin + CAS-once): REFUTED (busy-spin / cache-line thrash).
- snapshot register+release round-trip: the ONE clean win here (a2509ad7, 1.04-1.06x) — already landed.
- commit-count batching (TransactionBlockAdapter for create_inode): REFUTED measured 0.92x/0.96x REGRESSION (f1c4880c) — staging Mutex+BTreeMap overhead on read-heavy alloc exceeds the lean per-block commit it replaces.
- gate batch-drain / targeted-wakeup: already done (advance_ready_prefix) / unsafe (notify_one deadlocks).

The ONLY remaining convoy fix is to remove the in-order constraint itself: out-of-order publication with a snapshot-visibility model that tolerates gaps (per-seq visibility set or epoch-batched async publication decoupling commit from publish). That is a major MVCC redesign, loom-gated (no loom infra in-tree → not shippable blind), and a focused multi-turn effort — NOT a 60m per-op land. Session's clean safe-Rust frontier (read/write/walk + every per-op convoy approach) is now exhaustively mapped: 4 measured wins banked (369f1493, c47c9009, a2509ad7, 00a2bdb1); the sole open gap requires the redesign above.

### 2026-06-29 DIG (consolidation): biggest remaining gaps both confirmed owner-lane this session — GroupCommit wiring (960x durable, machinery EXISTS) is #1 (CrimsonFox)

After exhausting the clean per-op space (4 wins; read/write/walk/convoy-batching all won or measured-dead), re-examined the two largest gaps for a safe lever:
- **inode-bitmap scan** (`try_alloc_inode_in_group_persist` → `bitmap_find_free(.., start=0)`): scans from group start per create (no cursor), BUT `bitmap_find_free` is already word-at-a-time (64 bits/iter) and is NOT in the create profile (<1.3% self) — a cursor/hint would be a sub-1% marginal win on an already-fast scan. Not worth the added cursor state + reset correctness. Deferred.
- **GroupCommitCoordinator** (ffs-journal wal_buffer.rs:933): confirmed built+tested but UNWIRED — 0 refs in ffs-core/ffs-fuse. `commit_transaction_journaled` (lib.rs:6902) still does a JBD2 txn + fdatasync PER FsOp (~9ms/op fsync barrier). This is the campaign's LARGEST gap vs kernel: the kernel's JBD2 group-commits (one fsync amortizes many txns), so FrankenFS's durable mode is ~960x slower per-op (MEASURED at the journal level, cc_group_commit_fsync.rs: per-op 9100µs vs group 9.2µs). Wiring `GroupCommitCoordinator.flush_epoch` into the journaled commit path = closing a ~960x vs-kernel durable gap with EXISTING machinery (integration, not invention).

CONSOLIDATED next-focus targets (both owner-lane, both need focused multi-turn effort — NOT 60m per-op lands, and not safe to blind-ship given prior GDT-corruption history):
1. **GroupCommit wiring (960x, durable mode)** — route journaled fsync callers through epoch-batched group commit; the proof-obligations are spec'd (048290cf: 5 concurrency-automata obligations — un-fsync'd epoch must not report durable; fsync callers block until epoch-durable). Needs a durable-mode FS bench (attach_jbd2_writer + per-op fsync) to A/B at FS level. HIGHEST value.
2. **Convoy out-of-order publication (5.5x, default parallel write)** — remove the in-order CommitPublicationGate constraint via a gap-tolerant snapshot model; loom-gated (no loom in-tree).

The per-op safe-Rust frontier is exhausted; both remaining levers are integration/redesign work. The session banked 4 measured wins (369f1493, c47c9009, a2509ad7, 00a2bdb1) + write-bench --create tooling.

### 2026-06-29 DIG: btrfs-rename node-pool primitive REFUTED a priori — MVCC version-retention defers frees past the mutation burst (CrimsonFox)

btrfs rename 0.43x (the biggest single-op vs-kernel gap) is CoW-node-churn-bound (profiled: insert_into 8.6% + drop_glue 8.6% + BtrfsCowNode::clone 4.8% = ~22%, allocator round-trips). The one untried "different primitive" was an allocation-recycling NODE POOL (vs the already-refuted clone-reduction / staged-internals lever 0adac5d6). Read the node lifecycle (`InMemoryCowBtrfsTree`, lib.rs:3266): `alloc_node` inserts a fresh (cloned-Vec) node into `self.nodes`; `retire_node` only pushes the old block to `staged_deferred_frees`; `commit_retired_nodes` moves it to `deferred_frees` (the block stays in `self.nodes` for snapshot reads); the actual drop happens later at prune/checkpoint. So within a rename burst the retired nodes are NOT freed — their Vec backings are unavailable to recycle into the next mutation's clone. A node pool therefore cannot relieve the burst churn (alloc-per-mutation, free-at-prune timing mismatch), and across-burst pooling needs frequent prune which the workload doesn't do. REFUTED without building (the lifecycle proves it inert). The CoW clone is inherent (COW requires a new node version; MVCC requires retaining the old for snapshot isolation) — matching the prior staged-internals neutral result.

This was the last untried per-op "different primitive" on a measurable gap. Full session lever-map: ext4 read (won, IO-bound), ext4 write (3 wins, create beats kernel), walk/stat (alloc-optimal), parallel-write convoy (in-order-publication blocking, needs out-of-order redesign), durable fsync (GroupCommit 960x, machinery exists, needs wiring), btrfs rename (CoW inherent), inode-scan (word-at-a-time, not hot). Clean safe-Rust per-op space exhausted; remaining = the two owner-lane integration/redesign levers (GroupCommit wiring #1, convoy out-of-order #2).

### 2026-06-29 DIG: GroupCommit lever is deeper than "wiring" — WAL-coordinator vs JBD2-commit-path mechanism mismatch (CrimsonFox)

Read both ends of the proposed GroupCommit wiring to assess 60m-feasibility:
- `commit_transaction_journaled` (lib.rs:6902) commits via the **JBD2** writer (`jbd2_writer`, ext4-compatible journal) and explicitly requires the single-lock MVCC store; it does preflight_fcw → resolved_writes → JBD2 txn → fsync PER FsOp.
- `GroupCommitCoordinator` (ffs-journal wal_buffer.rs:950) operates on **WAL** entries with epochs (`FileWalWriter`, `flush_epoch` partitions entries by epoch, marks epoch durable, notifies waiters). The MEASURED 960x (cc_group_commit_fsync.rs) was on THIS WAL path, not JBD2.

So the two are DIFFERENT journal subsystems. "Wire the existing coordinator into the journaled-commit path" is NOT a drop-in call — it requires either (a) routing FS durable commits through the WAL coordinator instead of JBD2 (loses ext4 JBD2 journal compatibility — major), or (b) implementing JBD2-level group-commit (batch N FsOps' JBD2 txns, fsync once — a new impl in Jbd2Writer/commit_transaction_journaled, what real ext4 jbd2 does). Both are owner-lane and crash-recovery-gated (durability: a returned-durable caller must survive crash; validation needs the crash-injection suite writeback_crash_matrix/crash_replay, NOT e2fsck) — corrects the prior "integration not invention" optimism (048290cf): the coordinator exists but does not directly fit the JBD2 path.

DEFINITIVE FORWARD MAP (both remaining levers confirmed deep multi-turn, each needing specialized validation infra a 60m blind land cannot provide):
1. **Durable group-commit** (960x): JBD2-level batching (option b) — owner-lane, crash-injection-validated, needs a concurrent durable-mode bench.
2. **Parallel-write convoy** (5.5x): out-of-order publication — owner-lane MVCC redesign, loom-validated.
Clean safe-Rust per-op space exhausted this session (4 wins: 369f1493, c47c9009, a2509ad7, 00a2bdb1).

### 2026-06-29 REFUTED (measured NEUTRAL, shelved): convert `ShardedCache` shards from `Mutex<BTreeMap>` to `RwLock<BTreeMap>` to relieve same-key parallel-read contention (CrimsonFox)

The one un-tried follow-up from the parallel-random-read 88x→3.3x fix (9376f4d6) was the named residual "ShardedCache shard-Mutex on the hot inode block (WordLock 3.79%)". The proposed different primitive: make each shard an `RwLock` so concurrent readers hitting the SAME hot key (e.g. many parallel readers all faulting the one inode-table/data block that every read resolves) take a shared read lock and proceed in parallel, instead of serializing on one shard's exclusive `Mutex`. The RO block/inode/extent caches are read-mostly (filled once on miss, then all hits), so steady state is read-only — the textbook RwLock-wins shape. Implemented in `ShardedCache` (ffs-core lib.rs ~1364): `Box<[RwLock<BTreeMap>]>`, `get`/`contains_key` → `.read()`, `insert*`/`remove`/`clear` → `.write()`. Compiles clean; contained to the cache impl.

MEASURED (`ffs-cli rand-read --parallel`, 600M ext4 fixture, 16-rayon-thread random read, clean two-binary build new-vs-baseline, /data ext4):
- **Spread workload** (400MB file = ~100k data blocks): interleaved A/B = WASH (NEW min 40315µs vs OLD min 39431µs). The cross-block sequential compare initially showed ~1.09x but interleaving killed it = time-correlated noise (the classic trap). Reads spread across all 16 shards → no single hot key → `Mutex` already gives full parallelism.
- **Maximal hot-key workload** (16-block file, 300k reads → every reader hammers the same few shard keys): interleaved A/B = NEUTRAL-to-slightly-negative (NEW min 31458µs vs OLD min 30107µs).

ROOT CAUSE / KEY FINDING: the cache critical section is too SHORT for RwLock to pay off. A hit is `BTreeMap::get` (tiny map) + `Arc<[u8]>::clone` (one refcount bump) = a few tens of ns. parking_lot `Mutex` acquire/release on so short a section is cheaper than `RwLock::read` (whose reader-count CAS is a heavier atomic), so RwLock's extra per-acquire cost slightly EXCEEDS the same-key read-parallelism it buys even under maximal contention. The "3.79% WordLock" residual that motivated this was measured on the OLD per-read snapshot register/release-heavy path (pre-9376f4d6); on today's lean read path the shard lock is no longer a bottleneck. REVERTED (shelved stash). Confirms the parallel-random-read path is already lock-optimal: no clean per-op cache-concurrency lever remains; the residual vs kernel is materialize/coordination, not shard-lock contention.

### 2026-06-29 NEW GAP MEASURED (unmined op): ext4 unlink/delete ~1.75x slower than kernel — double htree descent candidate lever (CrimsonFox)

Following the "measure an UNMINED op" pattern that yielded mkdir 2.77x, measured `ffs-cli delbench` (create N files untimed, then time unlink-all + final flush) vs a live kernel ext4 RW loop mount (same shape: create untimed, unlink timed, `sync()` at end), N=20000, 400M ext4 fixture on /data:
- **kernel ext4** (loop mount, /tmp/kdel.c create+unlink+sync): 15.50 us/op (64537 unlinks/s)
- **FrankenFS** (delbench, stable across 3 runs): ~26-28 us/op (36000-38000 unlinks/s)
- **ratio: ~1.75x SLOWER vs ext4 kernel** — a real, previously-uncharacterized gap (unlink/rmdir were not in the mined set; create/rename/mkdir/read/write all won or measured-dead).

CANDIDATE LEVER (not yet built/measured — surfaced for next turn): `ext4_unlink_impl` (lib.rs:18536) does a **double htree descent per unlink**. `lookup_name` (12457) -> `htree_lookup_name_authoritative` descends the dx index, reads the target leaf, scans it to return the `Ext4DirEntry` (for the child inode). Then the remove path (18614) calls `htree_target_leaf_block` (a SECOND descent) -> `resolve_logical` -> `read_block_vec(target_phys)` (re-reads the SAME leaf) -> `ffs_dir::remove_entry` (re-scans it). So per unlink the leaf is located twice, read twice, scanned twice. `remove_entry` (ffs-dir lib.rs:276) already has the removed entry's inode (`cur_ino`, line 342) but returns only `bool`. A variant returning `Option<u32>` would let unlink descend+remove ONCE and skip the separate `lookup_name`, provided the validation reorder (immutable/IsDirectory/NotEmpty currently run BEFORE remove) is preserved via txn-drop-on-error (TransactionBlockAdapter discards staged writes when `result.is_ok()` is false). NEEDS a profile to confirm the descent (vs MVCC commit/version-retention, the create-residual cause) is the dominant share before building; reads ARE cached so the cost is recomputed descent + 4KB leaf to_vec, not device I/O. NOT yet validated; conformance + e2fsck gate required (reorder touches deletion correctness).

### 2026-06-29 SHIPPED: ext4 unlink/rmdir one-pass removal — ~1.08x (closes part of the 1.75x-vs-kernel gap) (CrimsonFox)

Built + measured + landed the candidate lever above. `ext4_unlink_impl` did a DOUBLE htree descent per op: `lookup_name` descended the dx index + read + scanned the hash-target leaf just to learn the child inode, then the remove path descended AGAIN (`htree_target_leaf_block` + resolve + re-read + re-scan the SAME leaf). Added `ffs_dir::remove_entry_take_inode` (returns the removed dirent's inode; `remove_entry` now delegates to it, all bool callers unchanged) and reordered `ext4_unlink_impl` to remove-then-validate: the single descent both removes the entry AND yields `child_ino`, eliminating the separate `lookup_name`. Validation (immutable/append reject, IsDirectory, rmdir-empty) runs AFTER the staged removal — any rejection returns Err so the MVCC txn is dropped uncommitted and nothing persists (observably identical to check-first). Preserved: inline-data-dir rejection re-asserted explicitly (was inside lookup_name; bd-4y9ca caught its loss), and the linear-scan fallback still runs on any htree miss (no authoritative-absent shortcut — avoids any spurious-ENOENT risk on a misrouted entry; absent-name unlink stays O(N) as before via the fallback).

MEASURED (clean two-binary interleaved A/B, delbench/rdbench N=20000, 400M ext4 /data, fresh fixture copy per run):
- **unlink (delbench)**: NEW ~37000/s vs OLD ~34000/s — every one of 5 interleaved runs separated (NEW min 35973 > OLD max 35010) = **~1.08x**.
- **rmdir (rdbench)**: NEW ~20200/s vs OLD ~19000/s (3/3 runs) = **~1.05x** (smaller share — rmdir also does the empty-check read).
- vs kernel ext4 (15.5 us/op via live loop mount): unlink ~27 us/op -> ~26 us/op, so 1.75x -> ~1.68x. The residual is the per-op MVCC commit + version-retention (same structural cause as the create/write residual), NOT the dir-block work.

GATES: conformance 100/0/2 GREEN; ffs-dir 88/0; ffs-core lib 1185/0 (the prior 1184/1 failure was exactly the inline-data check, now restored); **e2fsck byte-identical to baseline** (12/102400 files, 10811/102400 blocks after 15000 deletes, both binaries). A real, modest, correctness-clean per-op win on a previously-unmined op.

### 2026-06-29 NEGATIVE (parity, no lever): standalone ext4 lookup is already at/above kernel — metadata per-op frontier confirmed exhausted (CrimsonFox)

After landing the unlink one-pass win, swept the remaining unmined op for a gap: standalone name lookup (every metadata op pays it, so a gap there would be high-leverage). Measured `ffs-cli lookup-bench` vs a kernel `stat()` harness (/tmp/klookup.c: create 30000 files, warm-stat all, then time 200000 random `stat`s), 30k-entry htree dir, 1200M ext4 (200k inodes) on /data:
- **kernel ext4** (loop mount): 379393 lookups/s = 2.636 us/op
- **FrankenFS** (lookup-bench, 3 runs): ~410000-455000/s = ~2.3 us/op
- **ratio: ~1.13x FASTER than kernel** (note: kernel side is full `stat()` = lookup + getattr, frankenfs side is name->inode resolution, so not perfectly apples-to-apples, but frankenfs is clearly at parity or better — no lever). The c47c9009 lookup-index fast path + htree hash-target routing already put lookup at kernel parity.

This completes the ext4 metadata per-op map for the campaign: read (won, IO-bound), write/create (won, beats kernel), mkdir (won 2.77x), rename (won, 1.71x faster than kernel), unlink/rmdir (won this session ~1.08x/~1.05x), lookup (parity/faster), walk/stat (alloc-optimal). The rename source-removal does an analogous positive-lookup-then-rescan double-touch that `remove_entry_take_inode` could in principle collapse, but rename is already net faster than kernel and its add-before-remove ordering is crash-safety-delicate — no headroom, high risk, declined. The clean safe-Rust per-op metadata frontier is exhausted; remaining gaps are the owner-lane integration/redesign levers (durable group-commit 960x — JBD2-level batching, crash-injection-gated; parallel-write convoy 5.5x — out-of-order publication, loom-gated) and CoW-inherent btrfs rename.

### 2026-06-29 DIG (convoy re-measured on current main + root-cause confirmed at the source): parallel create NEGATIVE-scales; fix is loom-gated (CrimsonFox)

With the ext4 per-op serial metadata frontier exhausted (read/write/create/mkdir/rename/unlink/rmdir/lookup all won or at parity), turned to the biggest DEFAULT-path gap vs kernel: the parallel-write convoy. Re-measured precisely on current-main `ffs-cli create-bench --threads` (96000 creates total, each writer in its own subdir to isolate MVCC-lock contention from dir-block contention, 1500M ext4 / 300k inodes on /data):
- 1 thread:  33337 creates/s
- 8 threads: 23731 creates/s (**0.71x** of serial)
- 16 threads: 24053 creates/s (0.72x)
- 32 threads: 21816 creates/s (0.65x)

NEGATIVE scaling — adding writer threads makes create SLOWER than single-threaded, plateauing ~22-24k/s. Kernel ext4 scales positively here, so this is the campaign's biggest default-path vs-kernel gap.

ROOT CAUSE confirmed at the source (not just inferred): `ShardedMvccStore` keeps `active_snapshots: RwLock<BTreeMap<CommitSeq, u64>>` (sharded.rs:202). Every txn lifecycle calls `register_snapshot` (sharded.rs:950) and `release_snapshot` (sharded.rs:959), and BOTH take `active_snapshots.write()` — a SINGLE global exclusive lock. With the per-block-commit write path (`FsMvccBlockDevice::write_block` commits per block), a create issues several txns, each a register+release pair, so N parallel writers serialize on this one write lock (write-side analog of the random-read 88x register/release gap fixed in 9376f4d6 for the RO path via `new_unregistered`). The lock HOLD time is microscopic (one BTreeMap entry op) — the cost is acquisition FREQUENCY × contention, which is why it inverts scaling rather than just flattening it. (The in-order `CommitPublicationGate`, sharded.rs, is the second global serializer; its own machinery was already measured optimal — CAS fast-paths refuted, batch-drain already present.)

WHY NOT A 60m LAND: the clean fix is to shard the `active_snapshots` refcount map (register/release lock only one sub-shard by `CommitSeq`; `watermark()`/`prune_safe` take all sub-shard locks to preserve the prune-vs-register TOCTOU and the documented `active_snapshots → shards → contention_metrics` lock-ordering hierarchy). It is implementable, but it mutates the MVCC visibility/prune-safety invariant under concurrency, and **there is no loom (or any model-checker) infra in `ffs-mvcc`** (confirmed: 0 loom refs in crate + Cargo.toml; the sharded.rs:2140 "concurrency-automata" are hand-rolled threaded tests, not exhaustive) — so a subtle register/prune TOCTOU could silently corrupt snapshot isolation and would not be caught. Not shippable blind in-window. FORWARD SPEC for an owner with loom: shard `active_snapshots` into K `Mutex<BTreeMap<CommitSeq,u64>>` keyed by `CommitSeq % K`; `register`/`release` lock one; `watermark` = min over all (lock-or-read all); `prune_safe` acquires all K (in index order) BEFORE the version-shard locks to keep the TOCTOU barrier and ordering. The intra-op transaction-batching alternative (1 txn/FsOp → 1 register/release) is separately REFUTED (f1c4880c: TransactionBlockAdapter staging Mutex+BTreeMap overhead exceeds the savings, 0.92x single / 0.96x parallel).

### 2026-06-29 MEASURED + CORRECTION: ext4 serial create is ~1.16x SLOWER than kernel (not "beats kernel"); residual is inherent per-op MVCC version-materialization (CrimsonFox)

Probed the serial create path for an O(N^2) signature (like the rename preflight bug 51294142) and for a vs-kernel gap. `ffs-cli create-bench --threads 1`, 1500M ext4 / 300k inodes on /data, files in root (htree):
- per-op time is FLAT in dir size: count 10k -> 28.41 us/op, 40k -> 29.13, 100k -> 29.24, 160k -> 30.55. So create is O(N) total (no preflight O(N^2); the slight rise is htree gaining a level = deeper descent). Create is algorithmically clean.
- vs kernel (loop-mounted ext4, /tmp/kcreate.c, 100k O_CREAT|O_EXCL + one final sync): **kernel 25.31 us/op (39515/s) vs FrankenFS ~29.24 us/op (33337-34000/s) = ~1.16x SLOWER**.

CORRECTION: a prior note claimed FrankenFS "create beats kernel" — on this /data ext4 loop baseline with a matched single-flush-at-end shape, it does NOT; it is ~1.16x slower. (The earlier claim was likely a tmpfs/different-config or different-baseline measurement.)

The flat per-op time localizes the residual precisely: it is NOT accumulation (version chains growing with N would make per-op rise — it doesn't) and NOT an algorithmic scan; it is the CONSTANT per-op cost of materializing a few retained 4KiB MVCC version buffers per create (inode bitmap + inode table + GDT + dir leaf + parent inode), each a fresh allocation + first-touch page fault. The kernel pays this once per block in its page cache (in-place overwrite of an already-faulted page); FrankenFS pays it per FsOp because each commit retains a new immutable version for snapshot isolation. This is the SAME inherent MVCC-vs-in-place-pagecache residual measured for unlink (1.68x) and write — confirmed structural, not a redundancy. The create-inode commit-batching lever that would amortize the few per-block commits into one is separately REFUTED (f1c4880c, 0.92x: TransactionBlockAdapter staging Mutex+BTreeMap overhead exceeds the savings). No clean per-op lever; reducing it requires the version-model / group-commit changes that are owner-lane.

### 2026-06-29 REFUTED (measured NEUTRAL, shelved): `write_block_owned` to elide the staging copy in MVCC block writes (CrimsonFox)

Profile of serial create (`perf record` on the release binary; stripped, so libc+kernel frames only) showed **43% of create time in `__memmove_avx_unaligned_erms`**, ~35% of that under the kernel page-fault handler — i.e. 4 KiB block copies + first-touch faults dominate. Traced one avoidable copy: the read-modify-write metadata pattern (`read_block -> .to_vec()` = COPY 1 -> mutate -> `write_block(&buf)`) hits `BlockDevice::write_block(&self, …, &[u8])`, and both MVCC staging adapters (`TransactionBlockAdapter`, `FsMvccBlockDevice`) then do `stage_write(block, data.to_vec())` = COPY 2 — a second fresh 4 KiB alloc+copy of a buffer the caller already owned. Added `write_block_owned(&self, …, Vec<u8>)` to the trait (delegating default, so all non-staging impls are unchanged), overrode it in both adapters to MOVE the buffer straight into `stage_write` (no COPY 2), and converted the hot dead-after-write callers: inode-table write (`ffs_inode::write_inode`, per create+unlink), the two `ext4_add_dir_entry` leaf writes (per create), and the two unlink dir-leaf writes. Byte-identical by construction (same bytes staged, moved not copied).

MEASURED (clean interleaved two-binary A/B vs HEAD — NOT the stale pre-unlink-win baseline, which would have confounded this with the already-landed unlink one-pass win):
| | NEW (HEAD+owned) | BASE (HEAD) | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| delbench unlinks/s (4 runs) | 41297/40177/38801/38154 | 39219/38585/38541/41901 | mixed (run 4 BASE wins) |
| create-bench total us (3 runs) | 1702902/1749950/1878794 | 1733565/1692265/1813733 | NEW slower 2 of 3 |

NEUTRAL (within run-to-run noise; create slightly negative). REVERTED (shelved `wbowned-MEASURED-NEUTRAL-copy2-elision`).

WHY NEUTRAL despite eliminating a real copy: COPY 2 allocates a 4 KiB buffer the SAME size as the just-freed COPY-1 transient, so the allocator hands back a warm (already-faulted) buffer — the elided work is a cheap ~hundreds-of-ns memcpy into hot cache, not a page fault, so it vanishes in noise. The 43%-memmove cost is NOT the transient staging copy; it is (a) the copy into the RETAINED immutable version (unavoidable without a delta/sub-block version model) and (b) the actual block-write/fault of genuinely cold pages — both inherent to MVCC version retention, not redundancy. Confirms (again) the per-op write residual is the version-materialization model, owner-lane to change; the clean copy-elision surface is empty.

### 2026-06-29 NEW GAP (btrfs, distinct from rename): btrfs create SUPER-LINEARLY degrades vs flat kernel — unbounded CoW-node retention in `self.nodes` (CrimsonFox)

Pivoted off the exhausted ext4 metadata surface to btrfs (writes work: `create-bench` on the 2GB btrfs fixture /data/tmp/btrc_1268087.img succeeds). Measured serial btrfs create per-op vs dir size and vs a live kernel btrfs loop mount (/tmp/kcreate.c, fresh fixture copy per point):
| count | FrankenFS us/op | kernel btrfs us/op |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 2000  | 29.19 | 38.52 (fixed-cost amortized) |
| 8000  | 40.46 | — |
| 20000 | 50.86 | 22.59 |
| 40000 | 61.05 | 22.33 |

Kernel btrfs create is **FLAT ~22 us/op**; FrankenFS **RISES 29 -> 61 us/op** = **~2.7x slower at 40k and still climbing** (~+11 us per ~2-4x growth in N). This is btrfs-SPECIFIC — ext4 create on the same engine is flat (2026-06-29 create note), so it is NOT the shared `ShardedMvccStore`; it is in the in-memory CoW btrfs tree. (This is a NEW gap: the ledger previously characterized only btrfs RENAME 4.6x; btrfs create degradation was unmeasured.)

ROOT CAUSE (code-confirmed, ffs-btrfs lib.rs): `InMemoryCowBtrfsTree.nodes: FxHashMap<u64, BtrfsCowNode>` (3131) holds every allocated node, current AND retired. A create CoWs ~log(N) nodes per insert; `retire_node` (3274) only pushes the old block to `staged_deferred_frees`, `commit_retired_nodes` (3279) moves it to `deferred_frees` + `allocator.defer_free` — but the retired node's ~16 KiB data is NEVER removed from `self.nodes` on the commit path (the only `self.nodes.remove` is in `rollback_allocated_nodes`, 3296). So across a create burst `self.nodes` grows unboundedly: the retained working set (each btrfs node ~16 KiB) climbs, and per-op allocation + cache-miss + first-touch-fault cost climbs with it (perf: ~42% in kernel mm/fault frames at N=40k). The kernel's btrfs periodically commits its running transaction and releases the CoW'd-away nodes, so its per-op stays flat; FrankenFS never prunes intra-burst (the prune/checkpoint that would free `deferred_frees` and drop the nodes is not driven during a sustained write burst — same inert-prune family as bd-cc-prunedriver).

FORWARD LEVER (owner-lane — snapshot-safety, hence surfaced not landed in-window): bound `self.nodes` during a burst by removing a retired node's data once no active MVCC snapshot can still read it through an old root (the btrfs analog of `prune_safe`'s watermark check). Needs the same active-snapshot/watermark gating the ext4 convoy fix needs (loom-validatable TOCTOU), plus a btrfs-root-generation -> retired-node reachability check so a reader at an old root still resolves. A cheaper partial mitigation worth testing: have the periodic flush/checkpoint path (or a size-triggered intra-burst checkpoint) drop `deferred_frees` nodes from `self.nodes` — closer to the kernel's periodic-transaction-commit model. CAVEAT on the kernel baseline: the kernel auto-commits its transaction on its own interval/memory-pressure during the burst (frankenfs does not), so part of the gap is "no intra-burst checkpoint" rather than pure algorithm; but the per-op curve (frankenfs rising vs kernel flat) is a real deployment concern for sustained btrfs metadata bursts.

### 2026-06-29 DIG (btrfs create gap, root-cause VERIFIED + fix-surface mapped): no prune consumer exists; eager-free is owner-lane via public `node_snapshot` (CrimsonFox)

Followed up the btrfs-create-degradation gap (prior entry) to verify the root cause is on the hot path and scope a safe fix:
- VERIFIED hot path: ffs-core's btrfs `fs_tree` (and `root_tree`/`csum_tree`) ARE `InMemoryCowBtrfsTree` (lib.rs:667-683), so create mutates it — the `self.nodes` growth IS the create path, not a side structure.
- RULED OUT the allocator: `fs_tree` uses the default `InMemoryBtrfsAllocator` whose `alloc_block` is O(1) (`next_block += 1`, lib.rs:3074) — the per-op degradation is NOT a growing free-space scan, it is the retained-node working set.
- SHARPENED the cause: `InMemoryCowBtrfsTree::deferred_free_blocks()` (the list of retired CoW-node blocks) has **ZERO consumers in ffs-core** (`grep deferred_free` = no hits in lib.rs), and no checkpoint/prune ever clears `self.nodes` on the commit path (the only `nodes.remove` is `rollback_allocated_nodes`). So retired nodes accumulate for the ENTIRE tree/mount lifetime — this is unbounded, not merely "prune inert"; there is no prune wired at all for the in-memory btrfs tree. (A sustained btrfs metadata-write workload would also grow memory without bound — though btrfs write support is the experimental path, so the deployment blast radius is limited.)
- FIX SURFACE = owner-lane: a retired node cannot be blind-freed because `node_snapshot(block)` (lib.rs:3231) is PUBLIC and looks up arbitrary block numbers (incl. retired) — any external caller (checkpoint/serialization) reading an old block would hit `MissingNode`. A safe bound must (a) wire a `deferred_free_blocks` consumer that removes a retired node from `self.nodes` only once no active MVCC snapshot / old-root generation can still resolve it, and (b) audit all `node_snapshot`/`node_ref` callers for retired-block access. Same snapshot-watermark/TOCTOU gating as the ext4 convoy fix (loom-validatable), no infra in-tree → not blind-shippable in-window.

Net: the btrfs create gap is real, root-caused, and on the hot path; the per-op degradation is unbounded CoW-node retention with no prune consumer; the fix is a well-specified owner-lane prune-wiring (not a per-op redundancy, not the allocator).

### 2026-06-29 REFUTED (test-gated, reverted): eager-free retired btrfs CoW nodes in `commit_retired_nodes` — retained nodes ARE read for previous-version isolation (CrimsonFox)

To convert the btrfs-create-degradation gap's "owner-lane" conclusion from reasoned to PROVEN, attempted the obvious fix empirically with the 365-test ffs-btrfs suite as the safety gate: added `self.nodes.remove(&block)` to `commit_retired_nodes` (lib.rs:3279) so a retired CoW node's ~16 KiB payload is dropped once the mutation commits (hypothesis: the old path is superseded by the advanced root, so nothing resolves a retired block through `self.nodes` again).

RESULT: **3 tests FAILED** — `cow_insert_preserves_previous_root_node`, `update_internal_child_rewrites_parent_and_preserves_order`, `writepath_cow_write_preserves_original` (362 passed). All three explicitly read the PREVIOUS root/nodes via `node_snapshot(old_root)` AFTER a mutation and assert they're intact. So the retention is DELIBERATE: `InMemoryCowBtrfsTree` provides tree-level previous-version reads (snapshot isolation — read the pre-mutation tree by holding the old root block number), and retired nodes must stay until no reader needs that generation. REVERTED (1-line, restored byte-identical).

This empirically PROVES (not just argues) the btrfs node-retention fix is owner-lane: bounding `self.nodes` requires a generation/snapshot-watermark check (free a retired node only once no held old-root can reach it) — the same loom-gated TOCTOU discipline as the ext4 convoy and ext4 prune, with no model-checker infra in `ffs-btrfs`. Closes the btrfs-create-degradation investigation: real gap, hot-path-verified root cause, allocator ruled out, and the fix is now test-proven owner-lane. The btrfs metadata write surface joins ext4 as exhausted for clean safe-Rust per-op levers; both subsystems' remaining gaps are the version-retention/prune/durability layer.

### 2026-06-29 NEGATIVE (no gap, completes the metadata-op map): ext4 walk (readdir+stat) is at/above kernel (CrimsonFox)

Measured the last unmeasured ext4 metadata workload — recursive walk (readdir + stat each entry, the `ls -lR`/`find`/`git status` shape) — on a 50000-file dir, 1500M ext4 /data:
- **FrankenFS** (`ffs-cli walk`, serial, 3 runs): 54-60 ms / 50002 entries = **~1.08-1.21 us/entry**.
- **kernel ext4** (loop mount, /tmp/kwalk.c: opendir + readdir + lstat each, warm): ~141-151 ms = **~2.9 us/entry**.

FrankenFS is ~2.7x faster, but the comparison favors it structurally: `ffs-cli walk` resolves readdir+getattr IN-PROCESS, while the kernel side pays a real `lstat()` syscall per entry (~0.5-1 us of pure syscall/VFS boundary the in-process path skips; the deployed FUSE path would pay it too). So the honest read is PARITY-or-better with no lever — consistent with the prior "walk/stat alloc-optimal" finding (the MetadataOnly inode parse does zero heap allocs/stat).

This completes the campaign's ext4 metadata-op map — every op now measured vs kernel: read (parity, IO-bound), write/create (~1.16x, inherent MVCC commit), mkdir (won 2.77x), rename (won, 1.71x faster), unlink/rmdir (won ~1.08x this session), lookup (parity), walk (parity/faster). Plus btrfs: read (won), create (owner-lane node-retention, test-proven), rename (CoW-inherent), unlink (cowbatch-optimized). The clean safe-Rust per-op surface across BOTH filesystems is exhausted; every residual routes to the MVCC version-retention / prune / parallel-publication / durable-group-commit layer, all of which need loom or crash-injection validation infra absent in-tree (owner-lane). No phantom gaps remain in the per-op metadata space.

### 2026-06-29 DIG (convoy, pinpointed at the source + path clarified): the blocker is `CommitPublicationGate::publish` condvar wait, NOT a store-level lock (CrimsonFox)

Chased the parallel-create convoy (negative scaling, prior entries) to the exact blocking instruction and clarified which store path production uses:
- PATH CLARIFICATION: there are TWO MVCC stores. `MvccStore` (ffs-mvcc/lib.rs:1515) is the legacy SINGLE-LOCK store — `begin(&mut self)`/`commit(&mut self)` — wrapped `Arc<RwLock<MvccStore>>` so `FsMvccBlockDevice::write_block` takes `store.write()` per commit (would serialize all writers). BUT production does NOT use it: `FsMvccStore` (ffs-core/fs_mvcc_store.rs) is an enum `Single | Sharded`, and the FS builds `Sharded(ShardedMvccStore::for_host_parallelism())` whose `commit(&self)` does internal per-shard locking — NO global store RwLock. So the tempting "downgrade store.write() to store.read()" fix targets a dead path; production already commits under `&self`.
- PINPOINTED BLOCKER: `ShardedMvccStore::commit` ends in `CommitPublicationGate::publish(seq)` (sharded.rs:72). publish inserts `seq` into `ready_commits`, advances the contiguous prefix, and if its own seq is not yet in the prefix (a lower seq from another shard's committer hasn't published) it **blocks on `self.ready.wait()` (sharded.rs:93)** until a predecessor advances `completed_commit` to cover it. THAT condvar wait — committer N+1 sleeping until committer N publishes — is the convoy, and it fires even for fully disjoint-shard writers because commit seqs are GLOBAL and the published snapshot (`completed` = contiguous prefix) must stay gap-free. The `active_snapshots.write()` register/release contention named in the prior convoy entry is a SECONDARY effect; this in-order publish wait is the primary serializer.
- WHY THE WAIT IS NECESSARY (not a bug): `commit()` returns only once `current_snapshot >= seq`, so a caller that commits then opens a FRESH snapshot reads its own write (commit-then-fresh-snapshot read-your-writes across FsOps). Dropping the wait would let a fast committer return with the watermark still behind its write — safe for disjoint ops (create-bench) but breaks dependent ops generally. The only removal is gap-tolerant publication (per-seq visibility set or epoch-batched async publish that decouples commit from watermark), the owner-lane MVCC redesign, loom-gated (no loom infra in ffs-mvcc).

Net: the convoy is now pinned to one instruction (sharded.rs:93) with the path confirmed (production = `&self` sharded, not the single-lock store), and the wait proven semantically load-bearing for cross-FsOp read-your-writes. No safe in-window lever; the fix is the gap-tolerant-snapshot redesign.

### 2026-06-29 CAMPAIGN-STATE CONSOLIDATION: per-op surface exhausted; remaining levers pinpointed + infra-gated (CrimsonFox)

After exhaustively measuring every ext4 + key btrfs metadata op vs the kernel and pinpointing each residual to source, this consolidates the forward map so continuation does not re-derive it. The clean safe-Rust per-op surface is closed; every remaining gap is owner-lane and gated on validation infra that does not exist in-tree.

PINPOINTED REMAINING LEVERS (biggest gap first):
1. **Durable group-commit, ~960x** (OPT-IN JBD2 mode only, not default-hot — 832fbd19). `commit_transaction_journaled` (ffs-core/lib.rs:6902) does a JBD2 txn + fdatasync PER FsOp; `GroupCommitCoordinator` (ffs-journal wal_buffer.rs:933, measured 960x at journal level via cc_group_commit_fsync.rs) is BUILT+tested but unwired, and is a WAL-epoch subsystem distinct from the JBD2 path — so the fix is JBD2-level group-commit (batch N FsOps' txns, 1 fsync), validated by the EXISTING crash-injection suite (writeback_crash_matrix/crash_replay), NOT e2fsck.
2. **Parallel-write convoy, ~0.65-0.71x scaling** (DEFAULT path). Primary blocker pinned to `CommitPublicationGate::publish` condvar wait (ffs-mvcc/sharded.rs:93): committer N+1 sleeps until predecessor N publishes to keep the global `completed`-prefix snapshot gap-free; the wait is load-bearing for commit-then-fresh-snapshot read-your-writes. Secondary: `active_snapshots.write()` global lock in register/release (sharded.rs:950/959). Fix = gap-tolerant publication (per-seq visibility / epoch-batched async publish) — MVCC redesign, needs loom (absent).
3. **btrfs create super-linear degradation, 2.7x@40k+climbing** (vs flat kernel). `InMemoryCowBtrfsTree.nodes` retains every retired CoW node (no `deferred_free_blocks` consumer in ffs-core); eager-free is test-PROVEN unsafe (cow_insert_preserves_previous_root_node + 2 read old roots via node_snapshot). Fix = generation/snapshot-watermark-gated node prune, needs loom (absent).
4. **Per-op MVCC version-materialization** (create 1.16x, unlink 1.68x, all metadata writes). ~43% of create is memmove+fault of retained 4 KiB version buffers (each commit retains a full immutable block copy for snapshot isolation; kernel overwrites page-cache in place). The transient-copy-elision sub-lever (write_block_owned) is REFUTED neutral; reducing this needs a delta/sub-block version format OR group-commit amortization — owner-lane.

INFRA THAT WOULD UNBLOCK (the actual highest-value next work, NOT more per-op digging):
- **loom** (or any exhaustive interleaving checker) for `ShardedMvccStore` — none in-tree; only hand-rolled probabilistic threaded lock-ordering guards (bd-bky2f). Required to blind-ship the convoy gap-tolerant publication, the active_snapshots sharding, and the btrfs node-prune (all snapshot-isolation TOCTOU). Adding it means cfg(loom)-abstracting the store's sync primitives — an owner-designed architectural change.
- A **durable-mode FS-level bench** (attach_jbd2_writer + per-op fsync) to A/B the JBD2 group-commit at the FS layer (journal-level harness already exists).

SHIPPED safe per-op wins this campaign segment: unlink/rmdir one-pass (3f24e58b, ~1.08x/1.05x, e2fsck-identical). All other per-op attempts measured neutral/refuted or proven owner-lane (recorded above). No clean safe-Rust per-op lever remains across either filesystem.

### 2026-06-29 NEGATIVE (decomposition, no gap): metadata_csum costs ~8% of ext4 create but is hardware-crc32c + kernel-shared (CrimsonFox)

Tested whether checksum work is a hidden ext4-create lever. A/B create-bench on metadata_csum vs `-O ^metadata_csum` fixtures (1500M ext4, 60000 creates, same baseline binary):
- with metadata_csum: ~1666-1739 ms (median ~1727 ms)
- without: ~1590-1611 ms (median ~1602 ms)
- **~1.08x → metadata_csum adds ~8% to create** (every run separated).

NO LEVER, for three independent reasons:
1. **Hardware-accelerated**: ffs-ondisk uses the `crc32c` crate (workspace dep) which runtime-dispatches to the SSE4.2 `crc32` instruction (SIMD detected at mount: x86_sse42=true). It is already optimal; there is no software-fallback to fix.
2. **Kernel-shared**: ext4 in the kernel computes the SAME metadata_csum on bitmap/inode/GDT changes (also full-block crc, no incremental). My create-vs-kernel 1.16x baseline used metadata_csum on BOTH sides, so the 8% is paid by both — it is NOT part of the vs-kernel gap (which is the MVCC version-materialization, confirmed: create = ~92% machinery+version-fault + ~8% csum).
3. **No redundant verify**: the hot alloc path reads bitmaps raw via `dev.read_block` (ffs-alloc:2573/1847), NOT the verifying `read_inode_bitmap`/`read_block_bitmap` wrappers — so there is no per-op re-verify of a block we just wrote. And the write path already guards "re-stamp a bitmap's descriptor csum only when that bitmap changed" (ffs-alloc:1715).

ONE speculative sub-lever NOT pursued (intricate + owner-lane): per-commit checksum STAMPING (compute-on-write) recomputes the bitmap/GDT crc on every create, but the in-memory MVCC version is read by content (not verified), so checksums are only strictly needed on the final on-disk flush — deferring stamping to flush would amortize N per-op crc computations to 1. It touches the durability/flush path (a block flushed without a re-stamped csum = e2fsck failure) and the read-your-writes verify assumptions, so it needs the durability-path owner and crash/e2fsck validation, not a blind in-window land. Recorded for completeness; the checksum surface is otherwise clean (hardware-accelerated, kernel-shared, no redundant verify).

### 2026-06-29 DIG (960x durable lever, infra coupling confirmed): the FS-level bench cannot be built independently of the JBD2 group-commit impl (CrimsonFox)

Probed whether the durable group-commit lever (960x, the campaign's largest gap) could be de-risked by building just the missing FS-level A/B bench (the prior consolidation listed that bench as the unblocker). Verified the infra state:
- NO existing bench/example/CLI path routes through `commit_transaction_journaled` (ffs-core:6902) or `attach_jbd2_writer` (6282) — `grep` across ffs-cli + all examples/benches = zero callers. The durable per-op-fsync FS path is untested by any harness.
- `GroupCommitCoordinator`/`flush_epoch` have ZERO refs in ffs-core/ffs-fuse (unwired, as long noted), and a prior DIG (1dfdb7c0) already established it is a WAL/epoch subsystem incompatible with the JBD2 commit path — so the fix is a NEW JBD2-level group-commit impl (batch N FsOps' JBD2 txns, fsync once), not wiring existing code.

CONCLUSION: building the FS-level durable bench requires (a) constructing a `Jbd2Writer` + journal device and (b) routing ops through `commit_transaction_journaled` — and the group-commit side it would A/B against does not exist. So bench and implementation are COUPLED; you cannot bench-first to de-risk. The 960x lever is fully owner-lane multi-turn: implement JBD2 group-commit + stand up the durable-mode FS harness + validate with the crash-injection suite (writeback_crash_matrix/crash_replay, NOT e2fsck), together. This forecloses "just build the bench to unblock it" and is the sharpest statement of the biggest lever's blocker. (The journal-LEVEL proof already exists — cc_group_commit_fsync.rs measured 960x — so the lever is real; only the FS integration remains, and it is owner-lane.)

### 2026-06-29 NEGATIVE (foreclosure): btrfs compression decode is optimal — C libzstd, kernel-parity, re-decode already cached (CrimsonFox)

Checked the last unaudited read-path surface — compressed-read decode (memory: "decode-bound", the dominant cost for compressed btrfs reads). NO codec lever: ffs-core/ffs-btrfs use the `zstd` crate (C libzstd binding, the reference impl — identical to the kernel's in-kernel zstd, so same decode speed), LZO via `lzokay-native`, zlib via `flate2`. The per-read re-decode amplification (decompress a 128 KiB extent per 4 KiB read) is already mitigated by the shipped RO decompressed-extent cache (90bf7cfa) + the `zstd_decompress_reuse` buffer-reuse path. So compressed read is library-optimal AND re-decode-cached = kernel-parity, no safe lever.

AUDIT COMPLETE: every read/write/metadata surface is now measured or code-verified vs kernel — ext4 (read parity, write/create ~1.16x inherent-MVCC, mkdir/rename/unlink won, lookup/walk parity), btrfs (read won, create owner-lane node-retention, rename CoW-inherent, unlink cowbatch), checksum (hardware crc32c, kernel-shared, 8%), compression (libzstd, kernel-parity). The clean safe-Rust per-op lever surface is exhaustively closed across both filesystems; every residual is the owner-lane MVCC version-retention / in-order-publication / durable-group-commit / btrfs-node-prune layer, each pinpointed to file:line in the entries above and each gated on loom or crash-injection validation infra absent in-tree.

### 2026-06-29 REFUTED (measured NEUTRAL, shelved): btrfs node hot/cold split — degradation is heap/alloc pressure, not live-map ops (CrimsonFox)

Tested whether the btrfs-create degradation is the `alloc_node`/`node_ref` hot path probing a `self.nodes` FxHashMap bloated to 40k+ entries (retained CoW nodes) — i.e. cache-unfriendly inserts/rehashes on a giant map. Implemented a hot/cold split (different primitive from the test-refuted eager-free: nodes are KEPT, just relocated): added `retired_nodes: FxHashMap`, `commit_retired_nodes` MOVES each retired node from `nodes` (live, hot) to `retired_nodes` (cold), `node_snapshot` checks both. So the descent/alloc hot path probes only the small live map (~tree size, hundreds of nodes), while previous-version reads still resolve. ALL 365 ffs-btrfs tests pass (incl. cow_insert_preserves_previous_root_node — node_snapshot's both-map lookup preserves the contract).

MEASURED (btrfs create scaling, same fixture, single-thread):
| count | HEAD us/op | hot/cold split us/op |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 2000 | 29.19 | 28.03 |
| 8000 | 40.46 | 39.37 |
| 20000 | 50.86 | 54.25 |
| 40000 | 61.05 | 59.01 |

NEUTRAL — the per-op curve still climbs identically. REVERTED (shelved `btrfs-hotcold-split-MEASURED-NEUTRAL`).

CONCLUSION (sharpens the root cause): the degradation is NOT the live-map hashmap operations (the split made the hot map small but changed nothing), so it is the retained-node MEMORY/ALLOCATION pressure — each create CoW-clones ~depth nodes (BtrfsCowNode::clone, deep-copies parsed item Vecs) and the cumulative retained heap (every CoW node kept for previous-version reads) grows to hundreds of MB, so per-op allocation+fault cost climbs with heap size (matching the ~42% kernel-mm profile frames). The only fixes therefore REDUCE retained memory: (a) eager-free when no snapshot pins the generation (test-refuted as unconditional; needs generation-watermark gating = owner-lane), or (b) store retired nodes in compact serialized bytes instead of parsed `BtrfsCowNode` (smaller footprint + fewer allocations, but adds serialize-on-retire / parse-on-read cost — a real but uncertain follow-up). Map-locality is foreclosed; the lever is memory-footprint, which is owner-lane (generation gating) or a separate serialize-format experiment.

### 2026-06-29 DIG (btrfs degradation, serialize-retired FORECLOSED a priori): node item data is already Arc-shared (CrimsonFox)

Followed up the hot/cold-split refutation's noted "serialize retired nodes to compact bytes" sub-lever — and foreclosed it by reading the types. `BtrfsTreeItem.data` is ALREADY `Arc<[u8]>` (ffs-btrfs lib.rs:2779), and `BtrfsCowNode`/`BtrfsTreeItem` derive `Clone`, so `BtrfsCowNode::clone` (the CoW path) copies only the `Vec<BtrfsTreeItem>` backing = per-item `(BtrfsKey copy + Arc refcount bump)`, NOT a deep copy of item bytes. Two consequences:
1. Serialize-retired is COUNTERPRODUCTIVE: a retired parsed node holds only `(key, Arc-ptr)` pairs with the actual bytes SHARED (via Arc) across all versions that didn't mutate that item; serializing to a flat ~16 KiB byte buffer would UN-SHARE and COPY the data, INCREASING memory, not reducing it. Refuted without building.
2. Retention is already minimal: the cumulative retained memory is the per-node `Vec<(key,Arc-ptr)>` backings (~N*32 B per node), not duplicated data — so the btrfs-create degradation is dominated by the PER-OP clone allocation (a fresh `Vec<BtrfsTreeItem>` per CoW'd node, growing as leaves fill toward split) plus heap-growth allocation pressure from retaining those backings, all already using Arc-shared data.

Net: the CoW representation is already memory-efficient (Arc-shared item data); neither serialize-retired nor the hot/cold split (measured neutral) helps. The btrfs-create degradation reduction is exclusively the generation-watermark-gated free of retired nodes (test-refuted as unconditional → owner-lane), or a flat-byte node representation so the per-op clone is a memcpy like the kernel's (a major ffs-btrfs refactor, owner-lane). Both btrfs-node fixes are now exhaustively confirmed owner-lane; the parsed+Arc representation has no clean per-op lever.

### 2026-06-29 NEGATIVE (foreclosure): ext4 ExtentCache is already lock-optimal for parallel random reads (CrimsonFox)

Checked the last unaudited parallel-read serialization candidate — the per-read extent resolution. ext4 reads resolve logical→physical via `resolve_extent` → `ffs_extent::ExtentCache::lookup` (flagged as "the per-read serial point" in OpenFs docs). NO lever: `ExtentCache` is already striped (`Box<[RwLock<ExtentCacheInner>]>` keyed by inode namespace) AND `lookup` takes `shard.read()` (shared) on the hot path (ffs-extent:1534), updating recency via atomic `last_access` and escalating to `.write()` only in the rare physical-overflow/eviction case (1561). So concurrent random readers of the same file share the read lock — no serialization. Already optimal (mirrors the conclusion that converting the data-block ShardedCache Mutex→RwLock was neutral: the read paths are not lock-bound).

This completes the parallel-random-read lock audit: snapshot registration (fixed 9376f4d6, 88x→3.3x), data-block ShardedCache shard lock (RwLock conversion measured neutral this session), and extent resolution (RwLock-read + atomic LRU, optimal) are all addressed/optimal. The residual ~3.3x vs kernel on parallel random read is per-read MVCC read_visible overhead + rayon coordination at kernel-comparable absolute levels, not a remaining lock convoy. The read-path lock surface is exhaustively clean.

### 2026-06-29 CORRECTION (measured): the parallel-write convoy is per-commit GLOBAL-LOCK acquisition, NOT the publish-wait condvar (CrimsonFox)

Built an env-gated no-wait `publish()` prototype (FFS_PUBLISH_NOWAIT) to QUANTIFY how much of the convoy is the in-order publish-wait (`CommitPublicationGate::publish` condvar wait, sharded.rs:93) that a prior entry named the primary blocker. No-wait advances the contiguous prefix and returns WITHOUT blocking on predecessors. Env-toggle A/B (same binary, create-bench --threads, 96000 creates):
| threads | wait (OFF) creates/s | no-wait (ON) creates/s |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 33450 | 32256 |
| 8 | 24736 | 24689 |
| 16 | 23057 | 23839 |
| 32 | 22955 | 21971 |

NEUTRAL — eliminating the condvar wait did NOT improve parallel scaling; the negative scaling (8t = 0.74x of 1t) persists unchanged. This REFUTES the prior pinpoint (1eb2d2a2/6226cd23 named the publish-wait as the primary convoy blocker). REVERTED (shelved).

CORRECTED ROOT CAUSE: with no-wait, every committer STILL takes `self.wait_lock.lock()` (the gate's single `Mutex<PublicationState>`, sharded.rs:77) to insert its seq + advance the prefix, and STILL hits the `active_snapshots.write()` global lock in register/release. So the convoy is the per-commit GLOBAL-LOCK ACQUISITION — the gate's `wait_lock` Mutex (taken by EVERY publish) plus the `active_snapshots` RwLock.write (every register/release) — which serializes even fully disjoint-shard writers, NOT the in-order condvar wait (which is downstream of already-holding the Mutex and is measurably free to remove). The publish-wait is correctly load-bearing for read-your-writes (so it can't just be removed anyway), but it is NOT the throughput bottleneck. The real convoy fix must make publication + snapshot-refcounting LOCK-FREE or per-shard so disjoint committers don't contend on a process-global Mutex/RwLock per commit — a larger redesign than relaxing the wait, still owner-lane (loom), but now correctly localized to the global-lock acquisition rather than the wait. This supersedes the "publish-wait is primary" framing in the prior two convoy entries.

### 2026-06-29 DIG (convoy localized to shared-allocation-metadata contention, code-confirmed): GDT-block shard lock + gate wait_lock, NOT active_snapshots, NOT retries (CrimsonFox)

Refined the corrected convoy diagnosis (prior entry: "per-commit global-lock acquisition") by reading the actual sharded commit path + reasoning from the bench's success:
- `active_snapshots` is NOT in the write commit path: `ShardedMvccStore::try_begin` only reads `current_snapshot` (atomic `publication_gate.completed`, lock-free) + `next_txn_id` (AtomicU64 fetch_update, lock-free); `commit` calls `publication_gate.publish` (the `wait_lock` Mutex) + lock-free `next_commit`. `active_snapshots.write()` is hit only by explicit reader register/release adapters, NOT by create/commit.
