So the prior "active_snapshots per commit" was wrong for the write path — corrected.
- NOT conflict-retries: per-block commit returns `CommitError` on FCW conflict and `FsMvccBlockDevice::write_block` propagates it (create would ERROR). Parallel create-bench succeeds with no errors → there are NO FCW conflicts/retries. So the negative scaling is PURE LOCK CONTENTION, not wasted retry work.
- The shared hot resource: every inode allocation does an RMW of its group descriptor's `free_inodes` in the GDT block. For a small fs (5 groups) ALL descriptors live in ONE 4 KiB GDT block → ONE MVCC shard → every thread's commit takes that shard's write lock in install + the gate's `wait_lock` Mutex in publish. Disjoint-subdir Orlov placement spreads the inode-BITMAP across groups, but the GDT block is shared regardless. The `free_inodes` counter is a TRUE shared field (not disjoint-mergeable — both threads RMW the same count), so it cannot use the existing `MergeProof::DisjointBlocks` path (used for indirect-block writes); it is inherently serialized + lock-contended.
- CONFIRMING CONTRAST: parallel random READ scales fine (~28 GB/s, no negative scaling — measured this session) because reads touch no shared write metadata; only parallel WRITE negative-scales. So the convoy is specifically shared-allocation-metadata write contention, not a general parallelism defect.

CORRECTED FIX DIRECTION: the convoy is contention on the single hot GDT block (free_inodes/free_blocks counters) + the gate `wait_lock`, mirroring exactly what the kernel solves with per-cpu / per-group counters (percpu_counter + per-group spinlocks) instead of one shared counter block. The frankenfs analog is a per-group counter representation kept OUTSIDE the MVCC block (atomics, reconciled into the GDT at flush) so parallel allocations don't serialize on one block — plus gap-tolerant publication for the gate. Both owner-lane (counter-reconciliation correctness + loom), but now precisely localized to the kernel-style percpu-counter gap rather than a generic "global lock". This supersedes the active_snapshots framing for the WRITE path.

### 2026-06-29 ★MAJOR CORRECTION (code-confirmed): the parallel-create convoy is the COARSE GLOBAL `alloc_mutex`, NOT the MVCC layer (CrimsonFox)

Traced `ext4_create` (ffs-core/lib.rs:16388) lock scope and found the real convoy, superseding ALL the MVCC-layer convoy analysis in the entries above (publish-wait, wait_lock, active_snapshots, GDT-block shard — all masked/secondary):
- `ext4_create` takes `self.require_alloc_state()?` → a SINGLE process-global `RwLock<Ext4AllocState>` and does `alloc_mutex.write()` (16419), holding that write guard through `create_inode` (inode+bitmap+GDT allocation & block-commits) AND `ext4_add_dir_entry` (dir-block commits) AND every MVCC commit they issue — the guard drops only at function return (16491).
- Therefore ALL parallel creates FULLY SERIALIZE on this one coarse write lock. The MVCC sharding / publication gate / GDT-block contention I analyzed is MASKED: only one thread is ever inside the alloc lock, so only one commits at a time — the MVCC layer is never actually contended during parallel create. This is why the env-gated no-wait publish was MEASURED NEUTRAL (the gate isn't the bottleneck; the alloc lock upstream is), and why the GDT-block / active_snapshots framings, though real structures, are not the convoy.
- The negative scaling (8t = 0.74x of 1t) is the futex/cache-line contention OVERHEAD of N threads fighting for the single `alloc_mutex` on top of fully-serialized work. CONFIRMING CONTRAST: parallel reads (no alloc lock) scale to ~28 GB/s; only allocation-bearing writes convoy.

CORRECTED FIX DIRECTION — and it is NOT MVCC-owner-lane: shard `Ext4AllocState` per block-group (kernel-style per-group locks) so disjoint-group allocations (Orlov already spreads directories across groups) proceed in parallel, or narrow the `alloc_mutex` hold to the allocation itself (drop before the dir-entry insert + commits). This is an EXT4-ALLOCATOR change validated by conformance + e2fsck + the alloc unit tests — NOT the MVCC gap-tolerant-publication redesign (loom) the prior entries pointed to. Caveat: the allocator's group SCAN (Orlov + free-count fallback) reads many groups' counts, so per-group sharding needs a read-then-write-chosen-group scheme rather than a single per-group lock; tractable but a real refactor. This is the campaign's most actionable convoy lever and the first that is plausibly shippable (allocator, not MVCC-visibility). Next: prototype per-group alloc sharding (or reduced lock scope) + measure create --threads scaling + conformance/e2fsck.

### 2026-06-29 DIG (convoy fix fully designed): parallel-create needs a 3-part allocator refactor, and WHY each part is required (CrimsonFox)

Worked out the complete fix for the parallel-create convoy (the coarse `alloc_mutex` confirmed last entry) by tracing what would break if naively parallelized. `create_inode` takes `&dyn BlockDevice` so it CAN stage into a shared txn instead of per-block-committing — but three things must change together, and the analysis shows why no single one helps alone (each is masked by the others / by the alloc lock):

1. **Defer GDT-block persistence to flush.** Every create's `create_inode` → `persist_group_desc` writes the group-descriptor (free_inodes) into the GDT BLOCK. For a small fs ALL descriptors live in ONE 4 KiB GDT block, so EVERY create's write-set includes it. Under the current alloc-lock serialization this never conflicts (one committer at a time); the moment commits parallelize they ALL conflict on the shared GDT block → retry storm (worse than serial). Since the in-memory `GroupStats.free_inodes` is already the authoritative count (allocation reads it, not the block — ffs-alloc:2569/2680) and the default FS path already defers durability to flush, the GDT block write can be deferred to flush (persist in-memory counts once, re-stamp csum) — removing the shared block from per-op write-sets so per-op txns become DISJOINT (per-group bitmap/inode-table via Orlov spread + per-subdir dir blocks).
2. **Per-group alloc-state locking.** `ext4_create` takes one global `RwLock<Ext4AllocState>.write()`; for files `target_group = parent_group` (no multi-group scan in the common path, ffs-alloc:2468), so per-group locks would let different-group allocations mutate in-memory state in parallel. The rare cross-group fallback scan needs a read-then-write-chosen scheme.
3. **One txn per create, committed AFTER releasing the alloc lock.** The prior create-inode-batch refutation (f1c4880c, 0.96x parallel) committed the batched txn WHILE STILL HOLDING the alloc lock → still serialized → no gain. Releasing the lock before commit lets the (now-disjoint, post-#1) commits run concurrently.

All three are ALLOCATOR/PERSISTENCE-layer changes gated by conformance + e2fsck + alloc unit tests — NOT the loom-gated MVCC gap-tolerant-publication redesign that earlier convoy entries pointed to (those were masked by the alloc lock). This is a multi-part refactor (no 60m blind land — the error/rollback path when a post-release commit fails, plus GDT-deferral crash-consistency, need care), but it is the campaign's most tractable path to a parallel-write win and is plausibly shippable incrementally with the right validation. DEFINITIVE convoy spec; supersedes the MVCC-layer framings.

### 2026-06-29 ★MAJOR LEVER MEASURED (2.3x): per-op GDT-block write is ~55% of ext4 create — version-chain accumulation on the shared GDT block (CrimsonFox)

Env-gated prototype (FFS_SKIP_GDT) skipping the per-op GDT-block read+write in `persist_group_desc_with_bitmap_overrides` (ffs-alloc) to measure the upper bound on deferring GDT persistence to flush. Env-toggle A/B, single-thread create-bench 80000, 1500M ext4:
| iter | normal us | skip-GDT us |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 2781359 | 1182236 |
| 2 | 2360768 | 1247380 |
| 3 | 2909575 | 1076619 |
**~2.3x faster with the GDT write skipped** (every run separated) → the per-op GDT block read+write is ~55% of ext4 create cost. (e2fsck-DIRTY in skip mode — no flush pass persists the descriptors; this is a perf upper-bound, not a correct config. Prototype shelved `skip-gdt-perf-prototype-MEASURED-2.3x-upper-bound`.)

ROOT CAUSE: for a small fs ALL group descriptors live in ONE 4 KiB GDT block, so EVERY create writes that SAME block. Each write installs a NEW MVCC version → the GDT block's version chain grows by one per create (80000 versions = ~320 MB of retained 4 KiB GDT payloads for an 80k-create run), and each op also READS the GDT block (read_visible over the growing chain + a 4 KiB clone) + installs a new 4 KiB version. This single hot shared block's per-op read-clone + version-install + cumulative retention dominates create — far more than the inode-table/bitmap/dir blocks (which are Orlov-spread / change less). This is the same version-retention-pressure mechanism as the btrfs-node finding, but here on ONE block hit by every op.

THE FIX (clean, allocator/persistence-layer, NOT MVCC-owner-lane): defer GDT-block persistence to flush. The in-memory `GroupStats.free_inodes/free_blocks/used_dirs` is already the authoritative count (the allocator reads GroupStats, never the GDT block, ffs-alloc:2569/2680), and `flush_mvcc_to_device` already syncs superblock totals (bd-nd61w) — add an analogous per-group GDT sync that writes each descriptor from GroupStats ONCE at flush (re-reading the current bitmap per group to stamp the descriptor's bitmap checksum). That collapses 80000 per-op GDT versions to ~5 GDT-block writes at flush. Gate: conformance + e2fsck (descriptor free counts must match the bitmaps) + no hot-path reader of the GDT block's free counts (allocator uses GroupStats; read_group_desc consumers use the bitmap-block POINTER which is unchanged). This is the highest-value safe lever found in the campaign (~2.3x single-thread create) and also removes the shared GDT block from per-op write-sets, unblocking the parallel-create convoy fix. NEXT: build the flush-time GDT sync + validate e2fsck.

### 2026-06-29 GDT-defer flush-pass: BUILDS + 2.3x perf holds, but e2fsck FAILS on BG_*_UNINIT flags — diagnosed, needs fix (CrimsonFox)

Implemented the GDT-deferral fix (the measured ~2.3x create lever, prior entry): `gdt_persistence_deferred()` env-gate skips the per-op GDT write in `persist_group_desc_with_bitmap_overrides`; new `ext4_flush_group_descriptors` (ffs-core, called in `flush_mvcc_to_device` after `flush_to_device`) writes every descriptor once via the direct adapter from the authoritative in-memory `GroupStats`, re-reading each group's bitmaps to stamp the descriptor bitmap checksums (modeled on `ext4_sync_superblock_free_totals`). Compiles clean.

VALIDATION (FFS_SKIP_GDT=1, create 30000, then e2fsck):
- e2fsck FAILS: "Group 0 inode bitmap does not match checksum" + "block bitmap does not match checksum" + "Filesystem still has errors". Baseline (no defer) e2fsck CLEAN. Same file count/blocks (30012 files) — so allocation is correct; only the GDT descriptor metadata is wrong.

ROOT CAUSE (diagnosed): mke2fs lazily marks groups `BG_INODE_UNINIT`/`BG_BLOCK_UNINIT` (uninitialized bitmaps). The NORMAL per-op path clears those flags the first time a group is allocated from (the GDT descriptor is read from MVCC where a prior op already cleared them). In deferral mode that per-op GDT write is skipped, so the on-disk GDT keeps the original UNINIT flags; `ext4_flush_group_descriptors` reads that stale on-disk descriptor and PRESERVES the UNINIT flags (via `..existing` in persist_group_desc) while the inode bitmap is now populated → e2fsck computes the bitmap-csum against an empty/default bitmap for an UNINIT group and reports a mismatch. (Also implicates `bg_itable_unused` — the count of never-used inodes at the tail — which must shrink as inodes are allocated.)

THE FIX (next): `ext4_flush_group_descriptors` / `persist_group_desc_force` must, for any group with allocations (free < total or used_dirs>0), CLEAR `BG_INODE_UNINIT` (and `BG_BLOCK_UNINIT` when blocks were allocated) and update `bg_itable_unused`, rather than preserving the stale on-disk flags — OR track these flag/counter states in `GroupStats` and apply them at flush. The ~2.3x perf and the deferral design are validated; this is a contained GDT-descriptor-metadata correctness gap (UNINIT/itable_unused), not a perf regression. Shelved `gdt-defer-flush-pass-WIP-e2fsck-FAIL-uninit-flags-NEEDS-FIX`. conformance NOT run (e2fsck gate failed first — fix the descriptor metadata before re-validating).

### 2026-06-29 GDT-defer: deeper bug — deferring the per-op GDT write CHANGES allocation behavior (not just persistence) (CrimsonFox)

Second validation pass on the GDT-deferral fix (2.3x lever). Fixed the flush pass to read bitmaps via the DIRECT adapter (post-flush device bytes) so the descriptor csum is stamped over exactly what e2fsck reads — but e2fsck STILL fails the same way. `dumpe2fs` group-0 comparison (30000 single-thread creates, deferred vs baseline) reveals the real problem is NOT the checksum stamp but a BEHAVIORAL divergence:
- DEFERRED group 0: 13700 free blocks, **24996 free inodes**, 24996 unused inodes
- BASELINE group 0: 13477 free blocks, **0 free inodes** (group packed full, as expected for files whose parent is root/group 0)

So in deferral mode the allocator did NOT pack group 0 (only ~2285 inodes placed there vs ~27281 baseline) — allocation SPREAD differently. The descriptor counts my flush pass writes come straight from in-memory `GroupStats`, so `GroupStats` itself diverged. Conclusion: SKIPPING `persist_group_desc_with_bitmap_overrides` per-op removes a SIDE EFFECT the allocator depends on — that function clears `GD_FLAG_INODE_UNINIT`/`GD_FLAG_BLOCK_UNINIT` and shrinks `itable_unused`, and some part of the alloc path (group-fullness / uninit checks, e.g. ffs-alloc:2569/2828) evidently consults flag/itable state that the per-op GDT write was keeping in sync. In deferral the in-memory flag/itable state goes stale, changing which group inodes land in, and the e2fsck bitmap-csum mismatch is downstream of that.

So GDT deferral is NOT a clean "persistence-only" change: the per-op `persist_group_desc` also maintains UNINIT/itable_unused state that the allocator reads. A correct deferral must move that flag/itable maintenance into the in-memory `GroupStats` mutation (so allocation behaves identically) and only defer the BLOCK WRITE. That is a more invasive (but still allocator-layer, e2fsck-gated) change than the flush-pass alone. The ~2.3x perf upside is real and remains the campaign's biggest measured lever; the deferral needs the flag/itable in-memory decoupling first. Shelved `gdt-defer-WIP-DEEPER-BUG-deferral-changes-allocation-pattern`. NEXT: clear UNINIT + shrink itable_unused in GroupStats at alloc time (in-memory), decoupled from the GDT block write, then defer only the block write.

### 2026-06-29 GDT-defer CORRECTION: allocation is code-provably INDEPENDENT of the per-op GDT write — the bug is in the flush pass, not allocation (CrimsonFox)

Re-read the inode-alloc path to settle the prior turn's "deferral changes allocation" claim — it is WRONG. `try_alloc_inode_in_group_persist` (ffs-alloc:2680) writes the inode bitmap via `dev.write_block` (line 2720) and decrements `groups[gidx].free_inodes` (2722) DIRECTLY — both BEFORE and INDEPENDENT of the `persist_group_desc_with_bitmap_overrides` call (2731) that my env-gate skips. The fullness check reads `gs.free_inodes` (in-memory, 2680) and the bitmap (device, 2685); neither reads the GDT block. So skipping the GDT write provably CANNOT change which group/inode is allocated. The earlier dumpe2fs "group 0: 24996 free inodes (deferred) vs 0 (baseline)" is therefore a FLUSH-PASS descriptor-write bug, not allocation divergence — `ext4_flush_group_descriptors` is writing the wrong descriptor contents (candidate causes: the per-group read-modify-write loop on the direct adapter clobbering earlier descriptors if reads don't reflect prior writes, a descriptor-offset error, or a stale `..existing` field). The e2fsck "bitmap does not match checksum" is downstream of the wrong descriptor.

STATUS: the ~2.3x GDT-defer lever (the campaign's biggest measured single lever) is real, the per-op-skip half works, and the flush-pass half compiles but writes wrong descriptors. The fix is contained (correct the flush-pass descriptor write — most robustly: read each GDT block ONCE, update ALL its descriptors from `GroupStats` in the buffer, write ONCE, after re-reading each group's post-flush bitmap for the csum) but it is fiddly ext4-GDT-metadata work that needs a focused dedicated pass, not scattered turns. Shelved WIP stands (`gdt-defer-WIP-DEEPER-BUG-deferral-changes-allocation-pattern` — note: the "changes-allocation" label is now disproven; it is a flush-pass bug). This correction supersedes the prior entry's allocation-divergence claim.

### 2026-06-29 GDT-defer: STOP scattered debugging — confirmed deferral DOES change allocation (empirical), mechanism unexplained, needs dedicated session (CrimsonFox)

Third validation pass (uncached flush-pass adapter — to rule out cached-RYW clobbering across the per-group GDT read-modify-write loop). e2fsck STILL fails the same way, and dumpe2fs now disambiguates conclusively:
- BASELINE (no defer): group 0 = 0 free inodes (PACKED full ~27281), group 1 = 20004 free. Files pack group 0 first, as expected for `parent_group`-targeted file allocation.
- DEFERRED (FFS_SKIP_GDT=1): group 0 = 24996 free, group 1 = 25008 free — allocation SPREAD ~evenly across groups (~2285 each).

So deferral DOES change allocation (empirically confirmed on both images), despite `create_inode` → `alloc_inode_persist(parent_group=0)` → `try_alloc_inode_in_group_persist` writing the bitmap (2720) + decrementing `gs.free_inodes` (2722) INDEPENDENTLY of the skipped `persist_group_desc` (2731). The only way group 0 spreads is `try_alloc_inode_in_group_persist(0)` returning None early (gs[0].free_inodes hitting 0, or bitmap_find_free seeing group 0 as full) — which contradicts the apparent code independence. There is an unidentified interaction between skipping the per-op GDT write and per-group inode allocation that I cannot resolve without in-binary instrumentation.

DECISION: STOP scattered single-turn debugging of GDT-defer. It is the campaign's biggest measured lever (~2.3x single-thread create, real) but the correct implementation has a genuinely subtle allocation interaction that needs a focused dedicated session with instrumentation (log gs.free_inodes + bitmap_find_free results per alloc in deferred vs baseline) — not incremental turns, which have now spent ~4 cycles finding successive layers (UNINIT flags → flush-pass descriptor write → cached-RYW → allocation divergence). The lever and its 2.3x value are recorded (8451bb5e); the WIP is shelved (`gdt-defer-WIP-uncached-flushpass-STILL-e2fsck-FAIL-alloc-spreads-unexplained`). RECOMMENDATION: a future dedicated pass should instrument the allocation loop to find why skipping the GDT write changes group selection, before any further flush-pass work.

### 2026-06-29 GDT-defer INSTRUMENTED: allocation is CORRECT (packs group 0) — prior "spreads" was wrong; bug is flush-pass writes not landing (CrimsonFox)

Instrumented `try_alloc_inode_in_group_persist` (log free/iig/bitmap_set/find_free for group 0). Deferred-mode run (FFS_SKIP_GDT=1): group 0 PACKS exactly like baseline — `find_free=Some(12),Some(13)...Some(5011)`, `free` decrements 24996->19997 over 5000 creates, ZERO early-None returns. So allocation is IDENTICAL to baseline; the prior "deferral spreads allocation" conclusion was WRONG (it was reading the persisted descriptor, not the actual allocation).

So the ONLY bug is: `ext4_flush_group_descriptors` writes are NOT landing on disk. After a 30k deferred run the on-disk descriptor still shows the ORIGINAL mke2fs values (group 0: 24996 free, group 1: 25008 free) even though in-memory `gs.free_inodes` was correctly decremented to ~0 (group 0 packed full). The flush pass either is not being invoked on this path, returns early, or its direct writes are not synced to the image file — NOT a metadata-correctness or allocation problem. (A `DBG flush-gdt` print was added to confirm invocation; turn ended before the run completed.) Shelved `gdt-defer-WIP+instrumentation-flush-pass-writes-not-landing`. NEXT (contained): confirm whether ext4_flush_group_descriptors runs (the DBG print) and whether its direct-adapter writes are synced — likely a one-line wiring/sync fix, after which the ~2.3x win lands (allocation + per-op-skip are proven correct).

### 2026-06-29 GDT-defer BREAKTHROUGH: root cause was wiring — create-bench flushes via sync_all_to_device, not flush_mvcc_to_device (CrimsonFox)

Instrumented `ext4_flush_group_descriptors` with a debug print — it NEVER fired for create-bench. Root cause: create-bench's durability boundary is `sync_all_to_device` (ffs-core:29819), NOT `flush_mvcc_to_device` where I'd wired the GDT flush pass. (Confirms the prior "flush-pass writes not landing" narrowing: the pass simply wasn't on this path.) Added `self.ext4_flush_group_descriptors(cx)?` to `sync_all_to_device` too.

RESULT (FFS_SKIP_GDT=1, 30000 creates, e2fsck): MAJOR progress —
- group 0 now persists **0 free inodes** (correct, packed full) vs the prior stale 24996; group 1 = 20004 free (matches baseline).
- The bitmap-CHECKSUM errors are GONE (the descriptor csum/counts are now correct).
- ONE remaining e2fsck error: "Padding at end of inode bitmap is not set". Cause: my flush pass passes `Some(bitmap)` overrides for ALL groups, which clears `GD_FLAG_INODE_UNINIT` even for groups never allocated from — but an untouched group's on-disk inode bitmap still lacks the trailing padding bits (`fill_inode_bitmap_padding` only runs on the alloc path), so e2fsck flags a now-"initialized" group whose bitmap padding isn't set.

So allocation is proven correct (group 0 packs), descriptors now persist correctly, and the lever is one small fix from clean: the flush pass must NOT mark untouched groups initialized — pass the bitmap override (clear UNINIT) ONLY for groups that were actually allocated from (free_inodes reduced / used_dirs>0), and pass None (preserve original UNINIT descriptor) for untouched groups. Shelved `gdt-defer-WIP-WIRING-FIXED-group0-correct-only-padding-error-left`. The ~2.3x lever is now very close: alloc correct + descriptor persistence correct + only the untouched-group UNINIT/padding handling remains.

### 2026-06-29 ★SHIPPED: GDT-block persistence deferral — 2.3x single-thread ext4 create, e2fsck-clean, conformance GREEN (CrimsonFox)

LANDED the GDT-deferral lever (env FFS_SKIP_GDT, opt-in). For a small ext4 fs ALL group descriptors live in ONE 4 KiB GDT block, so every create's `persist_group_desc` writes that same block → an ~80k-deep MVCC version chain (~320 MB retained) + a 4 KiB read-clone + 4 KiB version-install PER create on the one hot shared block — ~55% of create cost. Since the in-memory `GroupStats` is the authoritative count (the allocator reads it, never the GDT block), the per-op GDT write is pure deferred-able persistence.

IMPLEMENTATION (ffs-alloc + ffs-core): `gdt_persistence_deferred()` env-gate; `persist_group_desc_with_bitmap_overrides` skips the per-op GDT write in deferral mode and delegates to a new pub `persist_group_desc_force`; `ext4_flush_group_descriptors` (wired into BOTH durability boundaries — `flush_mvcc_to_device` AND `sync_all_to_device`, the CLI/create-bench path) writes every descriptor once from `GroupStats` via an UNCACHED byte-device adapter (the per-group read-modify-write of the shared GDT block must see prior writes), reading each group's post-flush bitmap to stamp the descriptor csum. Clears UNINIT/itable_unused only for IN-USE groups (free < capacity) so untouched lazy-init groups keep their padding-less bitmaps (else e2fsck "padding not set").

MEASURED (env-toggle A/B, single binary, single-thread create-bench 60000, 1500M ext4):
| | normal (per-op GDT) | deferred | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| create | ~1670 ms | ~720 ms | **~2.32x** (3/3 runs) |

GATES: e2fsck CLEAN on the deferred 30k-create image (all 5 passes, only the cosmetic "extent tree could be narrower / Optimize? no" that baseline also shows); conformance 100/0/2 GREEN (default path unchanged — env OFF = per-op GDT write + flush pass no-op); ffs-alloc 213/0; ffs-core builds. The campaign's biggest measured single lever, now shipped (opt-in). FOLLOW-UP: validate unlink/mkdir/rename + FUSE flush paths under deferral, then consider default-on.

### 2026-06-29 GDT-defer default-on: NOT YET — 2 conformance tests go e2fsck-dirty under deferral; broad CLI ops validated (CrimsonFox)

Follow-up to the shipped opt-in GDT-defer 2.3x lever (78ed6a0d): validated MORE write ops under deferral (FFS_SKIP_GDT=1, e2fsck exit-0 = clean):
- delbench (unlink) 15k: CLEAN. rdbench (rmdir) 10k: CLEAN. rename-bench 10k: CLEAN. mkdir-bench 10k: CLEAN. write-bench (40k data-block writes, exercises BLOCK_UNINIT + free_blocks in-use check): CLEAN. So all single-op CLI metadata+data paths are e2fsck-clean under deferral.
- Crash-consistency unchanged: in BOTH modes the device GDT is only updated at the flush boundary (non-deferred flushes the GDT MVCC versions; deferred writes GDT directly from GroupStats — same boundary), so deferral does not worsen the durability model. FUSE destroy routes through flush_on_destroy -> flush_mvcc_to_device (GDT pass wired).

Then flipped default-ON (defer unless FFS_GDT_PER_OP) and ran full conformance: **2 FAILURES** — `ext4_e2compr_write_readback_conforms_for_gzip_and_lzo` and `full_conformance_gate_pass`. So at least one path those tests use persists/snapshots via a boundary the GDT flush pass is NOT wired into (candidate: the e2compr write-readback path, or an in-test device-snapshot/reopen that bypasses sync_all_to_device/flush_mvcc_to_device), leaving an e2fsck-dirty GDT. REVERTED to opt-in (the shipped 78ed6a0d behavior); conformance back to 100/0/2 GREEN.

CONCLUSION: the 2.3x lever ships opt-in (FFS_SKIP_GDT) and is broadly validated; DEFAULT-ON is blocked on wiring the GDT flush pass into the remaining persist boundary(ies) the e2compr/gate tests exercise. NEXT: identify that boundary (grep the e2compr write-readback + full_conformance_gate persist path), wire ext4_flush_group_descriptors there, re-run conformance default-on. Bounded, well-scoped follow-up to convert opt-in → default-path 2.3x.

### 2026-06-29 GDT-defer default-on CORRECTION: the 2 conformance failures are TEST-CONTRACT assertions, NOT e2fsck/correctness bugs (CrimsonFox)

Ran the failing e2compr test under deferral (FFS_SKIP_GDT=1) to get the exact failure — it is NOT e2fsck-dirty (the prior entry's "e2fsck-dirty" framing was wrong). The panic is a TEST ASSERTION: `conformance.rs:3432 "gzip: compressed write should update group descriptor free-block counters"`. The test reads the ON-DISK group descriptor right after a compressed write and asserts its free-block counter changed. Deferral intentionally defers that on-disk update to flush — the in-memory `GroupStats` IS updated immediately (allocation stays correct), and the descriptor is correct AFTER flush. So the test encodes the per-op-GDT-durability CONTRACT that deferral changes; `full_conformance_gate_pass` aggregates the same e2compr scenario.

So default-on is NOT blocked by a safety/e2fsck problem (deferral is e2fsck-clean across create/unlink/rmdir/rename/mkdir/write, validated). It is blocked by 2 tests that assert per-op on-disk GDT updates. Going default-on is a DESIGN DECISION: either (a) update those 2 tests to flush before reading the descriptor (the descriptor IS correct post-flush — legitimate), or (b) keep per-op GDT durability as a contract and ship deferral opt-in (current). Given that flipping a durability contract for all callers warrants owner sign-off, the lever correctly ships OPT-IN (FFS_SKIP_GDT, 78ed6a0d) and default-on is deferred to a deliberate decision — not a bug to chase. This corrects the prior "e2fsck-dirty / unwired boundary" diagnosis.

### 2026-06-29 GDT-defer BREADTH: the shipped lever speeds up ALL GDT-writing metadata ops (CrimsonFox)

The shipped GDT-deferral (FFS_SKIP_GDT, 78ed6a0d) skips the per-op write of the shared GDT block for EVERY op that touches a group descriptor, not just create. Measured env-toggle A/B (single binary, single-thread, 600M ext4):
| op | per-op GDT (off) | deferred (on) | ratio |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| create (60k) | ~1670 ms | ~720 ms | ~2.3x |
| mkdir (12k) | ~689 ms | ~417 ms | **~1.65x** |
| unlink/delbench (15k) | ~378 ms | ~305 ms | **~1.24x** |

mkdir benefits more than unlink because it allocates BOTH an inode and a directory block → two free-count updates churning the shared GDT block per op; unlink frees one inode (less GDT churn vs the version-chain it avoids). All e2fsck-clean under deferral. So the lever's value is metadata-write-wide, and notably DEFERRED create at ~12 us/op (720ms/60k) now BEATS the kernel's ~25 us/op (the prior 1.16x-slower create is reversed to ~2x-faster in deferred mode). This is the single largest perf improvement of the campaign, applying across create/mkdir/unlink/rmdir/rename/write.

### 2026-06-29 GDT-defer DEPTH: ~2x at ALL thread counts; profile confirms it removed THE concentrated cost; convoy residual is purely alloc_mutex (CrimsonFox)

Two more measurements on the shipped GDT-defer (FFS_SKIP_GDT):
- PROFILE of deferred create (60k, single-thread): `__memmove_avx` dropped from 43% (pre-defer) to 22.79%, and the kernel mm/page-fault frames from ~35% to ~1% — the GDT-block version chain WAS the bulk of the page-fault/alloc pressure. The remaining 22.79% memmove is DIFFUSE across the other per-op blocks (inode-bitmap, parent-inode mtime, inode-table, dir-leaf), no single GDT-like dominant block — so the next per-op lever is lower-concentration. Deferred create ~12 us/op now BEATS kernel ~25 us/op.
- PARALLEL create (96k, env-toggle A/B): GDT-defer gives ~2x at EVERY thread count — 1t 34924->78374 (2.24x), 8t 25542->54163 (2.12x), 16t 23793->46048 (1.93x). BUT the negative scaling PERSISTS in both modes (deferred 16t 46048 < deferred 1t 78374 = 0.59x), so the parallel-create convoy is INDEPENDENT of the GDT block — it is purely the coarse global `alloc_mutex` (RwLock<Ext4AllocState>.write held across the whole ext4_create, lib.rs:16419), confirming the prior convoy localization. The biggest remaining vs-kernel gap is now the convoy (kernel scales positively; frankenfs negative), fixable only by per-group alloc-state sharding / reduced alloc_mutex scope — a real but multi-part allocator refactor (conformance/e2fsck-gateable, NOT loom).

### 2026-06-29 ★CONVOY GAP QUANTIFIED vs kernel: parallel create 3.7x SLOWER at 16t (frankenfs negative-scales, kernel positive-scales) — the top remaining lever, now unblocked by GDT-defer (CrimsonFox)

Measured the kernel parallel-create baseline (/tmp/kpcreate.c, pthreads each in own subdir, loop-mounted ext4, 96000 creates) vs FrankenFS-deferred (FFS_SKIP_GDT=1):
| threads | kernel creates/s | frankenfs-deferred creates/s | frankenfs vs kernel |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 38282 | 78374 | **2.0x FASTER** (GDT-defer win) |
| 8 | 148564 | 54163 | 2.7x slower |
| 16 | 170976 | 46048 | **3.7x SLOWER** |

The kernel scales POSITIVELY (1t->16t = 4.47x); FrankenFS scales NEGATIVELY (1t->16t = 0.59x). So FrankenFS goes from 2x-faster (single-thread, post-GDT-defer) to 3.7x-slower (16t), and the gap WIDENS with thread count. This is the single biggest vs-kernel gap on the write path, and it is ENTIRELY the coarse global `alloc_mutex` (RwLock<Ext4AllocState>::write held across the whole `ext4_create` incl. all MVCC commits, lib.rs:16419) — confirmed: GDT-defer shifts the whole curve up ~2x but does NOT change the negative slope.

WHY IT IS NOW THE RIGHT LEVER (and tractable): GDT-defer (shipped) removed the shared-GDT-block per-op write, which was the one block ALL parallel creates conflicted on. With it deferred, parallel creates to DIFFERENT groups (Orlov spreads the per-thread subdirs across groups) now touch DISJOINT blocks (per-group inode-bitmap/inode-table + per-subdir dir-leaf + own child inode). So the 3-part convoy fix's blocker (#1 defer GDT) is DONE; the remaining work is per-group alloc-state locking (#2) so different-group allocations mutate in-memory state in parallel, + releasing the alloc_mutex before the now-disjoint MVCC commits (#3) so they parallelize. All allocator-layer, conformance/e2fsck-gateable (NOT loom). Estimated payoff: 1t is already 2x faster than kernel, so positive scaling would make 16t multiples faster than kernel's 170976/s — the campaign's largest remaining win. NEXT: dedicated pass to shard Ext4AllocState per-group + commit-after-release.

### 2026-06-29 POST-GDT-defer landscape: why GDT was the clean win, and the remaining version-chain costs are owner-lane (CrimsonFox)

Profiled deferred unlink (delbench 20k, FFS_SKIP_GDT=1): __memmove 38.69% + ~21% kernel-fault — STILL version-chain-dominated, MORE than deferred create (22.79%). The remaining hot blocks for a same-dir burst are the INODE BITMAP (parent group; one bit cleared/set per op -> ~Nk versions on one block) and the PARENT INODE (mtime updated per op -> ~Nk versions on one block), plus the dir leaf and inode-table blocks.

KEY INSIGHT (why GDT-defer was a CLEAN win and the rest is not): GDT-defer worked because `GroupStats` is (a) the in-memory AUTHORITATIVE free count AND (b) read-your-writes-FREE — the allocator never reads the GDT block, so the per-op GDT MVCC write was pure deferrable persistence. The OTHER hot blocks lack this:
- INODE BITMAP: read-your-writes is REQUIRED — every alloc/free reads the bitmap block (dev.read_block) to find/clear a bit, so it MUST be in MVCC per-op. Cannot be deferred like GDT. Collapsing its version chain needs VERSION COALESCING (on commit, replace the chain tail instead of appending when no active snapshot needs the superseded version). That is MVCC-store-internal + snapshot-safety-gated: the safe condition (no reader can resolve a coalesced-away version) has a register-vs-publish race that is exactly the loom-gated TOCTOU (a reader registering at a snapshot in [prev_seq, new_seq) before publish would read a stale version) — OWNER-LANE.
- PARENT INODE mtime: deferrable in principle (mtime not read during the burst) but it lives in a shared inode-TABLE block with no per-structure authoritative in-memory state like GroupStats — needs dirty-inode coalescing, structural.

So GDT-defer captured the ONE hot block that had the clean authoritative+RYW-free property; the remaining version-chain churn (bitmap, parent inode, dir leaf) is generalized by version-coalescing, which is owner-lane (snapshot-safety/loom). The biggest NON-owner-lane remaining lever is the parallel-create convoy (alloc_mutex, 3.7x vs kernel) — a multi-part allocator refactor (per-group locking + commit-after-release, now unblocked by GDT-defer), the clear top priority for a dedicated pass. No clean contained single-turn lever remains post-GDT-defer.

### 2026-06-29 GDT-defer default-on: 16 ffs-core lib tests confirm it's a read_group_desc/statfs SEMANTIC change — and pinpoint the fix (CrimsonFox)

Ran ffs-core lib tests UNDER deferral (FFS_SKIP_GDT=1) to harden the shipped opt-in win + scope default-on: 1169 passed, **16 FAILED** — all of the "descriptor/group-stats match device after op" family: `write_statfs_group_stats_match_device_descriptors_bd_qsmav`, `write_unlink_last_link_reclaims_inode_and_blocks_keeps_csum_valid`, `write_rmdir_reclaims_inode_and_block_keeps_csum_valid`, `write_rename_over_existing_file_reclaims_target_inode_and_blocks`, `write_compressed_uncompressed_fallback_preserves_sparse_tail`, etc. These read the ON-DISK / MVCC group descriptor (or statfs, which sums descriptors) RIGHT AFTER an op and assert the free-count changed. Under deferral the per-op GDT write is skipped, so the descriptor lags until flush → assertions fail.

CONFIRMS (16 data points + the 2 conformance tests): GDT-defer default-on is a real semantic change to `read_group_desc` / statfs (mid-session free-counts lag until flush). The shipped win is correctly OPT-IN (env OFF by default → all these tests pass; failures occur ONLY when deferral is explicitly enabled, so the shipped state is unaffected).

PINPOINTED DEFAULT-ON FIX (now concrete): the authoritative free count IS the in-memory `GroupStats` — so route the free-count READERS (statfs `ext4_free_block_counters`/`statfs`, and any `read_group_desc` consumer that needs current free counts) through `GroupStats` instead of the on-disk/MVCC descriptor. Then statfs is accurate under deferral AND the 16+2 tests pass (read the authoritative count). That + flipping the default is the well-scoped path to convert the 2.3x opt-in into a default-path win — a deliberate change (touches statfs semantics) for a dedicated pass, not a blind flip. This supersedes the vaguer "2 conformance tests / unwired boundary" framing with the precise readers-must-use-GroupStats fix.

### 2026-06-29 GDT-defer COVERAGE BOUNDARY: neutral on extent-coalesced block writes (CrimsonFox)

Measured GDT-defer on write-bench (40k block-allocating 4KiB writes, --create) OFF vs ON: ~190ms vs ~180ms = NEUTRAL (within run noise). Reason: block allocation is EXTENT-COALESCED (the shipped 2aa92946 coalescing lever), so the GDT/block-bitmap free-count updates happen per-EXTENT (large runs), not per-block — far fewer GDT writes, so the shared-GDT-block version chain stays short and there's little to defer. This bounds the GDT-defer lever precisely: its value is concentrated on INODE-allocation-heavy metadata ops where each op churns the GDT (create 2.3x, mkdir 1.65x, unlink 1.24x), and is NEUTRAL on extent-coalesced data-block writes. Confirms the lever is correctly scoped to the metadata create/delete path.

STATUS: GDT-defer (the campaign's biggest win) is shipped opt-in, broadly validated (create/mkdir/unlink/rmdir/rename/write all e2fsck-clean), and now coverage-bounded. No new single-turn lever remains: the two forward levers are (1) default-on — route free-count readers (statfs/read_group_desc) through authoritative GroupStats + update the 16+2 per-op-descriptor-persistence tests = owner-level semantic decision; (2) parallel-create convoy (3.7x vs kernel) — per-group alloc-state locking + commit-after-release, a multi-part allocator refactor (add_dir_entry is a 440-line internal-txn function to thread a shared txn through). Both are dedicated-pass efforts, not single-turn measurable levers.

### 2026-06-29 REFUTED (measured, shelved): convoy commit-after-release via shared TransactionBlockAdapter — staging overhead + shared-bitmap conflict (CrimsonFox)

Built the convoy fix prototype: extracted `ext4_add_dir_entry_dev` (external-txn staging variant; `ext4_add_dir_entry` is now a thin begin+commit wrapper — behavior-preserving, compiles clean) + an env-gated (FFS_CONVOY) path in `ext4_create` that stages create_inode + add_dir_entry into ONE TransactionBlockAdapter under the alloc lock, releases the lock, and commits after — so disjoint-group parallel commits should run concurrently. e2fsck CLEAN single-thread (the staging + commit-after-release is correct). But MEASURED a double rejection:
- SINGLE-THREAD: 35781 vs 74377 creates/s = **0.48x** (2x SLOWER). The TransactionBlockAdapter staging overhead (parking_lot Mutex + BTreeMap stage/staged-read on EVERY block read AND write) is even worse here than the prior create-inode-only batch (f1c4880c, 0.92x) because read-heavy `add_dir_entry` (htree descent, multiple dir-block reads) now also routes through the staging adapter.
- PARALLEL (8/16t): PANICS — `first-committer-wins conflict on block 189: snapshot=6175, observed=6180`. Block 189 is group 0's BLOCK BITMAP (dumpe2fs: "Block bitmap at 189"). Even with GDT deferred, parallel threads whose subdir/dir-block allocations land in the SAME group share that group's block bitmap (and inode bitmap), so commit-after-release exposes a real FCW conflict that the serial alloc_mutex was hiding.

REDIRECTS the convoy fix: commit-after-release via a staging adapter is DEAD (staging overhead regresses single-thread; shared bitmaps conflict parallel). The real fix is per-group alloc-state SHARDING so different-group allocations (a) mutate in-memory state under per-group locks AND (b) touch disjoint bitmap blocks — keeping the lean per-block commit path (no staging adapter) but letting disjoint-group creates run truly in parallel. That requires restructuring `Ext4AllocState.groups` for per-group ownership (create_inode takes `&mut [GroupStats]`) — a deeper allocator refactor than commit-after-release, and the only path that avoids BOTH the staging overhead and the shared-bitmap conflict. Shelved `convoy-prototype-WIP-add_dir_entry_dev-refactor+commit-after-release-UNMEASURED` (the add_dir_entry_dev extraction is reusable). conformance unaffected (FFS_CONVOY default-off; not landed).

### 2026-06-29 Convoy spec FINALIZED (diagnostic): inodes ARE spread → per-group sharding viable, but a residual block-189 conflict remains (CrimsonFox)

After the commit-after-release rejection (FCW conflict on block 189), diagnosed the group distribution of an 8-thread deferred create (24k, dumpe2fs free-inodes per group): the threads' inodes ARE spread — groups 2,3,5,6,7 each ~3000 inodes used (24000/8), groups 0/1/4 mostly free. So per-group INODE allocation already spreads across groups (different inode bitmaps), and per-group alloc-state sharding WOULD let those inode allocations + commits run in parallel.

BUT the commit-after-release still conflicted on block 189 = group 0's BLOCK bitmap, even though the inodes are in groups 2-7. So there is a residual BLOCK-level sharing: the per-thread subdirs' DIR-BLOCK growth (each file added grows the subdir, allocating a dir block) is landing in / falling back to group 0's block bitmap rather than the subdir's own group — a block-allocator locality gap, OR a root-relative allocation. So the convoy fix is TWO parts: (1) per-group alloc-state sharding (Ext4AllocState.groups -> per-group ownership; create_inode takes &mut [GroupStats]) so different-group allocations parallelize without the staging adapter; (2) ensure dir-block allocation is GROUP-LOCAL to the parent (so a subdir in group 5 allocates its dir blocks from group 5's bitmap, not group 0) — else the block bitmap stays a shared conflict point.

This FINALIZES the convoy fix spec (measured + diagnosed): a dedicated allocator refactor (per-group sharding + group-local block allocation), justified by the 3.7x-vs-kernel gap and the measured failure of the simpler commit-after-release. It is NOT a single-turn lever. The shipped GDT-defer (2.3x, single-thread create now beats kernel) remains the campaign's landed win; the convoy is the scoped, measured-justified top priority for a dedicated multi-turn pass. The reusable `ext4_add_dir_entry_dev` extraction is shelved for it.

### 2026-06-29 Convoy locality hypothesis REFUTED: block allocator is already group-local (CrimsonFox)

Investigated whether the convoy's residual block-189 conflict was a contained dir-block LOCALITY bug (dir blocks allocated from group 0 instead of the subdir's group). It is NOT: `alloc_blocks_persist` (ffs-alloc) builds its group order via `allocation_group_order` -> `legacy_allocation_goal_group(hint)` -> `push_legacy_group_order`, which STARTS the search at the goal_block's group, and the dir-grow path passes `goal_block: extents.last()` (the parent's last extent, group-local). So dir-block allocation already prefers the parent's group. The block-189 conflict is therefore a residual edge (first-block-of-a-fresh-subdir defaults toward group 0 when there's no last-extent goal, and/or root-directory growth shared across threads), NOT a general locality gap. There is no contained locality lever to extract here.

CLOSES the convoy investigation's contained-lever search: the simple commit-after-release is measured-dead (staging overhead + shared-bitmap FCW), the block allocator is already group-local, so the convoy fix is exclusively the per-group `Ext4AllocState` SHARDING refactor (a dedicated multi-turn allocator change), justified by the 3.7x-vs-kernel parallel-create gap. The campaign's landed win remains GDT-block deferral (2.3x single-thread create, beats kernel); the convoy is the scoped, measured-justified top priority for a dedicated pass; default-on is an owner-level read_group_desc/statfs semantic decision. No contained single-turn lever remains.

### 2026-06-29 Convoy PRIZE SIZED (upper-bound bench): ~9x achievable, would beat kernel (CrimsonFox)

Applied the "measure the upper bound first" discipline (the GDT-defer playbook) to size the convoy fix's prize before committing to the per-group-sharding refactor. Ran 8 INDEPENDENT deferred create-bench processes (12k creates each, separate 256M images, parallel via shell `&`+wait — no shared alloc_mutex, the ideal per-group-sharding ceiling):
- 8 independent procs: 96000 creates in 0.196s wall = **~489,000 creates/s aggregate** (6.3x the single-thread 78k/s; the <8x is host core contention, and the wall-clock includes process startup/fs-load so the true rate is higher).
- vs current single-image 8-thread deferred: **54,000/s**.

So eliminating the alloc_mutex serialization (per-group sharding) could recover up to **~9x** (54k -> ~489k creates/s) at 8 threads — and crucially would BEAT the kernel's measured parallel ceiling (170k/s at 16t). The convoy is the campaign's largest remaining prize by far. This upper-bound bench + the earlier measured rejection of commit-after-release (staging overhead + shared-bitmap FCW) + the group-local-allocator confirmation together fully justify and de-risk the dedicated per-group `Ext4AllocState`-sharding refactor as the next pass: shard groups for per-group ownership so different-group creates mutate state under per-group locks and commit concurrently via the lean per-block path (no staging adapter). Landed win remains GDT-defer (2.3x single-thread, beats kernel); convoy is the scoped, prize-sized, measured-justified top priority.

### 2026-06-29 Convoy IMPLEMENTATION PLAN (turnkey, supersedes scattered scoping notes) (CrimsonFox)

All convoy investigation is complete: prize ~9x (54k->~489k creates/s ceiling, beats kernel 170k); commit-after-release via shared TransactionBlockAdapter is measured-dead (0.48x staging + parallel FCW on shared bitmap); block allocator is already group-local. The fix is per-group concurrency on `Ext4AllocState`. Definitive plan for a dedicated pass:

DESIGN (interior-mutability, lower-blast-radius than restructuring `groups: Vec<GroupStats>`):
1. Make the create FAST PATH take `alloc_mutex.read()` (shared) instead of `.write()`, so concurrent creates aren't serialized at the outer lock. Structural ops (resize/mount) keep `.write()`.
2. Add per-group interior locks: `groups: Box<[Mutex<GroupStats>]>` (or `GroupStats` fields → atomics for the lock-free counters + a per-group `Mutex` for the bitmap RMW). `create_inode`/`alloc_inode_persist` change from `&mut [GroupStats]` to `&[Mutex<GroupStats>]`, locking ONLY the target group (the common file path: target=parent_group, no scan). The Orlov/fallback scan (rare) reads many group free-counts (use the atomics) then write-locks the chosen group.
3. Keep the LEAN per-block commit path (NOT the staging adapter — it regressed 0.48x). Disjoint-group creates touch disjoint inode-bitmap/inode-table/dir blocks → their per-block MVCC commits don't conflict (GDT already deferred). The CommitPublicationGate's in-order watermark is the only remaining global point; measure whether it bottlenecks at the ~489k ceiling (if so, the gap-tolerant-publication redesign is the follow-up, but per-group sharding alone should approach the ceiling since commits are brief).
4. Resolve the residual same-group block conflict (fresh-subdir first dir block / root growth defaulting toward group 0): ensure the fresh-subdir's first dir block uses the subdir's inode group as the goal (not a None->group-0 default).

MILESTONES (each build+conformance-green): (a) GroupStats free-counter atomics under the existing write lock (behavior-identical, buildable); (b) per-group bitmap Mutex + create_inode takes `&[Mutex<GroupStats>]` (still under outer write lock — behavior-identical); (c) flip ext4_create fast path to `.read()` + measure create --threads scaling vs the ~489k ceiling; revert if conflicts/regression. VALIDATION: e2fsck after parallel create + conformance + the existing bd-bky2f lock-ordering threaded tests.

This is the campaign's top remaining lever, fully measured/justified/de-risked. Landed win: GDT-defer (2.3x single-thread create, beats kernel). Reusable `ext4_add_dir_entry_dev` extraction shelved. Default-on (route statfs/read_group_desc through GroupStats) is the separate owner-level semantic follow-up.

## KEEP — 2026-07-01 — `bd-btrcow-evict` btrfs COW dead-version eviction (CrimsonFox)

**Lever (ffs-btrfs, KEEP/SHIPPED):** `InMemoryCowBtrfsTree::commit_retired_nodes`
left every superseded COW node version in the in-memory `nodes` FxHashMap
forever — the monotonic block allocator never reissues an id, and only the
rollback path removed nodes. So `nodes` grew with the operation count (~3
retired nodes/rename never reclaimed): an unbounded memory leak that (1) decayed
mutation throughput via ever-worsening FxHashMap cache misses and (2) paid a
giant one-shot drop when the tree was released (perf: `drop_glue::<InMemoryCowBtrfsTree>`
8.92% self + `insert_into`/`alloc_internal_node`/`clone` COW churn). Fix = a
two-generation eviction ring: retired nodes are parked in `prev_committed_retired`
for exactly one generation (so the COW snapshot contract — the immediately
previous root stays readable via `node_snapshot`, asserted by
`cow_insert_preserves_previous_root_node` — holds) and evicted at the next
commit when they are two generations back and unreachable from `root`.
Production only ever reads the current `root` (MVCC versioning lives in
`MvccStore`), so one generation back is the whole requirement.

**MEASURED (btrfs rename, `ffs-cli rename-bench`, real btrfs fixture, interleaved
two-binary A/B pre-fix vs post-fix, renames/s → us/op):**

| count | OLD (pre-fix) | NEW (post-fix) | speedup | NEW vs ORIG (kernel 36.5 us/op) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5000  | 9337 r/s (107.1 us) | 24109 r/s (41.5 us) | 2.58x | **1.14x** (near parity) |
| 20000 | 5874 r/s (170.2 us) | 17245 r/s (58.0 us) | 2.93x | **1.59x** |
| 40000 | 4667 r/s (214.3 us) | 13982 r/s (71.5 us) | 3.00x | 1.96x |

OLD is steeply O(N) in tree size (107→214 us/op, the leak); NEW is far flatter
(41→71 us, residual = genuine tree depth/working-set, shared with kernel).
**vs ORIG (live btrfs kernel RW loop mount, 20k create+rename+sync = 36.5 us/op):
the gap collapses from 4.66x slower to 1.59x slower (1.14x at 5k).** Helps EVERY
btrfs mutation (create/rename/unlink/mkdir), not just rename.

**Correctness:** `btrfs check` CLEAN on the NEW-produced image; OLD vs NEW images
STRUCTURALLY IDENTICAL (bytes used / tree bytes / fs-tree bytes / csum bytes all
match to the byte — the ring only reclaims dead in-memory versions, never the
persisted output, root, or free accounting). ffs-btrfs 365/0 (+38 doc, incl.
`cow_insert_preserves_previous_root_node`, `proptest_insert_many_matches_sequential`);
ffs-core btrfs 360/0; conformance 100/0/2 GREEN; clippy + fmt clean.

**Method note:** the biggest write-side gap (btrfs rename) was mis-attributed to
"the per-op commit/serialization layer, owner-lane" in prior notes; a fresh
`perf record` of `rename-bench` showed the cost is in `ffs_btrfs::InMemoryCowBtrfsTree`
node clone/alloc/**drop** (COW machinery), and the throughput-decays-with-N
signature pinpointed the retained-version leak. Profile FIRST beats trusting a
stale root-cause label.

## KEEP — 2026-07-01 — `bd-btrcow-sepkeep` btrfs delete separator-carry-forward (CrimsonFox)

**Lever (ffs-btrfs, KEEP/SHIPPED):** after the `bd-btrcow-evict` fix, a fresh
`perf record` of `rename-bench` showed `InMemoryCowBtrfsTree::alloc_internal_node`
at 17.3% self-time. It calls `compute_internal_keys`, which re-derives EVERY
separator of a COW'd internal node by descending to each child's leftmost leaf
via `first_key` — up to `max_items` descents (651 for a 16 KiB nodesize). The
delete path (`delete_from`) called it on every internal node it rebuilt, even
though only the one child on the delete path was COW-replaced and every other
separator still equals its unchanged child's subtree minimum. (The INSERT path
already carried separators forward; only DELETE recomputed.)

**Fix:** `rebalance_child` now returns whether it structurally changed the
children (rotate/merge — the rare underflow case); `delete_from` reuses the old
`keys` on the common no-underflow path and recomputes only `keys[idx-1]` (the
separator left of the touched child, whose subtree minimum may have shifted),
falling back to the full `alloc_internal_node` recompute only on the rare
structural change. O(max_items) → O(1) separator descents per delete.

**MEASURED (rename-bench, real btrfs fixture, interleaved A/B vs the eviction-only
binary; the win scales with tree size = wider internal nodes):**

| count | eviction-only | +sepkeep | ratio | NEW vs ORIG (kernel 36.5 us/op) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5000  | 25153 r/s | 26293 r/s | 1.045x | 1.04x (near parity) |
| 20000 | 17762 r/s | ~18900 r/s | ~1.06x | 1.45x |
| 40000 | 13761 r/s (median) | 15567 r/s | **1.13x** (clean sep: NEW min > OLD max) | **1.76x** |

Combined with `bd-btrcow-evict`, btrfs rename at 40k is now 1.76x vs kernel
(was 4.66x before either fix). Helps every btrfs delete/unlink/rename.

**Correctness:** `btrfs check` CLEAN; NEW vs eviction-only images STRUCTURALLY
IDENTICAL (bytes used / tree / fs-tree / csum all byte-equal — separators are
byte-for-byte the same, just computed cheaply). ffs-btrfs 365/0 (incl.
`validate_invariants`, which asserts `separator == first_key(child)` for every
internal node, + `proptest_insert_many_matches_sequential`); conformance
100/0/2 GREEN; clippy + fmt clean.

## REJECT (neutral) — 2026-07-01 — `bd-envcache` per-op env-var memoization (CrimsonFox)

**Context:** re-profiled the biggest remaining ext4 write-side gap, file removal,
with the same "profile-first, distrust the inherited label" lens that flipped
btrfs rename twice. ext4 file removal is now **~20 us/op (50k/s) = ~1.29x vs
kernel 15.5 us/op** — already improved from the 1.68x note (the one-pass removal
`3f24e58b` landed since). `perf record` of `delbench --count 40000` shows a FLAT
profile with no mineable hot frame: `__memmove` 10.1% (MVCC version-buffer
materialization), `ShardedMvccStore::commit` 5.7%, `crc32c` ~5.9% (metadata block
checksums), `parse_extent_leaf` 2.5%, kernel page-faults ~8%. This is inherent
per-op MVCC + checksum work the kernel also pays — no structural lever like the
btrfs COW leak.

**Lever tried (REJECTED, shelved `stash@{0}`):** the ONE clearly-wasteful frame
was `getenv` 1.09% self-time, from `OpenFs::block_device_adapter` calling
`std::env::var("FFS_WRITABLE_ADAPTER_REGISTER")` on every block op of every
writable-mount FsOp (plus the per-create `FFS_NO_HTREE_CREATE_DEDUP` check).
Memoized both via `OnceLock<bool>` (process-static flags, byte-identical).
**MEASURED NEUTRAL:** ext4 removal 40k interleaved 7x = **0.995x median** (NEW
overlaps OLD); ext4 create = 1.053x median but with NEW/OLD overlap (not clean
separation). WHY: for the DEFAULT unset var, `std::env::var` returns
`Err(NotPresent)` WITHOUT allocating a `String` (the alloc only happens when the
var is present), so the per-op cost is just a cheap `getenv` `environ` scan
(~50-100 ns) — real CPU (hence the 1.09% sample) but off the memmove/commit-bound
critical path, so removing it does not move wall-clock. Per the revert-~0-gain
rule, shelved rather than landed.

**Retry predicate:** revisit only if a block-op-dense writable workload (e.g.
large sequential metadata mutation) profiles `getenv` ABOVE the critical path, or
if a future change makes `block_device_adapter` allocation-bound. Separately,
`parse_extent_leaf` 2.5% during empty-file removal is worth a look — the directory
inode's extent tree may be re-parsed per op to map logical→physical dir blocks (a
caching opportunity), but it was not pursued here.

## SURFACE (owner-lane) — 2026-07-01 — ext4 parallel create negative scaling = the `alloc_mutex.write()` convoy (CrimsonFox)

**Biggest gap vs ORIG measured this turn.** ext4 parallel create (create-bench
`--threads N`, each thread in its OWN subdir so the only shared state is the
alloc/commit path) SCALES NEGATIVELY at HEAD — more threads = LOWER aggregate
throughput, where the kernel scales up:

| threads | creates/s (default) | creates/s (`FFS_SKIP_GDT=1`) |
|---|---|---|
| 1  | 35940 | 78667 |
| 2  | 30076 | — |
| 4  | 26684 | 55455 |
| 8  | 27488 | 58369 |
| 16 | 26042 | 48436 |

**Ruled out (fresh measurement, so these are NOT the fix):**
- **GDT shared-block write** — `FFS_SKIP_GDT=1` gives the known 2.2x SINGLE-thread
  win (35940→78667) but scaling stays negative (78667→48436). The shared
  group-descriptor block is not the parallel bottleneck.
- **MVCC layer** — the pure `sharded_mvcc_disjoint` bench (N writers, fully
  disjoint blocks, no alloc) does ~261k commits/s @8w → ~217k @16w = only MILD
  negative scaling (partly thread-spawn-in-`b.iter` overhead). Create achieves
  only ~135k block-commits/s @8t (27k creates × ~5 blocks), well under the MVCC
  ceiling — so the publication gate / commit path is NOT the create bottleneck.
  (Confirms the earlier `publish-nowait` NEUTRAL result — the gate is not the
  limiter.)

**Root cause (pinpointed):** `OpenFs::ext4_create` / `ext4_mkdir` take a single
GLOBAL `alloc_mutex.write()` over the ENTIRE `Ext4AllocState` (lib.rs:16793) for
every create — fully serializing parallel creates at the alloc layer regardless
of which group they touch. Secondary: inode allocation uses
`goal_group = parent_group` (lib.rs:16507/16813) with NO Orlov-style directory
spreading, so the per-thread subdirs (all created in root) inherit root's group,
and every thread's children then convoy through the SAME group's inode-bitmap
block. This is exactly the `bd-bhh0i` / convoy target: the git-log plan
(interior-mutability per-group locks: `alloc_mutex.read()` fast path + per-group
`GroupStats` Mutex/atomics) is the correct owner-lane fix. Upper bound already
sized (8 independent processes, no shared lock = ~489k creates/s = ~9x).

**Why not landed this turn:** the fix is a RwLock→per-group-locks refactor of the
alloc state (owner-lane, lock-ordering + FCW + e2fsck validation), not a
contained ~15-min change; and the tractable-looking Orlov dir-spreading
alternative changes on-disk placement (e2fsck/golden-layout risk) so it was not
rushed. No ~0-gain code was landed. Contained sub-lever candidate for a future
turn: spread fresh-directory inode allocation across groups (Orlov) so subdirs
don't cluster — reduces the secondary bitmap convoy without the full alloc-state
refactor, but still needs golden/e2fsck validation.

## KEEP — 2026-07-01 — `bd-btrcreate-dedup` btrfs create single-descent dir-entry check (CrimsonFox)

**Lever (ffs-btrfs/ffs-core, KEEP/SHIPPED):** profiled btrfs single-thread create
(`create-bench --threads 1`, real btrfs fixture). `insert_into` 44% (inherent COW
leaf clone) dominated, but `for_each_in_range` was **9.68%** — btrfs_create issued
THREE separate htree descents to the SAME parent DIR_ITEM leaf at hash(name):
`btrfs_preflight_dir_entry_insert` (parse-validation) + `btrfs_lookup_dir_entry`
(EEXIST) + an inline `range` (hash-collision flag) — plus a 4th always-empty
DIR_INDEX preflight descent to (parent, DIR_INDEX, next_objectid) (a fresh
never-used sequence slot). Collapsed to ONE `range(dir_item_key)` descent that
yields all three answers (parse each payload = validation; scan for name = EEXIST;
non-empty = collision); dropped the always-empty DIR_INDEX probe.

**MEASURED ~1.13x** btrfs create (create-bench 40k, interleaved A/B vs clean
no-dedup baseline: OLD ~68285 → NEW ~76848 creates/s median; clean runs
67-71k→77k). **Correctness:** `btrfs check` CLEAN on the NEW-created image;
ffs-core btrfs 360/0; conformance 100/0/2 GREEN. Same redundant-descent pattern
exists in `btrfs_mkdir`/`btrfs_mknod`/`btrfs_symlink` (fresh-oid create family) —
noted follow-up, not changed this turn.

## KEEP — 2026-07-01 — `bd-btrcreate-dedup` extended to mkdir/mknod/symlink (CrimsonFox)

**Follow-up to the create dedup above.** Extracted the single-descent check into a
shared `btrfs_create_dir_entry_check` helper (one `range(dir_item_key)` → parse
validation + EEXIST + collision flag) and applied it to `btrfs_mkdir`,
`btrfs_mknod`, and `btrfs_symlink`, which each did the same three redundant
descents (`preflight` + `lookup_dir_entry` + inline collision) plus the
always-empty DIR_INDEX probe. mknod/symlink use only the EEXIST+preflight result
(they always take the per-op insert path); mkdir also uses the collision flag for
its batch fast path.

**MEASURED ~1.074x** btrfs mkdir (mkdir-bench 20k, interleaved A/B vs the
create-dedup-only binary: OLD ~78528 → NEW ~84354 mkdirs/s median, every NEW run >
its paired OLD). **Correctness:** `btrfs check` CLEAN on a NEW-mkdir image;
ffs-core btrfs 360/0 (incl. `btrfs_mkdir_duplicate_name_returns_eexist`, the mknod
EEXIST/fifo/socket/dev tests, and the symlink btrfs-check test); conformance
100/0/2 GREEN; fmt clean.

## KEEP — 2026-07-01 — `bd-btrcow-sepkeep2` btrfs delete rebalance separator maintenance (CrimsonFox)

**Biggest btrfs mutation gap this turn.** Profiled btrfs file removal (`delbench`
30k, real btrfs fixture): `alloc_internal_node` was **19.71%** self-time (plus more
under `BtrfsCowNode::clone`). The earlier `bd-btrcow-sepkeep` fix only bypassed the
full `compute_internal_keys` recompute on the NO-underflow delete path; mass
deletion constantly underflows leaves → `rebalance_child` rotates/merges → the
parent rebuild fell back to `alloc_internal_node`, which re-derives EVERY separator
by descending to each child's leftmost leaf (up to `max_items` = 651 `first_key`
descents per rebalanced delete).

**Fix:** `rebalance_child` now maintains the parent's `keys` IN PLACE. Each
structural op touches at most the separators adjacent to `child_idx` — a
rotate-from-left refreshes `keys[idx-1]` (child's new min = borrowed item), a
rotate-from-right refreshes `keys[idx]` (right's new min) and `keys[idx-1]`
(child's delete-shifted min), a merge drops the single separator between the merged
pair — each via at most one `first_key`. `delete_from` no longer calls
`alloc_internal_node` at all (it remains only for the rare internal-sibling
rebalance). O(max_items) → O(1) separator descents per rebalanced delete.

**MEASURED ~1.53x** btrfs removal (delbench 30k, interleaved A/B vs the
create-family-dedup binary: OLD ~19808 → NEW ~30302 unlinks/s median, every NEW run
> every OLD run). **Correctness:** `btrfs check` CLEAN after 20k create + 20k delete
(maximum rotate/merge exercise); ffs-btrfs 365/0 (incl. `validate_invariants`
asserting `separator == first_key(child)` for every internal node, +
`proptest_insert_many_matches_sequential`); conformance 100/0/2 GREEN; fmt clean.

## SURFACE — 2026-07-01 — single-thread mutation frontier state after the btrfs COW sweep (CrimsonFox)

**btrfs mutations now at kernel PARITY** — the 6-commit COW sweep (dead-version
eviction 2.6-3.0x → sepkeep +1.13x → create-family dedup 1.13x/1.07x → rebalance
sepkeep 1.53x) closed the gap. Fresh measurement, current HEAD binary, real btrfs
fixture vs the live-kernel baseline (36.5 us/op): btrfs rename **30.7 us/op =
1.19x FASTER than kernel @5k**, **36.4 us = parity @20k**, 39.5 us = 0.92x @40k
(residual = per-op COW clone growing with tree depth — inherent, kernel COWs too).
Was 4.66x SLOWER before the sweep.

**ext4 single-thread mutations are inherent** (no contained algorithmic lever
left — the O(N^2) rename preflight and the create-path lookup dedup already
landed; the create/mkdir profiles are flat = per-op MVCC version-materialization
the kernel also pays). Measured this turn: ext4 create 29.4 us/op (~1.16x vs
kernel 25.3), ext4 mkdir 62.2 us/op, ext4 removal ~20 us (~1.29x).

**One concrete ext4 lead (NOT landed — marginal, needs a cross-crate refactor):**
`ext4_mkdir` writes the new inode table block TWICE — `ffs_inode::create_inode`
persists the inode body (its final `write_inode`), then `ext4_mkdir` calls
`write_inode` AGAIN (lib.rs:16911) after setting size/blocks/links_count=2/extent
root. `ext4_create` writes once (no post-alloc inode mutation). Eliminating the
redundant first write needs a deferred-body `create_inode` variant + moving the
single persist into each caller (create/mkdir/mknod/symlink) — estimated only
~1.03x on mkdir (one of ~6 block materializations), below the revert-near-zero
bar for the refactor risk, so shelved as a documented lead. **Remaining real
frontier = the parallel-create `alloc_mutex.write()` convoy (owner-lane, sized
~9x) and the read paths (already dominate).**

## SURFACE — 2026-07-02 — parallel random-read gap CLOSED (now beats kernel) (CrimsonFox)

Re-measured the parallel random-read path (the old `bd-xmh5g` 88x-slower gap that
was reduced to ~3.3x, with a flagged residual "ShardedCache shard-Mutex on the hot
inode block"). On the hot-block parallel case (all reads funnel through the same
cache shard — exactly the shard-mutex contention the residual named),
`ffs-cli rand-read --parallel` (64-way rayon) vs the kernel `krandread` (64
pthreads, `pread`) on the same ext4 image, warm:
- frankenfs **3,592,571 IOPS / 14033 MiB/s** vs kernel **2,767,175 IOPS / 10809
  MiB/s** = **frankenfs 1.30x FASTER** (a second run: 3.75M vs 2.39M = 1.57x).
So the shard-mutex residual is no longer a gap — the parallel random-read path now
BEATS the kernel. Closes that ledger item.

⚠️ Harness caveat: `write-bench --create` builds the rand-read fixture but the
kernel mount sees the file as **512 bytes** despite ~145k blocks allocated
(e2fsck-clean, inode extent tree present) — i_size is not persisted in a
kernel-visible way through that fixture flow, so `span` collapses to 1 and BOTH
sides read the hot single block (still a valid shard-contention microbench, but a
proper spread-random test needs the fixture builder fixed). Recorded so the next
random-read digger fixes the builder before testing spread-random.

## KEEP — 2026-07-02 — `bd-mkinode-defer` ext4 mkdir/mknod skip the redundant inode-body write (CrimsonFox)

**The mkdir double-inode-write lead (surfaced in `bb534d5d`) turned out FAR bigger
than the ~1.03x estimate.** `ffs_inode::create_inode` persists the new inode's body
(`write_inode`), but `ext4_mkdir` / `ext4_mknod` then modify the inode
(size/blocks/links_count/extent root; rdev) and call `write_inode` AGAIN — so the
inode-table block was materialized TWICE per op (each write = read-modify-write +
CRC32C recompute + a fresh MVCC version-chain entry + commit). Split `create_inode`
into `prepare_inode` (alloc number + bitmap + build inode, NO body write) +
`create_inode` (= prepare + write, unchanged for `ext4_create` and other callers);
`ext4_mkdir`/`ext4_mknod` now call `prepare_inode` and rely on their existing
single `write_inode`. Safe: the inode number is reserved in the bitmap and both
callers write the final body before any lookup can resolve the new name, so no
reader observes the unwritten slot.

**MEASURED ~1.526x ext4 mkdir** (mkdir-bench 20k, interleaved A/B vs the pre-defer
binary: OLD ~16532 → NEW ~25230 mkdirs/s median, every NEW run > every OLD run;
60.5 → 39.6 us/op). vs ORIG: frankenfs mkdir was already 1.64x faster than the
kernel (99.1 us/op, loop mount + os.mkdir + sync) and is now **2.5x faster**.
`ext4_mknod` gets the same fix (no bench, but it double-wrote identically).

**Symlink was REVERTED** (kept `create_inode`): its fast-target path stores the
link inline and does NOT re-write the inode, so deferring the body write left a
fast symlink unpersisted — caught immediately by 3 `write_symlink_slow_target*`
tests. Only ops that unconditionally `write_inode` the final body qualify.

**Correctness:** `e2fsck` CLEAN on a NEW-mkdir image; ffs-core mkdir 11/0, mknod
15/0, symlink 21/0 (post-revert); conformance 100/0/2 GREEN; fmt clean.

## SURFACE — 2026-07-02 — mkdir writes the GDT block TWICE; GDT-defer-default is the top lever, blocked by exactly 2 tests (CrimsonFox)

**Applied the "redundant per-op metadata WRITE is ~20us" lesson** (from bd-mkinode-defer)
with a throwaway `FFS_TRACE_WRITES` counter in `FsMvccBlockDevice::write_block`
(per-block commit point) — logged block numbers for single create vs single mkdir:
- **create**: writes 3 distinct blocks, none repeated (GDT block 1 once).
- **mkdir**: writes the GDT block (block 1) **TWICE** — `alloc_inode_persist`
  (free_inodes--) then `alloc_blocks_persist` for the dir data block (free_blocks--).
  (The inode-table block is now written once, post bd-mkinode-defer.)

So every mkdir (and any op that allocs both an inode AND a block) pays TWO GDT-block
MVCC commits. Sizing via the existing `FFS_SKIP_GDT` (defers ALL per-op GDT writes to
flush): **mkdir 22629 → 28750 mkdirs/s = 1.27x** (create is the documented 2.32x).

**GDT-defer-default (`bd-cc-gdt-defer-default`) is the single biggest remaining
single-thread lever** — it helps EVERY allocating op (create/mkdir/mknod/unlink/write).
Ran the full conformance suite with `FFS_SKIP_GDT=1`: **98 pass, exactly 2 fail** —
`ext4_e2compr_write_readback_conforms_for_gzip_and_lzo` and `full_conformance_gate_pass`
(both go e2fsck-dirty). `flush_on_destroy` DOES flush the GDT (via
`flush_mvcc_to_device`), so the blocker is NOT the general drop path — it is these two
tests' specific persist boundaries (the e2compr rewrite / gate path) where the deferred
GDT is not flushed before the e2fsck check.

**NOT landed:** flipping the default is a codebase-wide durability-semantics change the
maintainers deliberately left opt-in; making it safe needs the GDT flush wired into
those two persist boundaries + a full e2fsck sweep (owner-lane, correctness-sensitive —
a miss ships e2fsck-dirty images). A narrow per-op coalesce (skip the inode-alloc GDT
write, let the block-alloc's write capture both from GroupStats) is ~1.13x but is only
safe when both allocs land in the SAME group (parent_group) — the block-spill-to-another-
group case would drop the inode-group descriptor update, so it needs the same deferral
machinery. Instrumentation was reverted; no code landed.

## SURFACE (negative) — 2026-07-02 — ext4 write/create/overwrite paths have NO redundant block writes (CrimsonFox)

Extended the `FFS_TRACE_WRITES` per-block-commit trace to the create/write/overwrite/
append paths to hunt more mkdir-class double-writes. Result — the data-mutation paths
are CLEAN (each block committed exactly once per logical op):
- **ext4 create**: 3 blocks, none repeated (GDT once, inode-bitmap once, inode-table once).
- **ext4 overwrite** (`write-bench` no `--create`, count=1): exactly 2 writes — inode-table
  + the data block, each once. No zero-fill-then-overwrite (allocate_extent makes an
  UNWRITTEN extent — reads return zeroes virtually, no disk zero-fill), no double.
- **ext4 append**: block-bitmap + GDT + data + inode, each once. The apparent
  `[data, inode] × 2` in a `write-bench --create count=1` trace is a HARNESS ARTIFACT —
  `--create` runs a prefill write loop AND then the timed benchmark write loop, so the
  same offset is written twice as two legitimate `write()` ops, not one op double-writing.

So the only redundant per-op WRITE in the ext4 metadata paths is the mkdir/mknod GDT
double-write (surfaced above, owner-lane). create/write are at the inherent
per-op-MVCC-commit floor — no contained redundant-write lever remains on them.
⚠️Method note: `write-bench --create` prefills THEN benchmarks the same offsets; use the
plain overwrite mode (no `--create`, on a pre-sized file) to trace a single clean write.
Instrumentation reverted; no code landed.

## SURFACE — 2026-07-02 — ext4 lookup beats kernel 1.30x; GDT-defer-default blocker is ONE over-strict assertion (CrimsonFox)

Two findings that further close/sharpen the frontier:

**(1) ext4 lookup is DONE (beats kernel).** `lookup-bench` on a 40k-entry htree dir:
frankenfs **388756 lookups/s = 2.57 us/op** vs kernel (loop mount + random `os.stat`)
**300156/s = 3.33 us/op** = **frankenfs 1.30x faster** (htree O(log N) descent; kernel
also pays syscall+path-walk overhead, so the real gap is directionally in frankenfs's
favor). No lookup gap.

**(2) The GDT-defer-default lever (`bd-cc-gdt-defer-default`, the biggest remaining
single-thread win: 2.32x create + 1.27x mkdir + helps EVERY allocating op) is blocked by
EXACTLY ONE over-strict conformance assertion — not missing flush wiring.** Ran the two
failing tests under `FFS_SKIP_GDT=1`: both panic at the SAME line,
`crates/ffs-harness/tests/conformance.rs:3432`, `"compressed write should update group
descriptor free-block counters"` — it asserts the ON-DISK group-descriptor free-block
counter decreased eagerly after a write. Under deferral the GD is lazy (in-memory
`GroupStats` is authoritative; the SUPERBLOCK free-count assertion two lines up at 3428
still PASSES, so allocation IS accounted). GD counters are ext4 HINTS (the block bitmap
is authoritative; e2fsck recomputes them), so the assertion over-specifies eager
persistence. `flush_on_destroy` already flushes the GD, and the doc notes e2fsck is CLEAN
under deferral across create/unlink/rmdir/rename/mkdir/write — so the ONLY thing standing
between opt-in and default is relaxing this one assertion (flush-then-check, or read
`GroupStats`) + flipping the default.

**NOT done by me:** flipping a deliberately-opt-in durability default AND editing a
conformance assertion to make a perf change pass is an owner decision — a subtle miss
(a path relying on eager GD, or crash-recovery of GD counts) ships a durability
regression to the DEFAULT path. Precisely handed off: relax `conformance.rs:3432`, flip
`gdt_persistence_deferred` default, run the full suite + e2fsck sweep. No code landed.

## SURFACE — 2026-07-02 — ext4 rmdir is clean (no double-write); single-thread mutation frontier now fully mapped (CrimsonFox)

Traced ext4 rmdir (`rdbench`) via `FFS_TRACE_WRITES` — it commits ~1 block per op
(free inode-table slot + free bitmaps/entry, no redundant re-write). frankenfs rmdir
= **20660 rmdirs/s = 48.4 us/op** (the shell-`rmdir`-loop kernel baseline is
fork-dominated at 3422 us/op = meaningless; rmdir needs a C harness like `krandread`
for a fair kernel number, but the trace shows rmdir is at the inherent per-op-commit
floor, no double-write like mkdir had).

**Op-by-op frontier map is now COMPLETE** (all traced/measured this campaign): btrfs
create/mkdir/rename/unlink at kernel PARITY (6-commit COW sweep); ext4 create / write /
overwrite / append / unlink / rmdir CLEAN (each block committed once, inherent
per-op-MVCC floor); ext4 mkdir/mknod had the inode double-write (FIXED, 1.5x,
bd-mkinode-defer) and retain the GDT double-write (owner-lane); ext4 lookup 1.30x
FASTER, random read 1.30-1.57x FASTER, sequential reads + metadata walk dominate.
**No contained single-thread redundant-work lever remains.** The three real remaining
gaps are all owner-lane: (a) GDT-defer-default (2.32x create, one-assertion blocker at
conformance.rs:3432 + durability-default flip), (b) intra-op write batching (1.83x
create, shelved on a read-your-writes bug, stash@{0}), (c) the parallel-create
`alloc_mutex.write()` convoy (~9x, per-group interior-mutability refactor). Instrumentation
reverted; no code landed.

## REJECT (neutral) — 2026-07-02 — MVCC prune-interval tuning does NOT affect create (chain length is not the bottleneck) (CrimsonFox)

Tested the hypothesis that the create cost attributed to the hot shared GDT block is
its MVCC version-chain growing between prunes (`MVCC_COMMIT_PRUNE_INTERVAL = 256`,
`prune_after_commit_if_due` in `fs-core/src/fs_mvcc_store.rs`). Made the interval
env-tunable and A/B'd create-bench (40k, single-thread) across intervals 16 / 256 / 4096
/ 100000 (100000 = NO pruning at all during the run → the GDT chain grows to the full
40k). **MEASURED NEUTRAL: all ~33-34k creates/s regardless.** WHY: version installs
APPEND (monotonic `commit_seq` → `versions.insert` at the tail = O(1), no shift) and
reads binary-search (`newest_visible_index`, O(log N)), so even a 40k-long chain neither
slows the per-op commit nor the reads. Also confirms the `active_snapshots`-unregistered
writable adapter lets the prune watermark advance, so chains ARE bounded to ~256 in
normal runs — no unbounded leak like the btrfs COW case.

⭐So the GDT-defer 2.32x create win is PURELY the elimination of the per-create WRITE of
the shared GDT block (its commit machinery + 4 KiB version materialization), NOT
version-chain management — do NOT chase "prune more aggressively" as a create lever. The
only path to that 2.32x remains GDT-defer-default (owner-lane durability flip, blocker at
conformance.rs:3432). Env change reverted; no code landed.

## CORRECTION + REJECT — 2026-07-02 — GDT-defer-default is NOT a one-assertion fix (ATTEMPTED, reverted) (CrimsonFox)

⚠️CORRECTS the prior surface (3e5e1332) which claimed the GDT-defer-default blocker was
"exactly ONE over-strict assertion at conformance.rs:3432." That was WRONG. I ATTEMPTED
the full change with a safety gate:
- Flipped `gdt_persistence_deferred()` default ON (`FFS_SKIP_GDT=0` = eager opt-out).
- Fixed the assertion HONESTLY: made `ext4_free_block_counters` (used only by the e2compr
  test) `flush_mvcc_to_device` first, so the on-disk GD read reflects the PERSISTED state
  (a stronger invariant, valid in both modes — not a weakening).

**RESULT: the e2compr assertion STILL FAILS** (`after_first_gd_free_blocks <
baseline_gd_free_blocks`, now conformance.rs:3442) even with the flush. So flushing does
NOT make the on-disk GD reflect a compressed write under deferral — meaning the
compressed-write path (`ext4_write_compressed`) does not update the GD-visible free-block
count when GDT is deferred (the ORIGINAL doc comment on `gdt_persistence_deferred` was
right: "those paths persist via a boundary the GDT flush pass is not wired into"). The
bitmap check passes (bitmap eager), so it is specifically the GD/GroupStats accounting on
the compressed path that diverges under deferral. Fixing THAT (wire the compressed-write
GD update / flush pass into that path, then re-validate the full suite + e2fsck) is the
real owner-lane work — NOT a test tweak.

**Confirmed SAFE parts (so the owner has them):** e2fsck IS clean under `FFS_SKIP_GDT=1`
across create/mkdir/delbench (verified directly this turn); allocation stays correct
because it reads the authoritative bitmap (stale on-disk GD after crash is a cosmetic
free-count hint e2fsck recomputes). So the blocker is narrowly the compressed-write GD
path, not general durability. Attempt shelved (git stash, NOT dropped); tree clean; no
code landed on main.

**ATTEMPT 2 (also FAILED, shelved) — 2026-07-02:** tried the cleaner unblock that touches
NO conformance test — flip `gdt_persistence_deferred` default ON + force a
`ext4_flush_group_descriptors` (no-op in eager mode) at the END of `ext4_write_compressed`
so the compressed-write GD stays eager while the mainline create/write win defers. **The
